patient_vignette: A 59-year-old man presents with a firm, painless left level II cervical lymph node that has enlarged over 6 weeks. He has no dysphagia, odynophagia, otalgia, weight loss, or visible mucosal lesion on careful head and neck examination. Flexible nasopharyngolaryngoscopy is unrevealing, and contrast-enhanced CT of the neck does not identify a clear primary tumor. The neck mass is easily palpable and accessible for physical examination and biopsy. The clinician is considering PET/CT to look for nodal disease and to help localize a primary site before panendoscopy.
proposed_actions: Use PET/CT prior to panendoscopy to help identify the primary tumor in a patient with cervical lymph node metastases and an unknown primary after conventional imaging or clinical examination.
Do not rely on PET for assessment of the accessible neck mass because clinically accessible sites did not benefit from PET imaging.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Clinically N-0 neck
Guideline Action: Do not use PET as a better alternative to conventional imaging for nodal staging
Source Statement: In the clinically N-0 neck, PET does not appear to be better than conventional imaging, because of an unacceptably high false-negative rate.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Clinically accessible site of suspected recurrence or surveillance after treatment
Guideline Action: Do not rely on PET for assessment because accessible sites did not benefit from PET imaging
Source Statement: With respect to recurrence and tumour surveillance after treatment, the evidence suggests that sites of disease that are clinically accessible for assessment did not benefit from PET imaging.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: PREOP_STAGING
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient has head and neck squamous cell carcinoma and conventional imaging is equivocal, or treatment may be significantly modified
Guideline Action: Use PET for M staging and bilateral nodal staging
Source Statement: PET is recommended in the M and bilateral nodal staging of all patients with head and neck squamous cell carcinoma where conventional imaging is equivocal, or where treatment may be significantly modified.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: PREOP_STAGING
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Primary site is unknown after conventional imaging
Guideline Action: Use PET after conventional imaging and before or in addition to diagnostic panendoscopy
Source Statement: PET is recommended in all patients after conventional imaging and in addition to, or prior to, diagnostic panendoscopy where the primary site is unknown.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient has cervical lymph node metastases and the primary site is unknown after conventional imaging or clinical examination
Guideline Action: Use PET/CT prior to panendoscopy to help identify the primary tumor
Source Statement: One primary study indicated that patients with cervical metastasis and an unknown primary site after undergoing conventional imaging or clinical examination benefit from PET/CT prior to panendoscopy.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: PREOP_STAGING
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient has nasopharyngeal carcinoma and conventional imaging is equivocal
Guideline Action: Use PET for staging
Source Statement: PET is recommended for staging and assessment of recurrence of patients with nasopharyngeal carcinoma if conventional imaging is equivocal.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: RECURRENCE_OR_RESTAGING
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient has nasopharyngeal carcinoma and conventional imaging is equivocal for recurrence assessment
Guideline Action: Use PET for assessment of recurrence
Source Statement: PET is recommended for staging and assessment of recurrence of patients with nasopharyngeal carcinoma if conventional imaging is equivocal.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: PREOP_STAGING
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Need to detect distant metastatic disease in head and neck cancer
Guideline Action: Use PET because it is better than conventional imaging for distant metastasis detection
Source Statement: There is strong evidence that PET imaging is valuable in detecting distant metastatic disease and is better than conventional imaging.
proposed_actions_text:
- Use PET/CT prior to panendoscopy to help identify the primary tumor in a patient with cervical lymph node metastases and an unknown primary after conventional imaging or clinical examination.
- Do not rely on PET for assessment of the accessible neck mass because clinically accessible sites did not benefit from PET imaging.

patient_vignette: A 58-year-old postmenopausal woman is seen after lumpectomy for a 1.8 cm invasive ductal carcinoma. Pathology shows ER-positive, HER2-negative disease with 2 of 12 axillary lymph nodes involved, no distant metastases, and intermediate grade. She is otherwise healthy and is anxious to avoid chemotherapy if it would not add meaningful benefit. Her oncologist is deciding on adjuvant systemic therapy and is considering whether a genomic assay could help refine the chemotherapy discussion. The team plans to use the result for this single treatment decision and does not intend to send multiple different assays on the same specimen.
proposed_actions: Use a multigene profiling assay to support the adjuvant chemotherapy decision, since she has node-positive disease with 1-3 positive nodes and relevant clinical/pathological factors.
Request only one multigene profiling assay for this specific treatment decision.
Consider using a multigene profiling assay to help guide systemic therapy in this early-stage ER-positive, HER2-negative breast cancer case.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: breast cancer is HER2-positive
Guideline Action: Do not use multigene profiling assays because evidence is insufficient.
Source Statement: There is currently insufficient evidence to use multigene profiling assays among patients with either HER2-positive ... breast cancers.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: breast cancer is triple negative
Guideline Action: Do not use multigene profiling assays because evidence is insufficient.
Source Statement: There is currently insufficient evidence to use multigene profiling assays among patients with either HER2-positive or triple negative breast cancers.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: considering multiple multigene profiling assays on the same tumour specimen for one treatment decision
Guideline Action: Do not order multiple different multigene profiling assays on the same tumour specimen for a single decision.
Source Statement: Requesting multiple tests with different multigene profiling assays on an individual tumour specimen to guide a single treatment decision is discouraged.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: node-positive ER-positive HER2-negative breast cancer with higher risk features and considering withholding adjuvant chemotherapy
Guideline Action: Do not rely on Prosigna, EndoPredict/EPclin, or Breast Cancer Index to justify withholding adjuvant chemotherapy.
Source Statement: The Prosigna, EndoPredict/EPclin, and Breast Cancer Index assays can identify low-risk node-positive patients whose prognostic outcomes are favourable; however, these assays have not demonstrated predictive evidence to support withholding adjuvant chemotherapy among higher risk, node-positive, ER-positive, HER2-negative breast cancer patients.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: considering MammaPrint, Oncotype DX, Prosigna, or EndoPredict to extend adjuvant endocrine therapy
Guideline Action: Do not use these assays to guide extension of adjuvant endocrine therapy because evidence is insufficient.
Source Statement: MammaPrint, Oncotype DX, Prosigna, and EndoPredict currently have insufficient evidence to guide extension of adjuvant endocrine therapy;

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has lymph node-negative disease or 1-3 positive lymph nodes and is being considered for adjuvant chemotherapy, with supporting clinical/pathological/patient factors
Guideline Action: Use a multigene profiling assay to support the adjuvant chemotherapy decision.
Source Statement: Multigene profiling assays are recommended for use in patients with lymph node-negative or lymph node-positive (1-3 lymph nodes) disease who are under consideration for adjuvant chemotherapy if the use is supported by other clinical, pathological, or patient-related factors.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: one multigene profiling assay is being ordered for a patient
Guideline Action: Request only one assay per patient for a specific treatment decision.
Source Statement: One multigene profiling assay should be requested per patient to guide a specific treatment decision.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: early-stage ER-positive, HER2-negative breast cancer and systemic therapy decision is needed
Guideline Action: Consider using a multigene profiling assay to help guide systemic therapy.
Source Statement: In patients with early-stage estrogen receptor (ER)-positive/human epidermal growth factor 2 (HER2)-negative breast cancer, clinicians should consider using multigene profiling assays ... to help guide the use of systemic therapy.
proposed_actions_text:
- Use a multigene profiling assay to support the adjuvant chemotherapy decision, since she has node-positive disease with 1-3 positive nodes and relevant clinical/pathological factors.
- Request only one multigene profiling assay for this specific treatment decision.
- Consider using a multigene profiling assay to help guide systemic therapy in this early-stage ER-positive, HER2-negative breast cancer case.

patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man presents after a transrectal biopsy confirmed acinar adenocarcinoma of the prostate, Gleason 4+3=7, with PSA 14.8 ng/mL and cancer in 6 of 12 cores. He has no bone pain or urinary retention, and his exam is otherwise unremarkable. His urologist is discussing radical prostatectomy versus radiation-based treatment, and the team notes that knowing whether there is extraprostatic extension would affect the surgical plan and whether nerve-sparing could be attempted. He is otherwise fit and wants definitive treatment.
proposed_actions: Consider multiparametric MRI (mpMRI) for pre-treatment local staging to assess extraprostatic extension (EPE).
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: PREOP_STAGING
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Newly diagnosed biopsy-confirmed prostate cancer; intermediate- or high-risk; being considered for radical therapy; knowledge of extraprostatic extension would alter management
Guideline Action: Consider multiparametric MRI (mpMRI) for pre-treatment local staging to assess extraprostatic extension (EPE).
Source Statement: Multiparametric MRI (mpMRI) use for pre-treatment local staging of prostate cancer is a reasonable option for assessment of extraprostatic extension (EPE) in intermediate-and highrisk patients being considered for radical therapy if knowledge of EPE will alter management.
proposed_actions_text: - Consider multiparametric MRI (mpMRI) for pre-treatment local staging to assess extraprostatic extension (EPE).

patient_vignette: A 47-year-old woman presents with postcoital bleeding and pelvic pain. Biopsy confirms squamous cell carcinoma of the cervix. MRI shows a 5.5 cm cervical mass with parametrial extension but no distant metastases, consistent with locally advanced disease. Her oncology team is planning definitive pelvic radiotherapy with brachytherapy. She has adequate renal function and performance status to receive chemotherapy. The patient asks whether anything should be added to radiation to improve outcomes.
proposed_actions: Offer concurrent cisplatin with the course of radiotherapy.
Offer concurrent cisplatin-based chemotherapy with radiotherapy for locally advanced cervical cancer.
Consider weekly cisplatin 40 mg/m2 when selecting a concurrent cisplatin regimen.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Women with cervical cancer for whom radiotherapy is being considered
Guideline Action: Offer concurrent cisplatin with the course of radiotherapy.
Source Statement: Women with cervical cancer for whom treatment with radiotherapy is being considered (described above) should be offered concurrent cisplatin with their course of radiotherapy.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Women with cervical cancer receiving cisplatin-based chemotherapy plus radiotherapy compared with radiotherapy alone or hydroxyurea
Guideline Action: Use concurrent cisplatin-based chemotherapy with radiotherapy because pooled survival favored this approach.
Source Statement: Pooled survival rates detected a statistically significant effect in favour of cisplatin-based chemotherapy plus radiotherapy compared with radiotherapy alone or with hydroxyurea (relative risk of death, 0.74; 95% confidence interval, 0.64 to 0.86).

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Women with locally advanced cervical cancer treated with radiotherapy
Guideline Action: Offer concurrent cisplatin-based chemotherapy with radiotherapy.
Source Statement: The pooled relative risk of death among the six trials that enrolled only women with locally advanced cervical cancer was 0.78 (95% confidence interval, 0.67 to 0.90) in favour of cisplatin-based chemotherapy and radiotherapy.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Women with high-risk early-stage cervical cancer treated with radiotherapy
Guideline Action: Offer concurrent cisplatin-based chemotherapy with radiotherapy.
Source Statement: The pooled relative risk for the two trials in high-risk early-stage disease also demonstrated a significant benefit for the addition of cisplatin-based chemotherapy to radiotherapy (relative risk, 0.56; 95% confidence interval, 0.41 to 0.77).

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Women with bulky clinical stage IB cervical cancer (>4 cm) who are treated with radiotherapy
Guideline Action: Offer concurrent cisplatin with radiotherapy.
Source Statement: These recommendations apply to women with cervical cancer for whom primary treatment with radiotherapy is being considered: -those with bulky clinical stage IB (>4 cm) cervical cancer, who are treated with radiotherapy,

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Women with high-risk early-stage cervical cancer after hysterectomy who will be treated with radiotherapy
Guideline Action: Offer concurrent cisplatin with radiotherapy.
Source Statement: These recommendations apply to women with cervical cancer for whom primary treatment with radiotherapy is being considered: -those with high-risk early-stage cervical cancer (node-positive or margin-positive), who will be treated with radiotherapy following hysterectomy.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Women with locally advanced cervical cancer for whom primary treatment with radiotherapy is being considered
Guideline Action: Offer concurrent cisplatin with radiotherapy.
Source Statement: These recommendations apply to women with cervical cancer for whom primary treatment with radiotherapy is being considered: -those with locally advanced cervical cancer,

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: When choosing a cisplatin regimen for concurrent chemoradiation
Guideline Action: Consider weekly cisplatin 40 mg/m2 based on available toxicity data.
Source Statement: Based on the review of the available toxicity data from the randomized controlled trials, the Disease Site Group felt that cisplatinum should be given weekly (40 mg/m2).
proposed_actions_text:
- Offer concurrent cisplatin with the course of radiotherapy.
- Offer concurrent cisplatin-based chemotherapy with radiotherapy for locally advanced cervical cancer.
- Consider weekly cisplatin 40 mg/m2 when selecting a concurrent cisplatin regimen.

patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man underwent right hemicolectomy for a moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma found on screening colonoscopy. Final pathology shows pT3N0 stage II colon cancer with clear margins, 18 lymph nodes examined, no lymphovascular invasion, no perineural invasion, no bowel obstruction, and no perforation. He has recovered well after surgery and is asking whether he should start chemotherapy to reduce recurrence risk. His oncology team is reviewing the pathology and discussing postoperative management.
proposed_actions: Do not use routine adjuvant chemotherapy for all stage II patients.
Do not perform MSI/dMMR testing for this decision because the patient has stage II disease without high-risk features and the result would not influence the adjuvant chemotherapy decision.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: adult patient with resected stage II colon cancer
Guideline Action: Do not use routine adjuvant chemotherapy for all stage II patients.
Source Statement: The routine use of adjuvant chemotherapy for all patients with stage II colon cancer is not recommended.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: stage II patient without high-risk features who does not require adjuvant chemotherapy
Guideline Action: Do not perform MSI/dMMR testing for this decision.
Source Statement: In stage II (in the absence of high-risk features) where a patient does not require adjuvant chemotherapy, MSI/dMMR testing is not recommended as it will not influence that decision.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: stage II colon cancer with MSI/dMMR after surgery
Guideline Action: Do not give fluoropyrimidine monotherapy as adjuvant treatment.
Source Statement: Adjuvant chemotherapy with a fluoropyrimidine monotherapy regimen following surgery in patients who have MSI/dMMR is not recommended.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: high-risk stage II colon cancer treated with adjuvant therapy
Guideline Action: Use a fluoropyrimidine.
Source Statement: When treated with adjuvant therapy, high-risk stage II patients should receive a fluoropyrimidine.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: stage II patient being considered for adjuvant chemotherapy
Guideline Action: Perform MSI/dMMR testing.
Source Statement: MSI/dMMR testing should be performed for all stage II patients for whom adjuvant chemotherapy is being considered.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: adult patient with resected stage III colon cancer
Guideline Action: Use an oxaliplatin-containing regimen such as FOLFOX, FLOX, or XELOX.
Source Statement: These oxaliplatin-containing regimens have demonstrated superior overall survival when compared with 5-FU plus LV and are the recommended regimens.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: low-risk stage III colon cancer (T1-3 N1)
Guideline Action: Prefer 3 months of CAPOX over FOLFOX.
Source Statement: Low-risk stage III (T1-3 N1): 3 months of CAPOX is preferred over FOLFOX.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: high-risk stage III colon cancer (T4 +/- N2)
Guideline Action: Give 6 months of oxaliplatin-based chemotherapy with CAPOX or FOLFOX.
Source Statement: High-risk stage III (T4 +/-N2): 6 months of oxaliplatin-based chemotherapy (CAPOX or FOLFOX).
proposed_actions_text:
- Do not use routine adjuvant chemotherapy for all stage II patients.
- Do not perform MSI/dMMR testing for this decision because the patient has stage II disease without high-risk features and the result would not influence the adjuvant chemotherapy decision.

patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man is seen in clinic for follow-up after a recent oncology visit. He asks whether he should start any new treatment based on the document he was given, but the handout contains only report citation information, website details, and contact information for the guideline office. There are no clinical findings, diagnoses, staging details, or treatment recommendations included in the text he brought. He has no new symptoms and is mainly seeking clarification about what the document means for his care.
proposed_actions: Do not apply any guideline-based clinical management rule from this document.
Do not derive any clinical action from the report citation, website, or contact details alone.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 50
Recommendation Class: NO_RECOMMENDATION
Activation Condition: No clinical recommendations are present in the provided text
Guideline Action: Do not extract any treatment, staging, or diagnostic action from this text
Source Statement: Recommendations Section 2: Recommendations and Key Evidence Section 3: Guideline Methods Overview Section 4: Systematic Review Section 5: Internal and External Review

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 50
Recommendation Class: NO_RECOMMENDATION
Activation Condition: Document contains only administrative and copyright/contact information
Guideline Action: No clinical action should be derived from the document content
Source Statement: For information about this document, please contact Alex Sun, the lead author, through the PEBC via: Phone: 905-527-4322 ext. 42822 Fax: 905 526-6775 E-mail: ccopgi@mcmaster.ca

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 50
Recommendation Class: NO_RECOMMENDATION
Activation Condition: Text provides report citation and website/contact details only
Guideline Action: Do not apply any guideline-based clinical management rule
Source Statement: For information about the PEBC and the most current version of all reports, please visit the CCO website at http://www.cancercare.on.ca or contact the PEBC office at: Phone: 905-527-4322 ext. 42822 Fax: 905 526-6775 E-mail: ccopgi@mcmaster.ca

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 50
Recommendation Class: NO_RECOMMENDATION
Activation Condition: Text states copyright and reproduction restrictions only
Guideline Action: No clinical recommendation can be extracted from this statement
Source Statement: Copyright This report is copyrighted by Cancer Care Ontario; the report and the illustrations herein may not be reproduced without the express written permission of Cancer Care Ontario.
proposed_actions_text:
- Do not apply any guideline-based clinical management rule from this document.
- Do not derive any clinical action from the report citation, website, or contact details alone.

patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man presents with 8 months of lower urinary tract symptoms, including hesitancy, weak stream, and nocturia twice nightly. He denies bone pain, weight loss, fever, dysuria, hematuria, or erectile dysfunction. He has no known urinary infection symptoms and has not had any recent treatment for urinary issues. On review, his main concern is whether he might have prostate disease because of the persistent urinary symptoms. He is otherwise well and takes no regular medications. His exam is unremarkable aside from mild suprapubic fullness; no prior PSA testing has been done.
proposed_actions: Perform a digital rectal examination (DRE).
Discuss PSA testing and consider PSA based on individual risk assessment.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient has erectile dysfunction alone
Guideline Action: Do not use erectile dysfunction alone as a symptom prompting DRE and PSA testing for metastatic prostate cancer
Source Statement: The working group did not think it necessary for a man with erectile dysfunction to undergo a DRE and PSA test and therefore excluded it as a symptom of metastatic prostate cancer

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient has unexplained hematuria
Guideline Action: Do not treat unexplained hematuria as part of the prostate cancer care algorithm; arrange urologic assessment as appropriate
Source Statement: The working group also excluded unexplained hematuria as a symptom of metastatic prostate cancer... hematuria requires urologic assessment but is not part of a prostate cancer care algorithm

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient has symptoms of metastatic prostate cancer and is age 40 or older
Guideline Action: Perform digital rectal examination (DRE) and prostate-specific antigen (PSA) testing
Source Statement: patients with symptoms of metastatic prostate cancer should have a DRE and PSA was endorsed

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient has lower urinary tract symptoms (LUTS)
Guideline Action: Perform DRE
Source Statement: a man with LUTS should have a DRE

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient has LUTS and PSA testing is being considered
Guideline Action: Exclude urinary infection before PSA testing
Source Statement: The working group also endorsed the recommendations to exclude urinary infection before PSA testing

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient has LUTS and PSA testing is being considered
Guideline Action: Postpone PSA testing for at least one month after treatment
Source Statement: The working group also endorsed the recommendations to... postpone PSA testing for at least one month after treatment

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient has an abnormal incidental PSA result
Guideline Action: Perform DRE
Source Statement: if an incidental PSA test was abnormal, then standard practise would be to perform a DRE

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Patient has LUTS
Guideline Action: Discuss PSA testing and consider PSA based on individual risk assessment
Source Statement: a man with LUTS should have a DRE and a discussion about PSA testing
proposed_actions_text:
- Perform a digital rectal examination (DRE).
- Discuss PSA testing and consider PSA based on individual risk assessment.

patient_vignette: A 66-year-old man with newly diagnosed prostate adenocarcinoma presents after biopsy and staging show low-risk disease: PSA 5.2 ng/mL, Gleason 3+3=6, and cT1c exam. He has a life expectancy well beyond 10 years and, after discussing his options, says he prefers active treatment rather than surveillance because of significant anxiety about living with untreated cancer. He is otherwise healthy and eligible for local therapy. In clinic, he asks about surgery versus radiation and wants a clear explanation of the available approaches before deciding. The team reviews his case in a multidisciplinary setting and discusses the main treatment choices with him in a balanced way.
proposed_actions: Offer low-dose rate brachytherapy alone, external beam radiation therapy alone, and radical prostatectomy as treatment options for this eligible patient with low-risk prostate cancer.
Counsel the patient about all applicable management options in a balanced, objective manner, preferably from multiple disciplines.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: newly diagnosed low-risk prostate cancer requiring or choosing active treatment and eligible for treatment
Guideline Action: Offer low-dose rate brachytherapy alone, external beam radiation therapy alone, and/or radical prostatectomy.
Source Statement: For patients with low-risk prostate cancer who require or choose active treatment, LDR alone, EBRT alone, or RP should be offered to eligible patients.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: newly diagnosed intermediate-risk prostate cancer choosing EBRT with or without androgen-deprivation therapy and eligible for brachytherapy
Guideline Action: Offer brachytherapy boost using low-dose rate or high-dose rate brachytherapy.
Source Statement: For patients with intermediate-risk prostate cancer choosing EBRT with or without androgen-deprivation therapy (ADT), brachytherapy boost (LDR or high-dose rate should be offered to eligible patients.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: newly diagnosed high-risk prostate cancer receiving EBRT and androgen-deprivation therapy and eligible for brachytherapy
Guideline Action: Offer brachytherapy boost using low-dose rate or high-dose rate brachytherapy.
Source Statement: For patients with high-risk prostate cancer receiving EBRT and ADT, brachytherapy boost (LDR or HDR) should be offered to eligible patients.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient is receiving low-dose rate brachytherapy
Guideline Action: Use iodine-125 or palladium-103 as reasonable isotope options.
Source Statement: 125 I and 103 Pd are each reasonable isotope options for patients receiving LDR brachytherapy;

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient with prostate cancer is being managed under this guideline
Guideline Action: Encourage participation in clinical trials testing novel or targeted approaches.
Source Statement: Patients should be encouraged to participate in clinical trials to test novel or targeted approaches to this disease.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient is being counseled about management options
Guideline Action: Counsel about all applicable management options in a balanced, objective manner, preferably from multiple disciplines.
Source Statement: Patients should be counseled about all their management options (surgery, EBRT, active surveillance, as applicable) in a balanced, objective manner, preferably from multiple disciplines.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: brachytherapy is being performed
Guideline Action: Perform brachytherapy at a center following strict quality-assurance standards.
Source Statement: Brachytherapy should be performed at a center following strict quality-assurance standards.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has intermediate-risk prostate cancer and is choosing EBRT with or without ADT
Guideline Action: Offer brachytherapy boost with LDR or HDR to eligible patients.
Source Statement: For patients with intermediate-risk prostate cancer choosing EBRT with or without androgen-deprivation therapy (ADT), brachytherapy boost (LDR or high-dose rate should be offered to eligible patients.
proposed_actions_text:
- Offer low-dose rate brachytherapy alone, external beam radiation therapy alone, and radical prostatectomy as treatment options for this eligible patient with low-risk prostate cancer.
- Counsel the patient about all applicable management options in a balanced, objective manner, preferably from multiple disciplines.

patient_vignette: A 57-year-old postmenopausal woman presents with 3 weeks of intermittent left lower abdominal bloating and early satiety. Pelvic exam suggests a fullness in the left adnexa, and a recent office ultrasound was limited by overlying bowel gas, so the mass is not well characterized. She has no known prior ovarian disease. The clinician is considering the next imaging step to clarify whether this isolated ovarian mass is benign or malignant. Because the initial ultrasound is technically unreliable, additional imaging is needed to better define malignant potential. There is no clear evidence yet of extra-ovarian spread, but the adnexal lesion remains suspicious and incompletely assessed.
proposed_actions: Use MRI to help clarify the malignant potential because ultrasound may be unreliable.
Use sonography, particularly 3D sonography, MRI, or CT imaging to help differentiate malignant from benign ovarian masses.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: considering Doppler technology alone for adnexal mass evaluation
Guideline Action: Do not use Doppler technology alone to evaluate the mass.
Source Statement: Evaluation of an adnexal mass by Doppler technology alone is not recommended.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: considering serum CA-125 as a stand-alone test
Guideline Action: Do not use CA-125 alone to distinguish benign from malignant adnexal masses.
Source Statement: As a stand-alone modality, serum CA-125 is not recommended for distinguishing between benign and malignant adnexal masses.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: adult woman with a suspicious isolated ovarian mass and ultrasound is technically feasible
Guideline Action: Use transvaginal sonography as the first imaging modality.
Source Statement: Transvaginal sonography should be the first modality of choice, where technically feasible, in patients with a suspicious, isolated ovarian mass.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: need to differentiate malignant from benign ovarian masses
Guideline Action: Use sonography, particularly 3D sonography, MRI, or CT imaging for differentiation.
Source Statement: Sonography, particularly three-dimensional (3D) sonography, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), and computerized tomography (CT) imaging are each recommended for differentiating malignant from benign ovarian masses.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: ultrasound may be unreliable
Guideline Action: Use MRI to help clarify malignant potential.
Source Statement: MRI is the most appropriate test to help clarify the malignant potential in patients where ultrasound may be unreliable.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: extra-ovarian disease is suspected or needs to be ruled out
Guideline Action: Use CT imaging to assess for extra-ovarian disease.
Source Statement: CT is most useful in cases where extra ovarian disease is suspected or needs to be ruled out.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: using Doppler technology in adnexal mass evaluation
Guideline Action: Combine Doppler technology with morphological assessment.
Source Statement: Doppler technology should be combined with a morphological assessment.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: considering morphological scoring systems for adnexal mass evaluation
Guideline Action: Use a morphological scoring system to differentiate benign from malignant masses.
Source Statement: Morphological scoring systems can be used to differentiate benign from malignant adnexal masses.
proposed_actions_text:
- Use MRI to help clarify the malignant potential because ultrasound may be unreliable.
- Use sonography, particularly 3D sonography, MRI, or CT imaging to help differentiate malignant from benign ovarian masses.

patient_vignette: A 19-year-old woman presents for a routine visit and mentions that she has been using a tanning bed at a salon twice weekly for the past 3 months to “get a base tan” before spring break. She says several friends started tanning in high school, and she has been considering increasing her sessions because she likes the color it gives her skin. She has no current skin complaints and no personal history of skin cancer, but she is unaware of any specific risks associated with indoor tanning. She asks whether occasional tanning bed use is safe if she does not burn.
proposed_actions: Avoid use of indoor tanning devices to reduce melanoma risk.
Avoid use of indoor tanning devices, especially at a younger age.
Recognize that there is no safe lower limit of exposure to artificial UVR from indoor tanning devices.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: individual uses indoor tanning devices
Guideline Action: avoid use of indoor tanning devices to reduce melanoma risk
Source Statement: Use of indoor tanning devices should be avoided to reduce risk of melanoma.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: individual is considering or using indoor tanning devices
Guideline Action: avoid use of indoor tanning devices, especially at younger age
Source Statement: All individuals should avoid use of indoor tanning devices, especially those at a younger age.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 50
Recommendation Class: NO_RECOMMENDATION
Activation Condition: individual is exposed to artificial UVR from indoor tanning devices
Guideline Action: recognize that there is no safe lower limit of exposure
Source Statement: There is no safe lower limit of exposure to artificial UVR from indoor tanning devices.
proposed_actions_text:
- Avoid use of indoor tanning devices to reduce melanoma risk.
- Avoid use of indoor tanning devices, especially at a younger age.
- Recognize that there is no safe lower limit of exposure to artificial UVR from indoor tanning devices.

patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man presents with 3 weeks of progressive fatigue, easy bruising, and intermittent low-grade fevers. CBC shows hemoglobin 8.7 g/dL, platelets 42,000/µL, and WBC 18,400/µL with circulating blasts. Bone marrow biopsy confirms acute myeloid leukemia. Cytogenetic analysis shows a normal karyotype, and there is no prior history of myelodysplastic syndrome or chemotherapy exposure. He is being evaluated at initial diagnosis for prognostic and treatment-planning biomarkers.
proposed_actions: Include DNMT3A mutation testing as a biomarker test in this patient with cytogenetically normal AML.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient has cytogenetically normal AML
Guideline Action: Include DNMT3A mutation testing as a biomarker test
Source Statement: DNMT3A mutation testing should be included as a biomarker test in cytogenetically normal AML patients.
proposed_actions_text: - Include DNMT3A mutation testing as a biomarker test in this patient with cytogenetically normal AML.

patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man is newly diagnosed with symptomatic multiple myeloma after presenting with anemia, lytic bone lesions, and elevated serum protein. He has good functional status, walks daily, and has no major comorbidities. Baseline renal function and cardiac evaluation are acceptable, and he is being evaluated after induction therapy with high-dose chemotherapy for transplant-based treatment. He asks whether transplant should be part of his initial plan. He has not yet undergone any stem cell transplant and has no history of relapse or refractory disease.
proposed_actions: Offer autologous stem cell transplantation as part of the initial treatment plan.
Offer a single autologous stem cell transplant after initial high-dose chemotherapy, given that he is under 65 and free of severe comorbidities.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with newly diagnosed multiple myeloma being considered for initial transplant-based treatment
Guideline Action: Offer autologous stem cell transplantation as part of the initial treatment plan.
Source Statement: Autologous SCT is the recommended treatment option for patients with newly diagnosed MM, as part of the initial treatment plan.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Newly diagnosed MM patient is free from severe comorbidities and younger than 65 years after initial high-dose chemotherapy
Guideline Action: Offer a single autologous stem cell transplant.
Source Statement: Evidence included in four Clinical Practice Guidelines (CPGs) (1)(2)(3)(4) suggests that a single transplant with autologous SCT should be offered to all MM patients who are free from severe co-morbidities and who are younger than 65 years of age, following the initial treatment with high-dose chemotherapy.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: TREATMENT_RESPONSE
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient did not achieve complete remission after initial autologous SCT
Guideline Action: Offer a second autologous stem cell transplant.
Source Statement: Evidence included in two CPGs (1,2) suggests that double autologous SCT should be offered to patients who did not achieve a complete remission after their initial autologous SCT.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: RECURRENCE_OR_RESTAGING
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient relapses after a long remission following a single transplant
Guideline Action: Offer a second autologous transplant.
Source Statement: ...the SCT Steering Committee's consensus opinion is that patients who relapse after a long remission following a single transplant should be offered a second transplant.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Newly diagnosed MM patient is age 65 or older but has good performance status and no comorbidities that contraindicate transplantation
Guideline Action: Consider offering autologous stem cell transplantation.
Source Statement: The SCT Steering Committee acknowledged that, while the evidence upon which the CPGs were based almost uniformly excluded patients older than age 65, there was no reason not to offer SCT to patients 65 or older who have good performance status and no co-morbidities that would be a contraindication to transplantation.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: TREATMENT_RESPONSE
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Patient with MM responds to first autologous transplant with less than a very good partial response and has no progressive disease
Guideline Action: Consider tandem (double) autologous stem cell transplantation.
Source Statement: Tandem (double) autologous SCT is an option for patients with MM who respond to the first autologous transplant with less than a very good partial response, but not progressive disease.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: TREATMENT_RESPONSE
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Patient has high-risk MM
Guideline Action: Consider allogeneic transplantation, preferably within an investigative study.
Source Statement: Allogeneic transplantation is an option for patients with high-risk MM preferably within the context of an investigative study.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: TREATMENT_RESPONSE
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Patient with high-risk MM is younger than 50 years and is not expected to benefit from autologous SCT
Guideline Action: Consider allogeneic stem cell transplantation only in an investigative setting.
Source Statement: ...it may be offered to patients <50 years of age who are not expected to benefit from autologous SCT (e.g., chromosome 13 deletion) within the investigative setting only (1,2,4).
proposed_actions_text:
- Offer autologous stem cell transplantation as part of the initial treatment plan.
- Offer a single autologous stem cell transplant after initial high-dose chemotherapy, given that he is under 65 and free of severe comorbidities.

patient_vignette: A 63-year-old man presents to a community clinic asking for “a quick lung cancer scan” because his brother recently died of lung cancer. He has a 22 pack-year smoking history and quit 18 years ago. He feels well, has no respiratory symptoms, and has no known cancer diagnosis. The clinic is a small rural practice without a formal lung cancer screening pathway, specialist counseling support, or access to structured follow-up and diagnostic services. The patient asks whether he should be screened based on his family history and past smoking. The clinician is considering offering low-dose CT screening during the visit.
proposed_actions: Do not provide lung cancer screening in this setting because it lacks the experience and expertise needed for high-risk identification, interpretation, counselling, and diagnostic techniques.
Do not expand screening eligibility solely on the basis of family history or other non-smoking risk factors.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Screening is being offered outside a centre with experience and expertise in high-risk identification, interpretation, counselling, and diagnostic techniques
Guideline Action: Do not provide screening in that setting.
Source Statement: The Working Group believes strongly that screening outside a centre with experience and expertise in identifying the high-risk population, interpreting results and counselling patients, and performing the appropriate diagnostic techniques is ill advised.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Screening is being offered in a setting without comprehensive care
Guideline Action: Do not offer screening in that setting.
Source Statement: The ASCO guideline also supports screening of high-risk individuals, but only in the settings that can deliver comprehensive care such as that provided to NLST participants.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Person is being screened based on other risk factors alone
Guideline Action: Do not expand eligibility solely on the basis of those other risk factors.
Source Statement: there is currently insufficient evidence to include participants based on other risk factors such as family history, passive smoking, occupational exposure, radon exposure, previous cancer, and other diseases.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Person is high risk for lung cancer and eligible for screening
Guideline Action: Screen with low-dose CT (LDCT).
Source Statement: Screening for lung cancer with LDCT is recommended in high-risk populations defined as persons 55 to 74 years of age with a minimum smoking history of ≥30 pack-years who currently smoke or have quit within the past 15 years and are disease free at the time of screening.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Person is 55 to 74 years old, has at least 30 pack-years smoking history, currently smokes or quit within past 15 years, and is disease free
Guideline Action: Offer LDCT screening.
Source Statement: Screening for lung cancer with LDCT is recommended in high-risk populations defined as persons 55 to 74 years of age with a minimum smoking history of ≥30 pack-years who currently smoke or have quit within the past 15 years and are disease free at the time of screening.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Screening program is being designed or delivered
Guideline Action: Use an organized, population-based screening program rather than ad hoc opportunistic screening.
Source Statement: it is the opinion of the Working Group that screening should be conducted in a manner similar to the NLST trial: that is, in an organized fashion.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Screening program is implemented
Guideline Action: Include smoking-cessation counseling in the program.
Source Statement: The inclusion of smoking-cessation counseling within the screening program is crucial.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Screening program is implemented
Guideline Action: Build in data collection, monitoring, quality assurance, and evaluation from the start.
Source Statement: If elements of data collection and monitoring, quality assurance, and evaluation are built into the screening program from the start, it can be modified while in operation.
proposed_actions_text:
- Do not provide lung cancer screening in this setting because it lacks the experience and expertise needed for high-risk identification, interpretation, counselling, and diagnostic techniques.
- Do not expand screening eligibility solely on the basis of family history or other non-smoking risk factors.

patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man presents with 3 months of epigastric pain, 8 kg unintentional weight loss, and new jaundice. Contrast CT shows a 2.8 cm mass in the pancreatic head with mild biliary dilation, but the lesion is still indeterminate on imaging. Endoscopic ultrasound-guided biopsy is attempted twice and is nondiagnostic due to scant tissue. He has no known metastatic disease, and the surgical team is considering whether he could be a candidate for potentially curative resection if the diagnosis is confirmed. The oncology team asks whether PET/CT should be used next to help clarify the diagnosis and guide management.
proposed_actions: Use PET/CT if a positive result would lead to surgical resection for diagnosis and treatment.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Primary diagnosis of pancreatic cancer
Guideline Action: Do not use PET for primary diagnosis.
Source Statement: PET is not recommended for primary diagnosis of pancreatic cancer.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: TREATMENT_RESPONSE
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Evaluating treatment response outside a clinical trial
Guideline Action: Do not use PET to evaluate treatment response outside a clinical trial.
Source Statement: The above results are based on two small nonrandomized studies and therefore are not strong enough to make a recommendation for using PET in evaluating treatment response outside of a clinical trial.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: RECURRENCE_OR_RESTAGING
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Suspected recurrence of pancreatic cancer
Guideline Action: Do not use PET for clinical management of suspected recurrence.
Source Statement: PET is not recommended for clinical management of suspected recurrence, nor for restaging at the time of recurrence, due to insufficient evidence and lack of effective therapeutic options.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: RECURRENCE_OR_RESTAGING
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Restaging at the time of recurrence
Guideline Action: Do not use PET for restaging at recurrence.
Source Statement: PET is not recommended for clinical management of suspected recurrence, nor for restaging at the time of recurrence, due to insufficient evidence and lack of effective therapeutic options.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: RECURRENCE_OR_RESTAGING
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Recurrent disease after surgical resection
Guideline Action: Restrict PET imaging to clinical trials.
Source Statement: PET imaging in recurrent disease should be restricted to clinical trials.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: PREOP_STAGING
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient is a candidate for potentially curative surgical resection based on conventional staging
Guideline Action: Perform PET for staging.
Source Statement: PET is recommended for staging if a patient is a candidate for potentially curative surgical resection as determined by conventional staging.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Suspicious pancreatic mass and standard investigations are not confirmatory
Guideline Action: Consider PET/CT to help establish the diagnosis.
Source Statement: These pooled LRs suggest that PET and PET/CT offer small benefit in ruling in and ruling out pancreatic cancer when investigating a suspicious pancreatic mass; therefore, they may be useful in establishing a diagnosis when standard investigations are not confirmatory.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Biopsy is inconclusive or not possible and diagnosis remains in doubt
Guideline Action: Use PET/CT if a positive result would lead to surgical resection for diagnosis and treatment.
Source Statement: When biopsy is inconclusive or not possible and the diagnosis remains in doubt, the above evidence supports the use of PET/CT where a positive result would lead to surgical resection for purposes of both diagnosis and treatment.
proposed_actions_text: - Use PET/CT if a positive result would lead to surgical resection for diagnosis and treatment.

patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man presents with intermittent rectal bleeding and tenesmus. Colonoscopy shows a biopsy-proven adenocarcinoma in the low rectum, 5 cm from the anal verge. He is being evaluated for elective curative treatment and has no bowel obstruction, so a complete preoperative colonic examination is feasible. The surgical team is planning staging imaging before deciding on neoadjuvant therapy and resection strategy. The tumor is discussed as potentially close to the mesorectal fascia, but no prior imaging has yet been obtained. The patient asks whether transrectal ultrasound can determine whether the circumferential resection margin is involved.
proposed_actions: Do not use TRUS to predict CRM involvement.
Perform CT of the abdomen and pelvis for staging.
Perform CT of the chest or chest X-ray for chest staging.
Perform colonoscopy before surgery, if complete colonic examination is possible.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: low rectal cancer
Guideline Action: Do not use TRUS to predict CRM involvement.
Source Statement: TRUS should not be used to predict CRM involvement.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: considering changing preoperative therapy selection criteria
Guideline Action: Do not change selection criteria from T and N categories to distance to the mesorectal fascia, extramural depth of invasion, or extramural vascular invasion based on current evidence.
Source Statement: To date, there is insufficient evidence to change the current selection criteria from T and N categories to distance to the MRF (i.e., potential CRM), extramural depth of invasion (EMD) and/or extramural vascular invasion (EMVI).

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: all rectal cancer patients undergoing elective treatment
Guideline Action: Perform CT of the abdomen and pelvis.
Source Statement: Staging for all rectal cancer patients should include: CT of the abdomen and pelvis.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: all rectal cancer patients undergoing elective treatment
Guideline Action: Perform CT of the chest or chest X-ray for chest staging.
Source Statement: Staging for all rectal cancer patients should include: CT of the chest or chest X-ray.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: CT chest is used for postoperative surveillance
Guideline Action: Do CT of the chest at the same time as staging CT of the abdomen and pelvis.
Source Statement: If CT of the chest is used for postoperative surveillance, then CT of the chest should be done at the same time as staging CT of the abdomen and pelvis.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: chest X-ray is used for postoperative surveillance and chest X-ray shows abnormalities needing further workup
Guideline Action: Do CT of the chest.
Source Statement: If chest X-ray is used for postoperative surveillance, then CT of the chest is recommended only if abnormalities requiring further investigation were found on chest X-ray.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: CT chest is performed together with CT abdomen and pelvis
Guideline Action: Use intravenous contrast.
Source Statement: When CT of the chest is performed in combination with CT of the abdomen and pelvis, intravenous contrast is recommended.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: preoperative complete colonic examination is possible
Guideline Action: Perform colonoscopy before surgery.
Source Statement: Complete colonic examination by colonoscopy should be performed preoperatively, if possible.
proposed_actions_text:
- Do not use TRUS to predict CRM involvement.
- Perform CT of the abdomen and pelvis for staging.
- Perform CT of the chest or chest X-ray for chest staging.
- Perform colonoscopy before surgery, if complete colonic examination is possible.

patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man with biopsy-proven localized squamous cell carcinoma of the anal canal presents for treatment planning. Staging CT shows no distant metastases, and he is otherwise fit with good renal and marrow function. He asks whether he can start with a short course of chemotherapy before radiation to shrink the tumor first. The oncology team is also discussing whether radiation alone might be simpler, but he is able to tolerate combined treatment. He is HIV-negative and has no major comorbidities. The team plans definitive therapy in the coming weeks and is deciding on the concurrent regimen.
proposed_actions: Use concurrent chemotherapy and radiotherapy rather than radiotherapy alone to improve local control and decrease colostomy rates.
Do not use induction chemotherapy as standard care; treat it as investigational only.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with squamous cell cancer of the anal canal being considered for induction chemotherapy before concurrent chemoradiation
Guideline Action: Do not use induction chemotherapy as standard care; treat it as investigational only.
Source Statement: At this time, induction CT before concurrent CT and RT should be considered an investigational approach.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with squamous cell carcinoma of the anal canal after chemoradiation
Guideline Action: Do not give maintenance chemotherapy after chemoradiation.
Source Statement: James et al. 2013 (ACT II), studied maintenance chemotherapy versus none following chemoradiation and found that maintenance chemotherapy does not improve overall survival or colostomy-free survival. Therefore, maintenance chemotherapy following chemoradiation is not recommended in the management of squamous cell carcinoma of the anal canal.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with localized squamous cell cancer of the anal canal and consideration of radiotherapy alone
Guideline Action: Avoid radiotherapy alone when concurrent chemoradiation is feasible.
Source Statement: For all stages of localized squamous cell cancer of the anal canal, concurrent CT and RT is recommended over RT alone to improve local control and decrease colostomy rates.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with biopsy-proven localized squamous cell cancer of the anal canal
Guideline Action: Use concurrent chemotherapy and radiotherapy rather than radiotherapy alone to improve local control and decrease colostomy rates.
Source Statement: For all stages of localized squamous cell cancer of the anal canal, concurrent CT and RT is recommended over RT alone to improve local control and decrease colostomy rates.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with squamous cell cancer of the anal canal receiving concurrent chemoradiation
Guideline Action: Use 5-fluorouracil plus mitomycin C as the concurrent chemotherapy regimen with radiation.
Source Statement: The optimal CT drug combination for squamous cell cancer of the anal canal is 5fluorouracil (5FU) plus mitomycin C (MMC), given concurrently with radiation treatment.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult patient with squamous cell cancer of the anal canal who is HIV-positive
Guideline Action: Manage the patient the same way as a patient without known HIV, while monitoring for potentially higher toxicity.
Source Statement: It is the expert opinion of the Gastrointestinal Cancer Disease Site Group (GI DSG) that HIV-positive patients with squamous cell cancer of the anal canal should be managed in the same way as patients without known HIV. Treating physicians should be aware that a greater than average risk of toxicity is possible.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 50
Recommendation Class: NO_RECOMMENDATION
Activation Condition: Adult patient with squamous cell cancer of the anal canal and need to choose a mitomycin C schedule
Guideline Action: No preferred mitomycin C dosing schedule can be recommended from the available data.
Source Statement: In the trials using MMC in the 5FU-MMC combination regimens, MMC schedules include dose of 12 or 15mg/me day 1 only, and a 10mg/m2 Day 1, 29 dosing. There is no comparative data to allow a recommendation of a preferred schedule.
proposed_actions_text:
- Use concurrent chemotherapy and radiotherapy rather than radiotherapy alone to improve local control and decrease colostomy rates.
- Do not use induction chemotherapy as standard care; treat it as investigational only.

patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman with metastatic breast cancer is admitted to an oncology unit for her next cycle of chemotherapy. The unit recently expanded outpatient infusion services, and the pharmacy director notes that staff from pharmacy, nursing, and environmental services all handle hazardous drugs at different points in the medication circuit. During a quality review, several nurses report that spill response steps are not consistently taught during onboarding, and new float staff are uncertain about safe handling procedures. The oncology service is planning to update its hazardous-drug workflow and wants to reduce variation in practice across shifts. No medication error has occurred, but the team recognizes that the current process depends heavily on informal teaching and individual experience.
proposed_actions: Provide initial and ongoing hospital-approved education for all staff involved with hazardous drugs throughout the medication circuit, including safe handling and spill or leak management.
Provide initial and ongoing training for all staff working with hazardous drugs on current best-practice standards.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: all institutions administering hazardous drugs
Guideline Action: form a committee responsible for policies and procedures for hazardous drugs
Source Statement: It is strongly recommended that all institutions administering hazardous drugs form such a committee.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: committee for hazardous drugs is formed
Guideline Action: include representatives from occupational health and safety, joint health and safety committee, pharmacy, nursing, medical oncology, environmental services, risk management, and a patient representative
Source Statement: It is also strongly recommended that this committee include, but not be limited to, representatives from various departments and services such as: occupational health and safety, joint health and safety committee, pharmacy, nursing, medical oncology (physician), environmental services, risk management, and a patient representative.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: hazardous drug policies and procedures are being developed or revised
Guideline Action: use clear processes for developing, reviewing, and revising policies and procedures
Source Statement: This committee would be responsible for clear processes of developing, reviewing, and revising policies and procedures related to hazardous drugs.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: hazardous drug program oversight is in place
Guideline Action: routinely conduct risk assessment and gap analysis
Source Statement: A risk assessment and gap analysis should be routinely conducted to identify gaps and to inform policies and procedures.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: hazardous drug staff orientation and education are needed
Guideline Action: ensure a process for orientation and ongoing education for the target population
Source Statement: In addition, this committee is responsible for ensuring that there is a process in place for orientation and ongoing education for the identified target population.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: hazardous drug risk prevention management program is in place
Guideline Action: implement and follow up the Risk Prevention Management Program
Source Statement: This committee is responsible for implementation and follow-up of the Risk Prevention Management Program related to the use of hazardous drugs.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: staff are involved with hazardous drugs throughout the medication circuit
Guideline Action: provide initial and ongoing hospital-approved education including safe handling and spill or leak management
Source Statement: It is legislated that initial and ongoing hospital-approved education be provided to all staff involved with hazardous drugs throughout the medication circuit including safe handling and spill or leak management (6).

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: staff work with hazardous drugs
Guideline Action: provide initial and ongoing training on best practice standards
Source Statement: It is strongly recommended that all staff have initial and ongoing training related to best practice standards in place at the time.
proposed_actions_text:
- Provide initial and ongoing hospital-approved education for all staff involved with hazardous drugs throughout the medication circuit, including safe handling and spill or leak management.
- Provide initial and ongoing training for all staff working with hazardous drugs on current best-practice standards.

patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man with a prior diagnosis of glioblastoma multiforme completed chemoradiation 10 months ago. He now reports worsening headaches and mild word-finding difficulty. MRI shows a new enhancing lesion near the prior resection cavity, but the findings are equivocal for either tumor recurrence or radiation necrosis. His oncology team is considering PET/CT to help clarify the diagnosis before changing therapy. He has no fever, seizures, or other signs of infection, and his neurologic exam is otherwise stable. The main question is whether the new imaging abnormality represents recurrent tumor or treatment-related necrosis.
proposed_actions: PET/CT may be considered because anecdotal evidence suggests it may help differentiate radiation necrosis from tumour recurrence in glioblastoma multiforme.
Interpret any PET/CT result cautiously, recognizing that there is no gold standard for diagnosing radiation necrosis in glioblastoma multiforme.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient has glioma and PET is being considered to determine diagnosis or grading
Guideline Action: Do not use PET for diagnosis or grading in gliomas.
Source Statement: PET is not recommended for the determination of diagnosis or grading in gliomas.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient has glioma and PET is being considered to add diagnostic accuracy beyond MRI and histology
Guideline Action: Do not use PET to improve diagnostic accuracy or prognostic assessment beyond MRI and histology.
Source Statement: none have demonstrated any additional diagnostic accuracy or prognostic influence over and above that provided by magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) and histology in a multivariate model.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: TREATMENT_RESPONSE
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Patient with glioblastoma multiforme has suspected radiation necrosis versus tumour recurrence and PET/CT is being considered
Guideline Action: PET/CT may be considered as anecdotal evidence suggests it may differentiate radiation necrosis from tumour recurrence, recognizing the lack of a gold standard.
Source Statement: Anecdotal evidence exists that PET/CT may differentiate radiation necrosis from tumour recurrence, but there is no gold standard for the diagnosis of radiation necrosis in glioblastoma multiforme.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: TREATMENT_RESPONSE
Priority: 50
Recommendation Class: NO_RECOMMENDATION
Activation Condition: Patient with glioma is being assessed for treatment response using PET
Guideline Action: No recommendation can be made for or against PET for treatment response assessment.
Source Statement: A recommendation cannot be made for or against the use of PET for the assessment of treatment response in gliomas due to insufficient evidence.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: RECURRENCE_OR_RESTAGING
Priority: 50
Recommendation Class: NO_RECOMMENDATION
Activation Condition: Patient has recurrent glioma and PET or PET/CT is being considered for assessment
Guideline Action: No recommendation can be made for or against PET or PET/CT in recurrent gliomas.
Source Statement: A recommendation cannot be made for or against the use of PET or PET/CT in the assessment of patients with recurrent gliomas due to insufficient evidence.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: RECURRENCE_OR_RESTAGING
Priority: 50
Recommendation Class: NO_RECOMMENDATION
Activation Condition: Patient has recurrent glioma and FDG PET is being considered
Guideline Action: No recommendation can be made for FDG PET in recurrent gliomas because evidence is insufficient.
Source Statement: The evidence was insufficient to generate a recommendation on the use of FDG PET.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: RECURRENCE_OR_RESTAGING
Priority: 50
Recommendation Class: NO_RECOMMENDATION
Activation Condition: Patient with recurrent glioma is being evaluated and PET/CT before and after treatment is proposed to correlate with survival
Guideline Action: Do not assume evidence supports prospective PET/CT assessment before and after treatment for survival correlation; evidence is lacking.
Source Statement: PET or PET/CT has not been examined in a prospective cohort of gliomas to assess the treatment effect on PET imaging before and after treatment and correlate this with survival.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: RECURRENCE_OR_RESTAGING
Priority: 50
Recommendation Class: NO_RECOMMENDATION
Activation Condition: Patient with recurrent glioma is being assessed and radiation necrosis is a major diagnostic issue
Guideline Action: Recognize radiation necrosis as a major factor in assessing recurrent gliomas; no specific PET recommendation is provided.
Source Statement: Radiation necrosis is a major factor in assessing recurrent gliomas.
proposed_actions_text:
- PET/CT may be considered because anecdotal evidence suggests it may help differentiate radiation necrosis from tumour recurrence in glioblastoma multiforme.
- Interpret any PET/CT result cautiously, recognizing that there is no gold standard for diagnosing radiation necrosis in glioblastoma multiforme.

patient_vignette: A 64-year-old woman with newly diagnosed metastatic non-small cell lung cancer is referred after biopsy shows adenocarcinoma. She is a never-smoker and had been presumed to be a good candidate for an EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitor based on her sex, histology, and smoking history. However, her molecular testing has not yet been performed, and no EGFR mutation status is available. Her oncologist is considering starting gefitinib immediately because of her clinical profile, before mutation results return. She is otherwise stable, with no major organ dysfunction and no prior systemic therapy.
proposed_actions: Do not use first-line EGFR TKI therapy based on clinical selection alone.
Do not use Asian ethnicity, female sex, adenocarcinoma histology, or light/never smoking status to select the patient for first-line EGFR TKI therapy.
Obtain mutation testing before choosing EGFR TKI therapy.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: NSCLC patient has not had mutation testing or is only clinically selected by ethnicity, sex, histology, or smoking status
Guideline Action: Do not use first-line EGFR TKI therapy based on clinical selection alone
Source Statement: First-line therapy with an EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitor (TKI) is not recommended in unselected (patients who have not undergone mutation testing) or clinically selected populations of patients.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: NSCLC patient is being considered for first-line EGFR TKI based on Asian ethnicity, female sex, adenocarcinoma histology, or light/never smoking status
Guideline Action: Do not use these clinical characteristics to select patients for first-line EGFR TKI therapy
Source Statement: The use of clinical characteristics such as Asian ethnicity, female sex, adenocarcinoma histology and light/never smoking status is not recommended to select patients for first-line EGFR TKI therapy, as this strategy does not reliably select patients who have mutations.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: TREATMENT_RESPONSE
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient has progressed after chemotherapy and previously received gefitinib or erlotinib
Guideline Action: Do not use a second EGFR TKI such as afatinib because evidence is insufficient and no overall survival improvement is shown
Source Statement: There is insufficient evidence to recommend the use of a second EGFR TKI, such as afatinib, in patients whose disease has progressed following chemotherapy and gefitinib or erlotinib, as available data does not demonstrate any improvement in overall survival

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient is receiving high-dose gefitinib rather than standard-dose gefitinib
Guideline Action: Avoid higher-dose gefitinib because grade 3 or 4 toxicity is higher and ILD-type events occurred only with 500 mg/day in one trial
Source Statement: Two randomized phase II trials ... identified that grade 3 or 4 toxicity was higher with the higher dose gefitinib. Interstitial lung disease-type events occurred in only one of the two trials, and only with 500 mg/day gefitinib.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: NSCLC patient has confirmed EGFR mutation-positive disease
Guideline Action: Use first-line EGFR TKI therapy instead of platinum-based chemotherapy
Source Statement: In patients with EGFR mutation-positive NSCLC, first-line therapy with an EGFR TKI such as gefitinib, erlotinib or afatinib is the preferred treatment compared to platinum-based therapies.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: TREATMENT_RESPONSE
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient has progressed after platinum-based chemotherapy and is well enough to consider second-line chemotherapy
Guideline Action: Use an EGFR TKI as second- or third-line therapy
Source Statement: In patients well enough to consider second-line chemotherapy, an EGFR TKI can be recommended as second-or third-line therapy.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: TREATMENT_RESPONSE
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient is being considered for maintenance therapy after four cycles of platinum-doublet chemotherapy and has not progressed
Guideline Action: Use an EGFR TKI as maintenance therapy
Source Statement: An EGFR TKI is recommended as an option for maintenance therapy in patients who have not progressed after four cycles of a platinum-doublet chemotherapy.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: NSCLC patient has confirmed EGFR mutation-positive disease and an EGFR TKI is being chosen
Guideline Action: Choose among gefitinib, erlotinib, or afatinib based on expected toxicity and cost because no one agent is supported over another
