patient_vignette: A 14-year-old boy with localized Ewing’s sarcoma of the distal femur completed neoadjuvant chemotherapy and has had a marked radiographic and symptomatic response. Repeat MRI shows substantial shrinkage of the intramedullary and soft-tissue components, while the pre-chemotherapy MRI demonstrated more extensive marrow involvement. The orthopedic oncologist is planning limb-sparing surgery, but the lesion’s location near the metaphysis raises concern about achieving clear margins without excessive functional loss. The case is reviewed at a multidisciplinary tumor board with the patient and family, who prefer an approach that preserves function if oncologically safe. The team notes that complete resection appears feasible, though the final extent of surgery will depend on careful comparison of the pre- and post-chemotherapy MRI studies.
proposed_actions: Take both the pre-chemotherapy and post-chemotherapy MRI scans into consideration for surgical planning.
Use the post-chemotherapy MRI to help plan surgical resection margins when appropriate.

Decide local treatment at a multidisciplinary tumour board together with the patient, weighing age, tumour location, tumour size, response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy, comorbidities, expected benefit versus surgical/RT harms, and patient preference.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: localized Ewing's sarcoma of bone after neoadjuvant chemotherapy
Guideline Action: Do not use surgery plus radiation therapy as the initial local treatment option.
Source Statement: Either surgery alone or RT alone is a reasonable treatment option; the combination of surgery plus RT is not recommended as an initial treatment option.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: PREOP_STAGING
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: localized Ewing's sarcoma of bone and surgery is planned
Guideline Action: Take both pre-chemotherapy and post-chemotherapy MRI scans into consideration for surgical planning.
Source Statement: Both pre-chemotherapy and post-chemotherapy MRI scans should be taken into consideration for surgical planning.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: localized Ewing's sarcoma of bone after neoadjuvant chemotherapy
Guideline Action: Consider surgery alone as a local treatment option.
Source Statement: Either surgery alone or RT alone is a reasonable treatment option; the combination of surgery plus RT is not recommended as an initial treatment option.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: localized Ewing's sarcoma of bone after neoadjuvant chemotherapy
Guideline Action: Consider radiation therapy alone as a local treatment option.
Source Statement: Either surgery alone or RT alone is a reasonable treatment option; the combination of surgery plus RT is not recommended as an initial treatment option.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: localized Ewing's sarcoma of bone after neoadjuvant chemotherapy
Guideline Action: Decide local treatment with a multidisciplinary tumour board together with the patient, considering age, tumour location, tumour size, response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy, comorbidities, expected benefit versus surgical/RT harms, and patient preference.
Source Statement: The local treatment for an individual patient should be decided by a multidisciplinary tumour board together with the patient after consideration of the following: 1) patient characteristics (e.g., age, tumour location, tumour size, response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy, and existing comorbidities), 2) the potential benefit compared with the potential complications from surgery and/or toxicities associated with RT, and 3) patient preference.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: complete tumour resection is impossible
Guideline Action: Consider radiation therapy alone as the optimal local treatment.
Source Statement: If complete tumour resection is impossible, RT alone may be the optimal choice.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: postoperative residual tumour or positive margins
Guideline Action: Consider postoperative radiation therapy.
Source Statement: RT may be a treatment option postoperatively in patients who have residual tumours or positive margins.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: PREOP_STAGING
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: localized Ewing's sarcoma of bone, surgery is planned, and there is good chemotherapy response in certain anatomic locations
Guideline Action: Use the post-chemotherapy MRI to plan surgical resection margins when appropriate.
Source Statement: In certain anatomic locations with good chemotherapy response, the post-chemotherapy MRI may be the appropriate imaging modality to plan surgical resection margins.
proposed_actions_text:
- Take both the pre-chemotherapy and post-chemotherapy MRI scans into consideration for surgical planning.
- Use the post-chemotherapy MRI to help plan surgical resection margins when appropriate.
- Decide local treatment at a multidisciplinary tumour board together with the patient, weighing age, tumour location, tumour size, response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy, comorbidities, expected benefit versus surgical/RT harms, and patient preference.

patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man with ECOG 1 performance status has newly diagnosed unresectable metastatic colorectal adenocarcinoma with multiple bilobar liver metastases and small pulmonary nodules. He has no bowel obstruction or severe cancer-related symptoms, but he is anxious to start treatment quickly. His creatinine and liver tests are acceptable, and he has no major comorbidities other than well-controlled hypertension. After discussing goals of care, he states he wants treatment aimed at prolonging life while preserving quality of life. The oncology team reviews that both sequential chemotherapy and upfront combination chemotherapy are reasonable options in this palliative setting, and notes that the survival difference between approaches is likely small. They also discuss that combination therapy may increase the risk of neutropenia and febrile neutropenia.
proposed_actions: Consider either planned sequential chemotherapy or upfront combination chemotherapy as acceptable standards of care.
Consider that combination chemotherapy has a small survival advantage over sequential chemotherapy.
Consider that upfront combination chemotherapy causes more neutropenia.
Consider that upfront combination chemotherapy causes more febrile neutropenia.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer treated with palliative intent
Guideline Action: Consider either planned sequential chemotherapy or upfront combination chemotherapy as acceptable standards of care
Source Statement: Planned sequential chemotherapy and upfront combination chemotherapy are both acceptable standards of care.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer
Guideline Action: Choose treatment case by case based on patient and tumour characteristics, toxicity of each strategy, and patient preference
Source Statement: Therefore, choice of treatment should be made on a caseby-case basis based on considerations that include patient and tumour characteristics, toxicity of each strategy and patient preference.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer
Guideline Action: Use sequential chemotherapy as a strategy to provide access to all effective cytotoxic drugs
Source Statement: Based on the results of this systematic review, patients should have access to all effective cytotoxic drugs using a sequential strategy.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult patient with rapidly progressing, very symptomatic, or bulky life-threatening visceral disease
Guideline Action: Consider upfront combination chemotherapy
Source Statement: Combination chemotherapy may be more appropriate for patients with rapidly progressing, very symptomatic or bulky life-threatening visceral disease given their higher overall response rates.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer
Guideline Action: Consider that combination chemotherapy has a small survival advantage over sequential chemotherapy
Source Statement: A meta-analysis of five trials (1-5) demonstrates a survival advantage for combination chemotherapy (HR, 0.92; 95%CI, 0.86-0.99, p=0.02).

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer
Guideline Action: Consider that the survival advantage of combination chemotherapy is likely very small and may not be clinically significant
Source Statement: Median survival advantage in most trials is 3 to 6 weeks (range <1 week to 12 weeks). Therefore, any survival advantage that exists is likely to be very small and not clinically significant.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer
Guideline Action: Consider that upfront combination chemotherapy causes more neutropenia
Source Statement: Hematological toxicities include significantly more neutropenia (1,4) ... with upfront combination chemotherapy.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer
Guideline Action: Consider that upfront combination chemotherapy causes more febrile neutropenia
Source Statement: Hematological toxicities include significantly more neutropenia (1,4), febrile neutropenia (1) ... with upfront combination chemotherapy.
proposed_actions_text:
- Consider either planned sequential chemotherapy or upfront combination chemotherapy as acceptable standards of care.
- Consider that combination chemotherapy has a small survival advantage over sequential chemotherapy.
- Consider that upfront combination chemotherapy causes more neutropenia.
- Consider that upfront combination chemotherapy causes more febrile neutropenia.

patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with intermediate-risk acute myeloid leukemia was treated with induction chemotherapy and is now in first complete remission. His performance status is good, and he asks whether an autologous stem cell transplant would be the best way to reduce relapse risk. The team is considering post-remission options, including transplant versus chemotherapy. He has no matched sibling donor identified yet, and he is particularly focused on maximizing long-term disease control.
proposed_actions: Do not use autologous stem cell transplantation as the preferred post-remission strategy.
Do not use autologous stem cell transplantation when long-term survival is the primary outcome of interest.
If transplantation is being considered for this intermediate-risk AML in first complete remission, use allogeneic SCT rather than autologous SCT or chemotherapy.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with MDS
Guideline Action: Do not use autologous stem cell transplantation outside a clinical trial.
Source Statement: Autologous stem cell transplantation is not recommended for patients with MDS.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with MDS
Guideline Action: Do not recommend autologous SCT outside of a clinical trial.
Source Statement: One systematic review comprising a total of 22 studies did not detect any benefit associated with autologous SCT (ASCT), and does not recommend it outside of a clinical trial (1).

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with AML in CR1
Guideline Action: Do not use autologous SCT as the preferred post-remission strategy.
Source Statement: ASCT is not recommended for patients with AML in first complete remission.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with AML in CR1
Guideline Action: Do not use autologous SCT when long-term survival is the primary outcome of interest.
Source Statement: While associated with more favourable treatment-related mortality (TRM) rates, if longterm survival is the primary outcome of interest, then there is no evidence to support the use of ASCT in first complete remission.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: RECURRENCE_OR_RESTAGING
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with AML in second or subsequent remission
Guideline Action: Do not use autologous SCT as supported therapy.
Source Statement: There is insufficient evidence to support the use of ASCT for patients with AML in second or subsequent remission.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: RECURRENCE_OR_RESTAGING
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with AML in second or subsequent remission and long-term survival is the primary outcome
Guideline Action: Do not use autologous SCT for long-term survival benefit.
Source Statement: If long-term survival is the primary outcome of interest, then there is no evidence to support the use of ASCT in second or subsequent remission.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with AML in CR1 and intermediate-risk or poor-risk disease
Guideline Action: Use allogeneic SCT rather than autologous SCT or chemotherapy when transplant is being considered.
Source Statement: Significant benefits in favour of alloSCT for both intermediate and poor risk patients (p<0.01) were detected, but no difference was detected with good risk patients.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: RECURRENCE_OR_RESTAGING
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with AML who achieves a second or subsequent remission
Guideline Action: Recommend allogeneic transplantation.
Source Statement: Allogeneic transplantation is the recommended option for patients with AML who achieve a second or subsequent remission.
proposed_actions_text:
- Do not use autologous stem cell transplantation as the preferred post-remission strategy.
- Do not use autologous stem cell transplantation when long-term survival is the primary outcome of interest.
- If transplantation is being considered for this intermediate-risk AML in first complete remission, use allogeneic SCT rather than autologous SCT or chemotherapy.

patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man in Ontario is referred after a CT chest done for persistent cough shows an anterior mediastinal mass. Biopsy confirms a thymic epithelial tumour. He is otherwise well, with no myasthenic symptoms and normal baseline bloodwork. The case is being reviewed before any treatment decision is made. The oncology team plans formal staging and wants input from thoracic surgery, medical oncology, and radiation oncology, with consideration of consultation from a higher-volume centre because the tumour appears potentially resectable. The patient asks whether a specialized radionuclide therapy could be used instead of surgery or standard systemic treatment.
proposed_actions: Use the TNM staging system for this patient.
Discuss the case at a multidisciplinary cancer conference, and include a higher-volume centre when possible.
Do not use peptide receptor radionuclide therapy as an approved treatment for this thymic epithelial tumour in Ontario.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patients with thymic epithelial tumours in Ontario
Guideline Action: do not use peptide receptor radionuclide therapy as an approved treatment
Source Statement: Also, peptide receptor radionuclide therapy has not been approved for patients with thymic epithelial tumours in Ontario.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: all adult patients with thymic epithelial tumours
Guideline Action: use the TNM staging system
Source Statement: The TNM staging system should be used for all patients.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: all patients with thymic epithelial tumours undergoing surgery
Guideline Action: aim to achieve complete resection
Source Statement: The aim of surgery in all cases is to achieve a complete resection.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: all patients with thymic epithelial tumours
Guideline Action: discuss the patient at a multidisciplinary cancer conference, including higher-volume centres when possible
Source Statement: Discussion of all patients at multidisciplinary cancer conference (MCC) is strongly recommended, not just at local MCC but also with higher-volume centres.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: all patients with thymic epithelial tumours
Guideline Action: consider presentation at the International Thymic Malignancies Interest Group tumour board
Source Statement: Presentation at the International Thymic Malignancies Interest Group tumour board should be considered.
proposed_actions_text:
- Use the TNM staging system for this patient.
- Discuss the case at a multidisciplinary cancer conference, and include a higher-volume centre when possible.
- Do not use peptide receptor radionuclide therapy as an approved treatment for this thymic epithelial tumour in Ontario.

patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with no prior thoracic radiation presents with 3 weeks of worsening cough, intermittent hemoptysis, and dyspnea. CT chest shows a centrally located right upper lobe non-small cell lung cancer causing partial endobronchial obstruction of the segmental bronchus with post-obstructive atelectasis, but not complete lung collapse. Bronchoscopy confirms a friable endobronchial lesion narrowing the airway. He is being evaluated for palliative treatment to relieve respiratory symptoms. The oncology team is considering whether to add endobronchial brachytherapy to external beam radiation, but there is no prior EBRT history and no clear reason to prefer a combined approach over radiation alone.
proposed_actions: Use external beam radiation therapy alone for palliation of respiratory symptoms rather than HDREB alone.
Do not routinely use HDREB plus EBRT to improve symptom relief over EBRT alone.
Consider HDREB plus EBRT only in selected patients, recognizing this is an opinion-based option rather than a routine recommendation.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with symptomatic endobronchial non-small cell lung cancer and previously untreated disease
Guideline Action: Use HDREB alone for palliation of respiratory symptoms
Source Statement: For patients with previously untreated, symptomatic, endobronchial non-small cell lung cancer: External beam radiation therapy (EBRT) alone is more effective for palliation of respiratory symptoms than HDREB alone.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with symptomatic endobronchial non-small cell lung cancer and previously untreated disease
Guideline Action: Use routine HDREB plus EBRT to improve symptom relief over EBRT alone
Source Statement: The evidence does not provide conclusive results to suggest that routine use of HDREB and EBRT would provide improved symptom relief over EBRT alone.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with complete collapse of the lung due to endobronchial obstruction
Guideline Action: Use a surgical core out procedure before EBRT or EBRT with HDREB
Source Statement: December 2022: the recommendation "For patients with complete collapse of the lung due to endobronchial obstruction, a surgical core out procedure may be needed before EBRT or EBRT with HDREB" is no longer endorsed because the updated evidence does not show a benefit of this procedure.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient previously treated by EBRT who is symptomatic with endobronchial obstruction due to recurrent disease
Guideline Action: Use HDREB if technically feasible
Source Statement: For patients previously treated by EBRT who are symptomatic with endobronchial obstruction due to recurrent disease, HDREB is recommended, providing that endobronchial brachytherapy is technically feasible.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult patient with symptomatic endobronchial non-small cell lung cancer and previously untreated disease where selected combined treatment is being considered
Guideline Action: Consider HDREB plus EBRT in selected patients
Source Statement: June 2018: It is the opinion of the Lung Cancer Disease Site Group that HDREB and EBRT might be suitable in selected patients.
proposed_actions_text:
- Use external beam radiation therapy alone for palliation of respiratory symptoms rather than HDREB alone.
- Do not routinely use HDREB plus EBRT to improve symptom relief over EBRT alone.
- Consider HDREB plus EBRT only in selected patients, recognizing this is an opinion-based option rather than a routine recommendation.

patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man underwent wide local excision and sentinel node biopsy for a 2.1-mm ulcerated cutaneous melanoma on the upper back. Final pathology shows complete resection with two involved regional lymph nodes, placing him at high risk of recurrence (stage III). He is recovering well and asks about “older immune shots” he read about online. He has no known BRAF V600E or V600K mutation status yet, and no clinical evidence of persistent nodal disease on exam or imaging. He is being seen to discuss adjuvant treatment options after surgery.
proposed_actions: Do not use ipilimumab as adjuvant therapy.
Do not use interferon alpha for adjuvant treatment outside a clinical trial.
Do not use chemotherapy regimens, vaccines, levamisole, bevacizumab, Bacillus Calmette-Guérin, or isolated limb perfusion as adjuvant treatment except in a clinical trial.
Perform molecular testing to characterize mutations to help guide adjuvant treatment decisions.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient with completely resected cutaneous melanoma at high risk of recurrence
Guideline Action: do not use ipilimumab as adjuvant therapy
Source Statement: Ipilimumab is not recommended as adjuvant therapy for patients with completely resected cutaneous melanoma with high risk of recurrence.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient with completely resected cutaneous melanoma outside a clinical trial
Guideline Action: do not use interferon alpha for adjuvant treatment
Source Statement: Use of interferon alpha (IFN-α) for adjuvant treatment of cutaneous melanoma outside of a clinical trial is no longer recommended.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient with completely resected cutaneous melanoma
Guideline Action: do not use chemotherapy regimens, vaccines, levamisole, bevacizumab, Bacillus Calmette-Guerin, or isolated limb perfusion as adjuvant treatment except in a clinical trial
Source Statement: Chemotherapy regimens, vaccines, levamisole, bevacizumab, Bacillus Calmette-Guerin, and isolated limb perfusion are not recommended for adjuvant treatment of cutaneous melanoma except as part of a clinical trial.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: adult patient with completely resected cutaneous melanoma at high risk of recurrence and no BRAF V600E or V600K mutation
Guideline Action: use nivolumab or pembrolizumab as adjuvant therapy
Source Statement: Nivolumab or pembrolizumab is recommended as adjuvant therapy for patients with completely resected cutaneous melanoma without BRAF V600E or V600K mutations with high risk of recurrence (stage IIIA to IIID, IV).

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: adult patient with completely resected cutaneous melanoma at high risk of recurrence and BRAF V600E or V600K mutation
Guideline Action: use nivolumab, pembrolizumab, or dabrafenib plus trametinib as adjuvant therapy
Source Statement: Nivolumab, pembrolizumab, or dabrafenib plus trametinib is recommended as adjuvant therapy for patients with completely resected cutaneous melanoma with BRAF V600E or V600K mutations and high risk of recurrence (stage IIIA to IIID, IV).

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: high-risk melanoma patient being considered for adjuvant treatment
Guideline Action: perform molecular testing to characterize mutations to guide treatment decisions
Source Statement: Molecular testing of high-risk melanoma patients to characterize mutations should be conducted to help guide appropriate treatment decisions.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient with clinically positive lymph nodes before adjuvant therapy or adjuvant radiotherapy
Guideline Action: perform dissection of lymph nodes in the involved area before adjuvant therapy or adjuvant radiotherapy
Source Statement: These trials and recommendations regarding axillary resection do not apply to patients with clinically positive lymph nodes (by palpation or radiologic investigation), and the standard of care is dissection of lymph nodes in that area (axillary, groin, or head and neck) prior to adjuvant therapy or adjuvant radiotherapy.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has mucosal melanoma with high risk of recurrence
Guideline Action: use nivolumab or pembrolizumab, or targeted therapy if an actionable mutation is identified, as adjuvant therapy
Source Statement: Immune checkpoint inhibitors (nivolumab or pembrolizumab) or targeted therapy (in patients with identified mutations) are recommended for adjuvant therapy of mucosal melanoma with high risk of recurrence.
proposed_actions_text:
- Do not use ipilimumab as adjuvant therapy.
- Do not use interferon alpha for adjuvant treatment outside a clinical trial.
- Do not use chemotherapy regimens, vaccines, levamisole, bevacizumab, Bacillus Calmette-Guérin, or isolated limb perfusion as adjuvant treatment except in a clinical trial.
- Perform molecular testing to characterize mutations to help guide adjuvant treatment decisions.

patient_vignette: A 61-year-old man with biopsy-proven gastric gastrointestinal stromal tumor underwent complete surgical resection with negative margins after presenting with melena and iron-deficiency anemia. Final pathology confirmed a low-to-intermediate risk GIST, and postoperative recovery has been uncomplicated. At his oncology follow-up, he asks whether he should start imatinib to reduce the chance of recurrence. He has no evidence of residual or metastatic disease on postoperative imaging, and the tumor was not being treated as unresectable or metastatic disease. He feels well and is eager to do anything that might lower recurrence risk, but he has not started any systemic therapy yet.
proposed_actions: Do not use imatinib as adjuvant therapy after initial complete resection of GIST.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has undergone initial complete resection of GIST
Guideline Action: Do not use imatinib as adjuvant therapy
Source Statement: At present, there is insufficient evidence to support the use of imatinib as an adjuvant therapy in patients who have undergone initial complete resection of disease.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: considering neoadjuvant treatment with imatinib
Guideline Action: Do not use neoadjuvant imatinib
Source Statement: At present, the use of neoadjuvant imatinib is not recommended.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: adult patient with unresectable or metastatic GIST expressing KIT (CD117+)
Guideline Action: Use imatinib as therapy
Source Statement: In patients with KIT-expressing (CD117+) unresectable or metastatic GIST, treatment with imatinib is a recommended therapy.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: starting imatinib for KIT-expressing (CD117+) unresectable or metastatic GIST
Guideline Action: Prescribe imatinib 400 mg daily as the initial dose
Source Statement: Until additional data become available, the initial dose of imatinib should be prescribed at a dose of 400 mg daily.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient with stable disease on imatinib
Guideline Action: Continue imatinib until disease progression
Source Statement: Patients with stable disease should be treated until disease progression.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient develops serious toxicity on imatinib
Guideline Action: Discontinue imatinib
Source Statement: Treatment should be discontinued if serious toxicity develops.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient achieves complete clinical response and radiologic remission with imatinib
Guideline Action: Continue imatinib indefinitely until further data define optimal duration
Source Statement: For patients who achieve a complete clinical response and radiologic remission with imatinib, treatment should be continued indefinitely until further data is available regarding the optimum duration of therapy in such patients.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: adult patient with unresectable or metastatic GIST expressing KIT (CD117+)
Guideline Action: Apply these recommendations to this population
Source Statement: These recommendations apply to adult patients with unresectable or metastatic GIST expressing KIT (CD117+).
proposed_actions_text: - Do not use imatinib as adjuvant therapy after initial complete resection of GIST.

patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man with primary systemic AL amyloidosis presents after 5 months of bortezomib-based therapy with partial hematologic response. He has persistent nephrotic-range proteinuria and mild peripheral edema, but no symptomatic heart failure. Echocardiography shows preserved ejection fraction with mild concentric thickening, and his creatinine is stable at 1.1 mg/dL. He remains active, works full-time, and wants the most aggressive treatment that offers the best chance of long-term disease control. His hematologist discusses stem cell transplantation as a next step. Because he has AL amyloidosis and is being evaluated for SCT, the team reviews his organ involvement, overall functional status, comorbidities, and treatment goals before deciding whether he is an appropriate transplant candidate.
proposed_actions: Use autologous stem cell transplantation only in selected patients after weighing performance status, comorbidities, patient preferences, and treatment goals.
Base the transplant decision on disease-related characteristics, comorbidities, and patient preferences.
Consider careful patient selection based on the degree of light chain amyloidosis involvement and organ function to reduce transplant-related mortality.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with primary systemic AL amyloidosis being considered for allogeneic SCT
Guideline Action: Do not use allogeneic stem cell transplantation for AL amyloidosis
Source Statement: There is no evidence supporting the use of allogeneic SCT for patients with AL.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult patient with primary systemic AL amyloidosis being considered for treatment including bone marrow or stem cell transplantation
Guideline Action: Consider high-dose chemotherapy with autologous stem cell transplantation as an option for selected patients, preferably in an investigative setting
Source Statement: High-dose chemotherapy (CT) and autologous SCT is an option for selected patients with primary systemic amyloidosis, preferably within an investigative setting.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult patient with primary systemic AL amyloidosis being considered for autologous SCT
Guideline Action: Use autologous SCT only in selected patients after weighing performance status, comorbidities, patient preferences, and treatment goals
Source Statement: In consideration of the lack of curative treatment options and the limitations of the evidence reviewed, the Expert Panel believes that offering ASCT may be a reasonable option for some patients, depending on performance status, co-morbidities, patient preferences, and ultimate treatment goals.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult patient with primary systemic AL amyloidosis being considered for SCT
Guideline Action: Base the transplant decision on disease-related characteristics, comorbidities, and patient preferences
Source Statement: The patient selection process and the ultimate decision to perform an SCT should take into account not only disease-related characteristics, but also comorbidities and patient preferences.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult patient with primary systemic AL amyloidosis being considered for SCT
Guideline Action: Consider careful patient selection based on degree of light chain amyloidosis involvement and organ function to reduce transplant-related mortality
Source Statement: Careful patient selection based on degree of light chain amyloidosis involvement and organ function is an emerging concept in amyloidosis that should be considered to reduce transplant-related mortality.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult patient with primary systemic AL amyloidosis being considered for SCT
Guideline Action: Recognize that transplantation in amyloidosis is an evolving area and that first relapse transplantation may be considered as an emerging area pending new evidence
Source Statement: Transplantation in amyloidosis is an evolving area. New emerging areas include consideration of transplantation in first relapse and the impact of novel proteasome inhibitors on outcomes.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 50
Recommendation Class: NO_RECOMMENDATION
Activation Condition: Adult patient with primary systemic AL amyloidosis being considered for SCT
Guideline Action: No recommendation can be made on how newer agents affect the need for SCT
Source Statement: Newer agents are being investigated in the treatment of AL amyloidosis. At this time it is not known how they may impact the need for SCT.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 50
Recommendation Class: NO_RECOMMENDATION
Activation Condition: Adult patient with primary systemic AL amyloidosis being considered for SCT
Guideline Action: No recommendation can be made on the impact of novel proteasome inhibitors on transplantation outcomes
Source Statement: Transplantation in amyloidosis is an evolving area. New emerging areas include consideration of transplantation in first relapse and the impact of novel proteasome inhibitors on outcomes.
proposed_actions_text:
- Use autologous stem cell transplantation only in selected patients after weighing performance status, comorbidities, patient preferences, and treatment goals.
- Base the transplant decision on disease-related characteristics, comorbidities, and patient preferences.
- Consider careful patient selection based on the degree of light chain amyloidosis involvement and organ function to reduce transplant-related mortality.

patient_vignette: A 59-year-old man presents with persistent hoarseness 9 months after completing definitive radiation therapy for a T1 glottic squamous cell carcinoma of the left true vocal fold. Repeat laryngoscopy and biopsy confirm a small local recurrence confined to the glottis, still staged as early T1 disease. He remains otherwise well and asks whether another course of radiation could treat the recurrence. He is anxious to preserve voice but understands that the lesion is limited and potentially amenable to local salvage treatment.
proposed_actions: Do not use re-irradiation for this recurrence.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: RECURRENCE_OR_RESTAGING
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with early (T1) glottic cancer and recurrence after prior radiation therapy
Guideline Action: Do not use re-irradiation
Source Statement: However, re-irradiation is not an option in cases of recurrence.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with previously untreated early (T1) glottic cancer
Guideline Action: Offer endolaryngeal surgery, with or without laser, or radiation therapy as equally effective treatment options
Source Statement: For patients with early (T1) glottic cancer, recommended treatment options include the equally effective endolaryngeal surgery, with or without laser, or radiation therapy.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult patient with previously untreated early (T1) glottic cancer
Guideline Action: Choose between surgery and radiation based on patient and clinician preferences and general medical condition
Source Statement: The choice between treatment modalities should be based on patient and clinician preferences and general medical condition.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult patient with early (T1) glottic cancer and anterior commissure involvement
Guideline Action: Consider radiation therapy over surgery
Source Statement: Anterior commissure involvement may be a factor that favours a recommendation of radiotherapy over surgery due to a common opinion that voice outcomes are particularly affected.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult patient with early (T1) glottic cancer and tumor localized to the midportion of the vocal fold with uncompromised endoscopic accessibility
Guideline Action: Consider endolaryngeal surgery as an ideal candidate treatment
Source Statement: Tumours localized to the midportion of the vocal fold, and where endoscopic accessibility is uncompromised, may be considered ideal candidates for surgery.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult patient with early (T1) glottic cancer who cannot tolerate a general anaesthetic
Guideline Action: Prefer radiation therapy over surgery
Source Statement: Other important practical considerations include the ability for patients to tolerate a general anaesthetic, which is required for surgery.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult patient with early (T1) glottic cancer who cannot cooperate with daily treatment for four to six weeks
Guideline Action: Avoid radiation therapy and consider surgery if otherwise appropriate
Source Statement: In contrast, radiotherapy requires patient cooperation for daily treatment for four to six weeks.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult patient with early (T1) glottic cancer and local recurrence after surgery
Guideline Action: Consider partial laryngeal surgery, including revision endoscopic surgery
Source Statement: Partial laryngeal surgery, including revision endoscopic surgery, is possible for local recurrence following surgery.
proposed_actions_text: - Do not use re-irradiation for this recurrence.

patient_vignette: A 64-year-old man with COPD and a 45-pack-year smoking history is diagnosed with unresectable stage IIIA NSCLC with biopsy-proven N2 mediastinal lymph node involvement. He is being treated with curative intent and has started concurrent chemoradiation. The radiation oncology plan specifies 60 Gy in 30 once-daily fractions over six weeks with weekly on-treatment review. A medical oncologist asks whether the dose should be escalated to improve local control, but the team is otherwise proceeding with standard concurrent therapy. The patient has not yet completed chemoradiation and has no current contraindications to future immunotherapy.
proposed_actions: Use concurrent chemoradiation for curative-intent treatment of this unresectable, lymph node-positive stage III NSCLC.
Use standard radiation dose fractionation of 60 to 66 Gy in 2 Gy once-daily fractions over six weeks.
Do not escalate the radiation dose beyond 66 Gy with conventional fractionation.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient is receiving conventional fractionation radiotherapy for unresectable stage III NSCLC
Guideline Action: do not escalate dose beyond 66 Gy
Source Statement: Dose escalation beyond 66 Gy with conventional fractionation is not recommended.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient is receiving radiotherapy for unresectable stage III NSCLC
Guideline Action: do not use hyperfractionated radiotherapy regimens that do not accelerate the treatment course
Source Statement: Hyperformated radiotherapy regimens that do not result in acceleration of the treatment course, even though the total nominal radiotherapy dose may be modestly increased, are not recommended.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient with unresectable stage III NSCLC is being considered for induction chemotherapy before concurrent chemoradiotherapy
Guideline Action: do not routinely give induction chemotherapy before concurrent chemoradiotherapy
Source Statement: Routine use of induction chemotherapy prior to concurrent chemoradiotherapy is not recommended;

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient is being considered for durvalumab after chemoradiation
Guideline Action: do not exclude durvalumab solely because of EGFR or PD-L1 biomarker status
Source Statement: At this time, it is inappropriate to exclude patients from therapy based solely on a biomarker such as epidermal growth factor receptor or programmed death-ligand 1 status.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has unresectable, lymph node-positive stage III NSCLC (N2 or N3) and curative-intent treatment is planned
Guideline Action: use concurrent chemoradiation
Source Statement: Concurrent chemoradiation should be used for curative-intent treatment of patients with unresectable, lymph node-positive (N2 or N3) stage III NSCLC.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient is receiving concurrent chemoradiation for unresectable stage III NSCLC
Guideline Action: use standard radiation dose fractionation of 60 to 66 Gy in 2 Gy once-daily fractions over six weeks
Source Statement: A standard dose fractionation of 60 to 66 Gy given in fractions of 2 Gy once per day over six weeks is recommended.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has stage III NSCLC and has not progressed after chemoradiation and has no contraindications to an immune checkpoint inhibitor
Guideline Action: use durvalumab
Source Statement: Durvalumab should be used in patients with stage III NSCLC who have not progressed postchemoradiation and have no contraindications to an immune checkpoint inhibitor.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient is having chemoradiotherapy and surgery for stage IIIA/B-N2 NSCLC
Guideline Action: schedule surgery three to five weeks after chemoradiotherapy
Source Statement: For patients with stage IIIA/B-N2 NSCLC who are having chemoradiotherapy and surgery, ensure that their surgery is scheduled for three to five weeks after the chemoradiotherapy.
proposed_actions_text:
- Use concurrent chemoradiation for curative-intent treatment of this unresectable, lymph node-positive stage III NSCLC.
- Use standard radiation dose fractionation of 60 to 66 Gy in 2 Gy once-daily fractions over six weeks.
- Do not escalate the radiation dose beyond 66 Gy with conventional fractionation.

patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman with stage II, node-positive breast cancer has completed surgery and is being seen to finalize adjuvant therapy. Her tumor is ER-positive, PR-positive, and HER2-negative by IHC 1+. She is postmenopausal and has no cardiac history. The oncology team is discussing several options, including adding capecitabine to an anthracycline-taxane regimen and offering adjuvant trastuzumab because of her high-risk features. A colleague also suggests using Ki-67 and intrinsic molecular subtype results to refine recurrence risk and guide treatment intensity. The patient asks for the most evidence-based plan and wants to avoid unnecessary treatment.
proposed_actions: Do not add gemcitabine or capecitabine to the anthracycline-taxane regimen for adjuvant chemotherapy.
Do not offer adjuvant trastuzumab because the cancer is not HER2-positive.
Do not use Ki-67 as a standard parameter for routine risk stratification outside clinical trials.
Do not use intrinsic breast cancer subtypes for clinical decision making outside a clinical trial.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: considering adding gemcitabine or capecitabine to an anthracycline-taxane regimen for adjuvant chemotherapy
Guideline Action: do not add gemcitabine or capecitabine to the anthracycline-taxane regimen
Source Statement: The addition of gemcitabine or capecitabine to an anthracycline-taxane regimen is not recommended for adjuvant chemotherapy.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient is older than 65 years and capecitabine is being considered instead of adjuvant AC or CMF
Guideline Action: do not use capecitabine as the adjuvant chemotherapy option in lieu of AC or CMF
Source Statement: In patients older than 65 years, capecitabine is not recommended as an adjuvant chemotherapy option in lieu of adjuvant AC or CMF (oral cyclophosphamide).

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has ER-positive cancer and is premenopausal and considering ovarian ablation or suppression plus tamoxifen
Guideline Action: do not treat ovarian ablation or suppression plus tamoxifen as standard of care
Source Statement: In premenopausal patients with ER+ tumours (treated with or without chemotherapy) the addition of ovarian ablation or suppression to tamoxifen is not the standard of care.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has ER-positive cancer and is premenopausal and considering ovarian ablation or suppression plus 5 years of an aromatase inhibitor
Guideline Action: do not use ovarian ablation or suppression plus 5 years of an aromatase inhibitor as standard of care
Source Statement: In premenopausal patients with ER+ tumours, treated with or without chemotherapy, ovarian ablation or suppression plus five years of an AI is not the standard of care.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient does not have HER2-positive breast cancer
Guideline Action: do not offer adjuvant trastuzumab
Source Statement: Only patients with HER2+ breast cancer (IHC 3+, ISH ratio ≥2, or 6+ HER2 gene copies per cell nucleus) should be offered adjuvant trastuzumab.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient is receiving trastuzumab and has any of the following: LVEF under 55%, documented congestive heart failure, high-risk uncontrolled arrhythmias, angina requiring medication, clinically significant valvular disease, transmural infarction on ECG, or poorly controlled hypertension
Guideline Action: do not offer trastuzumab
Source Statement: trastuzumab should not be offered to patients with any of the following: a left ventricular ejection fraction LVEF of <55%; a history of documented congestive heart failure; high-risk uncontrolled arrhythmias; angina pectoris requiring medication; clinically significant valvular disease; evidence of transmural infarction on electrocardiograph (ECG); poorly controlled hypertension.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: considering Ki-67 for routine risk stratification in early-stage breast cancer
Guideline Action: do not use Ki-67 as a standard parameter outside clinical trials
Source Statement: It is premature to recommend its use as a standard parameter for patient risk stratification, although it may be evaluated in clinical trials.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: considering intrinsic breast cancer subtypes for clinical decision making outside a trial
Guideline Action: do not use intrinsic subtypes outside a clinical trial
Source Statement: At this point, the use of these subtypes in clinical decision making outside of a clinical trial is not recommended.
proposed_actions_text:
- Do not add gemcitabine or capecitabine to the anthracycline-taxane regimen for adjuvant chemotherapy.
- Do not offer adjuvant trastuzumab because the cancer is not HER2-positive.
- Do not use Ki-67 as a standard parameter for routine risk stratification outside clinical trials.
- Do not use intrinsic breast cancer subtypes for clinical decision making outside a clinical trial.

patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man is diagnosed with stage III squamous cell carcinoma of the oropharynx after biopsy of a persistent throat lesion and CT imaging showing a primary tumor with suspicious cervical nodes. He is referred to a multidisciplinary head and neck cancer clinic where the medical oncologist, radiation oncologist, head and neck surgeon, speech-language pathologist, dietitian, and social worker review his case together. The team discusses definitive treatment planning, swallowing rehabilitation, nutritional support, and survivorship needs. His family physician is informed of the diagnosis and remains responsible for his general medical care and supportive follow-up after cancer treatment. Because he may need surgery depending on response to therapy, the surgeon notes that reconstruction expertise would be required if an operation becomes necessary.
proposed_actions: Coordinate care among an experienced core team responsible for assessment, treatment, planning, management, survivorship, and rehabilitation.
Ensure the primary care provider is responsible for ongoing overall health and post-treatment supportive care.
Recognize that the primary care physician is not involved in day-to-day treatment but remains important for post-treatment supportive care and ongoing overall health.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patients with head and neck cancer
Guideline Action: coordinate care among an experienced core team responsible for assessment, treatment, planning, management, survivorship, and rehabilitation
Source Statement: The care of patients with head and neck cancer should be coordinated among members of an experienced Core Team, comprised of a group of physicians and allied healthcare providers who will be responsible for the assessment, treatment, planning, management, survivorship, and rehabilitation of the patient.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patients with head and neck cancer
Guideline Action: ensure the primary care provider is responsible for ongoing overall health and post-treatment supportive care
Source Statement: The Primary Care Provider will be responsible for the ongoing overall health of the patient and will offer supportive care after treatment.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patients with head and neck cancer
Guideline Action: involve an extended team as needed to facilitate treatment, planning, management, survivorship, and rehabilitation
Source Statement: The Extended Team will be called upon by the core team to facilitate treatment, planning, management, survivorship, and rehabilitation of the patient.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: members of the care team
Guideline Action: ensure team members have training or experience managing patients with head and neck cancers
Source Statement: Members of the Teams must have training or experience managing patients with head and neck cancers.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patients with head and neck cancer
Guideline Action: recognize the primary care physician as not involved in day-to-day treatment but important for post-treatment supportive care and ongoing overall health
Source Statement: The primary care physician is not involved in the day to day treatment of the head and neck cancer patient but plays an important role in post-treatment supportive care and is responsible for the ongoing overall health of the patient.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: surgical management of patients with head and neck tumours
Guideline Action: use reconstruction expertise from a fellowship-trained microvascular surgeon with specific training in head and neck reconstruction
Source Statement: Reconstruction expertise is required for the surgical management of patients with head and neck tumours and necessitates a fellowship-trained microvascular surgeon with specific training in head and neck reconstruction.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: reporting head and neck malignancies
Guideline Action: use CAP-CCO standards for pathology reporting
Source Statement: Pathologists are advised to use the CAP-CCO standards for reporting head and neck malignancies (5).

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: reporting nodal dissections
Guideline Action: include levels and structures in the specimen, number of involved and uninvolved nodes, level of nodes, and presence and location of extracapsular spread
Source Statement: The reporting of nodal dissections should include a description of the levels and structures included in the specimen, including number of involved and uninvolved nodes, level of these nodes, and the presence and location of extracapsular spread of tumour.
proposed_actions_text:
- Coordinate care among an experienced core team responsible for assessment, treatment, planning, management, survivorship, and rehabilitation.
- Ensure the primary care provider is responsible for ongoing overall health and post-treatment supportive care.
- Recognize that the primary care physician is not involved in day-to-day treatment but remains important for post-treatment supportive care and ongoing overall health.

patient_vignette: A 63-year-old man with progressive dysphagia to solids and a 7-kg weight loss is diagnosed with a mid-thoracic esophageal squamous cell carcinoma on endoscopy and biopsy. Staging CT and PET show no distant metastases, and endoscopic ultrasound suggests a locally advanced but resectable tumor with preserved performance status. Thoracic surgery feels he is an appropriate candidate for esophagectomy after neoadjuvant treatment. He has well-controlled hypertension and no major cardiopulmonary disease. In clinic, he asks whether he can proceed directly to surgery or if treatment before surgery would improve his chances. The oncology team is considering preoperative therapy options and discussing expected toxicities and the fact that any survival benefit may be modest. The patient says he values the best chance of cure but wants to understand how treatment might affect swallowing, fatigue, and recovery before making a decision.
proposed_actions: Use preoperative platinum-based chemotherapy plus radiotherapy as the preferred treatment modality.
Discuss potential adverse effects with the patient because the survival advantage may be minimal.
Base the decision on patient preferences, comorbidities, and suitability for trimodality therapy.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Considering radiotherapy alone given preoperatively or postoperatively
Guideline Action: Do not use radiotherapy alone as it showed no survival benefit compared with surgery alone
Source Statement: Randomized trials demonstrated no survival benefit for radiotherapy given alone, either preoperatively or postoperatively, compared with surgery alone.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Considering postoperative chemotherapy alone
Guideline Action: Do not use postoperative chemotherapy alone as it showed no survival benefit compared with surgery alone
Source Statement: Randomized trials demonstrated no survival benefit for postoperative chemotherapy given alone compared with surgery alone.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient has resectable, operable, potentially curable thoracic esophageal cancer and surgery is appropriate
Guideline Action: Use preoperative platinum-based chemotherapy plus radiotherapy as the preferred treatment modality
Source Statement: Preoperative cisplatin-based chemotherapy plus radiotherapy is recommended as the preferred modality for the management of surgically resectable patients with esophageal cancer.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult patient has resectable, operable, potentially curable thoracic esophageal cancer and surgery is appropriate
Guideline Action: Consider preoperative platinum-based chemotherapy alone as an alternative treatment option
Source Statement: Preoperative cisplatin-based chemotherapy alone is an alternative choice for the management of surgically resectable patients with esophageal cancer.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Considering preoperative therapy for resectable esophageal cancer
Guideline Action: Prefer preoperative chemoradiotherapy over surgery alone when choosing preoperative treatment
Source Statement: Based on the majority of the evidence available at this time, the GI DSG believes that preoperative chemoradiotherapy for resectable carcinoma of the esophagus is the preferred approach.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Considering preoperative therapy for resectable esophageal cancer
Guideline Action: Recognize that either preoperative chemotherapy or preoperative chemoradiotherapy may provide survival benefit compared with surgery alone
Source Statement: The GI DSG acknowledges there is evidence indicating survival benefits with either preoperative chemotherapy or chemoradiotherapy compared with surgery alone.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Before administering preoperative therapy
Guideline Action: Discuss potential adverse effects with the patient because the survival advantage may be minimal
Source Statement: Clinicians should recognize that the survival advantage of preoperative therapy may be minimal and a discussion with patients regarding potential adverse effects is required.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Deciding whether to give preoperative therapy
Guideline Action: Base the decision on patient preferences, comorbidities, and suitability for trimodality therapy
Source Statement: Decisions to administer preoperative therapy should be based on patient preferences, comorbidities, and suitability for trimodality therapy.
proposed_actions_text:
- Use preoperative platinum-based chemotherapy plus radiotherapy as the preferred treatment modality.
- Discuss potential adverse effects with the patient because the survival advantage may be minimal.
- Base the decision on patient preferences, comorbidities, and suitability for trimodality therapy.

patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman with ER-positive breast cancer presents with worsening deep aching pain in her pelvis and lower back. Imaging shows multiple lytic bone metastases without spinal cord compression. She is already receiving denosumab for skeletal-related event prevention. Despite the agent, her pain has progressed over the past month and is limiting sleep and walking. She has been using acetaminophen with only partial relief. Her oncology team is considering additional pain-directed treatment and referral for symptom control. She asks whether the bone-targeted therapy alone should be enough for the pain.
proposed_actions: Do not use the bone-modifying agent alone as the only treatment for her bone pain.
Apply standard supportive care and pain management, including analgesia, adjunct therapies, radiotherapy, surgery, systemic anticancer therapy, and referral to supportive care and pain management.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has bone pain from bone metastases and is receiving a BMA
Guideline Action: do not use the BMA alone as the only treatment for bone pain
Source Statement: The analgesic effects of BMAs are modest, and they should not be used alone for bone pain.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: monitoring BMA use with biochemical markers
Guideline Action: do not use biochemical markers to monitor BMA use routinely
Source Statement: this ASCO guideline update does not alter the prior recommendation (No. 7) that the use of the biochemical markers to monitor BMA use is not recommended for routine care.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has breast cancer with evidence of bone metastases
Guideline Action: treat with a bone-modifying agent (BMA)
Source Statement: patients with breast cancer who have evidence of bone metastases should be treated with BMAs.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient with bone metastases is being treated with denosumab
Guideline Action: use denosumab 120 mg subcutaneously every 4 weeks
Source Statement: Options include denosumab, 120 mg subcutaneously, every 4 weeks.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient with bone metastases is being treated with pamidronate
Guideline Action: use pamidronate 90 mg intravenously every 3 to 4 weeks
Source Statement: Options include ... pamidronate, 90 mg intravenously, every 3 to 4 weeks.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient with bone metastases is being treated with zoledronic acid
Guideline Action: use zoledronic acid 4 mg intravenously every 12 weeks or every 3 to 4 weeks
Source Statement: Options include ... zoledronic acid, 4 mg intravenously every 12 weeks or every 3 to 4 weeks.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient with bone metastases is receiving zoledronic acid
Guideline Action: administer zoledronic acid over no less than 15 minutes
Source Statement: If patients are treated with zoledronic acid, 4 mg intravenously administered over no less than 15 minutes, dosing options are every 12 weeks or every 3 to 4 weeks.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has bone pain from bone metastases
Guideline Action: apply standard supportive care and pain management, including analgesia, adjunct therapies, radiotherapy, surgery, systemic anticancer therapy, and referral to supportive care and pain management
Source Statement: The Update Committee recommends that the current standard of care for supportive care and pain management-analgesia, adjunct therapies, radiotherapy, surgery, systemic anticancer therapy, and referral to supportive care and pain management-be applied.
proposed_actions_text:
- Do not use the bone-modifying agent alone as the only treatment for her bone pain.
- Apply standard supportive care and pain management, including analgesia, adjunct therapies, radiotherapy, surgery, systemic anticancer therapy, and referral to supportive care and pain management.

patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man presents with a 3-month history of progressive sore throat, dysphagia to solids, and a painless left neck mass. Biopsy of the tonsillar lesion shows squamous cell carcinoma, and the pathology team plans HPV-status testing to help characterize the tumor. The specimen is being processed in a licensed, accredited laboratory using paraffin-embedded tissue sections cut at 4 microns. Initial p16 immunohistochemical staining shows diffuse, moderate-to-strong cytoplasmic and nuclear staining in about 80% of tumour cells. The oncologist asks whether the HPV result should change the treatment plan immediately.
proposed_actions: Routinely test the tumour for HPV status because this is an adult with oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma.
Use p16 immunohistochemical staining as the initial test to determine HPV status.
Consider the p16 result positive because the staining is cytoplasmic and nuclear, moderate to strong and diffuse, and present in at least 70% of tumour cells.
Perform the testing on paraffin-block sections or unstained slides cut at 4 microns in a licensed and accredited laboratory.
Do not alter management decisions based on HPV testing outside the context of a clinical trial.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Considering changing management based on HPV test results outside a clinical trial
Guideline Action: Do not alter management decisions based on HPV testing except in the context of a clinical trial
Source Statement: Altering management decisions based on results from HPV testing is not recommended beyond the context of a clinical trial at this time.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Using HPV PCR testing alone
Guideline Action: Avoid qualitative HPV PCR assay detection alone
Source Statement: Qualitative HPV PCR assay detection alone should be avoided.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient has oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma
Guideline Action: Routinely test the tumour for HPV status
Source Statement: The tumours of all adult patients presenting with oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinomas should be routinely tested for HPV status.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient has metastatic squamous cell carcinoma to neck nodes from an unknown head and neck primary
Guideline Action: Routinely test the neck nodal tissue for HPV status
Source Statement: It is recommended that the neck nodal tissue of patients with metastatic squamous cell carcinoma to neck nodes from an unknown head and neck primary be routinely tested for HPV status.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: HPV testing is being performed for oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma
Guideline Action: Use p16 immunohistochemical staining as the initial test to determine HPV status
Source Statement: It is recommended that HPV status in oropharyngeal SCC be initially determined using immunohistochemical (IHC) staining for p16.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: p16 staining shows cytoplasmic and nuclear staining, is moderate to strong and diffuse, and is present in at least 70% of tumour cells
Guideline Action: Consider the p16 result positive
Source Statement: IHC staining for p16 can be considered positive when the following three criteria are met: cytoplasmic and nuclear staining; staining is moderate to strong and diffuse; staining is present in at least 70% of tumour cells.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: HPV testing is being done in the pathology laboratory
Guideline Action: Perform analysis on sections from paraffin blocks or unstained slides cut at 4 microns
Source Statement: Analysis should be performed on sections from paraffin blocks or unstained slides cut at 4 microns.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: HPV-status testing is being performed
Guideline Action: Carry out testing in licensed and accredited laboratories
Source Statement: All testing should be carried out in licensed and accredited laboratories.
proposed_actions_text:
- Routinely test the tumour for HPV status because this is an adult with oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma.
- Use p16 immunohistochemical staining as the initial test to determine HPV status.
- Consider the p16 result positive because the staining is cytoplasmic and nuclear, moderate to strong and diffuse, and present in at least 70% of tumour cells.
- Perform the testing on paraffin-block sections or unstained slides cut at 4 microns in a licensed and accredited laboratory.
- Do not alter management decisions based on HPV testing outside the context of a clinical trial.

patient_vignette: A 52-year-old woman returns for surveillance 18 months after completing chemoradiation for stage IIB cervical cancer. She is clinically disease-free, reports no vaginal bleeding, pelvic pain, weight loss, or bowel/bladder changes, and her exam is unremarkable. Her oncologist is discussing whether to order a PET-CT because she feels anxious about recurrence, but the patient has no symptoms suggestive of relapse. The team reviews her follow-up plan and notes that she is still within the first two years after treatment. She asks how often she should be seen and what warning signs should prompt an earlier visit.
proposed_actions: Do not use PET-CT for routine follow-up surveillance.
Use this follow-up guideline for surveillance after primary treatment; do not apply it to women treated for cervical precancer.
Have follow-up care conducted and coordinated by a physician experienced in surveillance of patients with cancer.
Maintain continuity of care and discuss symptoms of recurrence with the patient to help detect recurrence early.
Schedule visits every 3 to 4 months during years 1 to 2, then every 6 to 12 months during years 3 to 5.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: considering PET-CT during follow-up
Guideline Action: Do not use PET-CT for routine follow-up surveillance.
Source Statement: Because their role has not been evaluated in a definitive manner, the following investigations are not advocated: o Positron emission tomography (PET) with computed tomography (PET-CT),

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: considering other imaging tests in an asymptomatic patient
Guideline Action: Do not use other imaging tests routinely in asymptomatic patients.
Source Statement: Because their role has not been evaluated in a definitive manner, the following investigations are not advocated: o Other imaging or biomarker tests in asymptomatic patients.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: considering biomarker tests in an asymptomatic patient
Guideline Action: Do not use biomarker tests routinely in asymptomatic patients.
Source Statement: Because their role has not been evaluated in a definitive manner, the following investigations are not advocated: o Other imaging or biomarker tests in asymptomatic patients.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient was treated with radiotherapy and is in follow-up beyond five years
Guideline Action: Do not perform cytological follow-up.
Source Statement: Cytological follow-up is not recommended for patients who have been treated with radiotherapy.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient is clinically disease-free and asymptomatic after potentially curative primary treatment for cervical cancer
Guideline Action: Use this follow-up guideline for surveillance after primary treatment; do not apply it to women treated for cervical precancer.
Source Statement: This practice guideline applies to women who are clinically disease free and asymptomatic after receiving potentially curative primary treatment for cervical cancer. This guideline does not apply to the follow-up of women who have been treated for cervical precancer.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: providing follow-up care after primary treatment for cervical cancer
Guideline Action: Have follow-up care conducted and coordinated by a physician experienced in surveillance of patients with cancer.
Source Statement: Follow-up care after primary treatment should be conducted and coordinated by a physician experienced in the surveillance of patients with cancer.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: during follow-up care after primary treatment
Guideline Action: Maintain continuity of care and discuss symptoms of recurrence with the patient to help detect recurrence early.
Source Statement: Continuity of care and dialogue between the healthcare professional and patient about symptoms of recurrence may enhance and facilitate early cancer recurrence detection because the majority of women who develop a recurrence have symptoms and signs that occur outside scheduled follow-up visits.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: follow-up within the first 5 years after treatment
Guideline Action: Schedule visits every 3 to 4 months during years 1 to 2, then every 6 to 12 months during years 3 to 5.
Source Statement: A reasonable follow-up strategy involves visits at the following intervals: o every three to four months within the first two years, o every six to 12 months from years 3 to 5.
proposed_actions_text:
- Do not use PET-CT for routine follow-up surveillance.
- Use this follow-up guideline for surveillance after primary treatment; do not apply it to women treated for cervical precancer.
- Have follow-up care conducted and coordinated by a physician experienced in surveillance of patients with cancer.
- Maintain continuity of care and discuss symptoms of recurrence with the patient to help detect recurrence early.
- Schedule visits every 3 to 4 months during years 1 to 2, then every 6 to 12 months during years 3 to 5.

patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man presents with a PSA of 11.8 ng/mL and an abnormal digital rectal exam showing mild left-sided induration. He has never had a prostate biopsy. He is otherwise healthy, has a life expectancy well beyond 10 years, and says he would want curative treatment if clinically significant prostate cancer is found. His urologist considers him at elevated risk for clinically significant, clinically localized prostate cancer and is planning diagnostic workup before biopsy.
proposed_actions: Perform multiparametric MRI prior to biopsy.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Biopsy-naive patient at elevated risk of clinically significant prostate cancer and a candidate for curative management with suspected clinically localized prostate cancer
Guideline Action: Perform multiparametric MRI prior to biopsy
Source Statement: MPMRI is recommended prior to biopsy in patients who are candidates for curative management with suspected clinically localized prostate cancer.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Biopsy-naive patient has a positive multiparametric MRI
