patient_vignette: A 29-year-old surgical resident is rotating through gastroenterology and asks to be signed off for independent colonoscopy practice before starting a community job next month. He has completed a short endoscopy elective and has assisted on several procedures, but his supervising physician notes that he has not yet completed a structured colonoscopy training period. During review, the resident says he is comfortable with basic scope handling but has not independently performed enough procedures to demonstrate consistent performance, and he is still learning indications, contraindications, and surveillance intervals.
proposed_actions: Acquire the necessary specific knowledge and technical training in colonoscopy over a period of at least six months before being qualified.

Assess cognitive competence, including contraindications, indications, screening and surveillance intervals, histologic classification of polyps, and management of findings encountered at colonoscopy.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: accessory labeled single use or disposable
Guideline Action: do not reprocess the accessory
Source Statement: Accessories labeled as either single use or disposable should not be reprocessed.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: withdrawal time as a quality metric
Guideline Action: do not require a specific withdrawal time target or audit withdrawal time for quality assurance
Source Statement: It is not necessary to achieve a specific withdrawal time target or to audit this indicator for quality assurance purposes.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: gastroenterologist seeking credentialing
Guideline Action: complete a formal two-year subspecialty training program, with an option for a third year, before entering full-time practice
Source Statement: To be considered for credentialing, gastroenterologists must complete a formal two-year subspecialty training program, with the option of a third year of subspecialty training, before entering full-time practice.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: other physician including surgical resident seeking qualification for colonoscopy
Guideline Action: acquire the necessary specific knowledge and technical training in colonoscopy over at least six months
Source Statement: Prior to being qualified, other physicians, including surgical residents, must acquire the necessary specific knowledge and technical training in colonoscopy over a period of at least six months.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: trainee being assessed for competence
Guideline Action: ensure average independent cecal intubation rate is at least 85% for all colonoscopies
Source Statement: To be considered competent colonoscopists, trainees should achieve an average independent cecal intubation rate (CIR) of at least 85% for all colonoscopies and are expected to have performed at least 300 colonoscopies during training.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: trainee completing colonoscopy training
Guideline Action: ensure at least 300 colonoscopies are performed during training
Source Statement: To be considered competent colonoscopists, trainees should achieve an average independent cecal intubation rate (CIR) of at least 85% for all colonoscopies and are expected to have performed at least 300 colonoscopies during training.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: trainee completing 300 colonoscopies during training
Guideline Action: ensure at least 50 polypectomies are anticipated during training
Source Statement: If 300 colonoscopies are performed during training, it is anticipated that at least 50 polypectomies would have been performed.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: trainee completing training
Guideline Action: assess cognitive competence including contraindications, indications, screening and surveillance intervals, histologic classification of polyps, and management of findings
Source Statement: In addition to proficiency in the technical aspects of colonoscopy, proficiency in cognitive aspects of the procedure is essential, including knowledge of appropriate contraindications and indications for colonoscopy, application of appropriate screening and surveillance intervals (4), histologic classification of polyps and their significance, and knowledge of how to deal with findings encountered at the time of colonoscopy.
proposed_actions_text:
- Acquire the necessary specific knowledge and technical training in colonoscopy over a period of at least six months before being qualified.
- Assess cognitive competence, including contraindications, indications, screening and surveillance intervals, histologic classification of polyps, and management of findings encountered at colonoscopy.

patient_vignette: A 63-year-old man with limited-stage small cell lung cancer completed four cycles of platinum-etoposide chemotherapy followed by concurrent thoracic radiotherapy. He has WHO performance status 1, no neurologic symptoms, and post-treatment CT shows no progression of disease. Brain MRI shows no metastases. He is recovering well, with mild fatigue but otherwise stable weight and appetite. At follow-up, the oncology team discusses next steps to reduce the risk of future brain metastases. The patient asks whether any additional treatment is recommended now that his cancer has not progressed on first-line therapy.
proposed_actions: Offer prophylactic cranial irradiation at 25 Gy in 10 fractions.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has limited-stage SCLC, WHO performance status 0 to 2, and disease has not progressed on first-line treatment
Guideline Action: Offer prophylactic cranial irradiation at 25 Gy in 10 fractions
Source Statement: Offer prophylactic cranial irradiation at a dose of 25 Gy in 10 fractions to people with limited-stage disease SCLC and World Health Organization (WHO) performance status 0 to 2, if their disease has not progressed on first-line treatment.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: patient has extensive-stage SCLC and has had a partial or complete response to chemotherapy within the thorax and at distant sites
Guideline Action: Consider thoracic radiotherapy with prophylactic cranial irradiation
Source Statement: Consider thoracic radiotherapy with prophylactic cranial irradiation for people with extensive-stage disease SCLC who have had a partial or complete response to chemotherapy within the thorax and at distant sites.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: patient has extensive-stage SCLC, WHO performance status 0 to 2, and disease has responded to first-line treatment
Guideline Action: Consider prophylactic cranial irradiation at 25 Gy in 10 fractions
Source Statement: Consider prophylactic cranial irradiation at a dose of 25 Gy in 10 fractions for people with extensive-stage disease SCLC and WHO performance status 0 to 2, if their disease has responded to first-line treatment.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: patient has extensive-stage SCLC and has responded to treatment
Guideline Action: Consider PCI because the recommendation strength is not strong
Source Statement: The committee agreed that "consider" is the appropriate strength for the recommendation on PCI.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: patient has extensive-stage SCLC and has responded to treatment
Guideline Action: Consider PCI when benefits are judged to probably outweigh harms, acknowledging uncertainty
Source Statement: The Working Group members agreed that the benefits are equal to or probably outweigh the harms, but they had some uncertainty about this. Therefore, they agreed with the NICE committee that the recommendation should not be strong and using the word "consider" was appropriate.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 50
Recommendation Class: NO_RECOMMENDATION
Activation Condition: patient has SCLC without brain metastases and the question is whether to use prophylactic cranial irradiation instead of routine MRI follow-up
Guideline Action: Support further research comparing prophylactic cranial irradiation with routine magnetic resonance imaging follow-up
Source Statement: What is the effectiveness and cost effectiveness of prophylactic cranial irradiation compared with routine magnetic resonance imaging follow-up in people with SCLC without brain metastases?

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 50
Recommendation Class: NO_RECOMMENDATION
Activation Condition: considering prophylactic cranial irradiation for SCLC
Guideline Action: Do not add extra recommendations beyond the endorsed NICE recommendations
Source Statement: The Working Group members decided to endorse all of the recommendations from NICE without any modifications.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 50
Recommendation Class: NO_RECOMMENDATION
Activation Condition: considering prophylactic cranial irradiation for SCLC
Guideline Action: Use the endorsed recommendations as written because the Working Group endorsed them without modifications
Source Statement: The Working Group members decided to endorse all of the recommendations from NICE without any modifications.
proposed_actions_text: - Offer prophylactic cranial irradiation at 25 Gy in 10 fractions.

patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with prostate adenocarcinoma is seen after staging workup for a rising PSA and abnormal MRI. Biopsy shows Gleason 9 disease with extracapsular extension, but CT and bone scan show no distant metastases. He has no bone pain, maintains a good performance status, and has not started androgen deprivation therapy yet. His creatinine clearance is 78 mL/min. He asks whether he should start a bone-strengthening medication now to prevent cancer spread to the bones. He is otherwise healthy and takes only tamsulosin for urinary symptoms.
proposed_actions: Do not use bisphosphonates to reduce the risk of first bone metastasis in this patient with high-risk localized prostate cancer.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: men with high-risk localized prostate cancer
Guideline Action: do not use bisphosphonates to reduce the risk of first bone metastasis
Source Statement: In men with high-risk localized prostate cancer, bisphosphonates are not recommended to reduce the risk of first bone metastasis.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: men with nonmetastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer
Guideline Action: do not use denosumab at the bone metastasis-indicated dosage to reduce the risk of first bone metastasis
Source Statement: In men with nonmetastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (CRPC), denosumab at the bone metastasis-indicated dosage is not recommended to reduce the risk of first bone metastasis.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: creatinine clearance is below 30 mL/min or serum creatinine is above 265 µmol/L in a man considered for zoledronic acid
Guideline Action: do not give zoledronic acid
Source Statement: ZA is not recommended below a CrCl of 30 mL/min (or SCr >265 µmol/L).

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: a patient is receiving denosumab and zoledronic acid together
Guideline Action: do not give denosumab and zoledronic acid in combination
Source Statement: Denosumab and ZA should not be given in combination.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: men with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer
Guideline Action: use zoledronic acid at the bone metastasis-indicated dosage for preventing or delaying skeletal-related events
Source Statement: In men with metastatic CRPC (mCRPC), either ZA (minimally symptomatic or asymptomatic disease) or denosumab (disease independent of symptoms) (both at bone metastasis-indicated dosages) is recommended for preventing or delaying SREs.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: men with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer
Guideline Action: use denosumab at the bone metastasis-indicated dosage for preventing or delaying skeletal-related events
Source Statement: In men with metastatic CRPC (mCRPC), either ZA (minimally symptomatic or asymptomatic disease) or denosumab (disease independent of symptoms) (both at bone metastasis-indicated dosages) is recommended for preventing or delaying SREs.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: men with symptomatic metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer
Guideline Action: use radium-223 to extend overall survival
Source Statement: In men with symptomatic mCRPC, Ra-223 is recommended to extend overall survival.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: men with symptomatic metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer and predominantly bony metastases with no visceral metastases or large nodal metastases
Guideline Action: use radium-223 to extend overall survival
Source Statement: This recommendation applies to men with predominantly bony metastases and no evidence of visceral metastases or large nodal metastases.
proposed_actions_text: - Do not use bisphosphonates to reduce the risk of first bone metastasis in this patient with high-risk localized prostate cancer.

patient_vignette: A 25-year-old woman presents to discuss breast cancer screening. She was treated for Hodgkin lymphoma at age 18 with mantle radiation totaling 30 Gy, and her current risk assessment places her in risk category 5. She has no breast symptoms and is anxious because a relative was recently diagnosed with breast cancer. She asks whether she should begin screening now, since she has heard that high-risk women need early imaging. Her clinician reviews her history and confirms that she falls into a high-risk group, but also notes that she is only 25 years old. The discussion focuses on what screening, if any, should be started at this age and whether MRI should be added to mammography later on.
proposed_actions: Do not start screening from age 25.
If/when screening is undertaken in risk category 5 patients who received at least 20 Gy radiation before age 30, use MRI in addition to mammography because the benefits outweigh the potential harms.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: woman is in risk category 5 and is age 25
Guideline Action: do not start screening from age 25
Source Statement: This (Ontario) Working Group, however, did not find justification for screening from age 25 in this group (or any of the other high risk groups).

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: woman is in target population risk category 1, 2, 3, or 5
Guideline Action: perform MRI in addition to mammography
Source Statement: MRI in addition to mammography is recommended for women in target population risk categories 1, 2, 3, and 5 above.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: women are being screened for high breast cancer risk
Guideline Action: screen annually
Source Statement: While there is insufficient evidence at this time to make a definitive recommendation regarding the appropriate screening frequency, it is the opinion of the Working Group that women should be screened annually, as this was the frequency typical of the identified studies on which the recommendation for screening is based.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: women are being screened for high breast cancer risk
Guideline Action: screen from age 30 to 69 years
Source Statement: While there is insufficient evidence at this time to make a definitive recommendation regarding the ages of patients who should be screened, it is the opinion of the Working Group that women should be screened annually from 30 to 69 years of age.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: woman is in risk category 5 and received at least 20 Gy radiation before age 30
Guideline Action: use MRI in addition to mammography because benefits outweigh harms
Source Statement: It is the opinion of the Working Group that the benefits of MRI in terms of increased sensitivity outweigh the potential harms of higher recall rates and biopsy rates for all women in risk category 5 who received ≥20 Gy radiation before the age of 30.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: woman is in risk category 4 with high risk benign biopsy or previous breast cancer plus breast density at least 50% and family history of breast cancer but not enough to be category 3
Guideline Action: consider adding MRI to mammography
Source Statement: With respect to risk category 4, there are preliminary data that a subgroup of patients in risk category 4 might benefit from the addition of MRI to mammography (e.g., women who in addition to a high risk benign biopsy or previous breast cancer, also have breast density ≥50% and a family history of breast cancer though insufficient to put them in category 3).

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 50
Recommendation Class: NO_RECOMMENDATION
Activation Condition: patient is in risk category 4
Guideline Action: do not make a recommendation for MRI screening based on current evidence
Source Statement: The evidence is insufficient to recommend MRI screening for patients in risk category 4.
proposed_actions_text:
- Do not start screening from age 25.
- If/when screening is undertaken in risk category 5 patients who received at least 20 Gy radiation before age 30, use MRI in addition to mammography because the benefits outweigh the potential harms.

patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man is 18 months after lobectomy and adjuvant chemotherapy for stage II non-small cell lung cancer. He completed curative-intent therapy and has been attending routine surveillance visits. Over the past 3 weeks, he has developed a persistent dry cough, new left-sided chest discomfort, and unintentional weight loss of 4 kg. He also reports increasing fatigue but no fever or infectious symptoms. He continues to smoke about 5 cigarettes per day despite prior quit attempts. On exam, he is thin but in no acute distress; oxygen saturation is 96% on room air, and lung exam is unchanged from baseline. Given the new symptoms in a lung cancer survivor, recurrence must be considered, while he also remains due for ongoing surveillance after curative-intent treatment.
proposed_actions: Evaluate the new cough, chest discomfort, weight loss, and fatigue according to symptoms suggestive of recurrence.
Provide surveillance follow-up care after completion of curative-intent therapy.
Ensure surveillance includes medical history, physical examination, and chest imaging.
Provide smoking cessation counselling.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: A lung cancer survivor develops symptoms suggestive of recurrence
Guideline Action: Evaluate according to the symptoms
Source Statement: Survivors who develop symptoms suggestive of recurrence, should be evaluated according to those symptoms.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: After curative-intent treatment for NSCLC
Guideline Action: Schedule follow-up visits with medical history, physical examination, and chest imaging every 3 months in years 1 and 2, every 6 months in year 3, then annually thereafter
Source Statement: Following curative-intent treatment for NSCLC, survivors should receive scheduled follow-up visits that include a medical history, physical examination and chest imaging. Clinical evaluations should be conducted every three months in years 1 and 2, every six months in year 3 and annually thereafter.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: After curative-intent treatment for SCLC
Guideline Action: Schedule follow-up visits with medical history, physical examination, and chest imaging every 3 months in years 1 and 2, every 6 months in year 3, then annually thereafter
Source Statement: Following curative-intent treatment for SCLC, survivors should receive scheduled follow-up visits that include a medical history, physical examination and chest imaging. Clinical evaluations should be conducted every three months in years 1 and 2, every six months in year 3 and annually thereafter.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Lung cancer survivor has late side effects of curative-intent therapy
Guideline Action: Provide support to help manage symptoms and improve quality of life
Source Statement: Health-related quality of life (QoL) is very important for long-term survivors suffering from late side effects of their curative-intent therapy (including surgery, chemotherapy and radiation therapy). The following is a summary of issues reported by survivors. Health care professionals need to aid lung cancer survivors in handling these symptoms to improve QoL.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Lung cancer survivor has completed curative-intent therapy
Guideline Action: Provide surveillance follow-up care
Source Statement: For lung cancer survivors who have completed curative-intent therapy, surveillance is required and may be provided by specialists, family physicians or hospital-based nurses.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Lung cancer survivor has completed curative-intent therapy for NSCLC or SCLC
Guideline Action: Provide smoking cessation counselling
Source Statement: Smoking cessation counselling is recommended for patients who have completed curativeintent therapy for NSCLC and SCLC.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Providing smoking cessation advice to a lung cancer survivor
Guideline Action: Use behavioural and pharmacotherapy support in addition to verbal advice when possible
Source Statement: Although verbal cessation advice from a health care professional is of benefit, interventions that involve behavioural and pharmacotherapy support in addition to verbal advice is recommended.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: A lung cancer survivor has completed curative-intent therapy and ongoing surveillance is being planned
Guideline Action: Ensure surveillance includes medical history, physical examination, and chest imaging
Source Statement: Following curative-intent treatment for NSCLC, survivors should receive scheduled follow-up visits that include a medical history, physical examination and chest imaging.
proposed_actions_text:
- Evaluate the new cough, chest discomfort, weight loss, and fatigue according to symptoms suggestive of recurrence.
- Provide surveillance follow-up care after completion of curative-intent therapy.
- Ensure surveillance includes medical history, physical examination, and chest imaging.
- Provide smoking cessation counselling.

patient_vignette: An 80-year-old man with localized prostate cancer diagnosed 4 years ago (Gleason 3+3) has been managed with active surveillance. His PSA has been stable around 5.1 ng/mL, his digital rectal exams have remained normal, and his confirmatory TRUS biopsy 3 years ago showed no upgrading. He feels well and asks whether he still needs scheduled biopsies now that he is older. He has no new urinary symptoms and no concerning changes in PSA kinetics or exam findings.
proposed_actions: Do not continue serial biopsy past age 80.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient on active surveillance reaches age 80.
Guideline Action: Do not continue serial biopsy past age 80.
Source Statement: Serial biopsy should not continue past the age of 80.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient has low-risk localized prostate cancer (Gleason score ≤6).
Guideline Action: Use active surveillance as the preferred disease management strategy.
Source Statement: For patients with low-risk (Gleason score ≤6) localized prostate cancer, AS is the preferred disease management strategy.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient is on active surveillance for localized prostate cancer.
Guideline Action: Include PSA testing every 3 to 6 months.
Source Statement: The AS protocol should include the following tests: -PSA test every 3 to 6 months.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient is on active surveillance for localized prostate cancer.
Guideline Action: Include digital rectal examination every year.
Source Statement: The AS protocol should include the following tests: -DRE every year.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient is on active surveillance for localized prostate cancer.
Guideline Action: Perform a 12- to 14-core confirmatory TRUS biopsy including anterior directed cores within 6 to 12 months.
Source Statement: The AS protocol should include the following tests: -12-to 14-core confirmatory transrectal ultrasound (TRUS) biopsy (including anterior directed cores) within 6 to 12 months, then serial biopsy a minimum of every 3 to 5 years thereafter.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient is on active surveillance after confirmatory biopsy.
Guideline Action: Repeat serial biopsy at least every 3 to 5 years.
Source Statement: The AS protocol should include the following tests: -12-to 14-core confirmatory transrectal ultrasound (TRUS) biopsy (including anterior directed cores) within 6 to 12 months, then serial biopsy a minimum of every 3 to 5 years thereafter.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient on active surveillance has rapidly rising PSA, PSA density over 0.2, higher PSA than expected for prostate size, DRE abnormality, or very low PSA free/total ratio.
Guideline Action: Investigate further with mpMRI or earlier repeat biopsy.
Source Statement: Discordant findings between a patient's clinical course and pathologic findings can include rapidly rising PSA, PSA density over 0.2, higher PSA than expected for prostate size, DRE abnormality, and very low PSA free/total ratio. Presence of these findings requires further investigation with mpMRI or earlier repeat biopsy.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient on active surveillance has discordant clinical findings and pathologic findings.
Guideline Action: Use mpMRI at entry or during follow-up when indicated.
Source Statement: However, being cognizant of both the high cost of mpMRI and its promise, it is recommended that when a patient's clinical findings are discordant with the pathologic findings, a mpMRI is indicated. When indicated, it may be considered at entry or during follow-up.
proposed_actions_text: - Do not continue serial biopsy past age 80.

patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man is referred to oncology after an incidental lung nodule is found on CT performed for persistent cough. At the end of the note, the document includes only publication details and contact information for the lead author, Dr. Eric Winquist, through the PEBC, along with a statement that the evidence review has been published as a supplement. It also notes that the report and illustrations are copyrighted by Cancer Care Ontario and may not be reproduced without written permission. No diagnostic algorithm, treatment recommendation, or follow-up plan is provided in the document excerpt.
proposed_actions: No clinical action can be extracted from this document excerpt.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 50
Recommendation Class: NO_RECOMMENDATION
Activation Condition: Document contains only publication/contact and copyright information, with no clinical recommendations.
Guideline Action: No clinical action can be extracted.
Source Statement: For information about this document, please contact Dr. Eric Winquist, the lead author, through the PEBC via: Phone: 905-527-4322 ext. 42822 Fax: 905 526-6775 E-mail: ccopgi@mcmaster.ca

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 50
Recommendation Class: NO_RECOMMENDATION
Activation Condition: Document states that the evidence review has been published as a supplement.
Guideline Action: No clinical action can be extracted.
Source Statement: The evidence review has been published as a Supplement

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 50
Recommendation Class: NO_RECOMMENDATION
Activation Condition: Document states copyright restrictions on reproduction of the report and illustrations.
Guideline Action: No clinical action can be extracted.
Source Statement: This report is copyrighted by Cancer Care Ontario; the report and the illustrations herein may not be reproduced without the express written permission of Cancer Care Ontario.
proposed_actions_text: - No clinical action can be extracted from this document excerpt.

patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man presents with newly diagnosed stage IV diffuse large B-cell lymphoma with bulky abdominal nodes, night sweats, and weight loss. His ECOG performance status is 1. He has well-controlled hypertension but no known cardiac disease, heart failure, or other major comorbid illness. Baseline labs show mild anemia and elevated LDH; renal and liver function are acceptable. The oncology team is planning first-line treatment and discusses CHOP-based chemotherapy. Because of his age, they consider whether to intensify treatment, add etoposide, or use a compressed 14-day schedule with growth factor support. The patient asks whether a more aggressive regimen would improve his chances of cure.
proposed_actions: Use CHOP combination chemotherapy with the same dose and schedule as in younger patients.
Add rituximab to CHOP.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: considering granulocyte-colony stimulating factor as routine primary therapy with chemotherapy in this population
Guideline Action: Do not use G-CSF routinely as primary therapy
Source Statement: There is insufficient evidence to support the routine use of granulocyte-colony stimulating factor as primary therapy.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: considering a fractionated weekly CHOP schedule instead of standard CHOP
Guideline Action: Use standard CHOP rather than fractionated weekly CHOP
Source Statement: No benefits from fractionating the treatment schedule have been detected; the potential for outcomes to be inferior is suggested.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: considering CHOP plus etoposide in a compressed 14-day schedule
Guideline Action: Avoid CHOEP-14 because of excessive toxicity
Source Statement: patients receiving CHOEP every 14 days experienced excessive toxicity

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: considering CHOP plus etoposide versus CHOP alone in older patients
Guideline Action: Do not add etoposide routinely to CHOP
Source Statement: No differences were detected in response rate, TTF, or OS, and the authors commented that the addition of etoposide was associated with significant toxicity.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: considering more intensive regimens than CHOP
Guideline Action: Avoid more intensive regimens when they add toxicity without survival benefit
Source Statement: Two studies testing chemotherapy regimens that are more intensive than CHOP both detected an increase in toxicity with no survival benefit detected.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: considering second- or third-generation regimens instead of CHOP
Guideline Action: Prefer CHOP because it is at least as effective or less toxic
Source Statement: Subset analyses comparing CHOP with "second and third generation" regimens show that CHOP is at least as effective or less toxic than these other regimens.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient is older than 60 with newly diagnosed advanced-stage aggressive histology non-Hodgkin lymphoma, ECOG less than 4, and no significant comorbid illness
Guideline Action: Use CHOP combination chemotherapy with the same dose and schedule as in younger patients
Source Statement: Combination chemotherapy with cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine and prednisone (CHOP) is recommended for patients with no apparent cardiac disease or significant comorbidity. Dose and schedule should be the same as that used in younger patients.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has diffuse large B-cell lymphoma and meets the guideline target population
Guideline Action: Add rituximab to CHOP
Source Statement: The addition of rituximab to CHOP is recommended for patients with diffuse large B-cell lymphoma.
proposed_actions_text:
- Use CHOP combination chemotherapy with the same dose and schedule as in younger patients.
- Add rituximab to CHOP.

patient_vignette: A 42-year-old man with newly diagnosed HIV-associated diffuse large B-cell lymphoma presents for treatment planning. He has no prior lymphoma therapy. His CD4 count is 34/mm3, HIV viral load is detectable, and staging shows advanced nodal disease but no CNS involvement. He is otherwise fit, with ECOG performance status 1, and the oncology team considers him a candidate for curative-intent combination chemotherapy. Baseline labs show normal renal and hepatic function. He asks whether rituximab should be included with his chemotherapy regimen.
proposed_actions: Do not add rituximab to chemotherapy.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: HIV-related lymphoma and CD4 count below 50/mm3
Guideline Action: Do not add rituximab to chemotherapy
Source Statement: The addition of rituximab to chemotherapy in patients with CD4 <50/mm 3 is not recommended.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Previously untreated aggressive histology CD20-positive B-cell lymphoma and candidate for curative-intent combination chemotherapy
Guideline Action: Give combination chemotherapy with rituximab
Source Statement: Previously untreated patients with aggressive histology CD20-positive B-cell lymphomas who are candidates for treatment with curative intent and will receive combination chemotherapy with curative intent ... should receive this therapy in combination with rituximab.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Previously untreated HIV-related lymphoma, CD4 count at least 50/mm3, and candidate for curative-intent combination chemotherapy
Guideline Action: Give combination chemotherapy with rituximab
Source Statement: Previously untreated patients with HIV-related lymphoma with a CD4 count ≥50/mm 3 who are candidates for treatment with curative intent and will receive combination chemotherapy with curative intent ... should receive this therapy in combination with rituximab.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Previously untreated indolent histology CD20-positive B-cell lymphoma excluding SLL and candidate for chemotherapy
Guideline Action: Give chemotherapy in combination with rituximab
Source Statement: Previously untreated patients with indolent histology CD20-positive B-cell lymphomas, excluding small lymphocytic lymphoma (SLL), who are appropriate candidates for chemotherapy, should receive their chemotherapy in combination with rituximab.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: RECURRENCE_OR_RESTAGING
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Previously treated indolent histology CD20-positive B-cell lymphoma excluding SLL, no prior rituximab, and candidate for chemotherapy
Guideline Action: Give chemotherapy in combination with rituximab or rituximab monotherapy
Source Statement: Patients who have not previously received rituximab and who are appropriate candidates for chemotherapy should receive this chemotherapy in combination with rituximab or as rituximab monotherapy.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: RECURRENCE_OR_RESTAGING
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Previously treated indolent histology CD20-positive B-cell lymphoma excluding SLL, prior rituximab exposure, response lasted at least one year, and candidate for therapy
Guideline Action: Give therapy in combination with rituximab or rituximab monotherapy
Source Statement: Patients who have previously received rituximab ... and who have achieved a response of at least one year's duration from the last rituximab administration and who are appropriate candidates for therapy should receive this therapy in combination with rituximab or as rituximab monotherapy.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Indolent histology CD20-positive B-cell lymphoma excluding SLL and response to combination chemotherapy and/or rituximab
Guideline Action: Follow treatment with maintenance rituximab
Source Statement: For patients with indolent histology CD20-positive B-cell lymphomas, excluding SLL, who respond to treatment with combination chemotherapy and/or rituximab, this treatment should be followed by the use of maintenance rituximab.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Any patient planned for rituximab treatment
Guideline Action: Screen for hepatitis B surface antigen and hepatitis B core antibody before rituximab
Source Statement: The Hematology Disease Site Group recommends that all patients be screened for surface antigen for hepatitis B (HBsAg) and for hepatitis B core antibody (HBcAb) prior to treatment with rituximab.
proposed_actions_text: - Do not add rituximab to chemotherapy.

patient_vignette: A 64-year-old man with extensive-stage small cell lung cancer completed first-line etoposide-cisplatin 5 months ago and had a partial response with marked symptom improvement. He now returns with worsening cough, weight loss, and new right hilar enlargement on CT, consistent with relapsed disease. His treatment-free interval is just over 5 months. He has mild residual peripheral neuropathy from prior cisplatin but remains ambulatory, independent in activities of daily living, and has an ECOG performance status of 1. He is interested in further treatment and asks about options. Because he previously responded and the relapse occurred more than 3 months after completion of chemotherapy, retreatment with the same regimen is being considered, along with other second-line options. Oral topotecan is also discussed as an alternative, since he responded initially and his response lasted longer than 45 days.
proposed_actions: Consider second-line chemotherapy after weighing the treatment-free interval, the extent of response to first-line therapy, residual toxicity from first-line therapy, and performance status.
Because relapse occurred more than 3 months after completion of first-line chemotherapy, consider retreatment with the same regimen that produced the initial response, generally etoposide-cisplatin.
Because he initially responded and the response duration was longer than 45 days, consider oral topotecan as an alternative second-line option.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: adult patient with relapsed SCLC
Guideline Action: Consider second-line chemotherapy only after weighing treatment-free interval, response to first-line therapy, residual toxicity, and performance status.
Source Statement: The selection of patients for treatment with second-line therapy should be dependent on the treatment-free interval, the extent of response to first-line therapy, residual toxicity from first-line therapy, and the performance status of the patient.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: patient relapses three or more months after completion of first-line chemotherapy
Guideline Action: Consider retreatment with the same regimen that produced the initial response, generally etoposide-cisplatin.
Source Statement: patients who relapse three or more months following the completion of first-line chemotherapy may benefit from retreatment with the same regimen that induced their initial response. This would generally mean retreatment with etoposide-cisplatin.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: patient relapses three or more months after completion of first-line chemotherapy
Guideline Action: Consider alternative regimens such as CAV or carboplatin plus etoposide.
Source Statement: Alternative regimens may include cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, and vincristine (CAV) or carboplatin and etoposide.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: patient initially responded to chemotherapy and response duration was 45 days or longer
Guideline Action: Consider oral topotecan as an alternative second-line option.
Source Statement: Oral topotecan is a possible alternative for patients who initially responded to chemotherapy and had a response duration of 45 days or longer.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: patient is not suitable for intravenous therapy and topotecan is being considered
Guideline Action: Consider oral topotecan because it is more convenient and may be an option when IV therapy is unsuitable.
Source Statement: Oral administration is more convenient and may be a treatment option for patients not suitable for intravenous therapy.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: topotecan is given orally
Guideline Action: Monitor for grade 3/4 diarrhea.
Source Statement: Oral administration is associated with a higher incidence of grade 3/4 diarrhea, whereas intravenous administration may result in a higher frequency of grade 3/4 neutropenia.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: topotecan is given intravenously
Guideline Action: Monitor for grade 3/4 neutropenia.
Source Statement: Oral administration is associated with a higher incidence of grade 3/4 diarrhea, whereas intravenous administration may result in a higher frequency of grade 3/4 neutropenia.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: patient fails to respond to first-line therapy or relapses shortly after first-line therapy
Guideline Action: Consider enrollment in a clinical trial to help determine the optimal treatment regimen.
Source Statement: Clinical trials are needed to determine the optimal treatment regimen.
proposed_actions_text:
- Consider second-line chemotherapy after weighing the treatment-free interval, the extent of response to first-line therapy, residual toxicity from first-line therapy, and performance status.
- Because relapse occurred more than 3 months after completion of first-line chemotherapy, consider retreatment with the same regimen that produced the initial response, generally etoposide-cisplatin.
- Because he initially responded and the response duration was longer than 45 days, consider oral topotecan as an alternative second-line option.

patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man with inoperable stage IV colorectal adenocarcinoma presents to discuss first-line systemic therapy. He has liver and lung metastases, good performance status, and no prior chemotherapy. After reading about treatment side effects, he says he would prefer a plan that includes treatment breaks if possible, because he wants to spend more time at home and maintain his energy for family activities. His oncologist reviews standard continuous therapy and explains that intermittent first-line systemic treatment is also a reasonable option to consider. The discussion includes the possibility that intermittent therapy may preserve quality of life without a statistically significant survival disadvantage compared with continuous therapy. The patient agrees that an intermittent approach fits his goals if disease monitoring is built in.
proposed_actions: Discuss intermittent systemic treatment strategies as a reasonable alternative to continuous administration as part of informed treatment decision-making.
Reassure the patient that intermittent first-line therapy is a reasonable alternative to continuous therapy.
Use intermittent therapy as an option because it does not show a statistically significant reduction in overall survival compared with continuous therapy.
Consider intermittent therapy because it may improve or maintain quality of life compared with continuous therapy.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with inoperable, advanced stage IV colorectal cancer being considered for first-line systemic therapy
Guideline Action: Discuss intermittent systemic treatment strategies as a reasonable alternative to continuous administration as part of informed treatment decision-making
Source Statement: Intermittent systemic treatment strategies should be part of an informed discussion of treatment options for this group of patients.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer wants a break from treatment
Guideline Action: Reassure the patient that intermittent first-line therapy is a reasonable alternative to continuous therapy
Source Statement: Patients who want a break from treatment can be reassured that intermittent strategies of administering first-line therapy are a reasonable alternative to continuous administration.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer is considering intermittent versus continuous first-line systemic therapy
Guideline Action: Use intermittent therapy as an option because it does not show a statistically significant reduction in overall survival compared with continuous therapy
Source Statement: Meta-analysis demonstrates no clinically significant survival difference between the continuous and intermittent chemotherapy strategies (HR, 1.02; 95%CI, 0.95-1.10, p=0.62).

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer is considering intermittent versus continuous first-line systemic therapy
Guideline Action: Consider intermittent therapy because it may improve or maintain quality of life compared with continuous therapy
Source Statement: Intermittent strategies of administering first-line systemic therapies to patients with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer (mCRC) do not result in a statistically significant reduction in overall survival and either improve or maintain quality of life compared to continuous administration of therapy.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer is receiving intermittent systemic therapy or a chemotherapy-free interval
Guideline Action: Continue best supportive care
Source Statement: During maintenance therapy or a chemotherapy-free interval, best supportive care should be continued for patients.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Intermittent strategy is being used for unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer
Guideline Action: Use 12 to 18 weeks of induction treatment as a reasonable guideline to consider
Source Statement: All but one of the intermittent strategies offered 12 to 18 weeks of induction treatment and were monitored with imaging at least every 8 to 12 weeks during the intermittent phase of treatment, with reintroduction of the induction chemotherapy at disease progression. These represent reasonable guidelines to consider when using an intermittent strategy, but adaptation of a strategy to individual circumstances should always be considered.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Intermittent strategy is being used for unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer
Guideline Action: Monitor with imaging at least every 8 to 12 weeks during the intermittent phase
Source Statement: All but one of the intermittent strategies offered 12 to 18 weeks of induction treatment and were monitored with imaging at least every 8 to 12 weeks during the intermittent phase of treatment, with reintroduction of the induction chemotherapy at disease progression. These represent reasonable guidelines to consider when using an intermittent strategy, but adaptation of a strategy to individual circumstances should always be considered.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Disease progresses during the intermittent phase
Guideline Action: Reintroduce the induction chemotherapy at disease progression
Source Statement: All but one of the intermittent strategies offered 12 to 18 weeks of induction treatment and were monitored with imaging at least every 8 to 12 weeks during the intermittent phase of treatment, with reintroduction of the induction chemotherapy at disease progression.
proposed_actions_text:
- Discuss intermittent systemic treatment strategies as a reasonable alternative to continuous administration as part of informed treatment decision-making.
- Reassure the patient that intermittent first-line therapy is a reasonable alternative to continuous therapy.
- Use intermittent therapy as an option because it does not show a statistically significant reduction in overall survival compared with continuous therapy.
- Consider intermittent therapy because it may improve or maintain quality of life compared with continuous therapy.

patient_vignette: A 27-year-old woman presents for a routine primary care visit and asks whether she should have a Pap test. She reports that she has never been sexually active and has no history of genital symptoms, abnormal bleeding, or prior cervical screening. She is otherwise healthy and has no immunocompromising conditions. After discussing her preventive care questions, she specifically asks whether cervical cancer screening is needed now.
proposed_actions: Do not perform cervical screening.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: woman has never been sexually active
Guideline Action: Do not perform cervical screening.
Source Statement: Women who have never been sexually active do not require cervical screening.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: woman is younger than 21 years old
Guideline Action: Do not screen routinely.
Source Statement: the authors of this guideline have concluded that the harms of screening women under 21 years of age significantly outweigh the benefits.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: organized screening program with adequate database infrastructure
Guideline Action: Use HPV DNA testing of cervical cells for primary cervical screening.
Source Statement: HPV DNA testing of cells collected from the cervix is recommended for primary cervical screening.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: positive HPV DNA test result
Guideline Action: Perform cytology screening as triage rather than using cytology as the primary screen.
Source Statement: Cytology screening, which was recommended for primary screening in the previous version of this guideline, is now recommended only in the event of a positive HPV DNA test result.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: woman is 30 years of age or older
Guideline Action: Use HPV-based primary screening.
Source Statement: the primary recommendations presented in Part 1 of this guideline are for HPVbased testing for women 30 years of age and over.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: woman is 30 to 65 years old and initial HPV test is negative
Guideline Action: Repeat HPV DNA testing at five-year intervals.
Source Statement: For women aged 30-65, HPV DNA testing is to occur at five-year intervals after an initial negative result.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: HPV test is positive
Guideline Action: Assess the result with cytology testing and do not refer directly to colposcopy.
Source Statement: HPV-positive tests should be assessed with cytology testing and not referred directly to colposcopy.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: HPV positive and cytology negative
Guideline Action: Repeat HPV testing after one year.
Source Statement: Repeat HPV testing for results of HPV positive/cytology negative should be conducted after one year.
proposed_actions_text: - Do not perform cervical screening.

patient_vignette: A 49-year-old woman presents with a 7 cm left breast mass, peau d’orange, and erythema involving more than one-third of the breast, consistent with inflammatory breast cancer. Core biopsy confirms invasive ductal carcinoma. She is being evaluated at diagnosis for local treatment options, and she asks whether she can have lumpectomy because she hopes to avoid mastectomy. The team is also planning neoadjuvant therapy, with surgery to be reconsidered afterward depending on response. No surgery has been performed yet.
proposed_actions: Do not advise breast-conserving surgery.
Consider mastectomy as the standard of care for most patients with locally advanced breast cancer after response to neoadjuvant therapy.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: inflammatory breast cancer
Guideline Action: Do not advise breast-conserving surgery.
Source Statement: BCS is not advised in inflammatory breast cancer because the extent of tumour involvement cannot be reliably ascertained.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: sentinel lymph node biopsy is being considered both before and after neoadjuvant chemotherapy
Guideline Action: Do not perform SLNB both before and after neoadjuvant chemotherapy.
Source Statement: This suggests that SLNB should not be performed both before and after NACT.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: cases being discussed for treatment planning
Guideline Action: Discuss cases in a multidisciplinary case conference.
Source Statement: Communication between oncologists, surgeons, radiologists, and pathologists is essential. A multidisciplinary case conference is the recommended forum for discussion of cases.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: specimen submitted after neoadjuvant therapy
Guideline Action: Indicate any prior neoadjuvant therapy on the pathology specimen submission.
Source Statement: Any prior use of neoadjuvant therapy should be indicated when specimens are submitted for pathologic examination.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: before starting neoadjuvant therapy
Guideline Action: Insert surgical clips marking the original pretreatment tumour location.
Source Statement: It is recommended that surgical clips marking the original (pretreatment) tumour location be inserted before administration of neoadjuvant therapy.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: female patient with locally advanced breast cancer and surgery is being planned after response to neoadjuvant therapy
Guideline Action: Consider mastectomy as the standard of care for most patients.
Source Statement: For most patients with LABC, mastectomy should be considered to be the standard of care.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient strongly desires breast-conserving surgery
Guideline Action: Perform wide excision of the remaining tumour in the original tumour bed plus radiotherapy.
Source Statement: Wide excision of the remaining tumour in the region of the original pre-neoadjuvant treatment tumour bed plus RT is recommended for patients with LABC who strongly desire BCS.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: female patient with locally advanced breast cancer after mastectomy
Guideline Action: Give postoperative radiotherapy.
Source Statement: Radiotherapy following mastectomy is recommended for patients with LABC.
proposed_actions_text:
- Do not advise breast-conserving surgery.
- Consider mastectomy as the standard of care for most patients with locally advanced breast cancer after response to neoadjuvant therapy.

patient_vignette: A 42-year-old man with B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia initially achieved remission after induction and consolidation but relapsed 14 months later. He received salvage chemotherapy and now has a documented second complete remission (CR2) with no detectable blasts on marrow exam. He is otherwise fit, without major cardiac, renal, or pulmonary comorbidities, and he has an HLA-matched sibling donor available. The transplant team is evaluating next steps for durable disease control. Because he is an adult patient with ALL in CR2 after relapse, the case meets criteria for transplant in the recurrence setting.
proposed_actions: Perform allogeneic stem cell transplantation (allo-SCT).
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: RECURRENCE_OR_RESTAGING
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult ALL patient in CR2 or greater, including refractory or relapsed disease
Guideline Action: Perform allo-SCT
Source Statement: Allo-SCT is recommended in CR2 or greater (refractory or relapsed).

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult ALL patient in first complete remission (CR1) and eligible for allo-SCT
Guideline Action: Consider allogeneic stem cell transplantation (allo-SCT) as a treatment option
Source Statement: Allogeneic stem cell transplantation (allo-SCT) is an option for adult patients with acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL) in first complete remission (CR1).

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult ALL patient in remission who is deemed unsuitable for standard myeloablative conditioning (MAC)
Guideline Action: Use reduced-intensity conditioning (RIC) for SCT
Source Statement: Reduced-intensity conditioning (RIC) is an option for patients with acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL) in remission when they are deemed unsuitable for the standard myeloablative conditioning (MAC) regimen.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult ALL patient in remission who is ineligible for MAC allo-SCT
Guideline Action: Consider RIC as an effective therapeutic option
Source Statement: Based on the evidence, the members of the Working Group have determined that RIC could be an effective therapeutic option for patients with ALL who are ineligible for MAC allo-SCT.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult ALL patient with high risk of treatment-related mortality from MAC
Guideline Action: Consider RIC instead of MAC
Source Statement: The systematic review (4) stated that RIC may be a potential therapeutic option in patients with high risk of treatment-related mortality (TRM) associated with MAC regimens, as there was a lack of overall survival benefit of MAC over RIC regimens.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Patient with Philadelphia chromosome-positive ALL (Ph+ ALL) after allo-SCT
Guideline Action: Use a BCR-ABL tyrosine-kinase inhibitor (TKI) post-transplant
Source Statement: Post-transplant use of a BCR-ABL tyrosine-kinase inhibitor (TKI) in patients with Philadelphia chromosome-positive ALL (Ph+ ALL) is a reasonable option.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Patient with Ph+ ALL undergoing allo-SCT
Guideline Action: Consider TKI therapy pre- and/or post-transplant
Source Statement: The consensus is that TKI therapy is useful pre and/or posttransplant.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult ALL patient in CR1 or later who lacks a suitable related or unrelated donor
Guideline Action: Consider haploidentical hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (haplo-SCT)
Source Statement: Haploidentical hematopoietic Stem Cell Transplantation (haplo-SCT) for patients with ALL in CR1 or later who lack a suitable related or unrelated donor is a reasonable option.
proposed_actions_text: - Perform allogeneic stem cell transplantation (allo-SCT).

patient_vignette: A 52-year-old woman with a newly diagnosed left breast invasive ductal carcinoma presents to discuss surgery. Imaging shows a 4.2 cm tumor with biopsy-proven axillary lymph node involvement. The breast surgeon recommends mastectomy, and the patient is interested in immediate reconstruction if feasible. Because she is node-positive and is expected to receive postoperative radiation, the surgical team reviews reconstruction options carefully. On exam, there is no clinical skin or nipple-areolar complex involvement, and she has no history of Paget disease. She has already had diagnostic mammography and breast MRI for staging. A multidisciplinary visit is arranged to coordinate surgical, reconstructive, and radiation planning before finalizing the operative approach.
proposed_actions: Do not use skin-sparing, nipple-sparing, or areola-sparing mastectomy because she intends to receive postoperative radiation and has lymph node-positive early breast cancer.
Initiate discussion of immediate or delayed breast reconstruction at the time mastectomy is offered.
Include a medical oncologist and/or radiation oncologist in the preoperative evaluation through formal consultation or a multidisciplinary cancer conference.
Obtain adequate preoperative breast imaging according to existing guideline recommendations.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: PREOP_STAGING
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient intends postoperative radiation and has lymph node positive early breast cancer, inflammatory breast cancer, or locally advanced breast cancer
Guideline Action: Do not use skin-sparing, nipple-sparing, or areola-sparing mastectomy
Source Statement: SSM, NSM, and ASM are not recommended for women intending to receive postoperative radiation with: early breast cancer who are lymph node positive, inflammatory breast cancer or locally advanced breast cancer who will require postoperative RT.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: PREOP_STAGING
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Invasive tumor involves the skin or nipple-areolar complex clinically
Guideline Action: Do not use skin-sparing, nipple-sparing, or areola-sparing mastectomy
Source Statement: SSM, NSM, and ASM are not recommended for women intending to receive postoperative radiation with: any clinical skin or nipple-areolar complex (NAC) involvement by invasive tumour

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: PREOP_STAGING
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient has Paget disease of the breast or a retroareolar tumor
Guideline Action: Do not perform nipple-sparing or areola-sparing mastectomy
Source Statement: NSM and ASM are not recommended for women with Paget disease of the breast or women with a retroareolar tumour.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Postmastectomy breast reconstruction has been performed
Guideline Action: Do not use surveillance mammography routinely in the reconstructed breast
Source Statement: There is insufficient evidence to support the use of postmastectomy surveillance mammography in the reconstructed breast.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: At the time mastectomy is offered for therapeutic mastectomy
Guideline Action: Initiate discussion of immediate or delayed breast reconstruction
Source Statement: The discussion of immediate or delayed breast reconstruction should be initiated at the time that mastectomy is offered by the general surgeon.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Immediate breast reconstruction is being sought for DCIS
Guideline Action: Perform preoperative evaluation with a general surgeon and a plastic surgeon
Source Statement: For women seeking immediate breast reconstruction for ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS), preoperative evaluation with a general surgeon and a plastic surgeon should be performed.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Immediate breast reconstruction is being sought for early stage breast cancer and adjuvant chemotherapy or radiotherapy may be needed
Guideline Action: Include a medical oncologist and/or radiation oncologist in preoperative evaluation, either by formal consultation or multidisciplinary cancer conference
Source Statement: For women seeking immediate breast reconstruction for early stage breast cancer who will potentially require adjuvant chemotherapy or radiotherapy (RT), a medical oncologist and/or radiation oncologist should be included in preoperative evaluation, either through a formal consultation or by a multidisciplinary cancer conference.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Immediate reconstruction is being planned
Guideline Action: Obtain adequate preoperative breast imaging according to existing guideline recommendations
Source Statement: For women seeking immediate reconstruction, there should be adequate preoperative imaging of the breasts, aligning with existing guideline recommendations.
proposed_actions_text:
- Do not use skin-sparing, nipple-sparing, or areola-sparing mastectomy because she intends to receive postoperative radiation and has lymph node-positive early breast cancer.
- Initiate discussion of immediate or delayed breast reconstruction at the time mastectomy is offered.
- Include a medical oncologist and/or radiation oncologist in the preoperative evaluation through formal consultation or a multidisciplinary cancer conference.
- Obtain adequate preoperative breast imaging according to existing guideline recommendations.

patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with metastatic lung cancer is admitted for progressive dyspnea and fatigue. His oncology team notes that advance care planning has been discussed inconsistently across visits: one clinician focused mainly on code status, while another gave a pamphlet about end-of-life decisions. The patient says he wants care that fits his priorities—staying alert enough to talk with family and avoiding prolonged ICU treatment if recovery is unlikely—but he has not had a structured conversation about these values. His daughter asks whether these discussions might upset him, since he becomes anxious when illness is mentioned. The team is considering a new outpatient ACP workflow and realizes that a few isolated steps have not changed care quality in the past.
proposed_actions: Do not rely on only one or a few ACP elements to achieve meaningful patient care impact.
Do not assume ACP discussions increase distress in children or parents.
Use a system-wide approach to integrate ACP into routine practice and maximize interprofessional team skills.
Focus tool development on enabling values-based discussions rather than treatment-focused discussions.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: When considering limited ACP implementation
Guideline Action: Do not rely on only one or a few ACP elements to achieve meaningful patient care impact.
Source Statement: Addressing only one, or a few, of these elements creates the risks of leaving critical gaps that could prevent positive impacts on quality patient care.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: When using education alone to improve ACP care
Guideline Action: Do not expect education alone to improve care or patient experience without supporting system changes.
Source Statement: However, high-quality education, although essential, on its own is unlikely to improve care or the patient experience in the absence of supporting systemic changes.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: When considering whether pediatric ACP causes distress
Guideline Action: Do not assume ACP discussions increase distress in children or parents.
Source Statement: Concerns that ACP discussions will cause distress in children and parents are not supported by the evidence.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: When considering ACP or GoCD tools for practice
Guideline Action: Use a system-wide approach to integrate ACP into routine practice and maximize interprofessional team skills.
Source Statement: Outcome evidence for ACP suggests the greatest impact is likely to be made by a system-wide approach to integrating ACP into practice, one that maximizes clinical skills of all interprofessional team members.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: When developing ACP tools or processes
Guideline Action: Focus tool development on enabling values-based discussions rather than treatment-focused discussions.
Source Statement: Considering these emerging themes from the evidence suggests that tool development should focus on enabling healthcare providers to facilitate values-based discussions.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: When evaluating ACP tools
Guideline Action: Use values-based outcome measures to assess impact on patient care.
Source Statement: Future studies with corresponding valuesbased outcome measures are needed to evaluate the impact on patient care.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: When implementing ACP programs
Guideline Action: Include community engagement, professional education, standard practices, quality improvement monitoring, supporting policies, and technology infrastructure.
Source Statement: A comprehensive approach to ACP includes community engagement, professional education, development of standard practices, monitoring of these practices through Quality Improvement initiatives, supporting policies and practices, and a technology infrastructures.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: When planning ACP implementation across care settings
Guideline Action: Apply ACP across public health and healthcare sectors with system-level coordination and planning.
Source Statement: This approach must be applied across public health and healthcare sectors to support the exchange of clinical information and will involve system-level coordination and planning.
proposed_actions_text:
- Do not rely on only one or a few ACP elements to achieve meaningful patient care impact.
- Do not assume ACP discussions increase distress in children or parents.
- Use a system-wide approach to integrate ACP into routine practice and maximize interprofessional team skills.
- Focus tool development on enabling values-based discussions rather than treatment-focused discussions.

patient_vignette: A 64-year-old woman with high-grade serous epithelial ovarian cancer presents with symptomatic recurrence 5 months after completing her last platinum-based regimen. She has received three prior anticancer regimens and now has increasing abdominal discomfort, early satiety, and a CT scan showing peritoneal progression without bowel obstruction. Her disease is therefore platinum-resistant and she is not eligible for the AURELIA trial criteria because she has had more than two prior anticancer regimens. She asks about the next treatment steps and whether the goal is cure.
proposed_actions: Do not apply the bevacizumab plus chemotherapy option described for AURELIA-eligible patients.
Set treatment goals around improving quality of life, symptom control, and prolonging progression-free interval or life if possible.
Consider single-agent paclitaxel, topotecan, pegylated liposomal doxorubicin, or gemcitabine as reasonable treatment options.
Assess the patient individually for recurrence status, platinum sensitivity, toxicity, ease of administration, and patient preference, recognizing that systemic therapy is not curative.
Offer participation in a clinical trial if appropriate.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: TREATMENT_RESPONSE
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: platinum-resistant recurrent ovarian cancer and not eligible for the AURELIA trial criteria
Guideline Action: Do not apply the bevacizumab plus chemotherapy option described for AURELIA-eligible patients.
Source Statement: Ineligible patients include those who have received >2 prior anticancer regimens or who had refractory disease, patients with a history of bowel obstruction (including subocclusive disease) related to underlying disease, a history of abdominal fistula, gastrointestinal perforation, or intra-abdominal abscess, or evidence of rectosigmoid involvement by pelvic examination, bowel involvement on computed tomography, or clinical symptoms of bowel obstruction.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: all patients with recurrent epithelial ovarian cancer
Guideline Action: Assess each patient individually for recurrence status, platinum sensitivity, toxicity, ease of administration, and patient preference; recognize systemic therapy is not curative.
Source Statement: Systemic therapy for recurrent ovarian cancer is not curative. As such, it is recognized that, to determine the optimal therapy, each patient needs to be assessed individually in terms of recurrence, sensitivity to platinum, toxicity, ease of administration, and patient preference.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: all patients with recurrent epithelial ovarian cancer
Guideline Action: Offer participation in clinical trials if appropriate.
Source Statement: All patients should be offered the opportunity to participate in clinical trials, if appropriate.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: TREATMENT_RESPONSE
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: platinum-sensitive recurrent ovarian cancer and a single platinum agent is not being considered because of toxicity or allergy
Guideline Action: Use monotherapy with paclitaxel, topotecan, or pegylated liposomal doxorubicin.
Source Statement: If a single platinum agent is not being considered (e.g., because of toxicity or allergy), then monotherapy with paclitaxel, topotecan, or pegylated liposomal doxorubicin is a reasonable treatment option.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: TREATMENT_RESPONSE
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: platinum-sensitive recurrent ovarian cancer with known BRCA1 or BRCA2 mutation, somatic or germline, after platinum-based chemotherapy for recurrent disease
Guideline Action: Use PARP inhibitor maintenance treatment post platinum-based chemotherapy.
Source Statement: PARP inhibitors are recommended for patients with known BRCA 1 or 2 mutation (somatic and germline) as maintenance treatment post platinum-based chemotherapy for recurrent disease.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: TREATMENT_RESPONSE
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: platinum-refractory or platinum-resistant recurrent ovarian cancer
Guideline Action: Set treatment goals to improving quality of life by extending symptom-free interval, reducing symptom intensity, increasing progression-free interval, or prolonging life if possible.
