patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man presents with intermittent rectal bleeding and tenesmus. Colonoscopy shows biopsy-proven adenocarcinoma in the mid rectum, 9 cm from the anal verge. He is being evaluated for elective curative treatment and has no bowel obstruction, so a complete preoperative colonic examination is feasible. The surgical team is planning staging imaging before deciding on neoadjuvant therapy and resection strategy. The tumor is discussed as potentially close to the mesorectal fascia, but no prior imaging has yet been obtained. The patient asks whether transrectal ultrasound can determine whether the circumferential resection margin is involved.
proposed_actions: Use transrectal ultrasound to predict circumferential resection margin involvement.
Order TRUS as the key test to refine local staging for CRM risk.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: low rectal cancer
Guideline Action: Do not use TRUS to predict CRM involvement.
Source Statement: TRUS should not be used to predict CRM involvement.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: considering changing preoperative therapy selection criteria
Guideline Action: Do not change selection criteria from T and N categories to distance to the mesorectal fascia, extramural depth of invasion, or extramural vascular invasion based on current evidence.
Source Statement: To date, there is insufficient evidence to change the current selection criteria from T and N categories to distance to the MRF (i.e., potential CRM), extramural depth of invasion (EMD) and/or extramural vascular invasion (EMVI).

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: all rectal cancer patients undergoing elective treatment
Guideline Action: Perform CT of the abdomen and pelvis.
Source Statement: Staging for all rectal cancer patients should include: CT of the abdomen and pelvis.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: all rectal cancer patients undergoing elective treatment
Guideline Action: Perform CT of the chest or chest X-ray for chest staging.
Source Statement: Staging for all rectal cancer patients should include: CT of the chest or chest X-ray.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: CT chest is used for postoperative surveillance
Guideline Action: Do CT of the chest at the same time as staging CT of the abdomen and pelvis.
Source Statement: If CT of the chest is used for postoperative surveillance, then CT of the chest should be done at the same time as staging CT of the abdomen and pelvis.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: chest X-ray is used for postoperative surveillance and chest X-ray shows abnormalities needing further workup
Guideline Action: Do CT of the chest.
Source Statement: If chest X-ray is used for postoperative surveillance, then CT of the chest is recommended only if abnormalities requiring further investigation were found on chest X-ray.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: CT chest is performed together with CT abdomen and pelvis
Guideline Action: Use intravenous contrast.
Source Statement: When CT of the chest is performed in combination with CT of the abdomen and pelvis, intravenous contrast is recommended.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: preoperative complete colonic examination is possible
Guideline Action: Perform colonoscopy before surgery.
Source Statement: Complete colonic examination by colonoscopy should be performed preoperatively, if possible.
proposed_actions_text:
- Use transrectal ultrasound to predict circumferential resection margin involvement.
- Order TRUS as the key test to refine local staging for CRM risk.

patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man presents with intermittent rectal bleeding and tenesmus. Colonoscopy shows a biopsy-proven adenocarcinoma in the low rectum, 5 cm from the anal verge. He is being evaluated for elective curative treatment, but staging CT of the abdomen and pelvis done the prior week at an outside facility already showed no metastatic disease. The surgical team is planning further evaluation before deciding on neoadjuvant therapy and resection strategy. The tumor is discussed as potentially close to the mesorectal fascia. The patient asks whether transrectal ultrasound can determine whether the circumferential resection margin is involved, and whether he still needs a colonoscopy before surgery.
proposed_actions: Repeat CT of the abdomen and pelvis as part of staging.
Proceed as if the full staging imaging package still needs to be ordered without checking prior results.
Use TRUS to assess CRM involvement.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: low rectal cancer
Guideline Action: Do not use TRUS to predict CRM involvement.
Source Statement: TRUS should not be used to predict CRM involvement.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: considering changing preoperative therapy selection criteria
Guideline Action: Do not change selection criteria from T and N categories to distance to the mesorectal fascia, extramural depth of invasion, or extramural vascular invasion based on current evidence.
Source Statement: To date, there is insufficient evidence to change the current selection criteria from T and N categories to distance to the MRF (i.e., potential CRM), extramural depth of invasion (EMD) and/or extramural vascular invasion (EMVI).

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: all rectal cancer patients undergoing elective treatment
Guideline Action: Perform CT of the abdomen and pelvis.
Source Statement: Staging for all rectal cancer patients should include: CT of the abdomen and pelvis.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: all rectal cancer patients undergoing elective treatment
Guideline Action: Perform CT of the chest or chest X-ray for chest staging.
Source Statement: Staging for all rectal cancer patients should include: CT of the chest or chest X-ray.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: CT chest is used for postoperative surveillance
Guideline Action: Do CT of the chest at the same time as staging CT of the abdomen and pelvis.
Source Statement: If CT of the chest is used for postoperative surveillance, then CT of the chest should be done at the same time as staging CT of the abdomen and pelvis.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: chest X-ray is used for postoperative surveillance and chest X-ray shows abnormalities needing further workup
Guideline Action: Do CT of the chest.
Source Statement: If chest X-ray is used for postoperative surveillance, then CT of the chest is recommended only if abnormalities requiring further investigation were found on chest X-ray.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: CT chest is performed together with CT abdomen and pelvis
Guideline Action: Use intravenous contrast.
Source Statement: When CT of the chest is performed in combination with CT of the abdomen and pelvis, intravenous contrast is recommended.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: preoperative complete colonic examination is possible
Guideline Action: Perform colonoscopy before surgery.
Source Statement: Complete colonic examination by colonoscopy should be performed preoperatively, if possible.
proposed_actions_text:
- Repeat CT of the abdomen and pelvis as part of staging.
- Proceed as if the full staging imaging package still needs to be ordered without checking prior results.
- Use TRUS to assess CRM involvement.

patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man with biopsy-proven squamous cell carcinoma involving the anal canal presents for treatment planning. Staging PET/CT shows no distant metastases, but the tumor extends into the distal rectum and is described as T2N1 with a suspicious mesorectal node rather than clearly localized disease confined to the anal canal. He is otherwise fit with good renal and marrow function and asks whether he can start with a short course of chemotherapy before radiation to shrink the mass first. The oncology team is also debating whether radiation alone might be simpler because the field is expected to be technically challenging, although he is likely able to tolerate combined treatment. He is HIV-negative and has no major comorbidities. Definitive therapy is being planned over the next few weeks, and the concurrent regimen is under discussion.
proposed_actions: Start concurrent chemoradiation without re-checking whether the disease truly fits the localized anal canal recommendation.
Choose radiotherapy alone because the nodal/rectal extension makes treatment seem simpler or more palliative.
Plan induction chemotherapy before definitive treatment as a routine way to downstage the tumor.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with squamous cell cancer of the anal canal being considered for induction chemotherapy before concurrent chemoradiation
Guideline Action: Do not use induction chemotherapy as standard care; treat it as investigational only.
Source Statement: At this time, induction CT before concurrent CT and RT should be considered an investigational approach.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with squamous cell carcinoma of the anal canal after chemoradiation
Guideline Action: Do not give maintenance chemotherapy after chemoradiation.
Source Statement: James et al. 2013 (ACT II), studied maintenance chemotherapy versus none following chemoradiation and found that maintenance chemotherapy does not improve overall survival or colostomy-free survival. Therefore, maintenance chemotherapy following chemoradiation is not recommended in the management of squamous cell carcinoma of the anal canal.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with localized squamous cell cancer of the anal canal and consideration of radiotherapy alone
Guideline Action: Avoid radiotherapy alone when concurrent chemoradiation is feasible.
Source Statement: For all stages of localized squamous cell cancer of the anal canal, concurrent CT and RT is recommended over RT alone to improve local control and decrease colostomy rates.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with biopsy-proven localized squamous cell cancer of the anal canal
Guideline Action: Use concurrent chemotherapy and radiotherapy rather than radiotherapy alone to improve local control and decrease colostomy rates.
Source Statement: For all stages of localized squamous cell cancer of the anal canal, concurrent CT and RT is recommended over RT alone to improve local control and decrease colostomy rates.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with squamous cell cancer of the anal canal receiving concurrent chemoradiation
Guideline Action: Use 5-fluorouracil plus mitomycin C as the concurrent chemotherapy regimen with radiation.
Source Statement: The optimal CT drug combination for squamous cell cancer of the anal canal is 5fluorouracil (5FU) plus mitomycin C (MMC), given concurrently with radiation treatment.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult patient with squamous cell cancer of the anal canal who is HIV-positive
Guideline Action: Manage the patient the same way as a patient without known HIV, while monitoring for potentially higher toxicity.
Source Statement: It is the expert opinion of the Gastrointestinal Cancer Disease Site Group (GI DSG) that HIV-positive patients with squamous cell cancer of the anal canal should be managed in the same way as patients without known HIV. Treating physicians should be aware that a greater than average risk of toxicity is possible.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 50
Recommendation Class: NO_RECOMMENDATION
Activation Condition: Adult patient with squamous cell cancer of the anal canal and need to choose a mitomycin C schedule
Guideline Action: No preferred mitomycin C dosing schedule can be recommended from the available data.
Source Statement: In the trials using MMC in the 5FU-MMC combination regimens, MMC schedules include dose of 12 or 15mg/me day 1 only, and a 10mg/m2 Day 1, 29 dosing. There is no comparative data to allow a recommendation of a preferred schedule.
proposed_actions_text:
- Start concurrent chemoradiation without re-checking whether the disease truly fits the localized anal canal recommendation.
- Choose radiotherapy alone because the nodal/rectal extension makes treatment seem simpler or more palliative.
- Plan induction chemotherapy before definitive treatment as a routine way to downstage the tumor.

patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man with biopsy-proven localized squamous cell carcinoma of the anal canal presents for treatment planning. Staging CT shows no distant metastases, and he is otherwise fit with good renal and marrow function. He asks whether he can start with a short course of chemotherapy before radiation to shrink the tumor first. The oncology team is discussing whether radiation alone might be simpler, but he is able to tolerate combined treatment. He is HIV-negative and has no major comorbidities. A recent colonoscopy, however, documented that the tumor is actually arising from the low rectum and only secondarily involving the anal verge, so the primary site assignment is uncertain. The team plans definitive therapy in the coming weeks and is deciding on the concurrent regimen.
proposed_actions: Apply the anal-cancer guideline directly and recommend concurrent 5-FU/mitomycin chemoradiation as if the primary were anal canal.
Dismiss the need to clarify the primary site because the disease is still in the anorectal region.
Treat induction chemotherapy as investigational for this presentation without first resolving whether the diagnosis fits the anal-canal pathway.

patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman with metastatic breast cancer is admitted to an oncology unit for her next cycle of chemotherapy. The unit recently expanded outpatient infusion services, and the pharmacy director notes that staff from pharmacy, nursing, and environmental services all handle hazardous drugs at different points in the medication circuit. During a quality review, several nurses report that spill response steps are not consistently taught during onboarding, and new float staff are uncertain about safe handling procedures. The oncology service is planning to update its hazardous-drug workflow and wants to reduce variation in practice across shifts. The only difference from prior workflow is that all current staff now completed an online orientation module last year, but there is no formal refresher schedule or competency reassessment. No medication error has occurred, but the team recognizes that the current process still depends heavily on informal teaching and individual experience.
proposed_actions: Order another immediate hospital-approved onboarding course for all staff as if none had been trained before.
Assume the existing online module fully satisfies ongoing best-practice training without any further assessment or refresher planning.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: all institutions administering hazardous drugs
Guideline Action: form a committee responsible for policies and procedures for hazardous drugs
Source Statement: It is strongly recommended that all institutions administering hazardous drugs form such a committee.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: committee for hazardous drugs is formed
Guideline Action: include representatives from occupational health and safety, joint health and safety committee, pharmacy, nursing, medical oncology, environmental services, risk management, and a patient representative
Source Statement: It is also strongly recommended that this committee include, but not be limited to, representatives from various departments and services such as: occupational health and safety, joint health and safety committee, pharmacy, nursing, medical oncology (physician), environmental services, risk management, and a patient representative.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: hazardous drug policies and procedures are being developed or revised
Guideline Action: use clear processes for developing, reviewing, and revising policies and procedures
Source Statement: This committee would be responsible for clear processes of developing, reviewing, and revising policies and procedures related to hazardous drugs.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: hazardous drug program oversight is in place
Guideline Action: routinely conduct risk assessment and gap analysis
Source Statement: A risk assessment and gap analysis should be routinely conducted to identify gaps and to inform policies and procedures.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: hazardous drug staff orientation and education are needed
Guideline Action: ensure a process for orientation and ongoing education for the target population
Source Statement: In addition, this committee is responsible for ensuring that there is a process in place for orientation and ongoing education for the identified target population.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: hazardous drug risk prevention management program is in place
Guideline Action: implement and follow up the Risk Prevention Management Program
Source Statement: This committee is responsible for implementation and follow-up of the Risk Prevention Management Program related to the use of hazardous drugs.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: staff are involved with hazardous drugs throughout the medication circuit
Guideline Action: provide initial and ongoing hospital-approved education including safe handling and spill or leak management
Source Statement: It is legislated that initial and ongoing hospital-approved education be provided to all staff involved with hazardous drugs throughout the medication circuit including safe handling and spill or leak management (6).

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: staff work with hazardous drugs
Guideline Action: provide initial and ongoing training on best practice standards
Source Statement: It is strongly recommended that all staff have initial and ongoing training related to best practice standards in place at the time.
proposed_actions_text:
- Order another immediate hospital-approved onboarding course for all staff as if none had been trained before.
- Assume the existing online module fully satisfies ongoing best-practice training without any further assessment or refresher planning.

patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman with metastatic breast cancer is admitted to an oncology unit for her next cycle of chemotherapy. The unit recently expanded outpatient infusion services, and the pharmacy director notes that staff from pharmacy, nursing, and environmental services all handle hazardous drugs at different points in the medication circuit. During a quality review, several nurses report that spill response steps are not consistently taught during onboarding, and new float staff are uncertain about safe handling procedures. The oncology service is planning to update its hazardous-drug workflow and wants to reduce variation in practice across shifts. However, the current infusion center has been temporarily closed for renovation, so all chemotherapy is being administered at an offsite ambulatory clinic that uses a separate contracted pharmacy and its own staff training program. No medication error has occurred, but the team recognizes that the current process depends heavily on informal teaching and individual experience.
proposed_actions: Apply the hospital’s hazardous-drug education policy to all staff in the offsite clinic without checking whether they are actually part of the institution’s medication circuit.
Treat the described onboarding problem as proof that the inpatient oncology unit is failing the rule, even though the active workflow has shifted elsewhere.

patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man with a prior diagnosis of glioblastoma multiforme completed chemoradiation 10 months ago. He now reports worsening headaches and mild word-finding difficulty. MRI shows a new enhancing lesion near the prior resection cavity, but the findings are equivocal for either tumor recurrence or treatment-related change. His oncology team is considering FDG PET to help clarify the diagnosis before changing therapy. He has no fever, seizures, or other signs of infection, and his neurologic exam is otherwise stable. The main question is whether the new imaging abnormality represents recurrent tumor or radiation necrosis.
proposed_actions: Order FDG PET to distinguish recurrence from treatment effect.
Use the PET result to confidently reclassify the lesion as recurrent glioma.
Escalate therapy based on the FDG PET finding alone.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient has glioma and PET is being considered to determine diagnosis or grading
Guideline Action: Do not use PET for diagnosis or grading in gliomas.
Source Statement: PET is not recommended for the determination of diagnosis or grading in gliomas.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient has glioma and PET is being considered to add diagnostic accuracy beyond MRI and histology
Guideline Action: Do not use PET to improve diagnostic accuracy or prognostic assessment beyond MRI and histology.
Source Statement: none have demonstrated any additional diagnostic accuracy or prognostic influence over and above that provided by magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) and histology in a multivariate model.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: TREATMENT_RESPONSE
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Patient with glioblastoma multiforme has suspected radiation necrosis versus tumour recurrence and PET/CT is being considered
Guideline Action: PET/CT may be considered as anecdotal evidence suggests it may differentiate radiation necrosis from tumour recurrence, recognizing the lack of a gold standard.
Source Statement: Anecdotal evidence exists that PET/CT may differentiate radiation necrosis from tumour recurrence, but there is no gold standard for the diagnosis of radiation necrosis in glioblastoma multiforme.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: TREATMENT_RESPONSE
Priority: 50
Recommendation Class: NO_RECOMMENDATION
Activation Condition: Patient with glioma is being assessed for treatment response using PET
Guideline Action: No recommendation can be made for or against PET for treatment response assessment.
Source Statement: A recommendation cannot be made for or against the use of PET for the assessment of treatment response in gliomas due to insufficient evidence.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: RECURRENCE_OR_RESTAGING
Priority: 50
Recommendation Class: NO_RECOMMENDATION
Activation Condition: Patient has recurrent glioma and PET or PET/CT is being considered for assessment
Guideline Action: No recommendation can be made for or against PET or PET/CT in recurrent gliomas.
Source Statement: A recommendation cannot be made for or against the use of PET or PET/CT in the assessment of patients with recurrent gliomas due to insufficient evidence.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: RECURRENCE_OR_RESTAGING
Priority: 50
Recommendation Class: NO_RECOMMENDATION
Activation Condition: Patient has recurrent glioma and FDG PET is being considered
Guideline Action: No recommendation can be made for FDG PET in recurrent gliomas because evidence is insufficient.
Source Statement: The evidence was insufficient to generate a recommendation on the use of FDG PET.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: RECURRENCE_OR_RESTAGING
Priority: 50
Recommendation Class: NO_RECOMMENDATION
Activation Condition: Patient with recurrent glioma is being evaluated and PET/CT before and after treatment is proposed to correlate with survival
Guideline Action: Do not assume evidence supports prospective PET/CT assessment before and after treatment for survival correlation; evidence is lacking.
Source Statement: PET or PET/CT has not been examined in a prospective cohort of gliomas to assess the treatment effect on PET imaging before and after treatment and correlate this with survival.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: RECURRENCE_OR_RESTAGING
Priority: 50
Recommendation Class: NO_RECOMMENDATION
Activation Condition: Patient with recurrent glioma is being assessed and radiation necrosis is a major diagnostic issue
Guideline Action: Recognize radiation necrosis as a major factor in assessing recurrent gliomas; no specific PET recommendation is provided.
Source Statement: Radiation necrosis is a major factor in assessing recurrent gliomas.
proposed_actions_text:
- Order FDG PET to distinguish recurrence from treatment effect.
- Use the PET result to confidently reclassify the lesion as recurrent glioma.
- Escalate therapy based on the FDG PET finding alone.

patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man with glioblastoma multiforme completed chemoradiation 10 months ago and has had progressive headaches and mild word-finding difficulty. MRI now shows a new enhancing lesion adjacent to the prior resection cavity, and the radiologist favors recurrent tumor over radiation necrosis because the lesion has been steadily enlarging on two serial scans. His oncology team is considering PET/CT before changing therapy. He has no fever, seizures, or other signs of infection, and his neurologic exam is otherwise stable. The main question is whether the new imaging abnormality represents recurrent tumor or treatment-related necrosis.
proposed_actions: Use PET/CT to confirm radiation necrosis versus recurrence.
Treat the PET/CT as a guideline-supported tie-breaker in this setting.
Delay other evaluation because PET/CT is being considered appropriately.

proposed_actions_text:

- Treat the PET/CT as a guideline-supported tie-breaker in this setting.
- Delay other evaluation because PET/CT is being considered appropriately.

patient_vignette: A 64-year-old woman with newly diagnosed metastatic non-small cell lung cancer is referred after biopsy shows adenocarcinoma. She is a never-smoker and has an EGFR exon 19 deletion confirmed on molecular testing from the biopsy specimen. Her oncologist is considering starting gefitinib immediately because she appears clinically suited to an EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitor and the mutation result has returned positive. She is otherwise stable, with no major organ dysfunction and no prior systemic therapy.
proposed_actions: Avoid EGFR TKI therapy because she is a never-smoker and female, which are not sufficient selectors
Delay targeted treatment pending more clinical confirmation despite the already positive mutation result
Treat as if this were still an unselected NSCLC case and default to a non-targeted regimen
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: NSCLC patient has not had mutation testing or is only clinically selected by ethnicity, sex, histology, or smoking status
Guideline Action: Do not use first-line EGFR TKI therapy based on clinical selection alone
Source Statement: First-line therapy with an EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitor (TKI) is not recommended in unselected (patients who have not undergone mutation testing) or clinically selected populations of patients.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: NSCLC patient is being considered for first-line EGFR TKI based on Asian ethnicity, female sex, adenocarcinoma histology, or light/never smoking status
Guideline Action: Do not use these clinical characteristics to select patients for first-line EGFR TKI therapy
Source Statement: The use of clinical characteristics such as Asian ethnicity, female sex, adenocarcinoma histology and light/never smoking status is not recommended to select patients for first-line EGFR TKI therapy, as this strategy does not reliably select patients who have mutations.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: TREATMENT_RESPONSE
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient has progressed after chemotherapy and previously received gefitinib or erlotinib
Guideline Action: Do not use a second EGFR TKI such as afatinib because evidence is insufficient and no overall survival improvement is shown
Source Statement: There is insufficient evidence to recommend the use of a second EGFR TKI, such as afatinib, in patients whose disease has progressed following chemotherapy and gefitinib or erlotinib, as available data does not demonstrate any improvement in overall survival

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient is receiving high-dose gefitinib rather than standard-dose gefitinib
Guideline Action: Avoid higher-dose gefitinib because grade 3 or 4 toxicity is higher and ILD-type events occurred only with 500 mg/day in one trial
Source Statement: Two randomized phase II trials ... identified that grade 3 or 4 toxicity was higher with the higher dose gefitinib. Interstitial lung disease-type events occurred in only one of the two trials, and only with 500 mg/day gefitinib.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: NSCLC patient has confirmed EGFR mutation-positive disease
Guideline Action: Use first-line EGFR TKI therapy instead of platinum-based chemotherapy
Source Statement: In patients with EGFR mutation-positive NSCLC, first-line therapy with an EGFR TKI such as gefitinib, erlotinib or afatinib is the preferred treatment compared to platinum-based therapies.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: TREATMENT_RESPONSE
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient has progressed after platinum-based chemotherapy and is well enough to consider second-line chemotherapy
Guideline Action: Use an EGFR TKI as second- or third-line therapy
Source Statement: In patients well enough to consider second-line chemotherapy, an EGFR TKI can be recommended as second-or third-line therapy.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: TREATMENT_RESPONSE
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient is being considered for maintenance therapy after four cycles of platinum-doublet chemotherapy and has not progressed
Guideline Action: Use an EGFR TKI as maintenance therapy
Source Statement: An EGFR TKI is recommended as an option for maintenance therapy in patients who have not progressed after four cycles of a platinum-doublet chemotherapy.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: NSCLC patient has confirmed EGFR mutation-positive disease and an EGFR TKI is being chosen
Guideline Action: Choose among gefitinib, erlotinib, or afatinib based on expected toxicity and cost because no one agent is supported over another
Source Statement: There is no evidence to support one EGFR TKI over another, so the decision about which EGFR TKI to use should take into consideration the expected toxicity of the drug as well as the cost.
proposed_actions_text:
- Avoid EGFR TKI therapy because she is a never-smoker and female, which are not sufficient selectors
- Delay targeted treatment pending more clinical confirmation despite the already positive mutation result
- Treat as if this were still an unselected NSCLC case and default to a non-targeted regimen

patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman with metastatic non-small cell lung cancer had an initial biopsy showing adenocarcinoma, and she was started on platinum-based chemotherapy while molecular testing was pending. She is a never-smoker and her oncologist had planned to switch her to gefitinib if the pathology or clinical profile continued to suggest an EGFR-driven tumor. However, her repeat tissue testing from the diagnostic specimen is now negative for EGFR mutations, and no activating alteration was identified on the assay performed. She remains clinically stable and has not yet received any EGFR-targeted therapy.
proposed_actions: Start first-line gefitinib based on sex, smoking history, and adenocarcinoma histology
Use the clinical phenotype to override the negative molecular result
Select an EGFR TKI because the disease still appears EGFR-like on superficial review

patient_vignette: A 41-year-old man is referred after a hereditary cardiomyopathy panel identified a heterozygous missense variant in the gene of interest. He has exertional dyspnea, and echocardiography shows borderline left ventricular hypertrophy with preserved systolic function. The ordering cardiologist notes that his phenotype seems to fit the known spectrum for this gene and asks whether that alone supports pathogenicity. The variant is also present in a public database, but the entry is recent and the report does not specify whether the database is curated, how often it is updated, or whether the listed observations came from independent submissions. The variant’s nomenclature is provided in a transcript that the lab did not verify against the genome build used in the database. On first glance, the case appears to mirror a classic inherited cardiomyopathy interpretation problem.
proposed_actions: Treat the phenotype match as supportive pathogenic evidence.
Rely on the public database entry without checking curation, update frequency, or independence.
Accept the variant name at face value without confirming genome build and transcript.
Use the database observations as if they were independent and well validated.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient phenotype matches the known spectrum for the gene
Guideline Action: Do not use phenotype match alone as evidence for pathogenicity.
Source Statement: In general, the fact that a patient has a phenotype that matches the known spectrum of clinical features for a gene is not considered evidence for pathogenicity given that nearly all patients undergoing diseasetargeted tests have the phenotype in question.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Applying variant classification rules to a GUS
Guideline Action: Do not use variant classification rules developed for recognized genotype-phenotype associations.
Source Statement: Special care must be taken when applying the recommended guidelines to a GUS. In such situations, utilizing variant classification rules developed for recognized genotype-phenotype associations is not appropriate.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Considering whether to replicate the ACMG/AMP guideline independently
Guideline Action: Do not invest extensive effort to replicate the ACMG/AMP guideline when endorsement is sufficient.
Source Statement: Further, the Working Group felt that investing extensive effort to replicate the ACMG/AMP guideline would not be justified given the number of experts involved in its creation.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Using public databases for variant interpretation
Guideline Action: Check how often the database is updated, whether curation is supported, and what curation methods were used.
Source Statement: When using databases, clinical laboratories should (i) determine how frequently the database is updated, whether data curation is supported, and what methods were used for curation;

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Using public databases for variant interpretation
Guideline Action: Confirm Human Genome Variation Society nomenclature and identify the genome build and transcript references used for naming variants.
Source Statement: (ii) confirm the use of Human Genome Variation Society nomenclature and determine the genome build and transcript references used for naming variants;

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Using public databases for variant interpretation
Guideline Action: Assess whether the data are validated for analytical accuracy and review any quality metrics, including associated publications if needed.
Source Statement: (iii) determine the degree to which data are validated for analytical accuracy (e.g., low-pass next-generation sequencing versus Sanger-validated variants) and evaluate any quality metrics that are provided to assess data accuracy, which may require reading associated publications;

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Using public databases for variant interpretation
Guideline Action: Determine the source of the observations and whether the observations are independent.
Source Statement: and (iv) determine the source and independence of the observations listed.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Allele frequency in a control population is greater than expected for a rare Mendelian disorder
Guideline Action: Use this as strong support for a benign interpretation (BS1).
Source Statement: In general, an allele frequency in a control population that is greater than expected for the disorder is considered strong support for a benign interpretation for a rare Mendelian disorder (BS1)
proposed_actions_text:
- Treat the phenotype match as supportive pathogenic evidence.
- Rely on the public database entry without checking curation, update frequency, or independence.
- Accept the variant name at face value without confirming genome build and transcript.
- Use the database observations as if they were independent and well validated.

patient_vignette: A 29-year-old woman with mild left ventricular hypertrophy on echocardiogram is seen in genetics clinic after a hereditary cardiomyopathy panel revealed a heterozygous missense variant. Her symptoms and imaging are compatible with the gene’s known disease spectrum, but her cardiologist is unsure whether that clinical match can be used to upgrade the variant. The same variant appears in a public database with multiple submissions and a relatively high allele frequency in a general population dataset. However, the report notes that the population frequency comes from a low-confidence aggregate file, and the database entry does not state whether the submissions are independent or derived from the same source cohort. The lab also did not confirm the exact HGVS expression against the transcript and genome build used by the database. The team is considering whether the frequency alone is enough to call the variant benign.
proposed_actions: Use the phenotype fit as evidence that the variant is pathogenic.
Treat the population frequency as definitive benign evidence despite low-confidence sourcing.
Ignore the missing transcript and genome build confirmation.
Assume the database submissions are independent without checking provenance.

patient_vignette: A 63-year-old man with progressive dysphagia and a 10-kg unintentional weight loss is newly diagnosed with distal esophageal adenocarcinoma on biopsy. CT chest/abdomen shows a circumferential distal esophageal mass with a few borderline paraesophageal nodes but no definite distant metastases. He is otherwise fit, has good functional status, and the oncology team is considering curative-intent chemoradiation followed by surgery if staging confirms localized disease. EUS is performed at a tertiary center and shows clear T3N0 disease without suspicious celiac or mediastinal nodes. Because the team wants the most accurate preoperative staging possible before proceeding, they are debating whether PET would add anything beyond the completed CT and EUS workup.
proposed_actions: Order PET because this is a newly diagnosed potentially resectable esophageal cancer
Use PET to improve M staging despite the completed EUS
Add PET/CT for nodal staging before proceeding to chemoradiation and surgery
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: PREOP_STAGING
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient has esophageal cancer and is a potential candidate for curative therapy
Guideline Action: Use PET to improve the accuracy of M staging
Source Statement: For the staging workup of patients with esophageal cancer who are potential candidates for curative therapy, PET is recommended to improve the accuracy of M staging.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: PREOP_STAGING
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient has esophageal cancer and staging workup is being done
Guideline Action: Use PET for its incremental value in detecting distant disease in addition to CT +/- endoscopic ultrasound (EUS)
Source Statement: There is a significant role for PET for its incremental value in detecting distant disease, in addition to CT +/-endoscopic ultrasound (EUS).

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: PREOP_STAGING
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient has esophageal cancer and EUS is not available
Guideline Action: Use PET because it provides incremental benefit when EUS is absent
Source Statement: Especially in the absence of EUS, PET provides an incremental benefit.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: PREOP_STAGING
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient has newly diagnosed esophageal cancer
Guideline Action: Consider PET/CT for nodal staging because it may be superior to PET alone
Source Statement: There is some evidence that PET/CT is superior to PET alone for nodal staging (Yuan et al, 2006.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: PREOP_STAGING
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient has newly diagnosed esophageal cancer
Guideline Action: Use PET or PET/CT to improve clinical management, prognostic stratification, prediction of regional and locoregional lymph nodes, and pretreatment staging accuracy compared with CT and EUS alone
Source Statement: also showed the significant impact of PET and PET/CT on the clinical management, prognostic stratification of patients with newly diagnosed esophageal cancer, prediction of regional and locoregional lymph nodes, and improvement on the accuracy of pretreatment staging compared to CT and EUS alone.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: TREATMENT_RESPONSE
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: PET is used early in treatment or at completion of neoadjuvant therapy
Guideline Action: Use PET as a possible predictor of complete pathologic response and longer-term survival or event-free survival
Source Statement: There is some evidence that PET, either early in treatment or at the completion of neoadjuvant therapy, can predict complete pathologic response, and therefore, predict the longer-term outcome in terms of survival and event-free survival.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: TREATMENT_RESPONSE
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Assessing response after neoadjuvant therapy
Guideline Action: Consider PET as it may be superior to CT and comparable to EUS for assessment of response and prognosis
Source Statement: One systematic review of four primary studies plus one additional study showed that PET may be superior to CT and comparable to EUS in the assessment of response and of prognosis after neoadjuvant therapy.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: TREATMENT_RESPONSE
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Assessing response after neoadjuvant therapy
Guideline Action: Consider PET/CT because it may be more sensitive than CT or endoscopic ultrasound for response evaluation
Source Statement: One additional study showed PET/CT to be more sensitive for the evaluation of response than either CT or endoscopic ultrasound.
proposed_actions_text:
- Order PET because this is a newly diagnosed potentially resectable esophageal cancer
- Use PET to improve M staging despite the completed EUS
- Add PET/CT for nodal staging before proceeding to chemoradiation and surgery

patient_vignette: A 63-year-old man with progressive dysphagia and a 10-kg unintentional weight loss is newly diagnosed with distal esophageal adenocarcinoma on biopsy. CT chest/abdomen shows a circumferential distal esophageal mass with a few borderline paraesophageal nodes but also a newly identified 1.8-cm liver lesion that is highly suspicious for metastatic disease. He remains functionally well, and the oncology team is reviewing whether any staging test should be added before deciding on management. The endoscopy team asks whether PET/CT should be obtained to refine nodal staging and pretreatment planning.
proposed_actions: Proceed with PET or PET/CT for preoperative staging
Use PET to improve M staging before curative-intent chemoradiation
Order PET/CT for nodal staging because the cancer is newly diagnosed

patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man presents for routine oncology follow-up after resection of a stage IIA cutaneous melanoma from the upper back 14 months ago. He had wide local excision with negative margins and a negative sentinel node biopsy; no completion lymph node dissection was performed because the node was negative. He feels well and denies weight loss, headaches, cough, bone pain, new skin lesions, or neurologic symptoms. Physical examination shows a well-healed scar, no palpable lymphadenopathy, and no concerning skin findings. He asks whether he should have blood tests to look for recurrence, since he is worried about metastatic disease. He is otherwise healthy and remains asymptomatic.
proposed_actions: Order routine blood biomarkers or tumor markers to screen for recurrence.
Schedule stage III-style oncology surveillance every 3 to 6 months.
Arrange serial draining-basin ultrasound surveillance despite the negative sentinel node.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has stage IIIA, IIIB, IIIC, IIID, or resected IV melanoma and is being screened for asymptomatic recurrence or metastatic disease
Guideline Action: do not order routine biomarker or blood tests for screening
Source Statement: 3.2 Routine biomarker or blood tests to screen for asymptomatic recurrence or metastatic disease are not recommended.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has stage IA, IB, or IIA melanoma and is clinically disease-free after curative-intent treatment
Guideline Action: perform clinical follow-up with history and physical examination including full skin and lymph node examination every 6 to 12 months for 3 years, then annually for 2 years or as clinically indicated; dermatologist, surgeon, family physician, or cancer nurse specialist may provide follow-up; photo-surveillance and dermoscopy if indicated
Source Statement: For patients with stage IA, IB, or IIA melanoma who are clinically disease-free after receiving curative-intent treatment: 1.1 Clinical follow-up with history and physical examination with full skin and lymph node examination by a dermatologist (with photo-surveillance and dermoscopy if indicated), and/or a surgeon, family physician, cancer nurse specialists should occur every six to 12 months for three years, then annually for two years or as clinically indicated.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has stage IIIA, IIIB, IIIC, IIID, or resected IV melanoma
Guideline Action: perform clinical follow-up with history and physical examination including full skin and lymph node examination every 3 to 6 months in years 1 to 3, then every 6 months in years 4 to 5, or as clinically indicated; dermatologist, surgeon, medical oncologist, or cancer nurse specialist may provide follow-up; photo-surveillance and dermoscopy if indicated
Source Statement: For patients with stage IIIA, IIIB, IIIC, IIID, or resected IV melanoma: 3.1 Clinical follow-up with history and physical examination with full skin and lymph node examination by a dermatologist (with photo-surveillance and dermoscopy if indicated), and/or a surgeon, medical oncologist, or cancer nurse specialist should occur every three to six months in years 1 to 3, then every six months in years 4 to 5, or as clinically indicated.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: brain MRI is being used for routine screening and is available
Guideline Action: prefer MRI of the brain without radiation
Source Statement: MRI (no radiation) of the brain is preferred for routine screening where available, otherwise, head CT may be considered after discussing with patients.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has a positive sentinel lymph node and no complete lymph node dissection was performed
Guideline Action: perform ultrasound scans of the draining nodal basin every 4 to 6 months for years 1 to 3, then every 6 months for years 4 to 5
Source Statement: 3.5 For patients with a positive sentinel lymph node, ultrasound scans of the draining nodal basin should be done every four to six months for years 1 to 3, and then every six months for years 4 to 5, if no complete lymph node dissection is performed.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: patient has stage IIIA, IIIB, IIIC, IIID, or resected IV melanoma and is being screened for asymptomatic recurrence or metastatic disease in years 1 to 3
Guideline Action: consider CT or PET/CT scans every 6 to 12 months
Source Statement: 3.3 CT or PET/CT scans every six to 12 months should be considered to screen for asymptomatic recurrence or metastatic disease in years 1 to 3, then annually in years 4 to 5.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: patient has stage IIIA, IIIB, IIIC, IIID, or resected IV melanoma and is being screened for asymptomatic recurrence or metastatic disease in years 4 to 5
Guideline Action: consider CT or PET/CT scans annually
Source Statement: 3.3 CT or PET/CT scans every six to 12 months should be considered to screen for asymptomatic recurrence or metastatic disease in years 1 to 3, then annually in years 4 to 5.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: patient has stage IIIA, IIIB, IIIC, IIID, or resected IV melanoma
Guideline Action: consider annual brain MRI for years 1 to 5
Source Statement: 3.4 Annual brain MRI can be considered for years 1 to 5.
proposed_actions_text:
- Order routine blood biomarkers or tumor markers to screen for recurrence.
- Schedule stage III-style oncology surveillance every 3 to 6 months.
- Arrange serial draining-basin ultrasound surveillance despite the negative sentinel node.

patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man presents for routine oncology follow-up after resection of a stage IIIC cutaneous melanoma from the upper back 14 months ago. He completed sentinel node biopsy showing one positive node, and he also underwent completion lymph node dissection shortly afterward. He feels well and denies weight loss, headaches, cough, bone pain, new skin lesions, or neurologic symptoms. Physical examination shows a well-healed scar, no palpable lymphadenopathy, and no concerning skin findings. He asks whether he should have blood tests to look for recurrence, since he is worried about metastatic disease. He is otherwise healthy and remains asymptomatic.
proposed_actions: Order routine blood tests or biomarkers because he has stage IIIC melanoma.
Continue the same draining nodal basin ultrasound schedule every 4 to 6 months.
Escalate to imaging solely because of the prior positive sentinel node.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has stage IIIA, IIIB, IIIC, IIID, or resected IV melanoma and is being screened for asymptomatic recurrence or metastatic disease
Guideline Action: do not order routine biomarker or blood tests for screening
Source Statement: 3.2 Routine biomarker or blood tests to screen for asymptomatic recurrence or metastatic disease are not recommended.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has stage IA, IB, or IIA melanoma and is clinically disease-free after curative-intent treatment
Guideline Action: perform clinical follow-up with history and physical examination including full skin and lymph node examination every 6 to 12 months for 3 years, then annually for 2 years or as clinically indicated; dermatologist, surgeon, family physician, or cancer nurse specialist may provide follow-up; photo-surveillance and dermoscopy if indicated
Source Statement: For patients with stage IA, IB, or IIA melanoma who are clinically disease-free after receiving curative-intent treatment: 1.1 Clinical follow-up with history and physical examination with full skin and lymph node examination by a dermatologist (with photo-surveillance and dermoscopy if indicated), and/or a surgeon, family physician, cancer nurse specialists should occur every six to 12 months for three years, then annually for two years or as clinically indicated.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has stage IIIA, IIIB, IIIC, IIID, or resected IV melanoma
Guideline Action: perform clinical follow-up with history and physical examination including full skin and lymph node examination every 3 to 6 months in years 1 to 3, then every 6 months in years 4 to 5, or as clinically indicated; dermatologist, surgeon, medical oncologist, or cancer nurse specialist may provide follow-up; photo-surveillance and dermoscopy if indicated
Source Statement: For patients with stage IIIA, IIIB, IIIC, IIID, or resected IV melanoma: 3.1 Clinical follow-up with history and physical examination with full skin and lymph node examination by a dermatologist (with photo-surveillance and dermoscopy if indicated), and/or a surgeon, medical oncologist, or cancer nurse specialist should occur every three to six months in years 1 to 3, then every six months in years 4 to 5, or as clinically indicated.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: brain MRI is being used for routine screening and is available
Guideline Action: prefer MRI of the brain without radiation
Source Statement: MRI (no radiation) of the brain is preferred for routine screening where available, otherwise, head CT may be considered after discussing with patients.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has a positive sentinel lymph node and no complete lymph node dissection was performed
Guideline Action: perform ultrasound scans of the draining nodal basin every 4 to 6 months for years 1 to 3, then every 6 months for years 4 to 5
Source Statement: 3.5 For patients with a positive sentinel lymph node, ultrasound scans of the draining nodal basin should be done every four to six months for years 1 to 3, and then every six months for years 4 to 5, if no complete lymph node dissection is performed.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: patient has stage IIIA, IIIB, IIIC, IIID, or resected IV melanoma and is being screened for asymptomatic recurrence or metastatic disease in years 1 to 3
Guideline Action: consider CT or PET/CT scans every 6 to 12 months
Source Statement: 3.3 CT or PET/CT scans every six to 12 months should be considered to screen for asymptomatic recurrence or metastatic disease in years 1 to 3, then annually in years 4 to 5.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: patient has stage IIIA, IIIB, IIIC, IIID, or resected IV melanoma and is being screened for asymptomatic recurrence or metastatic disease in years 4 to 5
Guideline Action: consider CT or PET/CT scans annually
Source Statement: 3.3 CT or PET/CT scans every six to 12 months should be considered to screen for asymptomatic recurrence or metastatic disease in years 1 to 3, then annually in years 4 to 5.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: patient has stage IIIA, IIIB, IIIC, IIID, or resected IV melanoma
Guideline Action: consider annual brain MRI for years 1 to 5
Source Statement: 3.4 Annual brain MRI can be considered for years 1 to 5.
proposed_actions_text:
- Order routine blood tests or biomarkers because he has stage IIIC melanoma.
- Continue the same draining nodal basin ultrasound schedule every 4 to 6 months.
- Escalate to imaging solely because of the prior positive sentinel node.

patient_vignette: A 64-year-old woman returns to oncology follow-up 8 months after total hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy for stage IB, grade 2 endometrioid endometrial cancer. Final pathology showed disease confined to the uterus, and she has no evidence of recurrence. She feels well and denies vaginal bleeding, pelvic pain, cough, weight loss, or bowel or bladder changes. However, her adjuvant treatment course included vaginal cuff brachytherapy after surgery. She asks what follow-up should include and who should see her, and she is anxious about recurrence. She also wonders whether she should be counseled about any treatment-related side effects she might still experience.
proposed_actions: Schedule semi-annual or annual general exams with history and pelvic-rectal exam as if this were uncomplicated low-risk surveillance.
Ignore treatment-related toxicity counseling because she is in remission and asymptomatic.
Treat the visit exactly like the original low-risk postoperative follow-up template without noting the radiation exposure.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: all patients with endometrial cancer after potentially curative primary treatment and no evidence of disease
Guideline Action: counsel about potential symptoms of recurrence
Source Statement: It is recommended that all patients receive counselling about the potential symptoms of recurrence of endometrial cancer, because the majority of recurrences in the identified studies were symptomatic.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient is surgically or pathologically confirmed low risk of recurrence (stage IA or IB, grade 1 or 2)
Guideline Action: perform general examination including complete history and pelvic-rectal examination semi-annually or annually for the first 3 years, then annually for the next 2 years
Source Statement: For patients at a surgically or pathologically confirmed low risk of recurrence (i.e., stage IA or IB, grade 1 or 2): A general examination, including a complete history and a pelvicrectal examination, conducted semi-annually or annually for the first three years and annually for the next two years.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient is high risk of recurrence (stage IA or IB, grade 3, or stage IC or advanced stage)
Guideline Action: perform general examination including complete history and pelvic-rectal examination every 3 to 6 months for the first 3 years, then semi-annually for the next 2 years
Source Statement: For patients at high risk of recurrence (i.e., stage IA or IB, grade 3, or stage IC or advanced stage). A general examination, including a complete history and a pelvic-rectal examination, every three to six months for the first three years and semi-annually for the next two years.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: planning follow-up setting or specialist type for any patient
Guideline Action: follow the patient with a health care professional knowledgeable about the natural history of the disease and comfortable performing speculum and pelvic exams
Source Statement: There is insufficient evidence to inform the optimum clinical setting or type of specialist required for follow-up; however, it is recommended that all patients be followed by a health care professional who is knowledgeable about the natural history of the disease, and who is comfortable performing speculum and pelvic exams, in order to diagnose or detect a local (vaginal) recurrence.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has symptoms suggestive of recurrence
Guideline Action: perform targeted investigation to rule out recurrence
Source Statement: It is recommended that all patients undergo a targeted investigation to rule out recurrence if symptomatic, since patients with local recurrence are potentially curable with further therapy.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: treatment includes radiotherapy
Guideline Action: counsel the patient on potential adverse effects of radiotherapy
Source Statement: Where treatment with radiotherapy is involved, it is recommended that patients be counselled on the potential adverse effects of radiotherapy.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: all patients with endometrial cancer after potentially curative primary treatment and no evidence of disease
Guideline Action: use a follow-up strategy based on recurrence risk and patient preferences
Source Statement: The most appropriate follow-up strategy is likely one based upon the risk of recurrence, with individual patient preferences for more or less follow-up taken into account.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: patient has remained recurrence-free for 5 years
Guideline Action: return to annual population-based general physical and pelvic examination
Source Statement: Since the majority of patients with recurrence were symptomatic and virtually all recurred within five years, it seems reasonable that patients return to annual population-based general physical and pelvic examination after five years of recurrence-free follow-up.
proposed_actions_text:
- Schedule semi-annual or annual general exams with history and pelvic-rectal exam as if this were uncomplicated low-risk surveillance.
- Ignore treatment-related toxicity counseling because she is in remission and asymptomatic.
- Treat the visit exactly like the original low-risk postoperative follow-up template without noting the radiation exposure.

patient_vignette: A 64-year-old woman is seen in oncology follow-up 8 months after undergoing total hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy for stage IB, grade 3 endometrioid endometrial cancer. Final pathology showed no disease outside the uterus, and she has no current evidence of recurrence. She reports feeling well and denies vaginal bleeding, pelvic pain, new cough, weight loss, or bowel or bladder changes. She completed treatment without radiotherapy. She asks what follow-up should include and who should see her, and she wants to know which symptoms should prompt an earlier visit. Her primary care clinician has offered to manage surveillance because she lives far from the cancer center.
proposed_actions: Use the low-risk surveillance schedule of semi-annual or annual exams for the first 3 years.
Reassure her that her follow-up can be spaced out the same way as stage IB, grade 2 disease.
Treat her as a routine low-risk survivor and omit the more frequent early follow-up interval.

patient_vignette: A 34-year-old woman presents for a routine primary care visit and asks whether she should have a full skin check. She has fair skin and no personal history of skin cancer, no organ transplant history, and no chronic immunosuppressive therapy. She reports about 24 common nevi, none atypical, and no prior PUVA treatment. She has not noticed any changing or symptomatic skin lesions. Her main concern is prevention because a friend recently had a melanoma diagnosis. She is otherwise healthy, but she mentions that her father was diagnosed with melanoma in his late 60s and no other first-degree relatives are affected. She is asking whether she should be referred for screening today.
proposed_actions: Perform a routine total-body skin examination as screening in primary care
Provide routine counseling on skin self-examination as screening
Label her as very high risk based on any family history of melanoma alone
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: general population at average or low risk
Guideline Action: Do not routinely perform total-body skin examination for screening.
Source Statement: Based on the limited evidence available at present, routine total body skin examination by primary care providers is not recommended for individuals at average or low risk for skin cancer (i.e., those not included in the increased risk groups described above).

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: general population at average or low risk
Guideline Action: Do not routinely counsel on skin self-examination for screening.
Source Statement: Based on the limited evidence available at present, routine counselling on skin selfexamination by primary care providers is not recommended for individuals at average or low risk for skin cancer.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: any patient with very high risk factors
Guideline Action: Identify the patient as very high risk.
Source Statement: Individuals at very high risk should be identified by their primary health care provider and offered total body skin examination by a dermatologist or a trained health care provider on a yearly basis.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: very high risk due to organ transplant immunosuppressive therapy
Guideline Action: Offer yearly total-body skin examination by a dermatologist or trained health care provider.
Source Statement: Individuals at very high risk should be identified by their primary health care provider and offered total body skin examination by a dermatologist or a trained health care provider on a yearly basis.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: very high risk due to personal history of skin cancer
Guideline Action: Offer yearly total-body skin examination by a dermatologist or trained health care provider.
Source Statement: Individuals at very high risk should be identified by their primary health care provider and offered total body skin examination by a dermatologist or a trained health care provider on a yearly basis.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: very high risk due to two or more first-degree relatives with melanoma
Guideline Action: Offer yearly total-body skin examination by a dermatologist or trained health care provider.
Source Statement: Individuals at very high risk should be identified by their primary health care provider and offered total body skin examination by a dermatologist or a trained health care provider on a yearly basis.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: very high risk due to more than 100 nevi or 5 or more atypical nevi
Guideline Action: Offer yearly total-body skin examination by a dermatologist or trained health care provider.
Source Statement: Individuals at very high risk should be identified by their primary health care provider and offered total body skin examination by a dermatologist or a trained health care provider on a yearly basis.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: very high risk due to more than 250 PUVA treatments for psoriasis
Guideline Action: Offer yearly total-body skin examination by a dermatologist or trained health care provider.
Source Statement: Individuals at very high risk should be identified by their primary health care provider and offered total body skin examination by a dermatologist or a trained health care provider on a yearly basis.
proposed_actions_text:
- Perform a routine total-body skin examination as screening in primary care
- Provide routine counseling on skin self-examination as screening
- Label her as very high risk based on any family history of melanoma alone

patient_vignette: A 34-year-old woman presents for a routine primary care visit and asks whether she should have a full skin check. She has fair skin, no personal history of skin cancer, no organ transplant history, no chronic immunosuppressive therapy, and no family history of melanoma. She reports about 22 common nevi, but on exam several are clinically atypical and she thinks one may have changed slightly over the past year. She denies bleeding or pain from the lesions and has no prior PUVA treatment. Her main concern is prevention because a friend recently had a melanoma diagnosis. She is otherwise healthy and asks if a screening skin exam would be a good idea today.
proposed_actions: Decline any skin assessment because she is otherwise healthy
Treat this as routine screening and counsel against self-examination without addressing the changing lesion
Reassure her that the number of nevi alone keeps her average risk
