patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man with metastatic well-differentiated neuroendocrine tumor of the ileum returns after prior debulking surgery. He continues to have flushing and diarrhea, and recent imaging shows persistent unresectable liver metastases. A somatostatin receptor scan demonstrates uptake in most lesions, but one enlarging hepatic lesion has uptake below liver background. His renal function is preserved, and the oncology team is considering peptide receptor radionuclide therapy with 177 Lu-DOTATATE pending eligibility review.
proposed_actions: Proceed with PRRT based on the overall metastatic burden and symptomatic progression
Schedule 177 Lu-DOTATATE without further review because the scan is mostly positive
Assume the patient remains guideline-eligible since only one site is less avid
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with neuroendocrine cancer who is inoperable, has residual disease after surgery or other ablative therapy, or has metastases
Guideline Action: Consider PRRT as an acceptable treatment option
Source Statement: PRRT appears to be an acceptable option in adult patients with neuroendocrine cancer who are inoperable, have residual disease following surgery or other ablative therapy, or have metastases.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Using PRRT, especially 90 Y-DOTALAN or 177 Lu-DOTATATE
Guideline Action: Use renal protection with lysine and arginine amino acid solution and monitor renal function
Source Statement: PRRT is relatively safe and well tolerated with renal protection using lysine and arginine amino acid solution, especially for 90 Y-DOTALAN and 177 Lu-DOTATATE. However, renal function must be monitored.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Providing PRRT in Ontario
Guideline Action: Conduct PRRT as part of one or more RCTs, or large comparative clinical trials if an RCT is not feasible, under a Clinical Trials Agreement
Source Statement: Treatment with PRRT in Ontario should be conducted as part of one or more RCTs, or in large comparative clinical trials if an RCT is not feasible, under the authority of a Clinical Trials Agreement, to clarify the further effects of PRRT.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Considering 177 Lu-DOTATATE for future PRRT trials
Guideline Action: Include 177 Lu-DOTATATE as an appropriate agent in future clinical trials
Source Statement: Therefore, 177 Lu-DOTATATE would be an appropriate agent to include in the future clinical trials described above.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient is to receive PRRT or 131 I-MIBG
Guideline Action: Confirm that the tumour demonstrates positive uptake of the related diagnostic agent before therapy
Source Statement: Prior to the administration of therapy, the tumours from NET patients who are to receive PRRT or 131 I-MIBG should demonstrate a positive uptake of the related diagnostic agent.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Using 131 I-MIBG
Guideline Action: Consider hematologic toxicity, severe infections, and secondary malignancies in decision-making
Source Statement: However, the hematologic toxicity, severe infections, and secondary malignancies possible afterwards should be considered.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Tumour uptake on octreoscan is higher than or equal to liver uptake before PRRT
Guideline Action: Proceed with PRRT only when uptake is positive on diagnostic imaging
Source Statement: All the patient tumours showed a higher or the same uptake on octreoscan than on liver uptake before PRRT.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Using 90 Y-DOTATOC, 90 Y-DOTATATE, or 177 Lu-DOTATATE
Guideline Action: Infuse lysine and arginine amino acid solution to protect kidney function
Source Statement: For studies investigating the effects of 90 Y-DOTATOC, 90 Y-DOTATATE, and 177 Lu-DOTATATE, lysine and arginine amino acid solution was infused to protect kidney function.
proposed_actions_text:
- Proceed with PRRT based on the overall metastatic burden and symptomatic progression
- Schedule 177 Lu-DOTATATE without further review because the scan is mostly positive
- Assume the patient remains guideline-eligible since only one site is less avid

patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man with metastatic well-differentiated neuroendocrine tumor of the ileum presents after prior debulking surgery with ongoing flushing and diarrhea. Imaging again shows persistent liver metastases that are not resectable. His somatostatin receptor scan is strongly positive, with uptake exceeding liver in the involved lesions. The team is discussing peptide receptor radionuclide therapy with 177 Lu-DOTATATE, but he has been enrolled in a standard institutional treatment pathway rather than a clinical trial, and the center is outside Ontario. Baseline renal function is normal.
proposed_actions: Proceed with PRRT as routine care without trial enrollment
Order the amino acid infusion and treat as if the pathway were fully guideline-based
Document eligibility for 177 Lu-DOTATATE solely from scan positivity and metastatic status
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with neuroendocrine cancer who is inoperable, has residual disease after surgery or other ablative therapy, or has metastases
Guideline Action: Consider PRRT as an acceptable treatment option
Source Statement: PRRT appears to be an acceptable option in adult patients with neuroendocrine cancer who are inoperable, have residual disease following surgery or other ablative therapy, or have metastases.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Using PRRT, especially 90 Y-DOTALAN or 177 Lu-DOTATATE
Guideline Action: Use renal protection with lysine and arginine amino acid solution and monitor renal function
Source Statement: PRRT is relatively safe and well tolerated with renal protection using lysine and arginine amino acid solution, especially for 90 Y-DOTALAN and 177 Lu-DOTATATE. However, renal function must be monitored.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Providing PRRT in Ontario
Guideline Action: Conduct PRRT as part of one or more RCTs, or large comparative clinical trials if an RCT is not feasible, under a Clinical Trials Agreement
Source Statement: Treatment with PRRT in Ontario should be conducted as part of one or more RCTs, or in large comparative clinical trials if an RCT is not feasible, under the authority of a Clinical Trials Agreement, to clarify the further effects of PRRT.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Considering 177 Lu-DOTATATE for future PRRT trials
Guideline Action: Include 177 Lu-DOTATATE as an appropriate agent in future clinical trials
Source Statement: Therefore, 177 Lu-DOTATATE would be an appropriate agent to include in the future clinical trials described above.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient is to receive PRRT or 131 I-MIBG
Guideline Action: Confirm that the tumour demonstrates positive uptake of the related diagnostic agent before therapy
Source Statement: Prior to the administration of therapy, the tumours from NET patients who are to receive PRRT or 131 I-MIBG should demonstrate a positive uptake of the related diagnostic agent.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Using 131 I-MIBG
Guideline Action: Consider hematologic toxicity, severe infections, and secondary malignancies in decision-making
Source Statement: However, the hematologic toxicity, severe infections, and secondary malignancies possible afterwards should be considered.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Tumour uptake on octreoscan is higher than or equal to liver uptake before PRRT
Guideline Action: Proceed with PRRT only when uptake is positive on diagnostic imaging
Source Statement: All the patient tumours showed a higher or the same uptake on octreoscan than on liver uptake before PRRT.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Using 90 Y-DOTATOC, 90 Y-DOTATATE, or 177 Lu-DOTATATE
Guideline Action: Infuse lysine and arginine amino acid solution to protect kidney function
Source Statement: For studies investigating the effects of 90 Y-DOTATOC, 90 Y-DOTATATE, and 177 Lu-DOTATATE, lysine and arginine amino acid solution was infused to protect kidney function.
proposed_actions_text:
- Proceed with PRRT as routine care without trial enrollment
- Order the amino acid infusion and treat as if the pathway were fully guideline-based
- Document eligibility for 177 Lu-DOTATATE solely from scan positivity and metastatic status

patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with chronic lymphocytic leukemia returns with worsening fatigue, night sweats, and enlarging cervical nodes after prior alkylating-agent therapy and later fludarabine-based treatment. His disease is clearly relapsed, and his oncologist is considering alemtuzumab as the next step. On exam, however, the largest cervical node now measures 5.5 cm, with several adjacent nodes approaching 6 cm, although there is no splenomegaly. He remains ambulatory and has mild anemia and lymphocytosis. He also has stable coronary artery disease and diabetes. The patient asks whether alemtuzumab is a reasonable option and wants to understand the expected benefits and risks before deciding.
proposed_actions: Recommend alemtuzumab based on prior alkylator and fludarabine exposure alone
Describe alemtuzumab as the guideline-preferred next-line option without qualification
Proceed as if the patient meets the non-bulky lymphadenopathy criterion
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient is receiving alemtuzumab consolidation after response to first-line fludarabine-containing chemotherapy
Guideline Action: Avoid routine consolidation with alemtuzumab because the trial was stopped early for severe infections
Source Statement: The trial was stopped after the accrual of 21 patients due to the occurrence of the National Cancer Institute Common Toxicity Criteria (NCI-CTC) Version 2.0 grade III/IV infections in seven of the first 11 patients randomized to alemtuzumab consolidation.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: adult patient with progressive and symptomatic CLL refractory to both alkylator-based and fludarabine-based regimens
Guideline Action: Consider alemtuzumab as a reasonable treatment option
Source Statement: Treatment with alemtuzumab is a reasonable option for patients with progressive and symptomatic CLL that is refractory to both alkylator-based and fludarabine-based regimens.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient was previously treated with alkylating agents and is refractory to fludarabine without bulky lymphadenopathy
Guideline Action: Consider alemtuzumab
Source Statement: the UK CLL Forum guideline recommended alemtuzumab for use in patients without bulky lymphadenopathy (<5cm), who were previously treated with alkylating agents and refractory to fludarabine.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: considering alemtuzumab for CLL
Guideline Action: Inform the patient that evidence for benefit on duration of response, quality of life, and overall survival is not established and remains speculative
Source Statement: Patients should be informed that any possible beneficial effect of alemtuzumab on other outcome measures such as duration of response, quality of life, and overall survival are not supported in evidence and remain speculative at this time.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: considering alemtuzumab for CLL
Guideline Action: Inform the patient that treatment has significant and potentially serious toxicities and that the balance of harm versus benefit is uncertain
Source Statement: Treatment with alemtuzumab is associated with significant and potentially serious adverse treatment-related toxicities. Patients must be carefully informed of the uncertain balance between potential risks of harm and the chance for benefit reported in studies.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: considering alemtuzumab for CLL
Guideline Action: Use patient preferences as an important factor in deciding whether to treat
Source Statement: Given the current substantial uncertainty in this balance, patient preferences will likely play a large role in determining the appropriate treatment choice.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: patient has important co-morbidities
Guideline Action: Use alemtuzumab cautiously because excessive risk may be present
Source Statement: Given the potential toxicities associated with alemtuzumab, and given the limited nature of the clinical trials testing its use in broad populations of patients with CLL, the use of alemtuzumab in patients with important co-morbidities may be associated with excessive risks.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: patient has relapsed or chemotherapy-refractory CLL
Guideline Action: Recognize alemtuzumab as an active agent that can produce responses, but note that complete remissions are uncommon
Source Statement: The DSG regards alemtuzumab as an active agent for the treatment of patients with relapsed or chemotherapy-refractory CLL. This conclusion is based on response data from single-arm studies that report partial responses in approximately one third of patients and recognizes that complete remissions are uncommon.
proposed_actions_text:
- Recommend alemtuzumab based on prior alkylator and fludarabine exposure alone
- Describe alemtuzumab as the guideline-preferred next-line option without qualification
- Proceed as if the patient meets the non-bulky lymphadenopathy criterion

patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with chronic lymphocytic leukemia presents with worsening fatigue, drenching night sweats, and enlarging cervical nodes after prior alkylating-agent therapy and later fludarabine-based therapy. His oncologist is considering alemtuzumab because the disease has become symptomatic and is progressing. He has no bulky adenopathy on exam, with the largest node measuring 3 cm. He is otherwise functional, although his history includes stable coronary artery disease and diabetes. Before treatment is chosen, a recent CBC shows only mild anemia and lymphocytosis, but the patient’s symptoms are currently being attributed in part to a concurrent viral upper respiratory illness that has been present for the past several days. He asks whether alemtuzumab should be started now.
proposed_actions: Treat immediately with alemtuzumab for presumed progressive symptomatic CLL
Document the patient as having straightforward chemotherapy-refractory symptomatic disease
Apply the relapsed/refractory CLL recommendation without clarifying symptom cause

Source Statement: The trial was stopped after the accrual of 21 patients due to the occurrence of the National Cancer Institute Common Toxicity Criteria (NCI-CTC) Version 2.0 grade III/IV infections in seven of the first 11 patients randomized to alemtuzumab consolidation.

patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with a 40-pack-year smoking history presents to his family doctor with 3 weeks of cough, intermittent blood-streaked sputum, and mild unintentional weight loss. He denies fever or recent infection. His oxygen saturation is normal, and he reports the hemoptysis has occurred only once after a forceful coughing fit last week. There is no stridor, no focal neurological deficit, and no symptoms of superior vena cava obstruction. The clinician is concerned about possible lung cancer and orders a chest X-ray the same day, while also asking the patient to bring a sputum sample for cytology before the referral is finalized.
proposed_actions: Arrange urgent chest CT or urgent thoracic referral for persistent hemoptysis
Send sputum cytology as part of the initial lung cancer investigation
Treat this as the same urgent cancer pathway as the source vignette
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: suspected lung cancer is being investigated
Guideline Action: do not use sputum cytology for the investigation
Source Statement: Sputum cytology is not recommended for the investigation of suspected lung cancer.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient presents with stridor
Guideline Action: refer to the emergency department
Source Statement: A person should be referred to the Emergency Department for the following: - Stridor

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient presents with massive hemoptysis
Guideline Action: refer to the emergency department
Source Statement: A person should be referred to the Emergency Department for the following: - Massive hemoptysis

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has new neurological signs suggestive of brain metastases or cord compression
Guideline Action: refer to the emergency department
Source Statement: A person should be referred to the Emergency Department for the following: - New neurological signs suggestive of brain metastases or cord compression

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has persistent non-massive hemoptysis
Guideline Action: arrange urgent chest CT and/or urgent referral to a diagnostic assessment program or thoracic surgeon
Source Statement: A person should be referred if presenting with any of the following: - Persistent non-massive hemoptysis (Multiple episodes of coughing blood or bloodstreaked sputum)

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has superior vena cava syndrome or obstruction
Guideline Action: arrange urgent chest CT and/or urgent referral to a diagnostic assessment program or thoracic surgeon
Source Statement: A person should be referred if presenting with any of the following: - Superior vena cava syndrome/obstruction

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has signs or symptoms suspicious of lung cancer
Guideline Action: order a chest X-ray within two working days
Source Statement: Indications for Chest X-ray A person should have a chest X-ray within two working days if they present with any of the following:

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: chest X-ray is suspicious for lung cancer
Guideline Action: ensure the report clearly notes suspicion of lung cancer and use direct notification methods to inform the family physician or other primary care provider
Source Statement: If the chest Xray is suspicious for lung cancer, this must be clearly noted on the X-ray report. Radiologists should consider using two or more mechanisms to directly inform the FP or other PCPs of the suspicion of lung cancer. (e.g., fax, flagging, telephone call, email)
proposed_actions_text:
- Arrange urgent chest CT or urgent thoracic referral for persistent hemoptysis
- Send sputum cytology as part of the initial lung cancer investigation
- Treat this as the same urgent cancer pathway as the source vignette

patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with a 40-pack-year smoking history presents to his family doctor with 3 weeks of cough, intermittent blood-streaked sputum, and mild unintentional weight loss. He denies fever or recent infection. His oxygen saturation is normal, and he reports the hemoptysis has occurred on several mornings over the past week. There is no stridor, no focal neurological deficit, and no symptoms of superior vena cava obstruction. A chest X-ray was obtained yesterday at an urgent care clinic and the report was read as normal. The clinician is concerned about possible lung cancer and considers repeating imaging or waiting for the result before arranging further assessment.
proposed_actions: Order another chest X-ray within two working days as if none had been done
Delay further workup because the prior radiograph was normal
Assume the normal chest X-ray rules out lung cancer

patient_vignette: A 64-year-old man returns after subtotal gastrectomy for distal gastric adenocarcinoma. Final pathology shows pT3N1 disease with negative margins, and he is being seen in tumor board to plan adjuvant therapy. He is recovering without major postoperative complications and wants additional treatment if appropriate. His medical history includes ischemic cardiomyopathy, but his left ventricular ejection fraction is documented at 52% on a recent echocardiogram, with no prior heart-failure admissions. Renal function is normal. The oncology team briefly discusses perioperative ECF versus postoperative chemoradiation and asks whether his cardiac history should limit therapy. He is otherwise ambulatory and has adequate nutrition after surgery.
proposed_actions: Avoid epirubicin because of presumed cardiac disease
Select a non-anthracycline regimen solely due to the past cardiomyopathy label
Assume perioperative ECF is contraindicated without reassessing current cardiac function
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has cardiac dysfunction and perioperative chemotherapy with ECF is being considered
Guideline Action: do not use epirubicin
Source Statement: The presence of cardiac or renal dysfunction would contraindicate the use of epirubicin and cisplatin, respectively.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has renal dysfunction and perioperative chemotherapy with ECF is being considered
Guideline Action: do not use cisplatin
Source Statement: The presence of cardiac or renal dysfunction would contraindicate the use of epirubicin and cisplatin, respectively.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient is being considered for perioperative chemotherapy and has cardiac dysfunction
Guideline Action: avoid epirubicin-containing perioperative chemotherapy
Source Statement: The presence of cardiac or renal dysfunction would contraindicate the use of epirubicin and cisplatin, respectively.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient is being considered for perioperative chemotherapy and has renal dysfunction
Guideline Action: avoid cisplatin-containing perioperative chemotherapy
Source Statement: The presence of cardiac or renal dysfunction would contraindicate the use of epirubicin and cisplatin, respectively.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has resectable gastric cancer and is being considered for adjunct treatment after curative surgery
Guideline Action: consider postoperative chemoradiation using the Macdonald protocol or perioperative ECF using the MAGIC protocol
Source Statement: OS in patients with resectable gastric cancer is significantly improved with the use of either postoperative chemoradiation implementing the Macdonald protocol (47-49) or perioperative ECF implementing the MAGIC protocol (30).

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: all patients with resectable gastric cancer
Guideline Action: perform a multidisciplinary assessment to determine the best plan of care
Source Statement: All patients with resectable gastric cancer should undergo a multidisciplinary assessment to determine the best plan of care.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient is undergoing gastric surgery with curative intent and there is no obvious contraindication due to poor performance status
Guideline Action: consider adjuncts to resection
Source Statement: Unless obviously contraindicated owing to poor performance status, all patients undergoing gastric surgery with curative intent should be considered for adjuncts to resection.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: planning postoperative chemoradiation target volume
Guideline Action: target the upper abdomen including the tumour bed and regional nodes, extending 2 cm beyond the proximal and distal margin of resection
Source Statement: The target volume is in the upper abdomen targeting the tumour bed and regional nodes, 2 cm beyond the proximal and distal margin of resection.
proposed_actions_text:
- Avoid epirubicin because of presumed cardiac disease
- Select a non-anthracycline regimen solely due to the past cardiomyopathy label
- Assume perioperative ECF is contraindicated without reassessing current cardiac function

patient_vignette: A 64-year-old man with resected distal gastric adenocarcinoma is reviewed after curative subtotal gastrectomy. Pathology shows pT2N1 disease with negative margins, and he is recovering well. The team discusses adjuvant treatment because he is motivated to proceed if recommended. He has chronic ischemic cardiomyopathy with an ejection fraction of 35% and exertional dyspnea, so the oncology note flags concern about epirubicin-containing therapy. However, a recent staging scan also shows a new 1.8-cm liver lesion suspicious for metastatic disease, and a repeat biopsy is pending. Renal function remains preserved, and there were no major postoperative complications. The case is brought to tumor board for planning.
proposed_actions: Proceed as though this is standard postoperative adjuvant gastric cancer
Recommend postoperative chemoradiation or perioperative ECF as routine adjunct treatment
Treat the case as a straightforward resected gastric cancer candidate for resection adjuncts

patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman with metastatic breast cancer on palliative chemotherapy returns for worsening fatigue and low mood over the past 3 weeks. She reports feeling “down and overwhelmed,” with reduced enjoyment, poor sleep, and lower appetite, but denies suicidal thoughts. Her symptoms are described as bothersome but she remains able to manage most daily activities, though she has been missing some social visits. She asks whether she should start an antidepressant right away because treatment has been emotionally draining. On review, her bone pain is well controlled with her current regimen, and constipation and nausea have improved. Recent labs show mild anemia, normal TSH, and normal B12 and folate levels.
proposed_actions: Start an antidepressant immediately for presumed mild depression
Focus the visit on symptom control adjustments for pain, constipation, or nausea despite those being stable
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patients with cancer with subthreshold depressive symptoms or mild depression
Guideline Action: do not use antidepressants routinely
Source Statement: Do not use antidepressants routinely to treat subthreshold depressive symptoms or mild depression, due to the higher risk-benefit ratio at this level of depression severity.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patients with cancer
Guideline Action: screen for depression or distress
Source Statement: Patients with cancer should be screened for depression.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patients with cancer
Guideline Action: link screening to effective interventions and use a clear diagnosis to guide treatment
Source Statement: To improve health outcomes, screening must be linked to effective interventions. A clear diagnosis of depression is required to guide treatment.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patients with cancer with depression
Guideline Action: provide psychoeducation about depression in cancer
Source Statement: Provide psychoeducation about the nature of depression in patients with cancer and consider providing handouts such as those published by the National Cancer Institute.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patients with cancer with depression
Guideline Action: inform patients about the impact of depression on cancer outcomes
Source Statement: Inform patients about the impact of depression on cancer outcomes, including reduced quality of life, intensification of physical symptoms, longer hospital stays, and reduced survival rates.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patients with cancer with depression
Guideline Action: destigmatize clinical depression as a serious problem requiring treatment
Source Statement: Destigmatize clinical depression in cancer by framing it as a serious problem requiring treatment, rather than as a personal weakness or failure to cope.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patients with cancer with depression
Guideline Action: investigate medical contributors such as hypothyroidism and vitamin B12, folate, or iron deficiency
Source Statement: Investigate medical contributors to depression such as hypothyroidism, or vitamin B12, folate, or iron deficiency.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patients with cancer with depression
Guideline Action: assess and optimize cancer-related physical symptom control
Source Statement: Assess and optimize cancer-related physical symptom control.
proposed_actions_text:
- Start an antidepressant immediately for presumed mild depression
- Focus the visit on symptom control adjustments for pain, constipation, or nausea despite those being stable

patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman with metastatic breast cancer on palliative chemotherapy comes to clinic for worsening fatigue and low mood over the past 3 weeks. She reports feeling “down and overwhelmed,” with reduced enjoyment, poor sleep, and less appetite, but denies suicidal thoughts. Her symptoms are mild and she remains able to manage most daily activities, though she has been missing some social visits. She wonders if she should start an antidepressant right away because she feels emotionally drained by treatment. On review, her pain from bone metastases is not well controlled, and she also notes constipation and nausea. Recent labs show mild anemia and a normal TSH; B12 and folate were checked last month and were normal, but her ferritin was low and she recently started iron supplementation.
proposed_actions: Avoid antidepressants because the symptoms are mild and simply wait after iron repletion
Assume the depression workup is complete and focus only on cancer symptom control
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patients with cancer with subthreshold depressive symptoms or mild depression
Guideline Action: do not use antidepressants routinely
Source Statement: Do not use antidepressants routinely to treat subthreshold depressive symptoms or mild depression, due to the higher risk-benefit ratio at this level of depression severity.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patients with cancer
Guideline Action: screen for depression or distress
Source Statement: Patients with cancer should be screened for depression.

patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man returns to a survivorship clinic 14 months after completing curative-intent external beam radiotherapy for localized prostate cancer. His PSA has remained undetectable on routine surveillance, and he reports worsening fatigue, mild urinary urgency, and intermittent hot flashes that are affecting sleep and daily activities. He is unsure which symptoms are expected long-term effects and asks what should prompt further review. He also asks whether a special PET scan would help if his PSA rises again and whether he should start a specific “prostate cancer diet” or supplement to reduce recurrence risk. He has no new bone pain, weight loss, or neurologic deficits. He has, however, recently transferred his routine follow-up entirely to a private oncology clinic and no family physician is involved in his survivorship care.
proposed_actions: Include the family physician in the survivorship care plan as if this were still guideline-concordant
Document routine survivorship care without addressing the absence of primary care involvement
Proceed as though all recommended survivorship partners are already engaged
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: RECURRENCE_OR_RESTAGING
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Considering NaF PET or choline PET
Guideline Action: Treat NaF PET and choline PET as experimental
Source Statement: Use of NaF and choline PET should be considered experimental

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Considering dietary advice for prostate cancer survivors
Guideline Action: Do not recommend any specific diet plan or food supplement for improved cancer outcomes
Source Statement: No diet plan can be recommended because no diet plan or food supplement has been associated with improved cancer outcomes.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: A man is not being evaluated through regularly scheduled clinical visits and develops symptoms
Guideline Action: Perform a PSA test and consider symptom-specific diagnostic imaging
Source Statement: In men who are not being evaluated through regularly scheduled clinical visits, a PSA test should be performed if the following symptoms develop. Additionally, diagnostic imaging specific to the patient's symptom(s) may be indicated.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Men after primary therapy with long-lasting treatment effects
Guideline Action: Ensure follow-up providers are aware of quality-of-life domains affected and available management options
Source Statement: Follow-up healthcare providers should be aware of the domains of quality of life potentially affected by treatment for prostate cancer and the management options available to combat them.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Following prostate cancer treatment when assessing quality of life
Guideline Action: Measure individual patient-reported outcomes
Source Statement: To ensure optimal quality of life in these men, individual patient-reported outcomes should be measured.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Prostate cancer survivor after completing curative-intent therapy
Guideline Action: Provide ongoing surveillance
Source Statement: For prostate cancer survivors who have completed curative-intent therapy, surveillance is required

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Any healthcare practitioner provides PSA surveillance
Guideline Action: Manage PSA according to the current CCO Prostate Cancer Pathway
Source Statement: All healthcare practitioners that provide PSA surveillance should manage PSA as per the current CCO Prostate Cancer Pathway.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Planning survivorship care models for prostate cancer survivors
Guideline Action: Include family physicians in survivorship care models
Source Statement: Expert opinion supports family physicians being involved in all survivorship care models.
proposed_actions_text:
- Include the family physician in the survivorship care plan as if this were still guideline-concordant
- Document routine survivorship care without addressing the absence of primary care involvement
- Proceed as though all recommended survivorship partners are already engaged

patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man completed curative-intent external beam radiotherapy for localized prostate cancer 14 months ago and is being followed in a survivorship clinic. He reports worsening fatigue, mild urinary urgency, and intermittent hot flashes that have affected sleep and daily activities. His PSA surveillance has been done through the clinic, but he is unsure which symptoms are expected long-term effects and which should prompt further review. He asks whether a special PET scan would help if his PSA rises again and also asks if he should start a specific “prostate cancer diet” or supplement to reduce recurrence risk. He has no new bone pain, weight loss, or neurologic deficits. On review, his recent PSA is 0.3 ng/mL, then 0.4 ng/mL six weeks later, and he has no documented biochemical recurrence or rising PSA trend beyond this borderline fluctuation.
proposed_actions: Order a NaF PET or choline PET to look for recurrent disease
Reassure the patient that advanced PET imaging is appropriate for any PSA increase
Treat the imaging request as guideline-concordant recurrence/restaging workup

patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman with previously treated diffuse large B-cell lymphoma completed six cycles of R-CHOP nine months ago and achieved a complete metabolic response. She returns for a routine follow-up visit and remains free of B symptoms, palpable adenopathy, and laboratory abnormalities. Her oncologist mentions that a prior CT done for unrelated abdominal pain showed a tiny 8 mm prevascular mediastinal node that has been unchanged on two scans and asks whether an FDG PET/CT should be ordered now to “make sure it isn’t lymphoma.” There is no new exam finding, no rising LDH, and no other clinical concern. The main question is whether to continue scheduled surveillance imaging or use PET/CT as a routine monitoring test.
proposed_actions: Order an FDG PET/CT now to evaluate the stable node
Continue serial PET/CT scans as routine surveillance after remission
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: routine monitoring or surveillance of lymphoma
Guideline Action: Do not use FDG PET/CT for routine surveillance.
Source Statement: An FDG PET/CT scan is not recommended for the routine monitoring and surveillance of lymphoma.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: PREOP_STAGING
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: functional imaging is important and anatomical imaging is equivocal
Guideline Action: Perform FDG PET/CT for staging.
Source Statement: When functional imaging is considered to be important in situations where anatomical imaging is equivocal, and/or in potentially curable cases, a FDG PET/CT scan is recommended.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: PREOP_STAGING
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: potentially curable case and functional imaging is important
Guideline Action: Perform FDG PET/CT for staging.
Source Statement: When functional imaging is considered to be important in situations where anatomical imaging is equivocal, and/or in potentially curable cases, a FDG PET/CT scan is recommended.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: PREOP_STAGING
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: functional imaging is important, anatomical imaging is equivocal, and treatment choices may be affected in limited stage indolent lymphoma
Guideline Action: Perform FDG PET/CT for staging.
Source Statement: When functional imaging is considered to be important in situations where anatomical imaging is equivocal and treatment choices may be affected in limited stage indolent lymphomas, a FDG PET/CT scan is recommended.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: TREATMENT_RESPONSE
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: early stage (I or II) Hodgkin lymphoma after 2 or 3 cycles of chemotherapy and chemotherapy is being considered as definitive single-modality therapy
Guideline Action: Perform FDG PET/CT to assess early response and inform whether to complete therapy or give more therapy.
Source Statement: An FDG PET/CT scan is recommended for the assessment of early response in early stage (I or II) HL following two or three cycles of chemotherapy when chemotherapy is being considered as the definitive single-modality therapy, to inform completion of therapy or if more therapy is warranted.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: RECURRENCE_OR_RESTAGING
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: potentially curable case with suspected recurrence and conventional imaging is equivocal
Guideline Action: Perform FDG PET/CT to investigate recurrence of Hodgkin or non-Hodgkin lymphoma.
Source Statement: In potentially curable cases, when functional imaging is considered to be important and conventional imaging is equivocal, a FDG PET/CT scan is recommended to investigate recurrence of HL or NHL.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: RECURRENCE_OR_RESTAGING
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: residual mass after chemotherapy in Hodgkin or non-Hodgkin lymphoma and further potentially curative therapy is being considered and biopsy cannot be safely or readily performed
Guideline Action: Perform FDG PET/CT to evaluate the residual mass.
Source Statement: An FDG PET/CT scan is recommended for the evaluation of residual mass(es) following chemotherapy in a patient with HL or NHL when further, potentially curative, therapy (such as radiation or stem cell transplantation) is being considered and when biopsy cannot be safely or readily performed.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: FDG PET is abnormal during initial diagnosis of lymphoma
Guideline Action: Use clinical follow-up or biopsy to confirm findings because false positives can occur.
Source Statement: FDG PET may disclose higher rates of systemic disease; however, due to false-positive results, FDG PET scans should be subject to clinical follow-up or biopsy.
proposed_actions_text:
- Order an FDG PET/CT now to evaluate the stable node
- Continue serial PET/CT scans as routine surveillance after remission

patient_vignette: A 61-year-old man with follicular lymphoma completed bendamustine-rituximab seven months ago and had a complete response on end-of-treatment imaging. He is seen for routine follow-up and feels well, with no fevers, night sweats, weight loss, or palpable adenopathy. His blood counts and LDH are normal. He asks whether he should undergo an FDG PET/CT at this visit because his original disease was “the kind that can come back quietly.” The oncologist notes that there is no new symptom, no equivocal exam finding, and no prior suspicious post-treatment scan. The question is whether to obtain PET/CT for surveillance or simply continue standard follow-up.
proposed_actions: Schedule FDG PET/CT for reassurance during routine follow-up
Add PET/CT to ongoing surveillance imaging because the lymphoma can recur silently

patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with metastatic non-small cell lung cancer was diagnosed 9 months ago and is now on second-line treatment after progression. He reports increasing fatigue, dyspnea on exertion, poor appetite, and intermittent anxiety about his family’s future. His wife says he has become more withdrawn and is struggling to talk about goals of care. He asks for help managing symptoms at home, but the oncology note states he is due for brain metastasis treatment next week and the plan is to focus on disease-directed therapy only; the team is not yet discussing palliative care beyond basic symptom checks. His spouse remains overwhelmed by caregiving responsibilities, and he has been told that supportive services will be revisited after the next scan.
proposed_actions: Start a team-based palliative care plan addressing physical symptoms, emotional distress, and caregiver support now.
Apply the broad adult palliative care guideline as if this were an established palliative-care encounter from diagnosis to death.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: When providing palliative care across the disease course
Guideline Action: Use a team-based approach that addresses both physical and non-physical symptoms and supports patients and families.
Source Statement: Palliative care focuses on care that addresses both physical and non-physical symptoms; it can be delivered throughout the course of the disease; it involves a team of healthcare providers and supports patients as well as their families.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: When selecting palliative care guidelines for review
Guideline Action: Include only papers that provide recommendations on palliative care and exclude papers that only describe guideline or consensus methods without recommendations.
Source Statement: Papers were included if they provided any recommendations on palliative care. Papers were excluded if they only provided guideline or consensus methods and no recommendations.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: When screening palliative care guidelines for this review
Guideline Action: Restrict inclusion to guidelines published from 2011 to 2016.
Source Statement: Due to the large number of guidelines, the Working Group decided to restrict the review to include only guidelines published in the past five years (2011 to 2016).

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: When selecting higher-quality guidelines for this review
Guideline Action: Include only guidelines with an AGREE Rigour of Development subsection score of 28 or higher.
Source Statement: To identify the best-quality guidelines, the AGREE Instrument Rigour of Development scale was used and only guidelines that scored 28 or above were included in this review.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: When using the broad palliative care guideline for adults
Guideline Action: Apply the guideline covering the palliative care path from first diagnosis of serious illness to death.
Source Statement: The "Palliative Care for Adults" guideline by McCusker et al. ... covers the palliative care path from the patient's first diagnosis of a serious illness to death.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: When caring for patients in the last days and hours of life
Guideline Action: Use the Registered Nurses Association of Ontario guideline for nurse-specific recommendations on assessment, decision support, care and management, education, and organization/policy.
Source Statement: This is a Canadian guideline about patient care in the last days and hours of life, and the recommendations in this guideline were specific to nurses. The guideline makes recommendations in the following areas: Assessment for end of life, Decision support at end of life, Care and management at end of life, Education, and Organization and policy recommendations.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: When assessing or preventing pressure ulcers in palliative care
Guideline Action: Use the Registered Nurses Association of Ontario guideline for nurse-specific recommendations on assessment, planning, interventions, discharge or transfer/care arrangements, and education/organization-policy.
Source Statement: The topic of this guideline was the assessment and prevention of pressure ulcers. This guideline does not focus solely on palliative care, but the treatment and assessment of pressure ulcers in palliative care is a part of the guideline. ... The guideline makes recommendations in the following areas: Assessment, Planning, Interventions, Discharge or transfer or the care arrangements, and Education and organization/policy.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: When managing chronic cough in patients receiving palliative care
Guideline Action: Use simple linctus, then a therapeutic trial of sodium cromoglycate, then an opioid or opioid derivative such as dextromethorphan, morphine, or codeine.
Source Statement: The recommendations include use of simple linctus, therapeutic trial of sodium cromoglycate, and then prescription of an opioid or opioid derivative (dextromethorphan, morphine, or codeine).
proposed_actions_text:
- Start a team-based palliative care plan addressing physical symptoms, emotional distress, and caregiver support now.
- Apply the broad adult palliative care guideline as if this were an established palliative-care encounter from diagnosis to death.

patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with metastatic non-small cell lung cancer was diagnosed 9 months ago and is now on second-line treatment after progression. He reports increasing fatigue, dyspnea on exertion, poor appetite, and intermittent anxiety about his family’s future. His wife says he has become more withdrawn and is struggling to talk about goals of care. He is not in the final days of life, but his symptoms are worsening and he asks for help managing them at home while continuing cancer-directed therapy. The oncology team notes that his physical symptoms are only part of the problem; he also needs emotional support and help for his wife, who is overwhelmed by caregiving responsibilities. However, the patient is enrolled in an outpatient palliative care study that excludes family support interventions and limits visits to medication review only.
proposed_actions: Recommend a comprehensive team-based palliative care plan including caregiver support and coping resources.
Treat this as a standard broad palliative-care pathway case from diagnosis through death.

Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: When caring for patients in the last days and hours of life
Guideline Action: Use the Registered Nurses Association of Ontario guideline for nurse-specific recommendations on assessment, decision support, care and management, education, and organization/policy.
Source Statement: This is a Canadian guideline about patient care in the last days and hours of life, and the recommendations in this guideline were specific to nurses. The guideline makes recommendations in the following areas: Assessment for end of life, Decision support at end of life, Care and management at end of life, Education, and Organization and policy recommendations.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: When assessing or preventing pressure ulcers in palliative care
Guideline Action: Use the Registered Nurses Association of Ontario guideline for nurse-specific recommendations on assessment, planning, interventions, discharge or transfer/care arrangements, and education/organization-policy.
Source Statement: The topic of this guideline was the assessment and prevention of pressure ulcers. This guideline does not focus solely on palliative care, but the treatment and assessment of pressure ulcers in palliative care is a part of the guideline. ... The guideline makes recommendations in the following areas: Assessment, Planning, Interventions, Discharge or transfer or the care arrangements, and Education and organization/policy.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: When managing chronic cough in patients receiving palliative care
Guideline Action: Use simple linctus, then a therapeutic trial of sodium cromoglycate, then an opioid or opioid derivative such as dextromethorphan, morphine, or codeine.
Source Statement: The recommendations include use of simple linctus, therapeutic trial of sodium cromoglycate, and then prescription of an opioid or opioid derivative (dextromethorphan, morphine, or codeine).
proposed_actions_text:
- Recommend a comprehensive team-based palliative care plan including caregiver support and coping resources.
- Treat this as a standard broad palliative-care pathway case from diagnosis through death.

patient_vignette: A 49-year-old woman with biopsy-proven cervical squamous cell carcinoma previously treated with definitive chemoradiation now has progressive pelvic pain, hydronephrosis, and enlarging para-aortic nodes on imaging. Her disease is deemed recurrent and not amenable to curative surgery or radiotherapy. She has an ECOG performance status of 1, normal renal and hepatic function, and adequate marrow counts. She has not received chemotherapy for recurrence. A randomized trial is available at her center, and she is interested in discussing enrollment. She has no active bleeding, no nonhealing wounds, and no history of inadequately anticoagulated thromboembolism. However, her tumor board notes the PET/CT also shows a new rectal wall invasion consistent with stage IVA disease. Her oncology team is discussing first-line systemic therapy options for recurrent disease.
proposed_actions: Add bevacizumab to cisplatin-paclitaxel because she otherwise looks like the trial population.
Treat her as fully eligible for the bevacizumab-containing regimen since performance status and labs are acceptable.
Proceed with bevacizumab without re-checking the staging exclusion.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has stage IIIB cervical cancer
Guideline Action: do not apply the bevacizumab recommendation from GOG-0240 eligibility
Source Statement: In addition, GOG-0240 did not include patients with stage IIIB cancer (local extension to pelvic sidewall) or IVA cancer (invasion into bladder or rectum).

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has stage IVA cervical cancer
Guideline Action: do not apply the bevacizumab recommendation from GOG-0240 eligibility
Source Statement: In addition, GOG-0240 did not include patients with stage IIIB cancer (local extension to pelvic sidewall) or IVA cancer (invasion into bladder or rectum).

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has recurrent, metastatic, or persistent cervical cancer and randomized clinical trials are available
Guideline Action: offer participation in randomized clinical trials evaluating systemic therapy regimens
Source Statement: It is recommended that all patients with recurrent, metastatic or persistent cervical cancer be offered the opportunity to participate in randomized clinical trials, if available, that evaluate the efficacy of and adverse effects of systemic therapy regimens.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has recurrent, metastatic, or persistent cervical cancer and systemic therapy is indicated
Guideline Action: use cisplatin with paclitaxel
Source Statement: Cisplatin with paclitaxel is recommended for this patient population

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has recurrent, metastatic, or persistent cervical cancer and a cisplatin combination is being used
Guideline Action: substitute carboplatin for cisplatin when appropriate
Source Statement: The substitution of carboplatin for cisplatin in these combinations is also recommended for this target population because carboplatin is associated with fewer adverse effects and greater ease of administration.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has stage IVB, recurrent, or persistent cervical cancer not amenable to curative surgery or radiotherapy, performance status 0 to 1, adequate renal hepatic and bone marrow function, and no prior chemotherapy for recurrence, nonhealing wounds, active bleeding, or inadequately anticoagulated thromboembolism
Guideline Action: use bevacizumab with cisplatin-paclitaxel
Source Statement: Bevacizumab in combination with cisplatin-paclitaxel is recommended for a specific subset of the target population, which includes only patients that match the characteristics of the GOG-0240 study population.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has recurrent, metastatic, or persistent cervical cancer and carboplatin-paclitaxel is feasible to administer
Guideline Action: use carboplatin-paclitaxel as a treatment option
Source Statement: Based on these results, and on the feasibility of administration, carboplatin-paclitaxel is recommended as a treatment option for recurrent, metastatic, or persistent cervical cancer.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: patient has recurrent, metastatic, or persistent cervical cancer and systemic therapy is indicated
Guideline Action: consider cisplatin-vinorelbine
Source Statement: cisplatin in other combinations, including cisplatin-vinorelbine, cisplatin-gemcitabine, and cisplatin-topotecan may also be considered.
proposed_actions_text:
- Add bevacizumab to cisplatin-paclitaxel because she otherwise looks like the trial population.
- Treat her as fully eligible for the bevacizumab-containing regimen since performance status and labs are acceptable.
- Proceed with bevacizumab without re-checking the staging exclusion.

patient_vignette: A 49-year-old woman with biopsy-proven cervical squamous cell carcinoma previously treated with definitive chemoradiation now has progressive pelvic pain and enlarging para-aortic nodes on imaging. Her disease is deemed recurrent and not amenable to curative surgery or radiotherapy. She has an ECOG performance status of 1, normal renal and hepatic function, and adequate marrow counts. She has not received chemotherapy for recurrence. A randomized trial is available at her center, but after reviewing the protocol she declines because she wants standard treatment immediately. She has no active bleeding, no nonhealing wounds, and no history of inadequately anticoagulated thromboembolism. Her oncology team is discussing first-line systemic therapy options for recurrent disease.
proposed_actions: Offer or document trial participation as if it were recommended despite her refusal.
Pursue trial consent again as the primary next step.
Record that she should be entered into the randomized study because one is available locally.

patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man with cutaneous melanoma of the left upper back underwent wide local excision and sentinel lymph node biopsy. The sentinel node shows a 0.4 mm micrometastatic deposit, and the oncology note describes the primary as a thin lesion with otherwise stage IIa features. He has no neurologic symptoms, no known distant disease, and no palpable adenopathy. The team is considering whether PET imaging should be ordered now to clarify the extent of disease before deciding on adjuvant management.
proposed_actions: Order PET to stage the melanoma before adjuvant treatment decisions.
Use PET to further characterize the sentinel node micrometastatic finding.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: PREOP_STAGING
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient has melanoma and sentinel lymph node micrometastatic disease is being diagnosed
Guideline Action: Do not use PET for diagnosis
Source Statement: PET is not recommended for the diagnosis of sentinel lymph node micrometastatic disease or for staging of I, IIa, or IIb melanoma.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: PREOP_STAGING
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient has melanoma stage I, IIa, or IIb
Guideline Action: Do not use PET for staging
Source Statement: PET is not recommended for the diagnosis of sentinel lymph node micrometastatic disease or for staging of I, IIa, or IIb melanoma.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: PREOP_STAGING
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient has suspected brain metastases from melanoma
Guideline Action: Do not routinely use PET to diagnose brain metastases
Source Statement: The routine use of PET or PET/CT is not recommended for the diagnosis of brain metastases.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: PREOP_STAGING
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient has primary uveal malignant melanoma
Guideline Action: Do not routinely use PET to detect primary uveal malignant melanoma
Source Statement: The routine use of PET is not recommended for the detection of primary uveal malignant melanoma.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: PREOP_STAGING
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient has melanoma and is high-risk with potentially resectable disease
Guideline Action: Use PET for staging
Source Statement: PET is recommended for staging of high-risk patients with potentially resectable disease.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: SOLITARY_METASTASIS_AT_RECURRENCE
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: At recurrence, an isolated metastasis is identified
Guideline Action: Use PET
Source Statement: PET is recommended for isolated metastases at time of recurrence or when contemplating metastectomy.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: SOLITARY_METASTASIS_AT_RECURRENCE
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: At recurrence, metastectomy is being contemplated
Guideline Action: Use PET before metastectomy
Source Statement: PET is recommended for isolated metastases at time of recurrence or when contemplating metastectomy.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: TREATMENT_RESPONSE
Priority: 50
Recommendation Class: NO_RECOMMENDATION
Activation Condition: Assessing treatment response in malignant melanoma
Guideline Action: No recommendation for or against PET
Source Statement: A recommendation cannot be made for or against the use of PET for the assessment of treatment response in malignant melanoma due to insufficient evidence.
proposed_actions_text:
- Order PET to stage the melanoma before adjuvant treatment decisions.
- Use PET to further characterize the sentinel node micrometastatic finding.

patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man with cutaneous melanoma of the left upper back underwent wide local excision and sentinel lymph node biopsy. Pathology now shows a single sentinel node with a 0.4 mm micrometastatic deposit. He has no neurologic symptoms and no known distant disease, but his oncology team is also reviewing a new 7 mm enhancing lesion in the left frontal lobe seen on MRI after he reported brief word-finding difficulty. The question is whether PET should be ordered to better define the brain lesion before additional management is chosen.
proposed_actions: Order PET to characterize the suspected brain metastasis.
Use PET/CT as the next diagnostic step for the intracranial lesion.

patient_vignette: A 15-year-old boy with localized Ewing’s sarcoma of the proximal tibia completed neoadjuvant chemotherapy and has had a clear symptomatic improvement. Restaging MRI shows shrinkage of the soft-tissue mass, but the orthopedic oncologist believes limb-sparing surgery would leave a close anterior margin because the tumor abuts the neurovascular bundle. At tumor board, the team discusses local control options with the family, who strongly prefer to avoid a major amputation if possible. Because the surgeon is worried about microscopic residual disease after resection, the initial plan being discussed is combined limb-sparing surgery followed by radiation therapy. The pre-chemotherapy and post-chemotherapy MRIs are both available for review, and the post-treatment scan is being used to estimate how much tissue remains to be removed.
proposed_actions: Proceed with limb-sparing surgery and add postoperative radiation as the initial local treatment plan.
Assume combined surgery plus RT is preferred because the margin will likely be close.
Treat the scenario as guideline-concordant because both MRI scans are available and the family wants function preservation.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: localized Ewing's sarcoma of bone after neoadjuvant chemotherapy
Guideline Action: Do not use surgery plus radiation therapy as the initial local treatment option.
Source Statement: Either surgery alone or RT alone is a reasonable treatment option; the combination of surgery plus RT is not recommended as an initial treatment option.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: PREOP_STAGING
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: localized Ewing's sarcoma of bone and surgery is planned
Guideline Action: Take both pre-chemotherapy and post-chemotherapy MRI scans into consideration for surgical planning.
Source Statement: Both pre-chemotherapy and post-chemotherapy MRI scans should be taken into consideration for surgical planning.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: localized Ewing's sarcoma of bone after neoadjuvant chemotherapy
Guideline Action: Consider surgery alone as a local treatment option.
Source Statement: Either surgery alone or RT alone is a reasonable treatment option; the combination of surgery plus RT is not recommended as an initial treatment option.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: localized Ewing's sarcoma of bone after neoadjuvant chemotherapy
Guideline Action: Consider radiation therapy alone as a local treatment option.
Source Statement: Either surgery alone or RT alone is a reasonable treatment option; the combination of surgery plus RT is not recommended as an initial treatment option.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: localized Ewing's sarcoma of bone after neoadjuvant chemotherapy
Guideline Action: Decide local treatment with a multidisciplinary tumour board together with the patient, considering age, tumour location, tumour size, response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy, comorbidities, expected benefit versus surgical/RT harms, and patient preference.
Source Statement: The local treatment for an individual patient should be decided by a multidisciplinary tumour board together with the patient after consideration of the following: 1) patient characteristics (e.g., age, tumour location, tumour size, response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy, and existing comorbidities), 2) the potential benefit compared with the potential complications from surgery and/or toxicities associated with RT, and 3) patient preference.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: complete tumour resection is impossible
Guideline Action: Consider radiation therapy alone as the optimal local treatment.
Source Statement: If complete tumour resection is impossible, RT alone may be the optimal choice.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: postoperative residual tumour or positive margins
Guideline Action: Consider postoperative radiation therapy.
Source Statement: RT may be a treatment option postoperatively in patients who have residual tumours or positive margins.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: PREOP_STAGING
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: localized Ewing's sarcoma of bone, surgery is planned, and there is good chemotherapy response in certain anatomic locations
Guideline Action: Use the post-chemotherapy MRI to plan surgical resection margins when appropriate.
Source Statement: In certain anatomic locations with good chemotherapy response, the post-chemotherapy MRI may be the appropriate imaging modality to plan surgical resection margins.
proposed_actions_text:
- Proceed with limb-sparing surgery and add postoperative radiation as the initial local treatment plan.
- Assume combined surgery plus RT is preferred because the margin will likely be close.
- Treat the scenario as guideline-concordant because both MRI scans are available and the family wants function preservation.

patient_vignette: A 13-year-old girl with localized Ewing’s sarcoma of the distal femur finished neoadjuvant chemotherapy and has shown a marked radiographic response. Her orthopedic surgeon is considering limb-sparing resection, and both the pre-chemotherapy and post-chemotherapy MRIs are available because the team wants to compare the original marrow extent with the current residual lesion. At multidisciplinary conference, the family requests the least morbid operation that still offers cure, and the team agrees the final operation should be individualized based on tumor size, location, and chemotherapy response. One detail complicates planning: the post-treatment MRI shows that the soft-tissue component has nearly resolved, but the tumor still extends farther proximally within the medullary canal than expected on the latest scan alone. Because of this discrepancy, the surgeon is relying primarily on the initial MRI to define the full bony extent before making a final resection plan.
proposed_actions: Use only the post-chemotherapy MRI to set resection margins because the mass has responded well.
Plan margins from the latest scan alone and ignore the original marrow extent.
Assume the good radiographic response makes post-treatment imaging sufficient for surgical planning in this location.
