patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man with ECOG 1 performance status presents with newly diagnosed metastatic colorectal adenocarcinoma involving multiple bilobar liver metastases and several small pulmonary nodules. He has no bowel obstruction or other major symptoms, and his creatinine and liver tests are acceptable. He wants treatment aimed at prolonging life while preserving quality of life. The oncology team reviews that either sequential chemotherapy or upfront combination chemotherapy could be considered in this palliative setting. However, the team also notes that his tumor is now being worked up with curative-intent liver-directed therapy because the liver disease has been deemed potentially resectable after response to treatment. The patient is eager to start systemic therapy quickly while planning for surgery if feasible.
proposed_actions: Treat this as unresectable metastatic disease and present sequential chemotherapy or upfront combination chemotherapy as equally acceptable palliative standards.
Recommend a palliative systemic strategy without clarifying the possibility of conversion to resectability and surgical planning.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer treated with palliative intent
Guideline Action: Consider either planned sequential chemotherapy or upfront combination chemotherapy as acceptable standards of care
Source Statement: Planned sequential chemotherapy and upfront combination chemotherapy are both acceptable standards of care.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer
Guideline Action: Choose treatment case by case based on patient and tumour characteristics, toxicity of each strategy, and patient preference
Source Statement: Therefore, choice of treatment should be made on a caseby-case basis based on considerations that include patient and tumour characteristics, toxicity of each strategy and patient preference.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer
Guideline Action: Use sequential chemotherapy as a strategy to provide access to all effective cytotoxic drugs
Source Statement: Based on the results of this systematic review, patients should have access to all effective cytotoxic drugs using a sequential strategy.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult patient with rapidly progressing, very symptomatic, or bulky life-threatening visceral disease
Guideline Action: Consider upfront combination chemotherapy
Source Statement: Combination chemotherapy may be more appropriate for patients with rapidly progressing, very symptomatic or bulky life-threatening visceral disease given their higher overall response rates.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer
Guideline Action: Consider that combination chemotherapy has a small survival advantage over sequential chemotherapy
Source Statement: A meta-analysis of five trials (1-5) demonstrates a survival advantage for combination chemotherapy (HR, 0.92; 95%CI, 0.86-0.99, p=0.02).

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer
Guideline Action: Consider that the survival advantage of combination chemotherapy is likely very small and may not be clinically significant
Source Statement: Median survival advantage in most trials is 3 to 6 weeks (range <1 week to 12 weeks). Therefore, any survival advantage that exists is likely to be very small and not clinically significant.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer
Guideline Action: Consider that upfront combination chemotherapy causes more neutropenia
Source Statement: Hematological toxicities include significantly more neutropenia (1,4) ... with upfront combination chemotherapy.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer
Guideline Action: Consider that upfront combination chemotherapy causes more febrile neutropenia
Source Statement: Hematological toxicities include significantly more neutropenia (1,4), febrile neutropenia (1) ... with upfront combination chemotherapy.
proposed_actions_text:
- Treat this as unresectable metastatic disease and present sequential chemotherapy or upfront combination chemotherapy as equally acceptable palliative standards.
- Recommend a palliative systemic strategy without clarifying the possibility of conversion to resectability and surgical planning.

patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man with ECOG 1 performance status has newly diagnosed unresectable metastatic colorectal adenocarcinoma with multiple bilobar liver metastases and small pulmonary nodules. He has no bowel obstruction or severe cancer-related symptoms, and his creatinine and liver tests are acceptable. After discussing goals of care, he says he wants treatment aimed at prolonging life while preserving quality of life. The team reviews that either sequential chemotherapy or upfront combination chemotherapy can be considered, but he is enrolled in a first-line trial that randomizes patients to a maintenance-based sequential strategy versus an oxaliplatin-containing doublet. Although the metastatic disease remains unresectable, the oncology team is waiting for protocol assignment before choosing the exact regimen.
proposed_actions: State that either sequential chemotherapy or upfront combination chemotherapy should be offered as standard-of-care options right away.
Ignore the trial enrollment and proceed as if the regimen choice were fully discretionary in routine practice.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer treated with palliative intent
Guideline Action: Consider either planned sequential chemotherapy or upfront combination chemotherapy as acceptable standards of care
Source Statement: Planned sequential chemotherapy and upfront combination chemotherapy are both acceptable standards of care.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer
Guideline Action: Choose treatment case by case based on patient and tumour characteristics, toxicity of each strategy, and patient preference
Source Statement: Therefore, choice of treatment should be made on a caseby-case basis based on considerations that include patient and tumour characteristics, toxicity of each strategy and patient preference.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer
Guideline Action: Use sequential chemotherapy as a strategy to provide access to all effective cytotoxic drugs
Source Statement: Based on the results of this systematic review, patients should have access to all effective cytotoxic drugs using a sequential strategy.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult patient with rapidly progressing, very symptomatic, or bulky life-threatening visceral disease
Guideline Action: Consider upfront combination chemotherapy
Source Statement: Combination chemotherapy may be more appropriate for patients with rapidly progressing, very symptomatic or bulky life-threatening visceral disease given their higher overall response rates.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer
Guideline Action: Consider that combination chemotherapy has a small survival advantage over sequential chemotherapy
Source Statement: A meta-analysis of five trials (1-5) demonstrates a survival advantage for combination chemotherapy (HR, 0.92; 95%CI, 0.86-0.99, p=0.02).

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer
Guideline Action: Consider that the survival advantage of combination chemotherapy is likely very small and may not be clinically significant
Source Statement: Median survival advantage in most trials is 3 to 6 weeks (range <1 week to 12 weeks). Therefore, any survival advantage that exists is likely to be very small and not clinically significant.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer
Guideline Action: Consider that upfront combination chemotherapy causes more neutropenia
Source Statement: Hematological toxicities include significantly more neutropenia (1,4) ... with upfront combination chemotherapy.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer
Guideline Action: Consider that upfront combination chemotherapy causes more febrile neutropenia
Source Statement: Hematological toxicities include significantly more neutropenia (1,4), febrile neutropenia (1) ... with upfront combination chemotherapy.
proposed_actions_text:
- State that either sequential chemotherapy or upfront combination chemotherapy should be offered as standard-of-care options right away.
- Ignore the trial enrollment and proceed as if the regimen choice were fully discretionary in routine practice.

patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with acute myeloid leukemia was treated with induction chemotherapy and is now in first complete remission. His performance status remains good, and he asks whether an autologous stem cell transplant would be the best way to reduce relapse risk. The team is weighing post-remission options, including transplant versus chemotherapy. He has no matched sibling donor identified yet, and he is particularly focused on maximizing long-term disease control. Cytogenetic testing, however, places him in a good-risk category rather than intermediate-risk disease.
proposed_actions: Proceed with autologous stem cell transplantation as the preferred consolidation strategy.
Choose allogeneic SCT over chemotherapy because the patient is in CR1 and wants maximal disease control.
Assume the original AML-in-CR1 transplant recommendation applies unchanged.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with MDS
Guideline Action: Do not use autologous stem cell transplantation outside a clinical trial.
Source Statement: Autologous stem cell transplantation is not recommended for patients with MDS.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with MDS
Guideline Action: Do not recommend autologous SCT outside of a clinical trial.
Source Statement: One systematic review comprising a total of 22 studies did not detect any benefit associated with autologous SCT (ASCT), and does not recommend it outside of a clinical trial (1).

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with AML in CR1
Guideline Action: Do not use autologous SCT as the preferred post-remission strategy.
Source Statement: ASCT is not recommended for patients with AML in first complete remission.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with AML in CR1
Guideline Action: Do not use autologous SCT when long-term survival is the primary outcome of interest.
Source Statement: While associated with more favourable treatment-related mortality (TRM) rates, if longterm survival is the primary outcome of interest, then there is no evidence to support the use of ASCT in first complete remission.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: RECURRENCE_OR_RESTAGING
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with AML in second or subsequent remission
Guideline Action: Do not use autologous SCT as supported therapy.
Source Statement: There is insufficient evidence to support the use of ASCT for patients with AML in second or subsequent remission.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: RECURRENCE_OR_RESTAGING
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with AML in second or subsequent remission and long-term survival is the primary outcome
Guideline Action: Do not use autologous SCT for long-term survival benefit.
Source Statement: If long-term survival is the primary outcome of interest, then there is no evidence to support the use of ASCT in second or subsequent remission.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with AML in CR1 and intermediate-risk or poor-risk disease
Guideline Action: Use allogeneic SCT rather than autologous SCT or chemotherapy when transplant is being considered.
Source Statement: Significant benefits in favour of alloSCT for both intermediate and poor risk patients (p<0.01) were detected, but no difference was detected with good risk patients.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: RECURRENCE_OR_RESTAGING
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with AML who achieves a second or subsequent remission
Guideline Action: Recommend allogeneic transplantation.
Source Statement: Allogeneic transplantation is the recommended option for patients with AML who achieve a second or subsequent remission.
proposed_actions_text:
- Proceed with autologous stem cell transplantation as the preferred consolidation strategy.
- Choose allogeneic SCT over chemotherapy because the patient is in CR1 and wants maximal disease control.
- Assume the original AML-in-CR1 transplant recommendation applies unchanged.

patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with intermediate-risk acute myeloid leukemia was treated with induction chemotherapy and is now in first complete remission. His performance status is good, and he asks whether an autologous stem cell transplant would be the best way to reduce relapse risk. The team is considering post-remission options, including transplant versus chemotherapy. He has no matched sibling donor identified yet, and he is particularly focused on maximizing long-term disease control. On review, however, he has already undergone a prior autologous stem cell transplant for lymphoma several years ago, which was complicated by prolonged cytopenias.
proposed_actions: Recommend autologous stem cell transplantation again because he is in CR1.
Frame autologous SCT as the preferred post-remission strategy since he has no donor.
Assume the intermediate-risk AML recommendation directly applies without considering prior transplant history.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: RECURRENCE_OR_RESTAGING
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with AML in second or subsequent remission and long-term survival is the primary outcome
Guideline Action: Do not use autologous SCT for long-term survival benefit.
Source Statement: If long-term survival is the primary outcome of interest, then there is no evidence to support the use of ASCT in second or subsequent remission.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with AML in CR1 and intermediate-risk or poor-risk disease
Guideline Action: Use allogeneic SCT rather than autologous SCT or chemotherapy when transplant is being considered.
Source Statement: Significant benefits in favour of alloSCT for both intermediate and poor risk patients (p<0.01) were detected, but no difference was detected with good risk patients.

- Frame autologous SCT as the preferred post-remission strategy since he has no donor.

patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man in Ontario is referred after a CT chest done for persistent cough shows an anterior mediastinal mass. Biopsy confirms a thymic epithelial tumour. He is otherwise well, with no myasthenic symptoms and normal baseline bloodwork. The case is being reviewed before any treatment decision is made. The oncology team plans formal staging and wants input from thoracic surgery, medical oncology, and radiation oncology, with consideration of consultation from a higher-volume centre because the tumour appears potentially resectable. The patient asks whether a specialized radionuclide therapy could be used instead of surgery or standard systemic treatment. A PET scan obtained for staging also shows multiple small liver lesions and diffuse bone uptake concerning for metastatic disease.
proposed_actions: Use the TNM staging system as though this were a potentially resectable localized tumour and proceed with the same surgical planning.
Push the case to a higher-volume thoracic surgery centre for resection-focused management without reassessing the metastatic burden.
Treat the radionuclide therapy question as if it were an appropriate alternative to standard staging and metastatic workup.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patients with thymic epithelial tumours in Ontario
Guideline Action: do not use peptide receptor radionuclide therapy as an approved treatment
Source Statement: Also, peptide receptor radionuclide therapy has not been approved for patients with thymic epithelial tumours in Ontario.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: all adult patients with thymic epithelial tumours
Guideline Action: use the TNM staging system
Source Statement: The TNM staging system should be used for all patients.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: all patients with thymic epithelial tumours undergoing surgery
Guideline Action: aim to achieve complete resection
Source Statement: The aim of surgery in all cases is to achieve a complete resection.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: all patients with thymic epithelial tumours
Guideline Action: discuss the patient at a multidisciplinary cancer conference, including higher-volume centres when possible
Source Statement: Discussion of all patients at multidisciplinary cancer conference (MCC) is strongly recommended, not just at local MCC but also with higher-volume centres.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: all patients with thymic epithelial tumours
Guideline Action: consider presentation at the International Thymic Malignancies Interest Group tumour board
Source Statement: Presentation at the International Thymic Malignancies Interest Group tumour board should be considered.
proposed_actions_text:
- Use the TNM staging system as though this were a potentially resectable localized tumour and proceed with the same surgical planning.
- Push the case to a higher-volume thoracic surgery centre for resection-focused management without reassessing the metastatic burden.
- Treat the radionuclide therapy question as if it were an appropriate alternative to standard staging and metastatic workup.

patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man in Ontario is referred after a CT chest done for persistent cough shows an anterior mediastinal mass. Biopsy confirms a thymic epithelial tumour. He is otherwise well, with no myasthenic symptoms and normal baseline bloodwork. The case is being reviewed before any treatment decision is made. The oncology team plans formal staging and wants input from thoracic surgery, medical oncology, and radiation oncology, with consideration of consultation from a higher-volume centre because the tumour appears potentially resectable. The patient asks whether a specialized radionuclide therapy could be used instead of surgery or standard systemic treatment. On review, the tumour is actually a metastatic deposit from a previously resected testicular seminoma, and the thymic lesion is not the primary site of disease.
proposed_actions: Apply thymic epithelial tumour staging and management pathways exactly as in the source vignette.
Conclude that peptide receptor radionuclide therapy is not approved for this diagnosis in Ontario and treat that as the key decision point.
Send the case to thymic surgery and thymic malignancy board review as if the mediastinal lesion were a primary thymic cancer.

Source Statement: Also, peptide receptor radionuclide therapy has not been approved for patients with thymic epithelial tumours in Ontario.

Rule ID: R002

Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: all adult patients with thymic epithelial tumours
Guideline Action: use the TNM staging system
Source Statement: The TNM staging system should be used for all patients.

Guideline Action: aim to achieve complete resection
Source Statement: The aim of surgery in all cases is to achieve a complete resection.

Guideline Action: discuss the patient at a multidisciplinary cancer conference, including higher-volume centres when possible

Guideline Action: consider presentation at the International Thymic Malignancies Interest Group tumour board
Source Statement: Presentation at the International Thymic Malignancies Interest Group tumour board should be considered.
proposed_actions_text:
- Apply thymic epithelial tumour staging and management pathways exactly as in the source vignette.
- Conclude that peptide receptor radionuclide therapy is not approved for this diagnosis in Ontario and treat that as the key decision point.
- Send the case to thymic surgery and thymic malignancy board review as if the mediastinal lesion were a primary thymic cancer.

patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with no prior thoracic radiation presents with 3 weeks of worsening cough, intermittent hemoptysis, and dyspnea. CT chest shows a centrally located right upper lobe non-small cell lung cancer causing partial endobronchial obstruction of the segmental bronchus with post-obstructive atelectasis, but not complete lung collapse. Bronchoscopy confirms a friable endobronchial lesion narrowing the airway. He is being evaluated for palliative treatment to relieve respiratory symptoms. The radiation oncologist notes that the obstruction is more distal than initially thought and that the endobronchial segment can be accessed only with difficulty, but the team is still discussing whether to add endobronchial brachytherapy to external beam radiation because symptoms are significant.
proposed_actions: Proceed with HDREB because the lesion is symptomatic and endobronchial.
Recommend brachytherapy as the preferred salvage option for the obstructing lesion.
Assume technical feasibility alone is enough to justify HDREB despite no prior EBRT.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with symptomatic endobronchial non-small cell lung cancer and previously untreated disease
Guideline Action: Use HDREB alone for palliation of respiratory symptoms
Source Statement: For patients with previously untreated, symptomatic, endobronchial non-small cell lung cancer: External beam radiation therapy (EBRT) alone is more effective for palliation of respiratory symptoms than HDREB alone.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with symptomatic endobronchial non-small cell lung cancer and previously untreated disease
Guideline Action: Use routine HDREB plus EBRT to improve symptom relief over EBRT alone
Source Statement: The evidence does not provide conclusive results to suggest that routine use of HDREB and EBRT would provide improved symptom relief over EBRT alone.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with complete collapse of the lung due to endobronchial obstruction
Guideline Action: Use a surgical core out procedure before EBRT or EBRT with HDREB
Source Statement: December 2022: the recommendation "For patients with complete collapse of the lung due to endobronchial obstruction, a surgical core out procedure may be needed before EBRT or EBRT with HDREB" is no longer endorsed because the updated evidence does not show a benefit of this procedure.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient previously treated by EBRT who is symptomatic with endobronchial obstruction due to recurrent disease
Guideline Action: Use HDREB if technically feasible
Source Statement: For patients previously treated by EBRT who are symptomatic with endobronchial obstruction due to recurrent disease, HDREB is recommended, providing that endobronchial brachytherapy is technically feasible.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult patient with symptomatic endobronchial non-small cell lung cancer and previously untreated disease where selected combined treatment is being considered
Guideline Action: Consider HDREB plus EBRT in selected patients
Source Statement: June 2018: It is the opinion of the Lung Cancer Disease Site Group that HDREB and EBRT might be suitable in selected patients.
proposed_actions_text:
- Proceed with HDREB because the lesion is symptomatic and endobronchial.
- Recommend brachytherapy as the preferred salvage option for the obstructing lesion.
- Assume technical feasibility alone is enough to justify HDREB despite no prior EBRT.

patient_vignette: A 72-year-old woman with no prior thoracic radiation presents with 4 weeks of progressive cough, scant hemoptysis, and shortness of breath. CT chest demonstrates a centrally located left lower lobe non-small cell lung cancer with partial endobronchial obstruction and associated post-obstructive atelectasis. Bronchoscopy confirms a friable lesion narrowing the airway, but the airway remains patent and there is no complete collapse. She is referred for palliative radiation to relieve respiratory symptoms. Because the lesion is centrally located and the family is asking for the most aggressive local approach, the team considers delivering endobronchial brachytherapy together with EBRT as the initial plan.
proposed_actions: Order routine HDREB plus EBRT to maximize symptom relief.
Assume combined local radiation is standard for all symptomatic endobronchial tumors.
Choose brachytherapy plus EBRT without noting that evidence does not support routine superiority over EBRT alone.
rules_text:

Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with symptomatic endobronchial non-small cell lung cancer and previously untreated disease
Guideline Action: Use HDREB alone for palliation of respiratory symptoms
Source Statement: For patients with previously untreated, symptomatic, endobronchial non-small cell lung cancer: External beam radiation therapy (EBRT) alone is more effective for palliation of respiratory symptoms than HDREB alone.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with symptomatic endobronchial non-small cell lung cancer and previously untreated disease
Guideline Action: Use routine HDREB plus EBRT to improve symptom relief over EBRT alone
Source Statement: The evidence does not provide conclusive results to suggest that routine use of HDREB and EBRT would provide improved symptom relief over EBRT alone.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with complete collapse of the lung due to endobronchial obstruction
Guideline Action: Use a surgical core out procedure before EBRT or EBRT with HDREB
Source Statement: December 2022: the recommendation "For patients with complete collapse of the lung due to endobronchial obstruction, a surgical core out procedure may be needed before EBRT or EBRT with HDREB" is no longer endorsed because the updated evidence does not show a benefit of this procedure.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient previously treated by EBRT who is symptomatic with endobronchial obstruction due to recurrent disease
Guideline Action: Use HDREB if technically feasible
Source Statement: For patients previously treated by EBRT who are symptomatic with endobronchial obstruction due to recurrent disease, HDREB is recommended, providing that endobronchial brachytherapy is technically feasible.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult patient with symptomatic endobronchial non-small cell lung cancer and previously untreated disease where selected combined treatment is being considered
Guideline Action: Consider HDREB plus EBRT in selected patients
Source Statement: June 2018: It is the opinion of the Lung Cancer Disease Site Group that HDREB and EBRT might be suitable in selected patients.
proposed_actions_text:
- Order routine HDREB plus EBRT to maximize symptom relief.
- Assume combined local radiation is standard for all symptomatic endobronchial tumors.
- Choose brachytherapy plus EBRT without noting that evidence does not support routine superiority over EBRT alone.

patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man returns after wide local excision and sentinel node biopsy for a 2.1-mm ulcerated cutaneous melanoma on the upper back. Final pathology shows clear margins, but one of the sentinel nodes is positive and a postoperative PET/CT now shows a small FDG-avid right axillary node, raising concern for clinically evident residual nodal disease. He is recovering well and asks about older immune shots he read about online. He has not yet had BRAF V600E/V600K testing. He is being seen to discuss postoperative treatment options after surgery, including whether any adjuvant therapy can start right away.
proposed_actions: Start adjuvant nivolumab or pembrolizumab as if this were fully resected stage III disease.
Skip additional nodal management and proceed directly to postoperative systemic therapy.
Order molecular testing only after choosing adjuvant treatment.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient with completely resected cutaneous melanoma at high risk of recurrence
Guideline Action: do not use ipilimumab as adjuvant therapy
Source Statement: Ipilimumab is not recommended as adjuvant therapy for patients with completely resected cutaneous melanoma with high risk of recurrence.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient with completely resected cutaneous melanoma outside a clinical trial
Guideline Action: do not use interferon alpha for adjuvant treatment
Source Statement: Use of interferon alpha (IFN-α) for adjuvant treatment of cutaneous melanoma outside of a clinical trial is no longer recommended.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient with completely resected cutaneous melanoma
Guideline Action: do not use chemotherapy regimens, vaccines, levamisole, bevacizumab, Bacillus Calmette-Guerin, or isolated limb perfusion as adjuvant treatment except in a clinical trial
Source Statement: Chemotherapy regimens, vaccines, levamisole, bevacizumab, Bacillus Calmette-Guerin, and isolated limb perfusion are not recommended for adjuvant treatment of cutaneous melanoma except as part of a clinical trial.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: adult patient with completely resected cutaneous melanoma at high risk of recurrence and no BRAF V600E or V600K mutation
Guideline Action: use nivolumab or pembrolizumab as adjuvant therapy
Source Statement: Nivolumab or pembrolizumab is recommended as adjuvant therapy for patients with completely resected cutaneous melanoma without BRAF V600E or V600K mutations with high risk of recurrence (stage IIIA to IIID, IV).

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: adult patient with completely resected cutaneous melanoma at high risk of recurrence and BRAF V600E or V600K mutation
Guideline Action: use nivolumab, pembrolizumab, or dabrafenib plus trametinib as adjuvant therapy
Source Statement: Nivolumab, pembrolizumab, or dabrafenib plus trametinib is recommended as adjuvant therapy for patients with completely resected cutaneous melanoma with BRAF V600E or V600K mutations and high risk of recurrence (stage IIIA to IIID, IV).

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: high-risk melanoma patient being considered for adjuvant treatment
Guideline Action: perform molecular testing to characterize mutations to guide treatment decisions
Source Statement: Molecular testing of high-risk melanoma patients to characterize mutations should be conducted to help guide appropriate treatment decisions.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient with clinically positive lymph nodes before adjuvant therapy or adjuvant radiotherapy
Guideline Action: perform dissection of lymph nodes in the involved area before adjuvant therapy or adjuvant radiotherapy
Source Statement: These trials and recommendations regarding axillary resection do not apply to patients with clinically positive lymph nodes (by palpation or radiologic investigation), and the standard of care is dissection of lymph nodes in that area (axillary, groin, or head and neck) prior to adjuvant therapy or adjuvant radiotherapy.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has mucosal melanoma with high risk of recurrence
Guideline Action: use nivolumab or pembrolizumab, or targeted therapy if an actionable mutation is identified, as adjuvant therapy
Source Statement: Immune checkpoint inhibitors (nivolumab or pembrolizumab) or targeted therapy (in patients with identified mutations) are recommended for adjuvant therapy of mucosal melanoma with high risk of recurrence.
proposed_actions_text:
- Start adjuvant nivolumab or pembrolizumab as if this were fully resected stage III disease.
- Skip additional nodal management and proceed directly to postoperative systemic therapy.
- Order molecular testing only after choosing adjuvant treatment.

patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man underwent wide local excision and sentinel node biopsy for a 2.1-mm ulcerated cutaneous melanoma on the upper back. Final pathology shows complete resection with two involved regional lymph nodes, placing him at high risk of recurrence. He is recovering well and asks about “older immune shots” he read about online. BRAF V600E/V600K testing was sent on the primary tumor last week and returned negative. He has no clinical evidence of persistent nodal disease on exam or imaging. At today’s visit, he is seen to discuss adjuvant treatment options after surgery, including whether he can start one of the newer immunotherapies.
proposed_actions: Recommend ipilimumab because the patient is high risk after resection.
Offer interferon alpha or an older chemotherapy-based adjuvant regimen.
Suggest vaccines or bevacizumab as adjuvant therapy because mutation testing is negative.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient with completely resected cutaneous melanoma at high risk of recurrence
Guideline Action: do not use ipilimumab as adjuvant therapy
Source Statement: Ipilimumab is not recommended as adjuvant therapy for patients with completely resected cutaneous melanoma with high risk of recurrence.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient with completely resected cutaneous melanoma outside a clinical trial
Guideline Action: do not use interferon alpha for adjuvant treatment
Source Statement: Use of interferon alpha (IFN-α) for adjuvant treatment of cutaneous melanoma outside of a clinical trial is no longer recommended.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient with completely resected cutaneous melanoma
Guideline Action: do not use chemotherapy regimens, vaccines, levamisole, bevacizumab, Bacillus Calmette-Guerin, or isolated limb perfusion as adjuvant treatment except in a clinical trial
Source Statement: Chemotherapy regimens, vaccines, levamisole, bevacizumab, Bacillus Calmette-Guerin, and isolated limb perfusion are not recommended for adjuvant treatment of cutaneous melanoma except as part of a clinical trial.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: adult patient with completely resected cutaneous melanoma at high risk of recurrence and no BRAF V600E or V600K mutation
Guideline Action: use nivolumab or pembrolizumab as adjuvant therapy
Source Statement: Nivolumab or pembrolizumab is recommended as adjuvant therapy for patients with completely resected cutaneous melanoma without BRAF V600E or V600K mutations with high risk of recurrence (stage IIIA to IIID, IV).

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: adult patient with completely resected cutaneous melanoma at high risk of recurrence and BRAF V600E or V600K mutation
Guideline Action: use nivolumab, pembrolizumab, or dabrafenib plus trametinib as adjuvant therapy
Source Statement: Nivolumab, pembrolizumab, or dabrafenib plus trametinib is recommended as adjuvant therapy for patients with completely resected cutaneous melanoma with BRAF V600E or V600K mutations and high risk of recurrence (stage IIIA to IIID, IV).

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: high-risk melanoma patient being considered for adjuvant treatment
Guideline Action: perform molecular testing to characterize mutations to guide treatment decisions
Source Statement: Molecular testing of high-risk melanoma patients to characterize mutations should be conducted to help guide appropriate treatment decisions.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient with clinically positive lymph nodes before adjuvant therapy or adjuvant radiotherapy
Guideline Action: perform dissection of lymph nodes in the involved area before adjuvant therapy or adjuvant radiotherapy
Source Statement: These trials and recommendations regarding axillary resection do not apply to patients with clinically positive lymph nodes (by palpation or radiologic investigation), and the standard of care is dissection of lymph nodes in that area (axillary, groin, or head and neck) prior to adjuvant therapy or adjuvant radiotherapy.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has mucosal melanoma with high risk of recurrence
Guideline Action: use nivolumab or pembrolizumab, or targeted therapy if an actionable mutation is identified, as adjuvant therapy
Source Statement: Immune checkpoint inhibitors (nivolumab or pembrolizumab) or targeted therapy (in patients with identified mutations) are recommended for adjuvant therapy of mucosal melanoma with high risk of recurrence.
proposed_actions_text:
- Recommend ipilimumab because the patient is high risk after resection.
- Offer interferon alpha or an older chemotherapy-based adjuvant regimen.
- Suggest vaccines or bevacizumab as adjuvant therapy because mutation testing is negative.

patient_vignette: A 61-year-old man with biopsy-proven gastric gastrointestinal stromal tumor underwent complete surgical resection with negative margins after presenting with melena and iron-deficiency anemia. Final pathology confirmed a low-to-intermediate risk GIST, and postoperative recovery has been uncomplicated. At his oncology follow-up, he asks whether he should start imatinib to reduce the chance of recurrence. However, the pathology report also notes a tiny 6-mm peritoneal implant sampled during surgery that was positive for metastatic GIST, though the primary gastric mass itself was fully resected. Postoperative imaging shows no measurable residual disease, and he has not started systemic therapy yet.
proposed_actions: Start imatinib as adjuvant therapy after resection
Treat this as a standard low-risk post-op GIST and withhold systemic therapy
Document complete resection and assume recurrence prevention is the only goal
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has undergone initial complete resection of GIST
Guideline Action: Do not use imatinib as adjuvant therapy
Source Statement: At present, there is insufficient evidence to support the use of imatinib as an adjuvant therapy in patients who have undergone initial complete resection of disease.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: considering neoadjuvant treatment with imatinib
Guideline Action: Do not use neoadjuvant imatinib
Source Statement: At present, the use of neoadjuvant imatinib is not recommended.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: adult patient with unresectable or metastatic GIST expressing KIT (CD117+)
Guideline Action: Use imatinib as therapy
Source Statement: In patients with KIT-expressing (CD117+) unresectable or metastatic GIST, treatment with imatinib is a recommended therapy.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: starting imatinib for KIT-expressing (CD117+) unresectable or metastatic GIST
Guideline Action: Prescribe imatinib 400 mg daily as the initial dose
Source Statement: Until additional data become available, the initial dose of imatinib should be prescribed at a dose of 400 mg daily.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient with stable disease on imatinib
Guideline Action: Continue imatinib until disease progression
Source Statement: Patients with stable disease should be treated until disease progression.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient develops serious toxicity on imatinib
Guideline Action: Discontinue imatinib
Source Statement: Treatment should be discontinued if serious toxicity develops.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient achieves complete clinical response and radiologic remission with imatinib
Guideline Action: Continue imatinib indefinitely until further data define optimal duration
Source Statement: For patients who achieve a complete clinical response and radiologic remission with imatinib, treatment should be continued indefinitely until further data is available regarding the optimum duration of therapy in such patients.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: adult patient with unresectable or metastatic GIST expressing KIT (CD117+)
Guideline Action: Apply these recommendations to this population
Source Statement: These recommendations apply to adult patients with unresectable or metastatic GIST expressing KIT (CD117+).
proposed_actions_text:
- Start imatinib as adjuvant therapy after resection
- Treat this as a standard low-risk post-op GIST and withhold systemic therapy
- Document complete resection and assume recurrence prevention is the only goal

patient_vignette: A 61-year-old man with biopsy-proven gastric gastrointestinal stromal tumor underwent complete surgical resection with negative margins after presenting with melena and iron-deficiency anemia. Final pathology confirmed a low-to-intermediate risk GIST, and postoperative recovery has been uncomplicated. At his oncology follow-up, he asks whether he should start imatinib to reduce the chance of recurrence. He has no evidence of residual disease on imaging, but the operative note documents that the tumor capsule was ruptured intraoperatively before the specimen was removed. He feels well and has not started any systemic therapy yet.
proposed_actions: Recommend adjuvant imatinib exactly as for a routine resected GIST
Assure the patient that negative margins mean recurrence risk is handled
Ignore the rupture because postoperative imaging is clear

- Assure the patient that negative margins mean recurrence risk is handled

patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man with primary systemic AL amyloidosis returns after 5 months of bortezomib-based therapy with a partial hematologic response. He has persistent nephrotic-range proteinuria and mild peripheral edema, but no symptomatic heart failure. Echocardiography shows preserved ejection fraction with mild concentric thickening, and his creatinine is stable at 1.1 mg/dL. He remains active, works full-time, and wants the most aggressive treatment that offers the best chance of long-term disease control. His hematologist discusses stem cell transplantation as a next step. However, he is currently being evaluated for an unrelated family history of cirrhosis and is found to have probable hereditary hemochromatosis with elevated ferritin and abnormal liver iron studies, prompting concern about additional transplant risk. The team still reviews his organ involvement, overall functional status, comorbidities, and treatment goals before deciding whether he is an appropriate transplant candidate.
proposed_actions: Proceed with autologous stem cell transplantation because he remains fit and wants aggressive therapy.
Treat the transplant discussion as guideline-concordant without further concern.
Assume his preserved EF and stable creatinine make him an appropriate SCT candidate.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with primary systemic AL amyloidosis being considered for allogeneic SCT
Guideline Action: Do not use allogeneic stem cell transplantation for AL amyloidosis
Source Statement: There is no evidence supporting the use of allogeneic SCT for patients with AL.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult patient with primary systemic AL amyloidosis being considered for treatment including bone marrow or stem cell transplantation
Guideline Action: Consider high-dose chemotherapy with autologous stem cell transplantation as an option for selected patients, preferably in an investigative setting
Source Statement: High-dose chemotherapy (CT) and autologous SCT is an option for selected patients with primary systemic amyloidosis, preferably within an investigative setting.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult patient with primary systemic AL amyloidosis being considered for autologous SCT
Guideline Action: Use autologous SCT only in selected patients after weighing performance status, comorbidities, patient preferences, and treatment goals
Source Statement: In consideration of the lack of curative treatment options and the limitations of the evidence reviewed, the Expert Panel believes that offering ASCT may be a reasonable option for some patients, depending on performance status, co-morbidities, patient preferences, and ultimate treatment goals.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult patient with primary systemic AL amyloidosis being considered for SCT
Guideline Action: Base the transplant decision on disease-related characteristics, comorbidities, and patient preferences
Source Statement: The patient selection process and the ultimate decision to perform an SCT should take into account not only disease-related characteristics, but also comorbidities and patient preferences.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult patient with primary systemic AL amyloidosis being considered for SCT
Guideline Action: Consider careful patient selection based on degree of light chain amyloidosis involvement and organ function to reduce transplant-related mortality
Source Statement: Careful patient selection based on degree of light chain amyloidosis involvement and organ function is an emerging concept in amyloidosis that should be considered to reduce transplant-related mortality.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult patient with primary systemic AL amyloidosis being considered for SCT
Guideline Action: Recognize that transplantation in amyloidosis is an evolving area and that first relapse transplantation may be considered as an emerging area pending new evidence
Source Statement: Transplantation in amyloidosis is an evolving area. New emerging areas include consideration of transplantation in first relapse and the impact of novel proteasome inhibitors on outcomes.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 50
Recommendation Class: NO_RECOMMENDATION
Activation Condition: Adult patient with primary systemic AL amyloidosis being considered for SCT
Guideline Action: No recommendation can be made on how newer agents affect the need for SCT
Source Statement: Newer agents are being investigated in the treatment of AL amyloidosis. At this time it is not known how they may impact the need for SCT.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 50
Recommendation Class: NO_RECOMMENDATION
Activation Condition: Adult patient with primary systemic AL amyloidosis being considered for SCT
Guideline Action: No recommendation can be made on the impact of novel proteasome inhibitors on transplantation outcomes
Source Statement: Transplantation in amyloidosis is an evolving area. New emerging areas include consideration of transplantation in first relapse and the impact of novel proteasome inhibitors on outcomes.
proposed_actions_text:
- Proceed with autologous stem cell transplantation because he remains fit and wants aggressive therapy.
- Treat the transplant discussion as guideline-concordant without further concern.
- Assume his preserved EF and stable creatinine make him an appropriate SCT candidate.

patient_vignette: A 62-year-old woman with primary systemic AL amyloidosis has completed 6 months of daratumumab- and bortezomib-based therapy with a partial hematologic response. She has improved edema but still has albuminuria, and she reports fatigue when climbing stairs. Echocardiography shows preserved ejection fraction with mild wall thickening, and her creatinine remains 0.9 mg/dL. She is interested in the next step and asks whether stem cell transplantation could offer the best chance of durable disease control. The transplant team begins review because she appears to have acceptable organ function. On closer assessment, however, she underwent a frailty evaluation and is unable to complete the 6-minute walk without stopping twice; her ECOG performance status is 3 due to progressive neuropathy and deconditioning after therapy. The clinicians discuss supportive options and further stabilization rather than moving directly to transplant.
proposed_actions: Recommend autologous SCT based on preserved ejection fraction and stable kidney function.
Proceed to transplant referral because she has already had initial therapy and wants aggressive treatment.
Ignore the frailty findings because the organ involvement seems limited.

- Recommend autologous SCT based on preserved ejection fraction and stable kidney function.
- Proceed to transplant referral because she has already had initial therapy and wants aggressive treatment.
- Ignore the frailty findings because the organ involvement seems limited.

patient_vignette: A 61-year-old man returns with progressive hoarseness 10 months after definitive radiation therapy for a T1 glottic squamous cell carcinoma of the left true vocal fold. Follow-up laryngoscopy shows a small recurrent lesion involving the posterior third of the glottis, and biopsy confirms recurrent squamous cell carcinoma. Imaging does not show nodal or distant spread, and he asks whether a second course of radiation could still be used to control the recurrence while preserving his larynx. He is otherwise healthy and strongly prefers nonoperative treatment if possible.
proposed_actions: Recommend re-irradiation as salvage because the recurrence is small and localized.
Proceed with a second definitive radiation course to preserve the larynx.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: RECURRENCE_OR_RESTAGING
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with early (T1) glottic cancer and recurrence after prior radiation therapy
Guideline Action: Do not use re-irradiation
Source Statement: However, re-irradiation is not an option in cases of recurrence.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with previously untreated early (T1) glottic cancer
Guideline Action: Offer endolaryngeal surgery, with or without laser, or radiation therapy as equally effective treatment options
Source Statement: For patients with early (T1) glottic cancer, recommended treatment options include the equally effective endolaryngeal surgery, with or without laser, or radiation therapy.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult patient with previously untreated early (T1) glottic cancer
Guideline Action: Choose between surgery and radiation based on patient and clinician preferences and general medical condition
Source Statement: The choice between treatment modalities should be based on patient and clinician preferences and general medical condition.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult patient with early (T1) glottic cancer and anterior commissure involvement
Guideline Action: Consider radiation therapy over surgery
Source Statement: Anterior commissure involvement may be a factor that favours a recommendation of radiotherapy over surgery due to a common opinion that voice outcomes are particularly affected.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult patient with early (T1) glottic cancer and tumor localized to the midportion of the vocal fold with uncompromised endoscopic accessibility
Guideline Action: Consider endolaryngeal surgery as an ideal candidate treatment
Source Statement: Tumours localized to the midportion of the vocal fold, and where endoscopic accessibility is uncompromised, may be considered ideal candidates for surgery.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult patient with early (T1) glottic cancer who cannot tolerate a general anaesthetic
Guideline Action: Prefer radiation therapy over surgery
Source Statement: Other important practical considerations include the ability for patients to tolerate a general anaesthetic, which is required for surgery.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult patient with early (T1) glottic cancer who cannot cooperate with daily treatment for four to six weeks
Guideline Action: Avoid radiation therapy and consider surgery if otherwise appropriate
Source Statement: In contrast, radiotherapy requires patient cooperation for daily treatment for four to six weeks.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult patient with early (T1) glottic cancer and local recurrence after surgery
Guideline Action: Consider partial laryngeal surgery, including revision endoscopic surgery
Source Statement: Partial laryngeal surgery, including revision endoscopic surgery, is possible for local recurrence following surgery.
proposed_actions_text:
- Recommend re-irradiation as salvage because the recurrence is small and localized.
- Proceed with a second definitive radiation course to preserve the larynx.

patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man presents with persistent hoarseness 8 months after completing definitive radiation therapy for a T1 glottic squamous cell carcinoma of the right true vocal fold. Repeat laryngoscopy shows a small recurrent lesion on the vocal fold, and biopsy confirms recurrent squamous cell carcinoma. The tumor remains superficial and there is no nodal disease. He asks whether another course of radiation could be used, since surgery would affect his voice. He is anxious to avoid an operation and notes that his first radiation course was hard but manageable.
proposed_actions: Offer re-irradiation because the recurrence is local and still small.
Treat the recurrence with another definitive radiation course to preserve the larynx.

patient_vignette: A 64-year-old man with COPD and a 45-pack-year smoking history is diagnosed with unresectable stage IIIA NSCLC with biopsy-proven N2 mediastinal lymph node involvement. He is being treated with curative intent and has started concurrent chemoradiation. The radiation oncology plan specifies 70 Gy in 35 once-daily fractions over seven weeks with weekly on-treatment review. A medical oncologist asks whether the dose should be escalated to improve local control, but the team is otherwise proceeding with standard concurrent therapy. The patient has not yet completed chemoradiation and has no current contraindications to future immunotherapy.
proposed_actions: Continue the planned concurrent chemoradiation without questioning the radiation dose.
Approve dose escalation to 70 Gy for better local control.
Assume the regimen remains guideline-concordant because it is still once-daily conventional fractionation.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient is receiving conventional fractionation radiotherapy for unresectable stage III NSCLC
Guideline Action: do not escalate dose beyond 66 Gy
Source Statement: Dose escalation beyond 66 Gy with conventional fractionation is not recommended.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient is receiving radiotherapy for unresectable stage III NSCLC
Guideline Action: do not use hyperfractionated radiotherapy regimens that do not accelerate the treatment course
Source Statement: Hyperformated radiotherapy regimens that do not result in acceleration of the treatment course, even though the total nominal radiotherapy dose may be modestly increased, are not recommended.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient with unresectable stage III NSCLC is being considered for induction chemotherapy before concurrent chemoradiotherapy
Guideline Action: do not routinely give induction chemotherapy before concurrent chemoradiotherapy
Source Statement: Routine use of induction chemotherapy prior to concurrent chemoradiotherapy is not recommended;

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient is being considered for durvalumab after chemoradiation
Guideline Action: do not exclude durvalumab solely because of EGFR or PD-L1 biomarker status
Source Statement: At this time, it is inappropriate to exclude patients from therapy based solely on a biomarker such as epidermal growth factor receptor or programmed death-ligand 1 status.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has unresectable, lymph node-positive stage III NSCLC (N2 or N3) and curative-intent treatment is planned
Guideline Action: use concurrent chemoradiation
Source Statement: Concurrent chemoradiation should be used for curative-intent treatment of patients with unresectable, lymph node-positive (N2 or N3) stage III NSCLC.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient is receiving concurrent chemoradiation for unresectable stage III NSCLC
Guideline Action: use standard radiation dose fractionation of 60 to 66 Gy in 2 Gy once-daily fractions over six weeks
Source Statement: A standard dose fractionation of 60 to 66 Gy given in fractions of 2 Gy once per day over six weeks is recommended.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has stage III NSCLC and has not progressed after chemoradiation and has no contraindications to an immune checkpoint inhibitor
Guideline Action: use durvalumab
Source Statement: Durvalumab should be used in patients with stage III NSCLC who have not progressed postchemoradiation and have no contraindications to an immune checkpoint inhibitor.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient is having chemoradiotherapy and surgery for stage IIIA/B-N2 NSCLC
Guideline Action: schedule surgery three to five weeks after chemoradiotherapy
Source Statement: For patients with stage IIIA/B-N2 NSCLC who are having chemoradiotherapy and surgery, ensure that their surgery is scheduled for three to five weeks after the chemoradiotherapy.
proposed_actions_text:
- Continue the planned concurrent chemoradiation without questioning the radiation dose.
- Approve dose escalation to 70 Gy for better local control.
- Assume the regimen remains guideline-concordant because it is still once-daily conventional fractionation.

patient_vignette: A 71-year-old woman with well-controlled COPD is diagnosed with unresectable stage IIIB NSCLC involving bulky mediastinal and supraclavicular nodes. She completes concurrent chemoradiation with 60 Gy in 30 once-daily fractions and recovers without significant toxicity. Restaging CT shows no progression, and she is being considered for consolidation therapy. Her oncologist notes that tumor testing from the diagnostic biopsy shows an EGFR exon 19 deletion and PD-L1 expression of 80%, and wonders whether those biomarker results should determine whether she receives immunotherapy. She has no autoimmune disease and no other contraindications to checkpoint blockade.
proposed_actions: Exclude durvalumab because the tumor is EGFR-mutated and PD-L1 positive.
Delay consolidation therapy while focusing on biomarker-directed options.
Assume she is not eligible for durvalumab based solely on molecular testing.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient is receiving conventional fractionation radiotherapy for unresectable stage III NSCLC
Guideline Action: do not escalate dose beyond 66 Gy
Source Statement: Dose escalation beyond 66 Gy with conventional fractionation is not recommended.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient is receiving radiotherapy for unresectable stage III NSCLC
Guideline Action: do not use hyperfractionated radiotherapy regimens that do not accelerate the treatment course
Source Statement: Hyperformated radiotherapy regimens that do not result in acceleration of the treatment course, even though the total nominal radiotherapy dose may be modestly increased, are not recommended.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient with unresectable stage III NSCLC is being considered for induction chemotherapy before concurrent chemoradiotherapy
Guideline Action: do not routinely give induction chemotherapy before concurrent chemoradiotherapy
Source Statement: Routine use of induction chemotherapy prior to concurrent chemoradiotherapy is not recommended;

- Exclude durvalumab because the tumor is EGFR-mutated and PD-L1 positive.

patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman with stage II, node-positive breast cancer returns after surgery to finalize adjuvant therapy. Her tumor is ER-positive, PR-positive, and HER2-negative by IHC 2+ with a negative ISH ratio. She is postmenopausal, has no cardiac history, and is otherwise healthy. The oncology team is discussing standard anthracycline-taxane chemotherapy, but one note suggests adding capecitabine because of the nodal involvement and another proposes adjuvant trastuzumab based on the borderline IHC result. A colleague also mentions ordering Ki-67 and intrinsic molecular subtype testing to better estimate recurrence risk before choosing intensity of treatment. The patient wants the most evidence-based plan and is trying to avoid extra toxicity.
proposed_actions: Add capecitabine to the anthracycline-taxane adjuvant regimen
Start adjuvant trastuzumab because the tumor is IHC 2+
Use Ki-67 to refine recurrence risk for routine decision making
Use intrinsic subtype testing to select adjuvant treatment intensity
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: considering adding gemcitabine or capecitabine to an anthracycline-taxane regimen for adjuvant chemotherapy
Guideline Action: do not add gemcitabine or capecitabine to the anthracycline-taxane regimen
Source Statement: The addition of gemcitabine or capecitabine to an anthracycline-taxane regimen is not recommended for adjuvant chemotherapy.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient is older than 65 years and capecitabine is being considered instead of adjuvant AC or CMF
Guideline Action: do not use capecitabine as the adjuvant chemotherapy option in lieu of AC or CMF
Source Statement: In patients older than 65 years, capecitabine is not recommended as an adjuvant chemotherapy option in lieu of adjuvant AC or CMF (oral cyclophosphamide).

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has ER-positive cancer and is premenopausal and considering ovarian ablation or suppression plus tamoxifen
Guideline Action: do not treat ovarian ablation or suppression plus tamoxifen as standard of care
Source Statement: In premenopausal patients with ER+ tumours (treated with or without chemotherapy) the addition of ovarian ablation or suppression to tamoxifen is not the standard of care.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has ER-positive cancer and is premenopausal and considering ovarian ablation or suppression plus 5 years of an aromatase inhibitor
Guideline Action: do not use ovarian ablation or suppression plus 5 years of an aromatase inhibitor as standard of care
Source Statement: In premenopausal patients with ER+ tumours, treated with or without chemotherapy, ovarian ablation or suppression plus five years of an AI is not the standard of care.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient does not have HER2-positive breast cancer
Guideline Action: do not offer adjuvant trastuzumab
Source Statement: Only patients with HER2+ breast cancer (IHC 3+, ISH ratio ≥2, or 6+ HER2 gene copies per cell nucleus) should be offered adjuvant trastuzumab.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient is receiving trastuzumab and has any of the following: LVEF under 55%, documented congestive heart failure, high-risk uncontrolled arrhythmias, angina requiring medication, clinically significant valvular disease, transmural infarction on ECG, or poorly controlled hypertension
Guideline Action: do not offer trastuzumab
Source Statement: trastuzumab should not be offered to patients with any of the following: a left ventricular ejection fraction LVEF of <55%; a history of documented congestive heart failure; high-risk uncontrolled arrhythmias; angina pectoris requiring medication; clinically significant valvular disease; evidence of transmural infarction on electrocardiograph (ECG); poorly controlled hypertension.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: considering Ki-67 for routine risk stratification in early-stage breast cancer
Guideline Action: do not use Ki-67 as a standard parameter outside clinical trials
Source Statement: It is premature to recommend its use as a standard parameter for patient risk stratification, although it may be evaluated in clinical trials.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: considering intrinsic breast cancer subtypes for clinical decision making outside a trial
Guideline Action: do not use intrinsic subtypes outside a clinical trial
Source Statement: At this point, the use of these subtypes in clinical decision making outside of a clinical trial is not recommended.
proposed_actions_text:
- Add capecitabine to the anthracycline-taxane adjuvant regimen
- Start adjuvant trastuzumab because the tumor is IHC 2+
- Use Ki-67 to refine recurrence risk for routine decision making
- Use intrinsic subtype testing to select adjuvant treatment intensity

patient_vignette: A 61-year-old woman with stage II, node-positive breast cancer is seen after lumpectomy to discuss adjuvant therapy. Her tumor is ER-positive, PR-positive, and HER2-negative by IHC 0. She is postmenopausal with no known cardiac disease and normal baseline blood pressure. Her oncologist recommends anthracycline-taxane chemotherapy, but a second opinion suggests adding capecitabine because of the nodal burden and adding trastuzumab as a precaution despite the negative HER2 result. The patient also asks whether a Ki-67 score and intrinsic molecular subtype report she found in her chart should influence how aggressive the treatment should be.
proposed_actions: Escalate to capecitabine-containing adjuvant chemotherapy
Offer trastuzumab despite HER2 IHC 0 status
Base recurrence-risk counseling on Ki-67 as a standard measure
Use intrinsic subtype testing to justify intensifying treatment

patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man is diagnosed with stage III squamous cell carcinoma of the oropharynx after biopsy of a persistent throat lesion and CT imaging showing a primary tumor with suspicious cervical nodes. He is referred to a multidisciplinary head and neck cancer clinic, but because the center does not have a dedicated speech-language pathologist or dietitian available that week, the initial plan is deferred to the surgeon and radiation oncologist alone. His family physician is updated by letter after the consultation, but the oncology team tells the patient that his primary care doctor should hold off on supportive follow-up until all cancer therapy is complete. The surgeon also notes that if surgery is needed later, reconstruction can be arranged at a separate referral center if a complication arises.
proposed_actions: Proceed as though the required experienced core team has already coordinated assessment, planning, and rehabilitation.
Assume the family physician should not be involved in ongoing overall health or post-treatment supportive care.
Treat the letter to primary care as sufficient despite the lack of active PCP responsibility in the plan.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patients with head and neck cancer
Guideline Action: coordinate care among an experienced core team responsible for assessment, treatment, planning, management, survivorship, and rehabilitation
Source Statement: The care of patients with head and neck cancer should be coordinated among members of an experienced Core Team, comprised of a group of physicians and allied healthcare providers who will be responsible for the assessment, treatment, planning, management, survivorship, and rehabilitation of the patient.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patients with head and neck cancer
Guideline Action: ensure the primary care provider is responsible for ongoing overall health and post-treatment supportive care
Source Statement: The Primary Care Provider will be responsible for the ongoing overall health of the patient and will offer supportive care after treatment.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patients with head and neck cancer
Guideline Action: involve an extended team as needed to facilitate treatment, planning, management, survivorship, and rehabilitation
Source Statement: The Extended Team will be called upon by the core team to facilitate treatment, planning, management, survivorship, and rehabilitation of the patient.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: members of the care team
Guideline Action: ensure team members have training or experience managing patients with head and neck cancers
Source Statement: Members of the Teams must have training or experience managing patients with head and neck cancers.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patients with head and neck cancer
Guideline Action: recognize the primary care physician as not involved in day-to-day treatment but important for post-treatment supportive care and ongoing overall health
Source Statement: The primary care physician is not involved in the day to day treatment of the head and neck cancer patient but plays an important role in post-treatment supportive care and is responsible for the ongoing overall health of the patient.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: surgical management of patients with head and neck tumours
Guideline Action: use reconstruction expertise from a fellowship-trained microvascular surgeon with specific training in head and neck reconstruction
Source Statement: Reconstruction expertise is required for the surgical management of patients with head and neck tumours and necessitates a fellowship-trained microvascular surgeon with specific training in head and neck reconstruction.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: reporting head and neck malignancies
Guideline Action: use CAP-CCO standards for pathology reporting
Source Statement: Pathologists are advised to use the CAP-CCO standards for reporting head and neck malignancies (5).

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: reporting nodal dissections
Guideline Action: include levels and structures in the specimen, number of involved and uninvolved nodes, level of nodes, and presence and location of extracapsular spread
Source Statement: The reporting of nodal dissections should include a description of the levels and structures included in the specimen, including number of involved and uninvolved nodes, level of these nodes, and the presence and location of extracapsular spread of tumour.
proposed_actions_text:
- Proceed as though the required experienced core team has already coordinated assessment, planning, and rehabilitation.
- Assume the family physician should not be involved in ongoing overall health or post-treatment supportive care.
- Treat the letter to primary care as sufficient despite the lack of active PCP responsibility in the plan.

patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man is diagnosed with stage III squamous cell carcinoma of the oropharynx after biopsy of a persistent throat lesion and CT imaging showing a primary tumor with suspicious cervical nodes. He is referred to a multidisciplinary head and neck cancer clinic where the medical oncologist, radiation oncologist, head and neck surgeon, speech-language pathologist, dietitian, and social worker review his case together. The family physician is informed of the diagnosis and will continue routine medical care and post-treatment support. However, because the patient is leaning toward organ-preserving chemoradiation and has no current plan for surgery, the surgeon notes that reconstruction expertise is not being arranged at this time and would only be considered if salvage surgery becomes necessary later.
proposed_actions: Automatically request a microvascular reconstruction surgeon as part of the initial management plan.
Document that reconstruction expertise is already required despite the absence of planned surgery.
Treat the case as if surgical reconstruction planning should proceed immediately.
