patient_vignette: A 59-year-old man with a recently diagnosed mid-rectal adenocarcinoma returns to discuss possible neoadjuvant treatment. He has mild intermittent rectal bleeding but no bowel obstruction. On digital rectal exam, the tumor is palpable on the posterior rectal wall, about 7 cm from the anal verge, with reduced mobility. A pelvic MRI has now been completed and suggests a T3 lesion with suspicious mesorectal nodes, but the scan also notes that the distal edge of the tumor is 12 cm from the anal verge, placing it in the upper rectum. The oncology team is considering preoperative chemoradiation because the lesion may be locally advanced. The patient asks whether the tumor is definitely stage III, and the team explains that preoperative staging can still be imperfect and the final extent may differ from initial tests.
proposed_actions: Proceed with preoperative chemoradiation as if this were a typical mid-rectal stage III tumor.
Assume staging is adequate and move directly to neoadjuvant therapy planning without reconsidering tumor location.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: PREOP_STAGING
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Before preoperative therapy is planned
Guideline Action: Perform adequate preoperative staging investigations including transrectal ultrasound and/or MRI for T category, MRI for N category, digital rectal exam, CT or MRI to assess mesorectal margin, CT or MRI of the abdomen for metastatic or stage IV disease, and chest x-ray for pulmonary imaging.
Source Statement: Recommendations for preoperative therapy presuppose adequate preoperative staging investigations, including transrectal ultrasound and/or magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) with surface or endorectal coil to assess the T category, MRI with surface or endorectal coil to assess the N category, a good digital rectal exam, computerized axial tomography (CAT) scan or MRI to assess the mesorectal margin, CAT scan or MRI of the abdomen to assess for potential metastatic or stage IV disease, and chest x-ray for pulmonary imaging.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: PREOP_STAGING
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: When preoperative staging may be inaccurate
Guideline Action: Discuss potential inaccuracies of preoperative testing on tumor staging with the patient to support informed decision-making.
Source Statement: Potential inaccuracies of preoperative testing on tumour staging should be discussed with patients to allow them to make informed decisions (1).

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: PREOP_STAGING
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: When planning rectal surgery after preoperative therapy
Guideline Action: Use total mesorectal excision (TME) principles for the eventual rectal surgery.
Source Statement: The eventual rectal surgery is expected to include total mesorectal excision (TME) principles.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: PREOP_STAGING
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: When considering preoperative treatment benefits
Guideline Action: Recognize that the quality of surgery strongly influences the potential benefits of preoperative treatments.
Source Statement: The quality of surgery greatly influences the potential benefits of preoperative treatments.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: PREOP_STAGING
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: When evaluating preoperative treatment evidence
Guideline Action: Account for the fact that many trials did not use currently recommended surgical standards, including TME.
Source Statement: A substantial number of trials included in the evidentiary base did not use currently recommended standards of surgery, including TME.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: PREOP_STAGING
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: When preoperative therapy is being considered
Guideline Action: Ensure preoperative therapy recommendations are applied only after adequate staging investigations have been completed.
Source Statement: Recommendations for preoperative therapy presuppose adequate preoperative staging investigations...

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: PREOP_STAGING
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: When discussing preoperative therapy with the patient
Guideline Action: Discuss the potential inaccuracies of staging tests so the patient can make an informed choice.
Source Statement: Potential inaccuracies of preoperative testing on tumour staging should be discussed with patients to allow them to make informed decisions (1).

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: PREOP_STAGING
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: When planning surgery after preoperative RT
Guideline Action: Plan surgery with TME principles in mind.
Source Statement: The eventual rectal surgery is expected to include total mesorectal excision (TME) principles.
proposed_actions_text:
- Proceed with preoperative chemoradiation as if this were a typical mid-rectal stage III tumor.
- Assume staging is adequate and move directly to neoadjuvant therapy planning without reconsidering tumor location.

patient_vignette: A 59-year-old man with a recently diagnosed mid-rectal adenocarcinoma is being evaluated for possible neoadjuvant treatment. He has mild intermittent rectal bleeding but no bowel obstruction. On digital rectal exam, the tumor is palpable on the posterior rectal wall, about 7 cm from the anal verge, with reduced mobility. The oncology team is considering preoperative chemoradiation because the lesion may be locally advanced. A pelvic MRI and chest imaging have already been obtained, but the abdomen has only been assessed by noncontrast CT that was done for renal colic, and it did not adequately evaluate the liver or peritoneum. The patient asks whether the tumor is definitely stage III, and the team explains that preoperative staging can be imperfect and the final extent may differ from initial tests.
proposed_actions: Start neoadjuvant chemoradiation based on the pelvic MRI alone.
Treat the patient as nonmetastatic without obtaining dedicated abdominal staging.
Tell the patient the staging workup is sufficiently complete to proceed.

- Treat the patient as nonmetastatic without obtaining dedicated abdominal staging.
- Tell the patient the staging workup is sufficiently complete to proceed.

patient_vignette: A 52-year-old man in Ontario presents with a newly diagnosed, biopsy-proven undifferentiated nasopharyngeal carcinoma. Staging MRI and PET-CT show a stage II locally advanced primary tumor with bilateral cervical nodal involvement but no distant metastases. He has good performance status and normal renal and hepatic function, and is otherwise chemotherapy-eligible. The oncology team discusses definitive treatment options and considers adding systemic therapy, with coordination between medical oncology and radiation oncology because radiation remains central to management. The patient asks about a treatment plan similar to what was discussed for more advanced disease.
proposed_actions: Proceed with a chemoradiotherapy-based management plan as if this were newly diagnosed stage III/IV disease.
Recommend induction chemotherapy followed by definitive chemoradiotherapy without rechecking whether the stage meets the guideline threshold.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: newly diagnosed, chemotherapy-eligible, locally advanced squamous cell or undifferentiated nasopharyngeal cancer (stage III or IV)
Guideline Action: Use chemoradiotherapy-based management with consideration of adding neoadjuvant, concurrent, or adjuvant chemotherapy as addressed in the guideline
Source Statement: The objectives of the guideline are to make recommendations regarding the addition of neoadjuvant/concurrent/adjuvant chemotherapy to chemoradiotherapy in the management of locally advanced squamous cell or undifferentiated nasopharyngeal cancer (NPC).

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patients with NPC receiving induction chemotherapy
Guideline Action: Administer induction chemotherapy every 3 weeks for 3 cycles; at minimum give 2 cycles
Source Statement: For patients with NPC receiving induction chemotherapy, the regimens should be administered every three weeks for a total of three cycles, or at the minimum two cycles.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patients with NPC receiving induction chemotherapy
Guideline Action: Start chemoradiotherapy within 21 to 28 days from the first day of the last induction chemotherapy cycle
Source Statement: For patients with NPC receiving induction chemotherapy, chemoradiotherapy should be commenced within 21-28 days from the first day of the last cycle of induction chemotherapy.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: all patients with NPC receiving adjuvant chemotherapy
Guideline Action: Offer PF every 4 weeks for a total of 3 cycles
Source Statement: For all patients with NPC receiving adjuvant chemotherapy, PF ... administered every four weeks for a total of three cycles should be offered.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patients with locally advanced NPC treated in Ontario context
Guideline Action: Apply the endorsed CSCO/ASCO recommendations as modified by Ontario Health endorsement
Source Statement: The Chemotherapy and Radiotherapy for Nasopharyngeal Cancer Development Group (GDG) of Ontario Health (Cancer Care Ontario) endorses the Chinese Society of Clinical Oncology (CSCO)/American Society of Clinical Oncology (ASCO) recommendations ... modified by the endorsement process described in this document.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patients with NPC receiving induction chemotherapy
Guideline Action: Use a 3-week cycle interval for induction chemotherapy
Source Statement: For patients with NPC receiving induction chemotherapy, the regimens should be administered every three weeks for a total of three cycles, or at the minimum two cycles.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patients with NPC receiving adjuvant chemotherapy
Guideline Action: Use a 4-week cycle interval for PF adjuvant chemotherapy
Source Statement: For all patients with NPC receiving adjuvant chemotherapy, PF ... administered every four weeks for a total of three cycles should be offered.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patients with NPC receiving induction chemotherapy followed by chemoradiotherapy
Guideline Action: Do not delay chemoradiotherapy beyond 28 days after the first day of the last induction cycle
Source Statement: For patients with NPC receiving induction chemotherapy, chemoradiotherapy should be commenced within 21-28 days from the first day of the last cycle of induction chemotherapy.
proposed_actions_text:
- Proceed with a chemoradiotherapy-based management plan as if this were newly diagnosed stage III/IV disease.
- Recommend induction chemotherapy followed by definitive chemoradiotherapy without rechecking whether the stage meets the guideline threshold.

patient_vignette: A 52-year-old man in Ontario presents with a newly diagnosed, biopsy-proven undifferentiated nasopharyngeal carcinoma. Staging MRI and PET-CT show stage IVB locally advanced disease with bilateral cervical nodal involvement and a small biopsy-confirmed lung metastasis. He has good performance status, normal renal and hepatic function, and is being evaluated for systemic treatment. The team discusses whether combined-modality curative-intent therapy is appropriate, but the presence of metastatic spread changes the discussion from the original localized plan.
proposed_actions: Treat this as locally advanced nonmetastatic disease and start induction chemotherapy followed by definitive chemoradiotherapy.
Assume the standard nasopharyngeal chemoradiotherapy pathway applies unchanged because the primary tumor still looks extensive.

patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man is seen in clinic after his primary care physician requested oncology guidance for a newly diagnosed condition. The only document available for review is a PDF that includes the guideline title page, section headings, author list, and copyright/contact information, but the body of the document is inaccessible because the file was truncated during upload. The patient’s symptoms, exam, and labs are otherwise stable, and the team is asking whether the source document supports any immediate management step before the next visit.
proposed_actions: Treat the document as if it still contains usable recommendations and proceed with a guideline-driven intervention.
Assume the presence of section headings implies the recommendation can be extracted from the source.
Order management based on the presumed guideline content instead of confirming the document is incomplete.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 50
Recommendation Class: NO_RECOMMENDATION
Activation Condition: When the document is used as a guideline reference
Guideline Action: No clinical action can be extracted from the provided text.
Source Statement: The provided text contains only document front matter, section headings, and copyright/contact information, with no clinical recommendations.
proposed_actions_text:
- Treat the document as if it still contains usable recommendations and proceed with a guideline-driven intervention.
- Assume the presence of section headings implies the recommendation can be extracted from the source.
- Order management based on the presumed guideline content instead of confirming the document is incomplete.

patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man presents for oncology guidance after a recent diagnosis, and the chart note specifically asks whether the reference document supports immediate treatment. On review, the available file is a scanned PDF that contains only the title page, table of contents, author affiliations, and publication/copyright information. There are no disease-specific sections, algorithms, or treatment statements in the accessible pages, although the clinician initially assumes the guideline may simply continue on later pages.
proposed_actions: Use the document as if it were a complete guideline and select a treatment pathway.
Infer recommendations from the title and table of contents.
Document that the guideline endorses a specific action despite the absence of clinical text.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL

Activation Condition: When the document is used as a guideline reference

Source Statement: The provided text contains only document front matter, section headings, and copyright/contact information, with no clinical recommendations.
proposed_actions_text:
- Use the document as if it were a complete guideline and select a treatment pathway.
- Infer recommendations from the title and table of contents.
- Document that the guideline endorses a specific action despite the absence of clinical text.

patient_vignette: A 64-year-old woman with a 35-pack-year smoking history underwent right upper lobectomy for a 2.1 cm lung adenocarcinoma. Final pathology showed a completely resected stage IB (pT2aN0) non-small cell lung cancer with clear margins and no nodal involvement on systematic sampling. Postoperative recovery was uncomplicated, and she is now seen in oncology clinic 4 weeks later asking whether she should start chemotherapy to reduce recurrence risk. Her performance status is good, and she has no evidence of residual disease on imaging.
proposed_actions: Recommend routine adjuvant platinum-based chemotherapy based on the prior early-stage lung cancer template.
Document the case as stage IA and advise no further treatment without rechecking the pathology report.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has completely resected stage IA NSCLC
Guideline Action: do not routinely give adjuvant chemotherapy
Source Statement: The 2007 joint CCO/ASCO guideline recommended chemotherapy for stage II and IIIA disease but not stage IA.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has completely resected stage IB to IIIA NSCLC with sensitizing EGFR mutation
Guideline Action: do not use EGFR-TKI routinely as adjuvant therapy
Source Statement: These data were considered insufficient to justify the routine use of EGFR-TKIs in patients with tumors with sensitizing EGFR mutations.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has completely resected stage IB to IIIA NSCLC without EGFR mutation selection
Guideline Action: do not use erlotinib routinely as adjuvant therapy
Source Statement: No significant differences in DFS or OS were detected in the overall unselected study population.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has completely resected stage IB to IIIA NSCLC without EGFR mutation selection
Guideline Action: do not use gefitinib routinely as adjuvant therapy
Source Statement: There was no benefit for either of the subgroups with EGFR wild-type tumors or the 15 tumors (4% of the total sample) with EGFR sensitizing mutations.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has completely resected stage I to IIIA NSCLC
Guideline Action: do not use MAGE-A3 immunotherapy routinely as adjuvant treatment
Source Statement: MAGRIT... found no significant difference in the primary outcome DFS... Because there was no difference between groups, the study was unable to identify a biomarker that would enable selection of patients for this treatment.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has completely resected stage I to IIIA NSCLC
Guideline Action: do not use bevacizumab added to chemotherapy routinely as adjuvant therapy
Source Statement: a phase III RCT... found that the addition of angiogenesis inhibitor bevacizumab to chemotherapy failed to improve DFS or OS for individuals with surgically resected earlystage NSCLCs compared with chemotherapy alone.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has completely resected stage I or II NSCLC
Guideline Action: do not give postoperative radiotherapy (PORT) routinely
Source Statement: there has been no new evidence that would alter the recommendation against PORT in patients with stage I or II disease.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has completely resected stage II or IIIA NSCLC
Guideline Action: offer adjuvant cisplatin-based chemotherapy
Source Statement: Cisplatin-based adjuvant chemotherapy was recommended for routine use in patients with stage II or IIIA disease extent
proposed_actions_text:
- Recommend routine adjuvant platinum-based chemotherapy based on the prior early-stage lung cancer template.
- Document the case as stage IA and advise no further treatment without rechecking the pathology report.

patient_vignette: A 64-year-old woman with a 35-pack-year smoking history underwent right upper lobectomy for a 2.1 cm lung adenocarcinoma. Final pathology showed a completely resected stage IA (pT1bN0) non-small cell lung cancer with clear margins and no nodal involvement on systematic sampling. During postoperative review, the surgeon notes a single indeterminate 8 mm right paratracheal node on the latest CT scan that was not present preoperatively, although the resection margins are negative and the patient feels well. She asks whether she should start chemotherapy to reduce recurrence risk. Her performance status is good.
proposed_actions: Say this is still stage IA and advise against any additional workup or treatment.
Apply the no-adjuvant-chemotherapy recommendation immediately without clarifying the new imaging finding.

Scenario: GENERAL

patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man with a history of cutaneous melanoma on the left calf underwent wide local excision and sentinel lymph node biopsy. Pathology shows a single positive sentinel node with a microscopic metastatic deposit, and staging scans show no distant disease. He asks whether he can avoid completion lymph node dissection (CLND) in favor of observation. The surgical team notes that the nodal basin could be followed with ultrasound, but the patient has moved to a rural area and states he cannot reliably return for the frequent surveillance visits over the next several years. He is otherwise well and wants the least invasive option, but follow-up logistics are uncertain.
proposed_actions: Recommend nodal observation with ultrasonography instead of CLND
Schedule routine ultrasound surveillance every 4 to 6 months despite poor access
Frame CLND as unnecessary because the sentinel node deposit is small
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: positive sentinel node and comparing CLND with observation
Guideline Action: Do not expect a survival benefit from CLND based on MSLT-II.
Source Statement: The non-significant difference in MSS and increase in adverse events of the CLND group indicates that CLND may not be optimal for patients and does not offer a survival benefit.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: positive sentinel node and comparing CLND with observation
Guideline Action: Do not expect benefit from CLND for any subgroup based on MSLT-II.
Source Statement: Although the majority of patients had low volume tumor metastases, sub set analysis did not demonstrate a benefit for any groups of patient receiving CLND.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: positive sentinel node and comparing CLND with observation
Guideline Action: Do not assume CLND improves distant metastasis-free survival, overall survival, or recurrence-free survival based on DeCOG-SLT.
Source Statement: The DeCOG-SLT trial found no difference in distant metastasis-free survival, overall survival, or recurrence-free survival when SLN positive patients who received CLND were compared to patients who were observed.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: positive sentinel node and considering CLND versus observation
Guideline Action: Avoid routine CLND when observation with ultrasonography is feasible because CLND increases adverse events.
Source Statement: Adverse events occurred with more frequency among the CLND patients than the observation group with lymphedema being the most common (24.1% of patients vs. 6.3% at last follow-up, p<0.001).

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient is managed with nodal observation after positive sentinel node
Guideline Action: Monitor the affected nodal basin with ultrasonography and clinical exam every 4 to 6 months for the first 2 years, then every 6 months from years 3 to 5.
Source Statement: Monitoring with ultrasonography of the affected nodal basin and clinical exam will be required, at minimum, every 4 to 6 months for the first 2 years and every 6 months from 3-5 years.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: ultrasound shows any two suspicious nodal features
Guideline Action: Confirm suspected nodal recurrence with biopsy of the basin.
Source Statement: Suspicions of nodal recurrence via ultrasound should be confirmed with a biopsy of the basin.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: patient has sentinel node metastasis
Guideline Action: Consider nodal observation with ultrasonography rather than completion lymph node dissection.
Source Statement: Patients with sentinel node metastasis should be considered for nodal observation with ultrasonography rather than CLND.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: patient has extensive sentinel node metastasis and CLND is the only option for local control
Guideline Action: Consider CLND as the better option than nodal observation with ultrasonography.
Source Statement: patients with extensive sentinel node metastasis in which CLND would be the only option for local control
proposed_actions_text:
- Recommend nodal observation with ultrasonography instead of CLND
- Schedule routine ultrasound surveillance every 4 to 6 months despite poor access
- Frame CLND as unnecessary because the sentinel node deposit is small

patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man with a history of cutaneous melanoma on the left calf underwent wide local excision and sentinel lymph node biopsy. Pathology shows a single positive sentinel node with a small metastatic deposit, and staging scans show no distant disease. He asks whether he should proceed with completion lymph node dissection (CLND) or be observed. The team explains that nodal observation with ultrasound is generally considered, but his ultrasound at baseline already shows two suspicious features in the involved groin basin, including a rounded node with cortical thickening and loss of the fatty hilum. He remains clinically well and prefers to avoid surgery if possible.
proposed_actions: Choose observation with serial ultrasound as if this were uncomplicated sentinel-node-positive disease
Defer tissue confirmation and continue routine follow-up only
Apply the standard 4 to 6 month surveillance schedule without biopsy

patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man with progressive dysphagia is newly diagnosed with mid-esophageal squamous cell carcinoma. Staging is otherwise limited, but PET/CT and endoscopic ultrasound now show T4aN1 disease with invasion into the adjacent pleura and a small-volume nodal burden, without distant metastases. He remains ECOG 1 and is still able to take soft foods. He has no major cardiac or renal comorbidities and declines surgery after discussion. The oncology team considers definitive non-surgical therapy and wonders whether concurrent chemoradiation should still be offered, given that he is otherwise fit and likely to tolerate treatment. The patient specifically asks whether a cisplatin-based regimen would be used if radiation is combined with chemotherapy and what toxicities to expect.
proposed_actions: Proceed with curative-intent concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy as for localized disease.
Select a cisplatin-based concurrent regimen by default.
Reassure the patient that the same guideline recommendation applies because he is ECOG 1 and non-surgical.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: sequential radiotherapy and chemotherapy is being considered
Guideline Action: Do not use sequential radiotherapy and chemotherapy as standard practice
Source Statement: Sequential radiotherapy and chemotherapy should not be recommended as standard practice.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: sequential radiotherapy and chemotherapy is being considered
Guideline Action: Avoid sequential radiotherapy and chemotherapy because no survival or local control benefit was shown and toxicity was increased
Source Statement: There was no evidence to suggest any survival or local control benefit using sequential RTCT compared with RT alone. Given these results and the increased adverse effects associated with sequential RTCT, this approach should not be recommended in standard practice.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: adult patient with localized esophageal carcinoma, good performance status, considering non-surgical curative treatment, and combined radiotherapy and chemotherapy can be tolerated
Guideline Action: Use concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy rather than radiotherapy alone
Source Statement: Concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy is recommended over radiotherapy alone.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: adult patient with localized esophageal carcinoma considering a non-surgical approach and combined radiotherapy and chemotherapy can be tolerated
Guideline Action: Consider concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy a reasonable option compared with radiotherapy alone
Source Statement: In patients with favourable performance status and who have a reasonable chance of completing concomitant RTCT, this approach is a reasonable option compared with radiotherapy alone.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has favourable performance status and a reasonable chance of completing concomitant RTCT
Guideline Action: Offer concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy as a curative-intent non-surgical approach
Source Statement: The current review illustrates a benefit in local control and survival with the use of concomitant RTCT.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: adult patient with localized (T1-3, small volume N1, M0) esophageal carcinoma and good performance status considering non-surgical treatment
Guideline Action: Apply the guideline recommendations to this patient group
Source Statement: These recommendations apply to adult patients with localized (T1-3, small volume N1, M0) carcinoma of the esophagus and good performance status who are considering a non-surgical approach and for whom combined radiotherapy and chemotherapy can be tolerated in the judgment of the treating oncologist.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: when concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy is being used
Guideline Action: Prefer a cisplatin-based chemotherapy regimen
Source Statement: a cisplatin-based chemotherapy regimen is a reasonable chemotherapy regimen to use when concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy is used.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: patient is being considered for concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy
Guideline Action: Inform the patient about increased acute toxicity with this approach
Source Statement: Patients should be made aware of the increased acute toxicity associated with this approach.
proposed_actions_text:
- Proceed with curative-intent concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy as for localized disease.
- Select a cisplatin-based concurrent regimen by default.
- Reassure the patient that the same guideline recommendation applies because he is ECOG 1 and non-surgical.

patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man with dysphagia is diagnosed with localized mid-esophageal squamous cell carcinoma. Imaging shows T2N1M0 disease with small-volume nodal involvement and no distant spread. He has an ECOG performance status of 1 and is otherwise medically fit. After surgical consultation, he declines esophagectomy and asks about a curative non-surgical approach. The oncology team feels he could likely tolerate combined treatment, but notes that he also has a chronic inflammatory bowel condition with recent severe diarrhea and weight loss from a flare, raising concern that he may not complete a full course of chemotherapy with radiation. The patient asks whether radiation alone would be less risky and whether a cisplatin-based regimen is standard if concurrent treatment is still chosen.
proposed_actions: Recommend concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy as the preferred curative non-surgical option.
Choose cisplatin-based chemoradiation without further concern.
Discourage discussion of treatment tolerance because the patient is otherwise fit.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: sequential radiotherapy and chemotherapy is being considered
Guideline Action: Do not use sequential radiotherapy and chemotherapy as standard practice
Source Statement: Sequential radiotherapy and chemotherapy should not be recommended as standard practice.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: sequential radiotherapy and chemotherapy is being considered
Guideline Action: Avoid sequential radiotherapy and chemotherapy because no survival or local control benefit was shown and toxicity was increased
Source Statement: There was no evidence to suggest any survival or local control benefit using sequential RTCT compared with RT alone. Given these results and the increased adverse effects associated with sequential RTCT, this approach should not be recommended in standard practice.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: adult patient with localized esophageal carcinoma, good performance status, considering non-surgical curative treatment, and combined radiotherapy and chemotherapy can be tolerated
Guideline Action: Use concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy rather than radiotherapy alone
Source Statement: Concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy is recommended over radiotherapy alone.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: adult patient with localized esophageal carcinoma considering a non-surgical approach and combined radiotherapy and chemotherapy can be tolerated
Guideline Action: Consider concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy a reasonable option compared with radiotherapy alone
Source Statement: In patients with favourable performance status and who have a reasonable chance of completing concomitant RTCT, this approach is a reasonable option compared with radiotherapy alone.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has favourable performance status and a reasonable chance of completing concomitant RTCT
Guideline Action: Offer concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy as a curative-intent non-surgical approach
Source Statement: The current review illustrates a benefit in local control and survival with the use of concomitant RTCT.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: adult patient with localized (T1-3, small volume N1, M0) esophageal carcinoma and good performance status considering non-surgical treatment
Guideline Action: Apply the guideline recommendations to this patient group
Source Statement: These recommendations apply to adult patients with localized (T1-3, small volume N1, M0) carcinoma of the esophagus and good performance status who are considering a non-surgical approach and for whom combined radiotherapy and chemotherapy can be tolerated in the judgment of the treating oncologist.

Rule ID: R007

Activation Condition: when concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy is being used
Guideline Action: Prefer a cisplatin-based chemotherapy regimen
Source Statement: a cisplatin-based chemotherapy regimen is a reasonable chemotherapy regimen to use when concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy is used.

Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60

Activation Condition: patient is being considered for concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy
Guideline Action: Inform the patient about increased acute toxicity with this approach
Source Statement: Patients should be made aware of the increased acute toxicity associated with this approach.
proposed_actions_text:
- Recommend concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy as the preferred curative non-surgical option.
- Choose cisplatin-based chemoradiation without further concern.
- Discourage discussion of treatment tolerance because the patient is otherwise fit.

patient_vignette: A 66-year-old man underwent radical prostatectomy for localized prostate adenocarcinoma. Final pathology shows pT2c disease with organ-confined tumor, negative surgical margins, and no extracapsular extension or seminal vesicle invasion. His postoperative PSA is currently undetectable, and he is recovering well with improving urinary continence. At follow-up, he asks whether he should wait for a PSA rise before doing anything further. The oncology team reviews the pathology and notes that despite the undetectable PSA, there are no adverse pathologic features that would place him in the higher-risk postoperative group discussed for immediate radiation.
proposed_actions: Offer adjuvant radiotherapy after prostatectomy
Recommend immediate postoperative radiation because the PSA is undetectable but the patient is post-prostatectomy
Frame adjuvant RT as improving overall survival and reducing distant metastases in this case
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient has undergone radical prostatectomy and has pathologic T3 or margin-positive prostate cancer
Guideline Action: Offer adjuvant radiotherapy after prostatectomy
Source Statement: In the only RCT for which long-term follow-up data are available, adjuvant RT following radical prostatectomy in patients with pathologic T3 or margin-positive prostate cancer has been shown to improve OS and reduce distant metastases compared to observation.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient has undergone radical prostatectomy and has positive surgical margins
Guideline Action: Offer adjuvant radiotherapy after prostatectomy
Source Statement: The OS benefit extends individually to the subgroups with positive surgical margins, extracapsular extension, and seminal vesicle invasion.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient has undergone radical prostatectomy and has extracapsular extension
Guideline Action: Offer adjuvant radiotherapy after prostatectomy
Source Statement: The OS benefit extends individually to the subgroups with positive surgical margins, extracapsular extension, and seminal vesicle invasion.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient has undergone radical prostatectomy and has seminal vesicle invasion
Guideline Action: Offer adjuvant radiotherapy after prostatectomy
Source Statement: The OS benefit extends individually to the subgroups with positive surgical margins, extracapsular extension, and seminal vesicle invasion.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient has undergone radical prostatectomy and has any of positive surgical margins, extracapsular extension, or seminal vesicle invasion
Guideline Action: Use adjuvant radiotherapy after prostatectomy
Source Statement: On the basis of the overall trial findings as well as these subgroup analyses, it can be concluded that men found at radical prostatectomy to have any of positive surgical margins, extracapsular extension, or seminal vesicle invasion benefit from adjuvant RT.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient is being considered for postoperative radiotherapy after prostatectomy and the comparison is observation with salvage therapy reserved
Guideline Action: Prefer adjuvant radiotherapy over observation with salvage therapy reserved
Source Statement: At a median follow-up of 12.6 years, adjuvant RT has now been shown to confer a significant benefit in terms of both OS and metastasis-free survival.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient is being considered for postoperative radiotherapy after prostatectomy
Guideline Action: Consider adjuvant radiotherapy because it improves overall survival
Source Statement: At a median follow-up of 12.6 years, adjuvant RT has now been shown to confer a significant benefit in terms of both OS and metastasis-free survival.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient is being considered for postoperative radiotherapy after prostatectomy
Guideline Action: Consider adjuvant radiotherapy because it reduces distant metastases
Source Statement: At a median follow-up of 12.6 years, adjuvant RT has now been shown to confer a significant benefit in terms of both OS and metastasis-free survival.
proposed_actions_text:
- Offer adjuvant radiotherapy after prostatectomy
- Recommend immediate postoperative radiation because the PSA is undetectable but the patient is post-prostatectomy
- Frame adjuvant RT as improving overall survival and reducing distant metastases in this case

patient_vignette: A 66-year-old man underwent radical prostatectomy for localized prostate adenocarcinoma. Final pathology shows pT3a disease with focal extracapsular extension, but the surgical margins are negative and there is no seminal vesicle invasion. His postoperative PSA is detectable at 0.4 ng/mL on the first check, and a repeat value two weeks later is 0.5 ng/mL. He is recovering well and asks whether he should proceed with postoperative radiation now. The oncology team reviews the pathology and notes that the concern is no longer purely adjuvant management because there is already measurable PSA persistence after surgery.
proposed_actions: Offer adjuvant radiotherapy after prostatectomy as if this were the same as the source case
Describe the case as a standard adjuvant RT candidate because of extracapsular extension
Apply the survival and metastasis-reduction rationale for adjuvant radiotherapy without addressing the detectable PSA

patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man with biopsy-proven localized prostate adenocarcinoma elected radical prostatectomy after counseling about management options and his preference for surgery. He has intermediate-risk disease, good baseline urinary control, and preserved erectile function. In the operating room, the surgeon is beginning the radical prostatectomy and plans to preserve continence and sexual function when oncologically appropriate. Because the preoperative MRI showed a small but distinct lesion abutting the left neurovascular bundle, the team discusses whether to widen the dissection on that side. The specimen is then handed off for routine pathologic processing. The pathologist notes the apex will be submitted carefully in standard sections rather than shaved en face. A frozen section is briefly considered only if the final margin appears unexpectedly close on gross inspection.
proposed_actions: Order frozen section analysis of the radical prostatectomy specimen for margin status.
Use intraoperative frozen section to guide additional margin resection if the specimen looks close.
Document the case as needing margin-status frozen section because surgery is underway.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: radical prostatectomy specimen is being handled in the operating room
Guideline Action: do not perform frozen section analysis for margin status
Source Statement: Frozen section analysis of the radical prostatectomy specimen (RPS) for margin status is not recommended.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: processing the apex of radical prostatectomy specimen
Guideline Action: do not use an en face shave technique at the apex
Source Statement: An en face (shave) technique is to be discouraged at the apex, as this approach can result in false-positive margin interpretation.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient is low-risk and surgery is the preferred option
Guideline Action: offer radical prostatectomy after full discussion and consideration of patient preferences
Source Statement: RP should be offered to low-risk and intermediate-risk patients for whom surgery is the preferred option after full discussion with patient and taking into account patient preferences.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient is intermediate-risk and surgery is the preferred option
Guideline Action: offer radical prostatectomy after full discussion and consideration of patient preferences
Source Statement: RP should be offered to low-risk and intermediate-risk patients for whom surgery is the preferred option after full discussion with patient and taking into account patient preferences.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient is high-risk
Guideline Action: offer referral for radiation consultation or review at a Multidisciplinary Cancer Conference (MCC)
Source Statement: High-risk patients should be offered a referral for radiation consultation or review at a Multidisciplinary Cancer Conference (MCC).

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: radical prostatectomy is being performed
Guideline Action: aim to eradicate the cancer-containing organ with negative surgical margins
Source Statement: The main goals of RP are (a) complete eradication of the cancer-containing organ with negative surgical margins, (b) preservation of urinary function, and (c) preservation of erectile function, where appropriate, but, in some cases, it is not possible to achieve all three.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: radical prostatectomy is being performed
Guideline Action: balance margin rates with functional outcomes
Source Statement: Radical Prostatectomy should aim at achieving a negative margin, while ensuring a balance between margin rates and functional outcomes

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: radical prostatectomy is being performed
Guideline Action: consider sparing the neurovascular bundles as the standard approach except in high-risk patients
Source Statement: Sparing of the neurovascular bundles should be considered the "standard approach" except for high-risk patients.
proposed_actions_text:
- Order frozen section analysis of the radical prostatectomy specimen for margin status.
- Use intraoperative frozen section to guide additional margin resection if the specimen looks close.
- Document the case as needing margin-status frozen section because surgery is underway.

patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man with biopsy-proven localized prostate adenocarcinoma elected radical prostatectomy after counseling about management options and his preference for surgery. He has intermediate-risk disease, good baseline urinary control, and preserved erectile function. In the operating room, the surgeon is beginning the radical prostatectomy and plans to preserve continence and sexual function when oncologically appropriate. The specimen is sent to pathology, and the apical portion is grossly close to the inked edge. The uropathologist notes that the apex will be handled with serial perpendicular sections because the interface is narrow, and the team discusses avoiding an en face shave. The rest of the specimen is processed routinely.
proposed_actions: Process the apex with an en face shave technique to maximize tissue evaluation.
Document apical shaving as routine margin assessment during prostatectomy processing.
Interpret the case as supporting shave sectioning because the apex is close to the inked edge.

patient_vignette: A 74-year-old man with severe COPD and poor pulmonary reserve is found to have a newly diagnosed 2.2 cm endobronchial squamous cell carcinoma in the right upper lobe bronchus. He is medically inoperable, and bronchoscopy shows the lesion is visibly endobronchial but extends beyond the bronchial wall with CT evidence of direct erosion into the adjacent pulmonary artery. There is no tracheoesophageal fistula, and he has no history of porphyria or porphyrin allergy. Because the tumor is otherwise early stage and bronchoscopically reachable, the team briefly discusses photodynamic therapy with Photofrin and postoperative airway surveillance. The patient also asks about light precautions because he works outdoors.
proposed_actions: Proceed with photodynamic therapy because the lesion is early stage and accessible by bronchoscopy.
Arrange routine post-PDT toilet bronchoscopy and airway monitoring as if PDT were appropriate.
Provide standard Photofrin photosensitivity counseling.
Treat this as a straightforward endobronchial PDT candidate despite the vascular involvement.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient has porphyria or known allergy to porphyrins
Guideline Action: Do not use PDT
Source Statement: There are a number of contraindications for the use of PDT in patients with lung cancer. These include porphyria or known allergies to porphyrins.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Tumor erodes into a major blood vessel
Guideline Action: Do not use PDT
Source Statement: There are a number of contraindications for the use of PDT in patients with lung cancer. These include ... tumours that erode into a major blood vessel.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Existing tracheoesophageal fistula
Guideline Action: Do not use PDT
Source Statement: There are a number of contraindications for the use of PDT in patients with lung cancer. These include ... existing tracheoesophageal fistulas.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient is pregnant, nursing, or a child
Guideline Action: Do not use Photofrin because its use has not been established
Source Statement: Its use in pregnant women, nursing women, and children has not been established, and, therefore, Photofrin® is not recommended for use in those cases (1).

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient receives endobronchial PDT
Guideline Action: Perform toilet bronchoscopy after treatment and repeat as indicated
Source Statement: Toilet bronchoscopies should always be completed following endobronchial PDT to minimize the risk of bronchial obstruction, and the risk of hemoptysis should be considered prior to therapy and monitored post therapy.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient receives PDT
Guideline Action: Monitor closely after the procedure for airway obstruction
Source Statement: Since tumour necrosis can result in post-treatment airway obstruction, patients should be closely monitored after undergoing the procedure and toilet bronchoscopies repeated as indicated.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient receives Photofrin-based PDT
Guideline Action: Advise avoidance of direct sunlight and brightly focused indoor light for at least the photosensitivity period
Source Statement: Patients must avoid exposure of eyes and skin to direct sunlight or brightly focused indoor light.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult patient with primary NSCLC and medically inoperable early-stage disease accessible by bronchoscopy
Guideline Action: Consider photodynamic therapy as an option
Source Statement: Photodynamic therapy could be considered as an option for the treatment of early stage lung cancer in patients with medically inoperable disease that is accessible by bronchoscopy.
proposed_actions_text:
- Proceed with photodynamic therapy because the lesion is early stage and accessible by bronchoscopy.
- Arrange routine post-PDT toilet bronchoscopy and airway monitoring as if PDT were appropriate.
- Provide standard Photofrin photosensitivity counseling.
- Treat this as a straightforward endobronchial PDT candidate despite the vascular involvement.

patient_vignette: A 69-year-old woman with severe COPD and limited pulmonary reserve has a newly diagnosed 1.6 cm endobronchial squamous cell carcinoma in the left mainstem bronchus. She is medically inoperable, and bronchoscopy confirms the lesion is accessible endobronchially without invasion of a major vessel or evidence of a tracheoesophageal fistula. She has no history of porphyria or porphyrin allergy. Because the lesion appears early stage and technically reachable, the team considers photodynamic therapy. However, she reports that she is currently 18 weeks pregnant, and the discussion is deferred while the team reviews safer options and the fetal risks of treatment. She also mentions that she works in an office and has no major sunlight exposure concerns.
proposed_actions: Offer Photofrin-based photodynamic therapy because the tumor is bronchoscopically accessible.
Plan standard post-procedure airway surveillance and toilet bronchoscopy.
Focus only on photosensitivity counseling and proceed with PDT.
Treat the case as a routine early-stage inoperable NSCLC candidate for PDT.

- Focus only on photosensitivity counseling and proceed with PDT.

patient_vignette: A 29-year-old man underwent right radical orchidectomy for a 2.1 cm pure seminoma. Final pathology shows focal invasion of the rete testis, and staging CT demonstrates a single borderline retroperitoneal node measuring 1.3 cm in short axis, though it was initially described as negative in the clinic note. He is otherwise healthy and asks whether the small primary tumor size could justify a less intensive follow-up plan. The oncology team discusses post-orchidectomy management options and notes that he is eager to avoid chemotherapy. Because the disease is no longer clearly stage I, the team decides surveillance would still be appropriate and plans a risk-adapted schedule based on tumor size and rete testis invasion.
proposed_actions: Choose surveillance as the preferred post-orchidectomy management strategy
Base follow-up intensity on tumor size and rete testis invasion
Reassure the patient that this remains stage I seminoma requiring routine surveillance only
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: adult patient with stage I testicular seminoma and proposed risk-adapted surveillance based on tumour size or rete testis invasion
Guideline Action: Do not use a risk-adapted approach as the basis for management
Source Statement: Thus, a risk-adapted approach cannot be recommended at this time.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: adult patient with stage I testicular seminoma after orchidectomy
Guideline Action: Use surveillance as the preferred post-orchidectomy management strategy
Source Statement: The DSG recommends surveillance as the preferred option, because adjuvant therapy is associated with important short and long-term toxicities and second malignancy risks with no evidence of improved survival.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: adult patient with stage I testicular seminoma on primary surveillance
Guideline Action: Follow a minimum surveillance program with physical examination every 3 to 4 months, chest X-ray every 6 to 12 months, and CT abdomen/pelvis every 3 to 4 months for the first 3 years, then less often thereafter
Source Statement: The minimum surveillance program should be a physical examination every three to four months, chest X-ray every six to twelve months, and computerised tomography (CT) of the abdomen and pelvis every three to four months in the first three years and then less often thereafter.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: adult patient with stage I testicular seminoma on primary surveillance
Guideline Action: Include follow-up investigations of sites at risk of relapse
Source Statement: In addition, follow-up should include appropriate investigations of sites at risk of relapse.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: adult patient with stage I testicular seminoma who prefers immediate treatment or is unsuitable for primary surveillance
Guideline Action: Offer adjuvant radiotherapy
Source Statement: For patients who prefer immediate treatment, or who are unsuitable for primary surveillance, adjuvant RT is the recommended option.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: adult patient with stage I testicular seminoma receiving adjuvant radiotherapy
Guideline Action: Use a radiotherapy dose of at least 20 Gy and no more than 30 Gy
Source Statement: When adjuvant RT is the preferred option, a radiation dose of at least 20 Gy and no more than 30 Gy is recommended.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: adult patient with stage I testicular seminoma treated with adjuvant therapy
Guideline Action: Continue post-treatment monitoring for relapse; except after extended-field radiotherapy, follow-up should be as thorough as surveillance
Source Statement: In patients treated with adjuvant therapy, post treatment monitoring for disease relapse is still necessary. Except in the specific case of extended-field radiotherapy, the follow-up after adjuvant therapy should be as thorough as the surveillance conducted in the absence of adjuvant therapy.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: adult patient with stage I testicular seminoma and planned adjuvant therapy with fertility concerns
Guideline Action: Offer sperm banking; if radiotherapy is used, offer scrotal shielding
Source Statement: If adjuvant therapy is planned, sperm banking (and scrotal shielding with RT) should be offered if future fertility is of concern to the patient.
proposed_actions_text:
- Choose surveillance as the preferred post-orchidectomy management strategy
- Base follow-up intensity on tumor size and rete testis invasion
- Reassure the patient that this remains stage I seminoma requiring routine surveillance only

patient_vignette: A 29-year-old man underwent right radical orchidectomy for a 2.1 cm pure seminoma confined to the testis, with negative margins and no metastatic disease on staging CT. He remains anxious about relapse and asks whether tumor size and rete testis invasion should guide the intensity of follow-up. He is otherwise healthy, but he lives 5 hours from the cancer center and says regular CT-based surveillance would be difficult. After counseling, he is told that surveillance is still the preferred option after orchidectomy and that management should not be risk-adapted by tumor size or rete testis invasion.
proposed_actions: Recommend surveillance as the preferred post-orchidectomy management strategy
Ignore access barriers and schedule routine surveillance anyway
Use a risk-adapted follow-up plan based on tumor size or rete testis invasion

patient_vignette: A 29-year-old surgical resident is rotating through gastroenterology and asks to be signed off for independent colonoscopy practice before starting a community job next month. He has completed a six-month endoscopy elective and has assisted on numerous procedures, but his supervising physician notes that he has only recently begun performing colonoscopies independently. During review, the resident says he is comfortable with basic scope handling and has completed nearly 300 colonoscopies during training, with an average independent cecal intubation rate above 85%. He still feels less confident with pathology interpretation and says he would want to review a few surveillance intervals before practicing on his own.
proposed_actions: Sign off the resident for independent colonoscopy practice based on his apparent procedural volume
Focus only on technical proficiency and ignore whether his cognitive knowledge is fully assessed
Apply the six-month training recommendation as if it were still unmet
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: accessory labeled single use or disposable
Guideline Action: do not reprocess the accessory
Source Statement: Accessories labeled as either single use or disposable should not be reprocessed.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: withdrawal time as a quality metric
Guideline Action: do not require a specific withdrawal time target or audit withdrawal time for quality assurance
Source Statement: It is not necessary to achieve a specific withdrawal time target or to audit this indicator for quality assurance purposes.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: gastroenterologist seeking credentialing
Guideline Action: complete a formal two-year subspecialty training program, with an option for a third year, before entering full-time practice
Source Statement: To be considered for credentialing, gastroenterologists must complete a formal two-year subspecialty training program, with the option of a third year of subspecialty training, before entering full-time practice.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: other physician including surgical resident seeking qualification for colonoscopy
Guideline Action: acquire the necessary specific knowledge and technical training in colonoscopy over at least six months
Source Statement: Prior to being qualified, other physicians, including surgical residents, must acquire the necessary specific knowledge and technical training in colonoscopy over a period of at least six months.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: trainee being assessed for competence
Guideline Action: ensure average independent cecal intubation rate is at least 85% for all colonoscopies
Source Statement: To be considered competent colonoscopists, trainees should achieve an average independent cecal intubation rate (CIR) of at least 85% for all colonoscopies and are expected to have performed at least 300 colonoscopies during training.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: trainee completing colonoscopy training
Guideline Action: ensure at least 300 colonoscopies are performed during training
Source Statement: To be considered competent colonoscopists, trainees should achieve an average independent cecal intubation rate (CIR) of at least 85% for all colonoscopies and are expected to have performed at least 300 colonoscopies during training.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: trainee completing 300 colonoscopies during training
Guideline Action: ensure at least 50 polypectomies are anticipated during training
Source Statement: If 300 colonoscopies are performed during training, it is anticipated that at least 50 polypectomies would have been performed.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: trainee completing training
Guideline Action: assess cognitive competence including contraindications, indications, screening and surveillance intervals, histologic classification of polyps, and management of findings
Source Statement: In addition to proficiency in the technical aspects of colonoscopy, proficiency in cognitive aspects of the procedure is essential, including knowledge of appropriate contraindications and indications for colonoscopy, application of appropriate screening and surveillance intervals (4), histologic classification of polyps and their significance, and knowledge of how to deal with findings encountered at the time of colonoscopy.
proposed_actions_text:
- Sign off the resident for independent colonoscopy practice based on his apparent procedural volume
- Focus only on technical proficiency and ignore whether his cognitive knowledge is fully assessed
- Apply the six-month training recommendation as if it were still unmet

patient_vignette: A 37-year-old internal medicine physician is being evaluated for credentialing in colonoscopy at a new outpatient center. She completed a formal two-year gastroenterology subspecialty program abroad and has since worked in endoscopy for several months, but the credentialing committee notes that her procedure log is incomplete for the local hospital requirements. She has performed about 240 colonoscopies during training, with an independent cecal intubation rate documented at 86%, and has done fewer than 40 polypectomies. She is otherwise competent with indications, contraindications, and common surveillance intervals, but the program is asking whether she can be approved now because she has substantial prior experience.
proposed_actions: Approve her for full-time practice immediately because she completed two years of subspecialty training
Treat her as already meeting colonoscopy competence standards based on an 86% cecal intubation rate
Ignore the low procedure and polypectomy counts and proceed with credentialing

patient_vignette: A 63-year-old man with limited-stage small cell lung cancer completed four cycles of platinum-etoposide chemotherapy followed by concurrent thoracic radiotherapy. He has WHO performance status 1 and no neurologic symptoms. Post-treatment CT shows no progression in the chest, but a follow-up brain MRI obtained before the visit now shows two tiny enhancing cerebellar lesions suspicious for early metastases. He is recovering well overall, with mild fatigue and stable appetite. At follow-up, the oncology team discusses whether additional treatment is recommended to reduce the risk of future brain involvement, and the patient asks if prophylactic cranial irradiation should be started now that his disease has otherwise responded.
proposed_actions: Offer prophylactic cranial irradiation at 25 Gy in 10 fractions
Schedule PCI urgently because the thoracic disease has not progressed
Reassure him that the negative chest CT is enough to proceed with prophylactic brain treatment
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has limited-stage SCLC, WHO performance status 0 to 2, and disease has not progressed on first-line treatment
Guideline Action: Offer prophylactic cranial irradiation at 25 Gy in 10 fractions
Source Statement: Offer prophylactic cranial irradiation at a dose of 25 Gy in 10 fractions to people with limited-stage disease SCLC and World Health Organization (WHO) performance status 0 to 2, if their disease has not progressed on first-line treatment.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: patient has extensive-stage SCLC and has had a partial or complete response to chemotherapy within the thorax and at distant sites
Guideline Action: Consider thoracic radiotherapy with prophylactic cranial irradiation
Source Statement: Consider thoracic radiotherapy with prophylactic cranial irradiation for people with extensive-stage disease SCLC who have had a partial or complete response to chemotherapy within the thorax and at distant sites.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: patient has extensive-stage SCLC, WHO performance status 0 to 2, and disease has responded to first-line treatment
Guideline Action: Consider prophylactic cranial irradiation at 25 Gy in 10 fractions
Source Statement: Consider prophylactic cranial irradiation at a dose of 25 Gy in 10 fractions for people with extensive-stage disease SCLC and WHO performance status 0 to 2, if their disease has responded to first-line treatment.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: patient has extensive-stage SCLC and has responded to treatment
Guideline Action: Consider PCI because the recommendation strength is not strong
Source Statement: The committee agreed that "consider" is the appropriate strength for the recommendation on PCI.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: patient has extensive-stage SCLC and has responded to treatment
Guideline Action: Consider PCI when benefits are judged to probably outweigh harms, acknowledging uncertainty
Source Statement: The Working Group members agreed that the benefits are equal to or probably outweigh the harms, but they had some uncertainty about this. Therefore, they agreed with the NICE committee that the recommendation should not be strong and using the word "consider" was appropriate.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 50
Recommendation Class: NO_RECOMMENDATION
Activation Condition: patient has SCLC without brain metastases and the question is whether to use prophylactic cranial irradiation instead of routine MRI follow-up
Guideline Action: Support further research comparing prophylactic cranial irradiation with routine magnetic resonance imaging follow-up
Source Statement: What is the effectiveness and cost effectiveness of prophylactic cranial irradiation compared with routine magnetic resonance imaging follow-up in people with SCLC without brain metastases?

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 50
Recommendation Class: NO_RECOMMENDATION
Activation Condition: considering prophylactic cranial irradiation for SCLC
Guideline Action: Do not add extra recommendations beyond the endorsed NICE recommendations
Source Statement: The Working Group members decided to endorse all of the recommendations from NICE without any modifications.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 50
Recommendation Class: NO_RECOMMENDATION
Activation Condition: considering prophylactic cranial irradiation for SCLC
Guideline Action: Use the endorsed recommendations as written because the Working Group endorsed them without modifications
Source Statement: The Working Group members decided to endorse all of the recommendations from NICE without any modifications.
proposed_actions_text:
- Offer prophylactic cranial irradiation at 25 Gy in 10 fractions
- Schedule PCI urgently because the thoracic disease has not progressed
- Reassure him that the negative chest CT is enough to proceed with prophylactic brain treatment

patient_vignette: A 67-year-old woman with extensive-stage small cell lung cancer completed four cycles of carboplatin-etoposide and had a good partial response in the thorax and liver. She remains WHO performance status 1 and has no headaches, focal deficits, or gait changes. Her oncologist reviews restaging scans, which show continued response systemically. However, her brain MRI, done as part of restaging, demonstrates several small asymptomatic metastatic lesions without mass effect. The patient asks whether she should now receive brain-directed preventive treatment, since the cancer has responded well elsewhere.
proposed_actions: Consider prophylactic cranial irradiation at 25 Gy in 10 fractions
Plan PCI because the systemic response is favorable
Proceed with prophylactic brain irradiation while monitoring the known lesions

patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with prostate adenocarcinoma returns after staging for a rising PSA and MRI showing extracapsular extension. Biopsy demonstrates Gleason 9 disease. CT and bone scan show no definite distant metastases, but the bone scan notes a single indeterminate sclerotic focus in the left iliac wing that the radiologist recommends correlating with follow-up imaging. He has no bone pain, good performance status, and has not started androgen deprivation therapy. Creatinine clearance is 78 mL/min. He asks whether he should start a bisphosphonate now to prevent future spread to the bones. He takes only tamsulosin and otherwise feels well.
proposed_actions: Start a bisphosphonate now to reduce the risk of first bone metastasis.
Treat him as straightforward high-risk localized disease and apply the no-bisphosphonate rule without further workup.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: men with high-risk localized prostate cancer
Guideline Action: do not use bisphosphonates to reduce the risk of first bone metastasis
Source Statement: In men with high-risk localized prostate cancer, bisphosphonates are not recommended to reduce the risk of first bone metastasis.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: men with nonmetastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer
Guideline Action: do not use denosumab at the bone metastasis-indicated dosage to reduce the risk of first bone metastasis
Source Statement: In men with nonmetastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (CRPC), denosumab at the bone metastasis-indicated dosage is not recommended to reduce the risk of first bone metastasis.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: creatinine clearance is below 30 mL/min or serum creatinine is above 265 µmol/L in a man considered for zoledronic acid
Guideline Action: do not give zoledronic acid
Source Statement: ZA is not recommended below a CrCl of 30 mL/min (or SCr >265 µmol/L).

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: a patient is receiving denosumab and zoledronic acid together
Guideline Action: do not give denosumab and zoledronic acid in combination
Source Statement: Denosumab and ZA should not be given in combination.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: men with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer
Guideline Action: use zoledronic acid at the bone metastasis-indicated dosage for preventing or delaying skeletal-related events
Source Statement: In men with metastatic CRPC (mCRPC), either ZA (minimally symptomatic or asymptomatic disease) or denosumab (disease independent of symptoms) (both at bone metastasis-indicated dosages) is recommended for preventing or delaying SREs.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: men with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer
Guideline Action: use denosumab at the bone metastasis-indicated dosage for preventing or delaying skeletal-related events
Source Statement: In men with metastatic CRPC (mCRPC), either ZA (minimally symptomatic or asymptomatic disease) or denosumab (disease independent of symptoms) (both at bone metastasis-indicated dosages) is recommended for preventing or delaying SREs.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: men with symptomatic metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer
Guideline Action: use radium-223 to extend overall survival
Source Statement: In men with symptomatic mCRPC, Ra-223 is recommended to extend overall survival.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: men with symptomatic metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer and predominantly bony metastases with no visceral metastases or large nodal metastases
Guideline Action: use radium-223 to extend overall survival
Source Statement: This recommendation applies to men with predominantly bony metastases and no evidence of visceral metastases or large nodal metastases.
proposed_actions_text:
- Start a bisphosphonate now to reduce the risk of first bone metastasis.
- Treat him as straightforward high-risk localized disease and apply the no-bisphosphonate rule without further workup.

patient_vignette: A 72-year-old man with prostate cancer is followed for rising PSA despite castrate testosterone on ongoing androgen deprivation therapy. Restaging CT shows new sclerotic bone lesions and a mildly enlarged retroperitoneal node, but no visceral organ metastases. He reports intermittent back pain controlled with acetaminophen and remains ambulatory with an ECOG performance status of 1. His oncologist asks whether radium-223 should be started to help with survival and bone symptoms. He has not previously received chemotherapy and is interested in bone-targeted treatment.
proposed_actions: Start radium-223 because he has symptomatic mCRPC with bone metastases.
Assume the absence of visceral metastases is enough and ignore the enlarged retroperitoneal node.
