Activation Condition: men with nonmetastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer
Guideline Action: do not use denosumab at the bone metastasis-indicated dosage to reduce the risk of first bone metastasis
Source Statement: In men with nonmetastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (CRPC), denosumab at the bone metastasis-indicated dosage is not recommended to reduce the risk of first bone metastasis.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: creatinine clearance is below 30 mL/min or serum creatinine is above 265 µmol/L in a man considered for zoledronic acid
Guideline Action: do not give zoledronic acid
Source Statement: ZA is not recommended below a CrCl of 30 mL/min (or SCr >265 µmol/L).

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: a patient is receiving denosumab and zoledronic acid together
Guideline Action: do not give denosumab and zoledronic acid in combination
Source Statement: Denosumab and ZA should not be given in combination.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: men with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer
Guideline Action: use zoledronic acid at the bone metastasis-indicated dosage for preventing or delaying skeletal-related events
Source Statement: In men with metastatic CRPC (mCRPC), either ZA (minimally symptomatic or asymptomatic disease) or denosumab (disease independent of symptoms) (both at bone metastasis-indicated dosages) is recommended for preventing or delaying SREs.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: men with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer
Guideline Action: use denosumab at the bone metastasis-indicated dosage for preventing or delaying skeletal-related events
Source Statement: In men with metastatic CRPC (mCRPC), either ZA (minimally symptomatic or asymptomatic disease) or denosumab (disease independent of symptoms) (both at bone metastasis-indicated dosages) is recommended for preventing or delaying SREs.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: men with symptomatic metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer
Guideline Action: use radium-223 to extend overall survival
Source Statement: In men with symptomatic mCRPC, Ra-223 is recommended to extend overall survival.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: men with symptomatic metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer and predominantly bony metastases with no visceral metastases or large nodal metastases
Guideline Action: use radium-223 to extend overall survival
Source Statement: This recommendation applies to men with predominantly bony metastases and no evidence of visceral metastases or large nodal metastases.
proposed_actions_text:
- Start radium-223 because he has symptomatic mCRPC with bone metastases.
- Assume the absence of visceral metastases is enough and ignore the enlarged retroperitoneal node.

patient_vignette: A 25-year-old woman presents to discuss breast cancer screening. She was treated for Hodgkin lymphoma at age 18 with mantle radiation totaling 18 Gy, and her current risk assessment places her in risk category 5 based on her history. She has no breast symptoms and is anxious because a relative was recently diagnosed with breast cancer. Her clinician reviews her history and confirms that she is still considered high risk, but notes that her prior chest radiation dose was just below the threshold often used for enhanced imaging recommendations. The discussion centers on whether she should begin any screening now and whether MRI should be added to mammography in the near future.
proposed_actions: Start high-risk breast screening immediately because she is 25 and high risk.
Order annual breast MRI plus mammography now based on her Hodgkin lymphoma history.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: woman is in risk category 5 and is age 25
Guideline Action: do not start screening from age 25
Source Statement: This (Ontario) Working Group, however, did not find justification for screening from age 25 in this group (or any of the other high risk groups).

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: woman is in target population risk category 1, 2, 3, or 5
Guideline Action: perform MRI in addition to mammography
Source Statement: MRI in addition to mammography is recommended for women in target population risk categories 1, 2, 3, and 5 above.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: women are being screened for high breast cancer risk
Guideline Action: screen annually
Source Statement: While there is insufficient evidence at this time to make a definitive recommendation regarding the appropriate screening frequency, it is the opinion of the Working Group that women should be screened annually, as this was the frequency typical of the identified studies on which the recommendation for screening is based.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: women are being screened for high breast cancer risk
Guideline Action: screen from age 30 to 69 years
Source Statement: While there is insufficient evidence at this time to make a definitive recommendation regarding the ages of patients who should be screened, it is the opinion of the Working Group that women should be screened annually from 30 to 69 years of age.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: woman is in risk category 5 and received at least 20 Gy radiation before age 30
Guideline Action: use MRI in addition to mammography because benefits outweigh harms
Source Statement: It is the opinion of the Working Group that the benefits of MRI in terms of increased sensitivity outweigh the potential harms of higher recall rates and biopsy rates for all women in risk category 5 who received ≥20 Gy radiation before the age of 30.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: woman is in risk category 4 with high risk benign biopsy or previous breast cancer plus breast density at least 50% and family history of breast cancer but not enough to be category 3
Guideline Action: consider adding MRI to mammography
Source Statement: With respect to risk category 4, there are preliminary data that a subgroup of patients in risk category 4 might benefit from the addition of MRI to mammography (e.g., women who in addition to a high risk benign biopsy or previous breast cancer, also have breast density ≥50% and a family history of breast cancer though insufficient to put them in category 3).

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 50
Recommendation Class: NO_RECOMMENDATION
Activation Condition: patient is in risk category 4
Guideline Action: do not make a recommendation for MRI screening based on current evidence
Source Statement: The evidence is insufficient to recommend MRI screening for patients in risk category 4.
proposed_actions_text:
- Start high-risk breast screening immediately because she is 25 and high risk.
- Order annual breast MRI plus mammography now based on her Hodgkin lymphoma history.

patient_vignette: A 25-year-old woman comes to discuss breast cancer screening. She was treated for Hodgkin lymphoma at age 18 with mantle radiation totaling 30 Gy and is otherwise well. Her chart initially lists her as risk category 5, but on review by the clinician she is classified as risk category 4 because her personal and family history do not meet the criteria for the highest-risk category. She has no breast symptoms and wants to know if she should start screening now because she expects to turn 26 soon. The conversation focuses on whether she needs MRI in addition to mammography at this age and whether the prior chest radiation changes that plan.
proposed_actions: Begin screening now because she had 30 Gy chest radiation before age 30.
Order MRI plus mammography as if she were still in risk category 5.
Assume annual high-risk screening from age 25 is indicated.

Source Statement: MRI in addition to mammography is recommended for women in target population risk categories 1, 2, 3, and 5 above.

Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60

Guideline Action: consider adding MRI to mammography

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 50
Recommendation Class: NO_RECOMMENDATION
Activation Condition: patient is in risk category 4
Guideline Action: do not make a recommendation for MRI screening based on current evidence
Source Statement: The evidence is insufficient to recommend MRI screening for patients in risk category 4.
proposed_actions_text:
- Begin screening now because she had 30 Gy chest radiation before age 30.
- Order MRI plus mammography as if she were still in risk category 5.
- Assume annual high-risk screening from age 25 is indicated.

patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man is 18 months after lobectomy and adjuvant chemotherapy for stage II non-small cell lung cancer. He completed curative-intent therapy and has been attending routine surveillance visits. Over the past 3 weeks, he has had intermittent dry cough and mild fatigue, but no chest pain, hemoptysis, weight loss, or fevers. He continues to smoke about 5 cigarettes per day despite prior quit attempts. On exam, he is thin but in no acute distress; oxygen saturation is 96% on room air, and lung exam is unchanged from baseline. A surveillance chest CT obtained last week showed only stable postoperative scarring with no new nodules, adenopathy, or pleural effusion. He presents mainly to review the scan and to discuss whether he needs any further cancer testing at this time.
proposed_actions: Escalate immediately to a full recurrence evaluation based on the cough alone
Order urgent additional cancer-directed imaging or biopsy despite the negative surveillance CT
Treat the visit as symptomatic recurrence until proven otherwise
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: A lung cancer survivor develops symptoms suggestive of recurrence
Guideline Action: Evaluate according to the symptoms
Source Statement: Survivors who develop symptoms suggestive of recurrence, should be evaluated according to those symptoms.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: After curative-intent treatment for NSCLC
Guideline Action: Schedule follow-up visits with medical history, physical examination, and chest imaging every 3 months in years 1 and 2, every 6 months in year 3, then annually thereafter
Source Statement: Following curative-intent treatment for NSCLC, survivors should receive scheduled follow-up visits that include a medical history, physical examination and chest imaging. Clinical evaluations should be conducted every three months in years 1 and 2, every six months in year 3 and annually thereafter.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: After curative-intent treatment for SCLC
Guideline Action: Schedule follow-up visits with medical history, physical examination, and chest imaging every 3 months in years 1 and 2, every 6 months in year 3, then annually thereafter
Source Statement: Following curative-intent treatment for SCLC, survivors should receive scheduled follow-up visits that include a medical history, physical examination and chest imaging. Clinical evaluations should be conducted every three months in years 1 and 2, every six months in year 3 and annually thereafter.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Lung cancer survivor has late side effects of curative-intent therapy
Guideline Action: Provide support to help manage symptoms and improve quality of life
Source Statement: Health-related quality of life (QoL) is very important for long-term survivors suffering from late side effects of their curative-intent therapy (including surgery, chemotherapy and radiation therapy). The following is a summary of issues reported by survivors. Health care professionals need to aid lung cancer survivors in handling these symptoms to improve QoL.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Lung cancer survivor has completed curative-intent therapy
Guideline Action: Provide surveillance follow-up care
Source Statement: For lung cancer survivors who have completed curative-intent therapy, surveillance is required and may be provided by specialists, family physicians or hospital-based nurses.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Lung cancer survivor has completed curative-intent therapy for NSCLC or SCLC
Guideline Action: Provide smoking cessation counselling
Source Statement: Smoking cessation counselling is recommended for patients who have completed curativeintent therapy for NSCLC and SCLC.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Providing smoking cessation advice to a lung cancer survivor
Guideline Action: Use behavioural and pharmacotherapy support in addition to verbal advice when possible
Source Statement: Although verbal cessation advice from a health care professional is of benefit, interventions that involve behavioural and pharmacotherapy support in addition to verbal advice is recommended.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: A lung cancer survivor has completed curative-intent therapy and ongoing surveillance is being planned
Guideline Action: Ensure surveillance includes medical history, physical examination, and chest imaging
Source Statement: Following curative-intent treatment for NSCLC, survivors should receive scheduled follow-up visits that include a medical history, physical examination and chest imaging.
proposed_actions_text:
- Escalate immediately to a full recurrence evaluation based on the cough alone
- Order urgent additional cancer-directed imaging or biopsy despite the negative surveillance CT
- Treat the visit as symptomatic recurrence until proven otherwise

patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man is 18 months after lobectomy and adjuvant chemotherapy for stage II non-small cell lung cancer. He completed curative-intent therapy and has been attending routine surveillance visits. Over the past 3 weeks, he has developed a persistent dry cough and new left-sided chest discomfort, with unintentional weight loss of 4 kg and increasing fatigue. He also reports waking at night with reflux and occasional choking after meals. He continues to smoke about 5 cigarettes per day despite prior quit attempts. On exam, he is thin but in no acute distress; oxygen saturation is 96% on room air, and lung exam is unchanged from baseline. A recent contrast CT chest and upper abdomen shows a new distal esophageal mass with associated mediastinal widening, while the remaining lung parenchyma is stable and there is no discrete pulmonary nodule or pleural recurrence. The question is whether his symptoms should be managed as lung cancer relapse or evaluated through another diagnostic pathway.
proposed_actions: Assume this is lung cancer recurrence and proceed with lung cancer relapse management
Order lung-directed staging as if the chest symptoms were from recurrent NSCLC
Document the symptoms as suggestive of recurrent lung cancer without further differential workup

patient_vignette: An 80-year-old man with localized prostate cancer diagnosed 4 years ago (Gleason 3+3) has been followed on active surveillance. His PSA has recently risen from 5.0 to 7.4 ng/mL over 8 months, and his PSA density is now 0.23 based on a relatively small prostate volume. He still feels well, but his recent digital rectal exam was slightly more firm than prior exams, though not frankly nodular. A confirmatory TRUS biopsy 3 years ago showed no upgrading, and he asks whether he can simply continue with routine scheduled biopsies now that he is older. He has no urinary obstruction or bone pain.
proposed_actions: Stop all serial biopsies solely because he is 80
Assure him no further workup is needed since the prior biopsy was stable
Continue the same surveillance schedule without reassessment
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient on active surveillance reaches age 80.
Guideline Action: Do not continue serial biopsy past age 80.
Source Statement: Serial biopsy should not continue past the age of 80.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient has low-risk localized prostate cancer (Gleason score ≤6).
Guideline Action: Use active surveillance as the preferred disease management strategy.
Source Statement: For patients with low-risk (Gleason score ≤6) localized prostate cancer, AS is the preferred disease management strategy.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient is on active surveillance for localized prostate cancer.
Guideline Action: Include PSA testing every 3 to 6 months.
Source Statement: The AS protocol should include the following tests: -PSA test every 3 to 6 months.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient is on active surveillance for localized prostate cancer.
Guideline Action: Include digital rectal examination every year.
Source Statement: The AS protocol should include the following tests: -DRE every year.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient is on active surveillance for localized prostate cancer.
Guideline Action: Perform a 12- to 14-core confirmatory TRUS biopsy including anterior directed cores within 6 to 12 months.
Source Statement: The AS protocol should include the following tests: -12-to 14-core confirmatory transrectal ultrasound (TRUS) biopsy (including anterior directed cores) within 6 to 12 months, then serial biopsy a minimum of every 3 to 5 years thereafter.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient is on active surveillance after confirmatory biopsy.
Guideline Action: Repeat serial biopsy at least every 3 to 5 years.
Source Statement: The AS protocol should include the following tests: -12-to 14-core confirmatory transrectal ultrasound (TRUS) biopsy (including anterior directed cores) within 6 to 12 months, then serial biopsy a minimum of every 3 to 5 years thereafter.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient on active surveillance has rapidly rising PSA, PSA density over 0.2, higher PSA than expected for prostate size, DRE abnormality, or very low PSA free/total ratio.
Guideline Action: Investigate further with mpMRI or earlier repeat biopsy.
Source Statement: Discordant findings between a patient's clinical course and pathologic findings can include rapidly rising PSA, PSA density over 0.2, higher PSA than expected for prostate size, DRE abnormality, and very low PSA free/total ratio. Presence of these findings requires further investigation with mpMRI or earlier repeat biopsy.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient on active surveillance has discordant clinical findings and pathologic findings.
Guideline Action: Use mpMRI at entry or during follow-up when indicated.
Source Statement: However, being cognizant of both the high cost of mpMRI and its promise, it is recommended that when a patient's clinical findings are discordant with the pathologic findings, a mpMRI is indicated. When indicated, it may be considered at entry or during follow-up.
proposed_actions_text:
- Stop all serial biopsies solely because he is 80
- Assure him no further workup is needed since the prior biopsy was stable
- Continue the same surveillance schedule without reassessment

patient_vignette: A 79-year-old man with localized prostate cancer diagnosed 4 years ago (Gleason 3+3) remains on active surveillance. His PSA has stayed around 5.2 ng/mL, his PSA density is 0.12, and his digital rectal exams have remained normal. He had a confirmatory TRUS biopsy 3 years ago that showed no upgrading. He asks whether he still needs scheduled biopsies, since he is approaching 80 and feels well. He has no new urinary symptoms and no concerning changes in PSA kinetics or exam findings.
proposed_actions: Recommend stopping serial biopsy now because he is nearly 80
Discontinue surveillance biopsies based on age alone
Tell him no further tissue sampling is needed

- Discontinue surveillance biopsies based on age alone
- Tell him no further tissue sampling is needed

patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man is referred to oncology after an incidental lung nodule is found on CT performed for persistent cough. The printed consult packet he brings to clinic appears to be from Cancer Care Ontario and includes the lead author’s contact information, Dr. Eric Winquist, through the PEBC, plus a note that the evidence review was published as a supplement. However, unlike a usual guideline handout, the final page also contains a brief management algorithm recommending repeat imaging in 3 months for small nodules and biopsy for enlarging lesions. The report still states that the text and illustrations are copyrighted by Cancer Care Ontario and may not be reproduced without written permission.
proposed_actions: Treat the document as a full guideline and follow the included nodule surveillance algorithm without further verification
Assume the publication/contact page itself constitutes a valid recommendation source for this patient
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 50
Recommendation Class: NO_RECOMMENDATION
Activation Condition: Document contains only publication/contact and copyright information, with no clinical recommendations.
Guideline Action: No clinical action can be extracted.
Source Statement: For information about this document, please contact Dr. Eric Winquist, the lead author, through the PEBC via: Phone: 905-527-4322 ext. 42822 Fax: 905 526-6775 E-mail: ccopgi@mcmaster.ca

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 50
Recommendation Class: NO_RECOMMENDATION
Activation Condition: Document states that the evidence review has been published as a supplement.
Guideline Action: No clinical action can be extracted.
Source Statement: The evidence review has been published as a Supplement

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 50
Recommendation Class: NO_RECOMMENDATION
Activation Condition: Document states copyright restrictions on reproduction of the report and illustrations.
Guideline Action: No clinical action can be extracted.
Source Statement: This report is copyrighted by Cancer Care Ontario; the report and the illustrations herein may not be reproduced without the express written permission of Cancer Care Ontario.
proposed_actions_text:
- Treat the document as a full guideline and follow the included nodule surveillance algorithm without further verification
- Assume the publication/contact page itself constitutes a valid recommendation source for this patient

patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man is referred to oncology after an incidental lung nodule is found on CT performed for persistent cough. The note he brings includes the same Cancer Care Ontario footer, the lead author contact details for Dr. Eric Winquist through the PEBC, and a statement that the evidence review was published as a supplement. It also includes the copyright notice stating the report and illustrations may not be reproduced without written permission. The misleading difference is that the patient’s packet is not just a single-page excerpt: the front page is followed by unrelated pulmonary function test results and a local clinic template asking the oncologist to decide next steps. No actual diagnostic algorithm or treatment recommendation is present anywhere in the document itself.
proposed_actions: Treat the packet as if it contains a clinical recommendation because it mentions a supplement and copyright notice
Search for a treatment plan in the attached clinic template and assume the guideline directs management
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 50
Recommendation Class: NO_RECOMMENDATION
Activation Condition: Document contains only publication/contact and copyright information, with no clinical recommendations.
Guideline Action: No clinical action can be extracted.
Source Statement: For information about this document, please contact Dr. Eric Winquist, the lead author, through the PEBC via: Phone: 905-527-4322 ext. 42822 Fax: 905 526-6775 E-mail: ccopgi@mcmaster.ca

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 50
Recommendation Class: NO_RECOMMENDATION
Activation Condition: Document states that the evidence review has been published as a supplement.
Guideline Action: No clinical action can be extracted.
Source Statement: The evidence review has been published as a Supplement

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 50
Recommendation Class: NO_RECOMMENDATION
Activation Condition: Document states copyright restrictions on reproduction of the report and illustrations.
Guideline Action: No clinical action can be extracted.
Source Statement: This report is copyrighted by Cancer Care Ontario; the report and the illustrations herein may not be reproduced without the express written permission of Cancer Care Ontario.
proposed_actions_text:
- Treat the packet as if it contains a clinical recommendation because it mentions a supplement and copyright notice
- Search for a treatment plan in the attached clinic template and assume the guideline directs management

patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man presents with newly diagnosed stage IV diffuse large B-cell lymphoma with bulky abdominal nodes, night sweats, and weight loss. His ECOG performance status is 1. He has well-controlled hypertension and no known cardiac disease, heart failure, or other major comorbid illness. Baseline labs show mild anemia and elevated LDH; renal and liver function are acceptable. The oncology team is planning first-line treatment and discusses CHOP-based chemotherapy. Because of his age, they consider whether to intensify treatment, add etoposide, or use a compressed 14-day schedule with growth factor support. The patient asks whether a more aggressive regimen would improve his chances of cure. During treatment planning, the family mentions a recent inpatient admission for atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response, but he converted quickly and has had no structural heart disease on prior echo.
proposed_actions: Start standard-dose CHOP and add rituximab without further discussion
Assume he meets the older-adult CHOP recommendation because he has no major comorbidity
Proceed as if cardiac status is clearly adequate despite the recent arrhythmia admission
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: considering granulocyte-colony stimulating factor as routine primary therapy with chemotherapy in this population
Guideline Action: Do not use G-CSF routinely as primary therapy
Source Statement: There is insufficient evidence to support the routine use of granulocyte-colony stimulating factor as primary therapy.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: considering a fractionated weekly CHOP schedule instead of standard CHOP
Guideline Action: Use standard CHOP rather than fractionated weekly CHOP
Source Statement: No benefits from fractionating the treatment schedule have been detected; the potential for outcomes to be inferior is suggested.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: considering CHOP plus etoposide in a compressed 14-day schedule
Guideline Action: Avoid CHOEP-14 because of excessive toxicity
Source Statement: patients receiving CHOEP every 14 days experienced excessive toxicity

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: considering CHOP plus etoposide versus CHOP alone in older patients
Guideline Action: Do not add etoposide routinely to CHOP
Source Statement: No differences were detected in response rate, TTF, or OS, and the authors commented that the addition of etoposide was associated with significant toxicity.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: considering more intensive regimens than CHOP
Guideline Action: Avoid more intensive regimens when they add toxicity without survival benefit
Source Statement: Two studies testing chemotherapy regimens that are more intensive than CHOP both detected an increase in toxicity with no survival benefit detected.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: considering second- or third-generation regimens instead of CHOP
Guideline Action: Prefer CHOP because it is at least as effective or less toxic
Source Statement: Subset analyses comparing CHOP with "second and third generation" regimens show that CHOP is at least as effective or less toxic than these other regimens.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient is older than 60 with newly diagnosed advanced-stage aggressive histology non-Hodgkin lymphoma, ECOG less than 4, and no significant comorbid illness
Guideline Action: Use CHOP combination chemotherapy with the same dose and schedule as in younger patients
Source Statement: Combination chemotherapy with cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine and prednisone (CHOP) is recommended for patients with no apparent cardiac disease or significant comorbidity. Dose and schedule should be the same as that used in younger patients.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has diffuse large B-cell lymphoma and meets the guideline target population
Guideline Action: Add rituximab to CHOP
Source Statement: The addition of rituximab to CHOP is recommended for patients with diffuse large B-cell lymphoma.
proposed_actions_text:
- Start standard-dose CHOP and add rituximab without further discussion
- Assume he meets the older-adult CHOP recommendation because he has no major comorbidity
- Proceed as if cardiac status is clearly adequate despite the recent arrhythmia admission

patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man presents with newly diagnosed stage IV diffuse large B-cell lymphoma with bulky abdominal nodes, night sweats, and weight loss. His ECOG performance status is 1. He has well-controlled hypertension but no known cardiac disease, heart failure, or other major comorbid illness. Baseline labs show mild anemia and elevated LDH; renal and liver function are acceptable. The oncology team is planning first-line treatment and discusses CHOP-based chemotherapy. Because of his age, they consider whether to intensify treatment, add etoposide, or use a compressed 14-day schedule with growth factor support. The patient asks whether a more aggressive regimen would improve his chances of cure. PET/CT staging is completed before therapy and shows disease confined to cervical, mediastinal, and abdominal nodal stations below the diaphragm, with no extranodal involvement or marrow disease.
proposed_actions: Treat as advanced-stage disease and apply the older-adult CHOP recommendation automatically
Add rituximab to CHOP on the assumption that the guideline target population is met
Ignore the staging result because the rest of the case appears typical for first-line DLBCL

patient_vignette: A 42-year-old man with newly diagnosed HIV-associated diffuse large B-cell lymphoma presents for treatment planning. He has no prior lymphoma therapy. His CD4 count is 58/mm3, HIV viral load is detectable, and staging shows advanced nodal disease without CNS involvement. He is otherwise fit, with ECOG performance status 1, and the oncology team considers him a candidate for curative-intent combination chemotherapy. Baseline labs show normal renal and hepatic function. He asks whether rituximab should be included with his chemotherapy regimen.
proposed_actions: Automatically omit rituximab because the patient has HIV-associated lymphoma
Treat this as the low-CD4 exception and choose chemotherapy alone
Focus on the detectable viral load and assume rituximab is contraindicated
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: HIV-related lymphoma and CD4 count below 50/mm3
Guideline Action: Do not add rituximab to chemotherapy
Source Statement: The addition of rituximab to chemotherapy in patients with CD4 <50/mm 3 is not recommended.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Previously untreated aggressive histology CD20-positive B-cell lymphoma and candidate for curative-intent combination chemotherapy
Guideline Action: Give combination chemotherapy with rituximab
Source Statement: Previously untreated patients with aggressive histology CD20-positive B-cell lymphomas who are candidates for treatment with curative intent and will receive combination chemotherapy with curative intent ... should receive this therapy in combination with rituximab.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Previously untreated HIV-related lymphoma, CD4 count at least 50/mm3, and candidate for curative-intent combination chemotherapy
Guideline Action: Give combination chemotherapy with rituximab
Source Statement: Previously untreated patients with HIV-related lymphoma with a CD4 count ≥50/mm 3 who are candidates for treatment with curative intent and will receive combination chemotherapy with curative intent ... should receive this therapy in combination with rituximab.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Previously untreated indolent histology CD20-positive B-cell lymphoma excluding SLL and candidate for chemotherapy
Guideline Action: Give chemotherapy in combination with rituximab
Source Statement: Previously untreated patients with indolent histology CD20-positive B-cell lymphomas, excluding small lymphocytic lymphoma (SLL), who are appropriate candidates for chemotherapy, should receive their chemotherapy in combination with rituximab.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: RECURRENCE_OR_RESTAGING
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Previously treated indolent histology CD20-positive B-cell lymphoma excluding SLL, no prior rituximab, and candidate for chemotherapy
Guideline Action: Give chemotherapy in combination with rituximab or rituximab monotherapy
Source Statement: Patients who have not previously received rituximab and who are appropriate candidates for chemotherapy should receive this chemotherapy in combination with rituximab or as rituximab monotherapy.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: RECURRENCE_OR_RESTAGING
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Previously treated indolent histology CD20-positive B-cell lymphoma excluding SLL, prior rituximab exposure, response lasted at least one year, and candidate for therapy
Guideline Action: Give therapy in combination with rituximab or rituximab monotherapy
Source Statement: Patients who have previously received rituximab ... and who have achieved a response of at least one year's duration from the last rituximab administration and who are appropriate candidates for therapy should receive this therapy in combination with rituximab or as rituximab monotherapy.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Indolent histology CD20-positive B-cell lymphoma excluding SLL and response to combination chemotherapy and/or rituximab
Guideline Action: Follow treatment with maintenance rituximab
Source Statement: For patients with indolent histology CD20-positive B-cell lymphomas, excluding SLL, who respond to treatment with combination chemotherapy and/or rituximab, this treatment should be followed by the use of maintenance rituximab.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Any patient planned for rituximab treatment
Guideline Action: Screen for hepatitis B surface antigen and hepatitis B core antibody before rituximab
Source Statement: The Hematology Disease Site Group recommends that all patients be screened for surface antigen for hepatitis B (HBsAg) and for hepatitis B core antibody (HBcAb) prior to treatment with rituximab.
proposed_actions_text:
- Automatically omit rituximab because the patient has HIV-associated lymphoma
- Treat this as the low-CD4 exception and choose chemotherapy alone
- Focus on the detectable viral load and assume rituximab is contraindicated

patient_vignette: A 42-year-old man with newly diagnosed HIV-associated diffuse large B-cell lymphoma presents for treatment planning. He has no prior lymphoma therapy. His CD4 count is 34/mm3, HIV viral load is detectable, and staging shows advanced nodal disease. Brain MRI is negative, but lumbar puncture shows low-level CSF involvement without neurologic symptoms. He is otherwise fit, with ECOG performance status 1, and the oncology team is considering curative-intent combination chemotherapy. Baseline labs show normal renal and hepatic function. He asks whether rituximab should be included with his chemotherapy regimen.
proposed_actions: Continue with the original plan of adding rituximab to chemotherapy
Apply the HIV lymphoma rule without reconsidering the new CNS disease
Ignore the CSF finding because the patient has no neurologic symptoms

patient_vignette: A 64-year-old man with extensive-stage small cell lung cancer completed first-line etoposide-cisplatin 2 months ago and initially had a partial response with symptom improvement. He now returns with worsening cough, weight loss, and new right hilar enlargement on CT, consistent with relapsed disease. His treatment-free interval is a little under 3 months. He has mild residual peripheral neuropathy from prior cisplatin but remains ambulatory, independent in activities of daily living, and has an ECOG performance status of 1. He is interested in further treatment and asks whether the same chemotherapy can be repeated, since it worked before. The team is also discussing topotecan as a second-line option because of the earlier response. The relapse timing is the main question, but otherwise his presentation resembles a typical platinum-sensitive recurrence.
proposed_actions: Retreat with etoposide-cisplatin as if this were a platinum-sensitive relapse
Offer oral topotecan based on the prior response alone
Proceed as though the timing automatically supports standard second-line rechallenge
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: adult patient with relapsed SCLC
Guideline Action: Consider second-line chemotherapy only after weighing treatment-free interval, response to first-line therapy, residual toxicity, and performance status.
Source Statement: The selection of patients for treatment with second-line therapy should be dependent on the treatment-free interval, the extent of response to first-line therapy, residual toxicity from first-line therapy, and the performance status of the patient.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: patient relapses three or more months after completion of first-line chemotherapy
Guideline Action: Consider retreatment with the same regimen that produced the initial response, generally etoposide-cisplatin.
Source Statement: patients who relapse three or more months following the completion of first-line chemotherapy may benefit from retreatment with the same regimen that induced their initial response. This would generally mean retreatment with etoposide-cisplatin.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: patient relapses three or more months after completion of first-line chemotherapy
Guideline Action: Consider alternative regimens such as CAV or carboplatin plus etoposide.
Source Statement: Alternative regimens may include cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, and vincristine (CAV) or carboplatin and etoposide.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: patient initially responded to chemotherapy and response duration was 45 days or longer
Guideline Action: Consider oral topotecan as an alternative second-line option.
Source Statement: Oral topotecan is a possible alternative for patients who initially responded to chemotherapy and had a response duration of 45 days or longer.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: patient is not suitable for intravenous therapy and topotecan is being considered
Guideline Action: Consider oral topotecan because it is more convenient and may be an option when IV therapy is unsuitable.
Source Statement: Oral administration is more convenient and may be a treatment option for patients not suitable for intravenous therapy.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: topotecan is given orally
Guideline Action: Monitor for grade 3/4 diarrhea.
Source Statement: Oral administration is associated with a higher incidence of grade 3/4 diarrhea, whereas intravenous administration may result in a higher frequency of grade 3/4 neutropenia.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: topotecan is given intravenously
Guideline Action: Monitor for grade 3/4 neutropenia.
Source Statement: Oral administration is associated with a higher incidence of grade 3/4 diarrhea, whereas intravenous administration may result in a higher frequency of grade 3/4 neutropenia.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: patient fails to respond to first-line therapy or relapses shortly after first-line therapy
Guideline Action: Consider enrollment in a clinical trial to help determine the optimal treatment regimen.
Source Statement: Clinical trials are needed to determine the optimal treatment regimen.
proposed_actions_text:
- Retreat with etoposide-cisplatin as if this were a platinum-sensitive relapse
- Offer oral topotecan based on the prior response alone
- Proceed as though the timing automatically supports standard second-line rechallenge

patient_vignette: A 67-year-old woman with extensive-stage small cell lung cancer completed first-line carboplatin-etoposide 6 months ago and had a partial response with marked improvement in dyspnea. She now presents with new liver lesions and recurrent mediastinal adenopathy on CT, consistent with relapsed disease. Her performance status is ECOG 2 because of increasing fatigue, and she has persistent grade 2 hearing loss from prior platinum therapy, though she remains self-caring at home. She asks about additional treatment. Because the relapse occurred well beyond 3 months after chemotherapy, the oncologist wonders whether the original platinum-based regimen could be repeated. Oral topotecan is also mentioned because she did respond initially and the response lasted more than 45 days. The question is complicated by her residual toxicities and borderline functional status.
proposed_actions: Automatically repeat carboplatin-etoposide because the relapse is after 3 months
Select oral topotecan without considering her residual toxicities and performance status
Treat her as an uncomplicated candidate for routine second-line chemotherapy

patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man with metastatic colorectal adenocarcinoma returns to discuss first-line systemic therapy. He has biopsy-proven liver and lung metastases, excellent performance status, and no prior chemotherapy. He says he would prefer a plan with treatment breaks so he can stay active with his family and avoid feeling worn down continuously. His oncologist reviews standard continuous therapy and asks about his goals, but also notes that the disease is now considered potentially resectable after a favorable response to staging, with surgery being discussed at a multidisciplinary conference. The patient still asks whether intermittent therapy could be an option if he needs systemic treatment first. The team explains that treatment scheduling will depend on the surgical plan and that they need to clarify intent before choosing a regimen.
proposed_actions: Discuss intermittent first-line systemic therapy as a standard option right away
Reassure him that intermittent therapy is a guideline-supported alternative to continuous therapy
Present the survival and quality-of-life data for intermittent versus continuous therapy as directly applicable
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with inoperable, advanced stage IV colorectal cancer being considered for first-line systemic therapy
Guideline Action: Discuss intermittent systemic treatment strategies as a reasonable alternative to continuous administration as part of informed treatment decision-making
Source Statement: Intermittent systemic treatment strategies should be part of an informed discussion of treatment options for this group of patients.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer wants a break from treatment
Guideline Action: Reassure the patient that intermittent first-line therapy is a reasonable alternative to continuous therapy
Source Statement: Patients who want a break from treatment can be reassured that intermittent strategies of administering first-line therapy are a reasonable alternative to continuous administration.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer is considering intermittent versus continuous first-line systemic therapy
Guideline Action: Use intermittent therapy as an option because it does not show a statistically significant reduction in overall survival compared with continuous therapy
Source Statement: Meta-analysis demonstrates no clinically significant survival difference between the continuous and intermittent chemotherapy strategies (HR, 1.02; 95%CI, 0.95-1.10, p=0.62).

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer is considering intermittent versus continuous first-line systemic therapy
Guideline Action: Consider intermittent therapy because it may improve or maintain quality of life compared with continuous therapy
Source Statement: Intermittent strategies of administering first-line systemic therapies to patients with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer (mCRC) do not result in a statistically significant reduction in overall survival and either improve or maintain quality of life compared to continuous administration of therapy.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer is receiving intermittent systemic therapy or a chemotherapy-free interval
Guideline Action: Continue best supportive care
Source Statement: During maintenance therapy or a chemotherapy-free interval, best supportive care should be continued for patients.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Intermittent strategy is being used for unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer
Guideline Action: Use 12 to 18 weeks of induction treatment as a reasonable guideline to consider
Source Statement: All but one of the intermittent strategies offered 12 to 18 weeks of induction treatment and were monitored with imaging at least every 8 to 12 weeks during the intermittent phase of treatment, with reintroduction of the induction chemotherapy at disease progression. These represent reasonable guidelines to consider when using an intermittent strategy, but adaptation of a strategy to individual circumstances should always be considered.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Intermittent strategy is being used for unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer
Guideline Action: Monitor with imaging at least every 8 to 12 weeks during the intermittent phase
Source Statement: All but one of the intermittent strategies offered 12 to 18 weeks of induction treatment and were monitored with imaging at least every 8 to 12 weeks during the intermittent phase of treatment, with reintroduction of the induction chemotherapy at disease progression. These represent reasonable guidelines to consider when using an intermittent strategy, but adaptation of a strategy to individual circumstances should always be considered.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Disease progresses during the intermittent phase
Guideline Action: Reintroduce the induction chemotherapy at disease progression
Source Statement: All but one of the intermittent strategies offered 12 to 18 weeks of induction treatment and were monitored with imaging at least every 8 to 12 weeks during the intermittent phase of treatment, with reintroduction of the induction chemotherapy at disease progression.
proposed_actions_text:
- Discuss intermittent first-line systemic therapy as a standard option right away
- Reassure him that intermittent therapy is a guideline-supported alternative to continuous therapy
- Present the survival and quality-of-life data for intermittent versus continuous therapy as directly applicable

patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man with inoperable stage IV colorectal adenocarcinoma presents to discuss first-line systemic therapy. He has liver and lung metastases and has not received chemotherapy before. He remains active, but over the last several weeks he has developed worsening abdominal pain, reduced oral intake, and mild jaundice from progressive liver involvement. He says he would like treatment breaks if possible, because he wants more time at home with his family. His oncologist discusses continuous chemotherapy and mentions that intermittent therapy is sometimes considered in metastatic disease, but also notes that because his bilirubin is rising and symptoms are progressing quickly, they are not yet in a position to plan a treatment holiday strategy safely. The team emphasizes the need to stabilize his current disease burden and organ function first.
proposed_actions: Reassure him that intermittent first-line therapy is a reasonable option
Recommend an intermittent schedule because he prefers time at home
Apply the survival and quality-of-life data for intermittent therapy without considering current instability

Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult patient with inoperable, advanced stage IV colorectal cancer being considered for first-line systemic therapy
Guideline Action: Discuss intermittent systemic treatment strategies as a reasonable alternative to continuous administration as part of informed treatment decision-making
Source Statement: Intermittent systemic treatment strategies should be part of an informed discussion of treatment options for this group of patients.

patient_vignette: A 27-year-old woman presents for a routine primary care visit and asks about cervical cancer screening. She reports that she has never been sexually active and has no history of genital symptoms, abnormal bleeding, or prior abnormal Pap results. She is otherwise healthy and not immunocompromised. Her only change from a typical preventive visit is that she mentions she is planning to become sexually active later this year and wonders if she should have screening now “just to be safe.”
proposed_actions: Advise no cervical screening solely because she has not yet been sexually active today.
Document that cervical cancer screening is unnecessary regardless of upcoming sexual activity.
Reassure her without discussing that screening recommendations will change once she becomes sexually active or based on age-based screening guidance.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: woman has never been sexually active
Guideline Action: Do not perform cervical screening.
Source Statement: Women who have never been sexually active do not require cervical screening.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: woman is younger than 21 years old
Guideline Action: Do not screen routinely.
Source Statement: the authors of this guideline have concluded that the harms of screening women under 21 years of age significantly outweigh the benefits.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: organized screening program with adequate database infrastructure
Guideline Action: Use HPV DNA testing of cervical cells for primary cervical screening.
Source Statement: HPV DNA testing of cells collected from the cervix is recommended for primary cervical screening.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: positive HPV DNA test result
Guideline Action: Perform cytology screening as triage rather than using cytology as the primary screen.
Source Statement: Cytology screening, which was recommended for primary screening in the previous version of this guideline, is now recommended only in the event of a positive HPV DNA test result.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: woman is 30 years of age or older
Guideline Action: Use HPV-based primary screening.
Source Statement: the primary recommendations presented in Part 1 of this guideline are for HPVbased testing for women 30 years of age and over.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: woman is 30 to 65 years old and initial HPV test is negative
Guideline Action: Repeat HPV DNA testing at five-year intervals.
Source Statement: For women aged 30-65, HPV DNA testing is to occur at five-year intervals after an initial negative result.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: HPV test is positive
Guideline Action: Assess the result with cytology testing and do not refer directly to colposcopy.
Source Statement: HPV-positive tests should be assessed with cytology testing and not referred directly to colposcopy.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: HPV positive and cytology negative
Guideline Action: Repeat HPV testing after one year.
Source Statement: Repeat HPV testing for results of HPV positive/cytology negative should be conducted after one year.
proposed_actions_text:
- Advise no cervical screening solely because she has not yet been sexually active today.
- Document that cervical cancer screening is unnecessary regardless of upcoming sexual activity.
- Reassure her without discussing that screening recommendations will change once she becomes sexually active or based on age-based screening guidance.

patient_vignette: A 27-year-old woman presents for a routine primary care visit and asks whether she should have a Pap test. She reports that she has never been sexually active and has no history of genital symptoms, abnormal bleeding, or prior cervical screening. She is otherwise healthy and has no immunocompromising conditions. On review of records, however, she had a brief sexual relationship two years ago that included vaginal intercourse, though she does not recall discussing it previously and says she has not been sexually active since. She asks whether screening is needed now.
proposed_actions: Recommend no cervical screening because she is currently not sexually active.
Assume her self-report alone satisfies the exemption and defer screening indefinitely.
Dismiss the old history as irrelevant and tell her she needs no cervical cancer screening.

patient_vignette: A 49-year-old woman presents with a 7 cm left breast mass, peau d’orange, and erythema involving most of the upper outer quadrant, but not the full one-third of the breast. Core biopsy confirms invasive ductal carcinoma. She is being evaluated at diagnosis for local treatment options, and she asks whether she can have lumpectomy because she hopes to avoid mastectomy. The team is also planning neoadjuvant therapy, with surgery to be reconsidered afterward depending on response. No surgery has been performed yet. Imaging shows a large tumor, but the skin findings are limited and the breast surgeon notes that this does not meet criteria for inflammatory breast cancer.
proposed_actions: Advise against breast-conserving surgery as if this were inflammatory breast cancer.
Proceed assuming mastectomy is automatically required for all locally advanced presentations.
Document the case as inflammatory breast cancer without further clarification.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: inflammatory breast cancer
Guideline Action: Do not advise breast-conserving surgery.
Source Statement: BCS is not advised in inflammatory breast cancer because the extent of tumour involvement cannot be reliably ascertained.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: sentinel lymph node biopsy is being considered both before and after neoadjuvant chemotherapy
Guideline Action: Do not perform SLNB both before and after neoadjuvant chemotherapy.
Source Statement: This suggests that SLNB should not be performed both before and after NACT.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: cases being discussed for treatment planning
Guideline Action: Discuss cases in a multidisciplinary case conference.
Source Statement: Communication between oncologists, surgeons, radiologists, and pathologists is essential. A multidisciplinary case conference is the recommended forum for discussion of cases.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: specimen submitted after neoadjuvant therapy
Guideline Action: Indicate any prior neoadjuvant therapy on the pathology specimen submission.
Source Statement: Any prior use of neoadjuvant therapy should be indicated when specimens are submitted for pathologic examination.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: before starting neoadjuvant therapy
Guideline Action: Insert surgical clips marking the original pretreatment tumour location.
Source Statement: It is recommended that surgical clips marking the original (pretreatment) tumour location be inserted before administration of neoadjuvant therapy.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: female patient with locally advanced breast cancer and surgery is being planned after response to neoadjuvant therapy
Guideline Action: Consider mastectomy as the standard of care for most patients.
Source Statement: For most patients with LABC, mastectomy should be considered to be the standard of care.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient strongly desires breast-conserving surgery
Guideline Action: Perform wide excision of the remaining tumour in the original tumour bed plus radiotherapy.
Source Statement: Wide excision of the remaining tumour in the region of the original pre-neoadjuvant treatment tumour bed plus RT is recommended for patients with LABC who strongly desire BCS.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: female patient with locally advanced breast cancer after mastectomy
Guideline Action: Give postoperative radiotherapy.
Source Statement: Radiotherapy following mastectomy is recommended for patients with LABC.
proposed_actions_text:
- Advise against breast-conserving surgery as if this were inflammatory breast cancer.
- Proceed assuming mastectomy is automatically required for all locally advanced presentations.
- Document the case as inflammatory breast cancer without further clarification.

patient_vignette: A 57-year-old woman is diagnosed with a 6.5 cm left breast invasive ductal carcinoma with biopsy-proven axillary node involvement. She is discussed at a multidisciplinary conference and starts neoadjuvant chemotherapy. Before treatment, the interventional radiologist places a clip in the tumor bed. At surgery planning, the patient says she strongly wants to preserve her breast if possible, and the surgeon asks whether a wide excision of the residual tumor bed plus radiotherapy would be appropriate. Post-treatment imaging shows a near-complete response, but the residual abnormality is still multifocal within the original breast quadrant. The team is deciding between breast-conserving surgery and mastectomy.
proposed_actions: Recommend wide excision of the original tumor bed plus radiotherapy as if breast-conserving surgery were straightforward.
Treat her preference alone as sufficient to proceed with BCS.
Ignore the multifocal residual disease and offer standard lumpectomy planning.

Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: inflammatory breast cancer
Guideline Action: Do not advise breast-conserving surgery.
Source Statement: BCS is not advised in inflammatory breast cancer because the extent of tumour involvement cannot be reliably ascertained.

patient_vignette: A 42-year-old man with B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia initially achieved remission after induction and consolidation but relapsed 14 months later. He received salvage chemotherapy and now has a documented second complete remission (CR2) with no detectable blasts on marrow exam. He is otherwise fit, without major cardiac, renal, or pulmonary comorbidities, but the donor search found no HLA-matched sibling and no suitably matched unrelated donor. The transplant team is evaluating next steps for durable disease control. Because he is an adult patient with ALL in CR2 after relapse, the case appears to meet criteria for transplant in the recurrence setting.
proposed_actions: Proceed directly with standard allogeneic stem cell transplantation using the sibling donor
Document the case as guideline-concordant for allo-SCT in CR2 without further donor consideration
Overlook the missing donor and continue transplant planning as if a matched donor were available
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: RECURRENCE_OR_RESTAGING
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult ALL patient in CR2 or greater, including refractory or relapsed disease
Guideline Action: Perform allo-SCT
Source Statement: Allo-SCT is recommended in CR2 or greater (refractory or relapsed).

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult ALL patient in first complete remission (CR1) and eligible for allo-SCT
Guideline Action: Consider allogeneic stem cell transplantation (allo-SCT) as a treatment option
Source Statement: Allogeneic stem cell transplantation (allo-SCT) is an option for adult patients with acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL) in first complete remission (CR1).

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult ALL patient in remission who is deemed unsuitable for standard myeloablative conditioning (MAC)
Guideline Action: Use reduced-intensity conditioning (RIC) for SCT
Source Statement: Reduced-intensity conditioning (RIC) is an option for patients with acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL) in remission when they are deemed unsuitable for the standard myeloablative conditioning (MAC) regimen.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult ALL patient in remission who is ineligible for MAC allo-SCT
Guideline Action: Consider RIC as an effective therapeutic option
Source Statement: Based on the evidence, the members of the Working Group have determined that RIC could be an effective therapeutic option for patients with ALL who are ineligible for MAC allo-SCT.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult ALL patient with high risk of treatment-related mortality from MAC
Guideline Action: Consider RIC instead of MAC
Source Statement: The systematic review (4) stated that RIC may be a potential therapeutic option in patients with high risk of treatment-related mortality (TRM) associated with MAC regimens, as there was a lack of overall survival benefit of MAC over RIC regimens.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Patient with Philadelphia chromosome-positive ALL (Ph+ ALL) after allo-SCT
Guideline Action: Use a BCR-ABL tyrosine-kinase inhibitor (TKI) post-transplant
Source Statement: Post-transplant use of a BCR-ABL tyrosine-kinase inhibitor (TKI) in patients with Philadelphia chromosome-positive ALL (Ph+ ALL) is a reasonable option.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Patient with Ph+ ALL undergoing allo-SCT
Guideline Action: Consider TKI therapy pre- and/or post-transplant
Source Statement: The consensus is that TKI therapy is useful pre and/or posttransplant.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult ALL patient in CR1 or later who lacks a suitable related or unrelated donor
Guideline Action: Consider haploidentical hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (haplo-SCT)
Source Statement: Haploidentical hematopoietic Stem Cell Transplantation (haplo-SCT) for patients with ALL in CR1 or later who lack a suitable related or unrelated donor is a reasonable option.
proposed_actions_text:
- Proceed directly with standard allogeneic stem cell transplantation using the sibling donor
- Document the case as guideline-concordant for allo-SCT in CR2 without further donor consideration
- Overlook the missing donor and continue transplant planning as if a matched donor were available

patient_vignette: A 42-year-old man with B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia initially achieved remission after induction and consolidation but relapsed 14 months later. He received salvage chemotherapy and now has a marrow showing persistent low-level lymphoblasts, with the most recent assessment documenting approximately 3% blasts rather than a full second remission. He is otherwise fit, without major cardiac, renal, or pulmonary comorbidities, and he has an HLA-matched sibling donor available. The transplant team is evaluating next steps for durable disease control. The overall situation still resembles post-relapse transplant planning, but the marrow response has not fully cleared.
proposed_actions: Recommend allogeneic stem cell transplantation immediately as if he were in CR2
Label the case as relapse in second remission and proceed to transplant planning without reassessing disease status
Treat the residual blasts as clinically insignificant and ignore remission criteria

Activation Condition: Patient with Ph+ ALL undergoing allo-SCT
Guideline Action: Consider TKI therapy pre- and/or post-transplant
Source Statement: The consensus is that TKI therapy is useful pre and/or posttransplant.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: Adult ALL patient in CR1 or later who lacks a suitable related or unrelated donor
Guideline Action: Consider haploidentical hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (haplo-SCT)
Source Statement: Haploidentical hematopoietic Stem Cell Transplantation (haplo-SCT) for patients with ALL in CR1 or later who lack a suitable related or unrelated donor is a reasonable option.
proposed_actions_text:
- Recommend allogeneic stem cell transplantation immediately as if he were in CR2
- Label the case as relapse in second remission and proceed to transplant planning without reassessing disease status
- Treat the residual blasts as clinically insignificant and ignore remission criteria

patient_vignette: A 52-year-old woman with a newly diagnosed left breast invasive ductal carcinoma returns to discuss surgery. Imaging shows a 4.2 cm tumor with biopsy-proven axillary lymph node involvement, but her staging workup also reveals a small contralateral lung nodule that is being followed and has not been characterized. The breast surgeon recommends mastectomy, and the patient is interested in immediate reconstruction if feasible. Because she is node-positive and may need postoperative radiation, the team reviews reconstruction options carefully. On exam, there is no clinical skin or nipple-areolar complex involvement, and she has no history of Paget disease. She has already had diagnostic mammography and breast MRI for local staging. A multidisciplinary visit is arranged to coordinate surgical and reconstructive planning, but the final treatment sequence is still being determined pending workup of the lung lesion.
proposed_actions: Proceed with skin-sparing or nipple-sparing mastectomy because the breast findings are otherwise favorable.
Treat the patient as a routine immediate reconstruction candidate and finalize plastics consultation without clarifying the lung nodule.
Assume postoperative radiation planning is straightforward despite incomplete metastatic evaluation.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: PREOP_STAGING
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient intends postoperative radiation and has lymph node positive early breast cancer, inflammatory breast cancer, or locally advanced breast cancer
Guideline Action: Do not use skin-sparing, nipple-sparing, or areola-sparing mastectomy
Source Statement: SSM, NSM, and ASM are not recommended for women intending to receive postoperative radiation with: early breast cancer who are lymph node positive, inflammatory breast cancer or locally advanced breast cancer who will require postoperative RT.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: PREOP_STAGING
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Invasive tumor involves the skin or nipple-areolar complex clinically
Guideline Action: Do not use skin-sparing, nipple-sparing, or areola-sparing mastectomy
Source Statement: SSM, NSM, and ASM are not recommended for women intending to receive postoperative radiation with: any clinical skin or nipple-areolar complex (NAC) involvement by invasive tumour

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: PREOP_STAGING
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient has Paget disease of the breast or a retroareolar tumor
Guideline Action: Do not perform nipple-sparing or areola-sparing mastectomy
Source Statement: NSM and ASM are not recommended for women with Paget disease of the breast or women with a retroareolar tumour.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Postmastectomy breast reconstruction has been performed
Guideline Action: Do not use surveillance mammography routinely in the reconstructed breast
Source Statement: There is insufficient evidence to support the use of postmastectomy surveillance mammography in the reconstructed breast.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: At the time mastectomy is offered for therapeutic mastectomy
Guideline Action: Initiate discussion of immediate or delayed breast reconstruction
Source Statement: The discussion of immediate or delayed breast reconstruction should be initiated at the time that mastectomy is offered by the general surgeon.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Immediate breast reconstruction is being sought for DCIS
Guideline Action: Perform preoperative evaluation with a general surgeon and a plastic surgeon
Source Statement: For women seeking immediate breast reconstruction for ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS), preoperative evaluation with a general surgeon and a plastic surgeon should be performed.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Immediate breast reconstruction is being sought for early stage breast cancer and adjuvant chemotherapy or radiotherapy may be needed
Guideline Action: Include a medical oncologist and/or radiation oncologist in preoperative evaluation, either by formal consultation or multidisciplinary cancer conference
Source Statement: For women seeking immediate breast reconstruction for early stage breast cancer who will potentially require adjuvant chemotherapy or radiotherapy (RT), a medical oncologist and/or radiation oncologist should be included in preoperative evaluation, either through a formal consultation or by a multidisciplinary cancer conference.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Immediate reconstruction is being planned
Guideline Action: Obtain adequate preoperative breast imaging according to existing guideline recommendations
Source Statement: For women seeking immediate reconstruction, there should be adequate preoperative imaging of the breasts, aligning with existing guideline recommendations.
proposed_actions_text:
- Proceed with skin-sparing or nipple-sparing mastectomy because the breast findings are otherwise favorable.
- Treat the patient as a routine immediate reconstruction candidate and finalize plastics consultation without clarifying the lung nodule.
- Assume postoperative radiation planning is straightforward despite incomplete metastatic evaluation.

patient_vignette: A 52-year-old woman with a newly diagnosed left breast invasive ductal carcinoma presents to discuss surgery. Imaging shows a 4.2 cm tumor with biopsy-proven axillary lymph node involvement, and the surgeon recommends mastectomy. The patient is interested in immediate reconstruction if feasible. Because she is node-positive and is expected to receive postoperative radiation, the surgical team reviews reconstruction options carefully. On exam, there is no clinical skin or nipple-areolar complex involvement, and she has no history of Paget disease. She has had diagnostic mammography, but breast MRI has not yet been completed because she has a documented iodinated contrast allergy and is waiting on a noncontrast protocol discussion. A multidisciplinary visit is arranged to coordinate surgical, reconstructive, and radiation planning before finalizing the operative approach.
proposed_actions: Approve immediate reconstruction as fully worked up.
Move straight to mastectomy without obtaining the missing preoperative breast imaging.
Document that imaging requirements are already satisfied because mammography was completed.
