patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with metastatic lung cancer is admitted for progressive dyspnea and fatigue. His oncology team notes that advance care planning has been addressed in a piecemeal way: one visit focused on completing a code-status form, and another included a brief handout about hospice services, but no broader conversation about the patient’s goals has occurred. The patient says he wants care that fits his priorities—staying alert enough to talk with family and avoiding prolonged ICU treatment if recovery is unlikely—but he has not had a structured discussion about those values. His daughter asks whether these conversations might upset him, since he becomes anxious when illness is mentioned. The team is considering a new outpatient workflow, but the current proposal would only add a single scripted ACP checklist at discharge rather than changing clinic processes or team roles.
proposed_actions: Recommend the single ACP checklist as sufficient to improve care quality.
Treat the brief code-status discussion and handout as adequate advance care planning.
Assume the existing limited ACP elements are enough to achieve meaningful patient impact.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: When considering limited ACP implementation
Guideline Action: Do not rely on only one or a few ACP elements to achieve meaningful patient care impact.
Source Statement: Addressing only one, or a few, of these elements creates the risks of leaving critical gaps that could prevent positive impacts on quality patient care.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: When using education alone to improve ACP care
Guideline Action: Do not expect education alone to improve care or patient experience without supporting system changes.
Source Statement: However, high-quality education, although essential, on its own is unlikely to improve care or the patient experience in the absence of supporting systemic changes.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: When considering whether pediatric ACP causes distress
Guideline Action: Do not assume ACP discussions increase distress in children or parents.
Source Statement: Concerns that ACP discussions will cause distress in children and parents are not supported by the evidence.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: When considering ACP or GoCD tools for practice
Guideline Action: Use a system-wide approach to integrate ACP into routine practice and maximize interprofessional team skills.
Source Statement: Outcome evidence for ACP suggests the greatest impact is likely to be made by a system-wide approach to integrating ACP into practice, one that maximizes clinical skills of all interprofessional team members.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: When developing ACP tools or processes
Guideline Action: Focus tool development on enabling values-based discussions rather than treatment-focused discussions.
Source Statement: Considering these emerging themes from the evidence suggests that tool development should focus on enabling healthcare providers to facilitate values-based discussions.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: When evaluating ACP tools
Guideline Action: Use values-based outcome measures to assess impact on patient care.
Source Statement: Future studies with corresponding valuesbased outcome measures are needed to evaluate the impact on patient care.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: When implementing ACP programs
Guideline Action: Include community engagement, professional education, standard practices, quality improvement monitoring, supporting policies, and technology infrastructure.
Source Statement: A comprehensive approach to ACP includes community engagement, professional education, development of standard practices, monitoring of these practices through Quality Improvement initiatives, supporting policies and practices, and a technology infrastructures.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: When planning ACP implementation across care settings
Guideline Action: Apply ACP across public health and healthcare sectors with system-level coordination and planning.
Source Statement: This approach must be applied across public health and healthcare sectors to support the exchange of clinical information and will involve system-level coordination and planning.
proposed_actions_text:
- Recommend the single ACP checklist as sufficient to improve care quality.
- Treat the brief code-status discussion and handout as adequate advance care planning.
- Assume the existing limited ACP elements are enough to achieve meaningful patient impact.

patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with metastatic lung cancer is admitted for progressive dyspnea and fatigue. His oncology team has tried to improve advance care planning, but the only intervention in place so far is a short educational session for clinicians on how to complete resuscitation forms; there are no standardized clinic prompts, role changes, or follow-up processes. The patient says he wants care that fits his priorities—staying alert enough to talk with family and avoiding prolonged ICU treatment if recovery is unlikely—but those values have not yet been explored in a structured way. His daughter asks whether discussing prognosis and future care might upset him, since he becomes anxious when illness is mentioned. The team wonders whether the brief teaching session alone will be enough to improve patient experience and documentation.
proposed_actions: Assume the clinician education session will be enough to improve ACP quality and patient experience.
Rely on teaching staff how to complete forms without adding workflow changes.
Proceed as if no additional system redesign is needed.

patient_vignette: A 64-year-old woman with high-grade serous epithelial ovarian cancer returns with increasing abdominal discomfort and early satiety 5 months after finishing her last platinum-based regimen. She has had only two prior anticancer regimens in total, and CT imaging shows diffuse peritoneal progression without bowel obstruction. On pelvic exam there is no rectosigmoid involvement, no fistula history, and no intra-abdominal abscess. Her disease remains platinum-resistant, and she asks what treatment comes next. Because she seems to fit the general profile of recurrent ovarian cancer, the team is considering the bevacizumab-plus-chemotherapy approach used in AURELIA-eligible patients.
proposed_actions: Start bevacizumab plus chemotherapy as if she were AURELIA-eligible.
Treat the case as a straightforward candidate for the bevacizumab-based recurrent ovarian cancer pathway.
Overlook the need to verify all AURELIA exclusion criteria before choosing the regimen.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: TREATMENT_RESPONSE
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: platinum-resistant recurrent ovarian cancer and not eligible for the AURELIA trial criteria
Guideline Action: Do not apply the bevacizumab plus chemotherapy option described for AURELIA-eligible patients.
Source Statement: Ineligible patients include those who have received >2 prior anticancer regimens or who had refractory disease, patients with a history of bowel obstruction (including subocclusive disease) related to underlying disease, a history of abdominal fistula, gastrointestinal perforation, or intra-abdominal abscess, or evidence of rectosigmoid involvement by pelvic examination, bowel involvement on computed tomography, or clinical symptoms of bowel obstruction.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: all patients with recurrent epithelial ovarian cancer
Guideline Action: Assess each patient individually for recurrence status, platinum sensitivity, toxicity, ease of administration, and patient preference; recognize systemic therapy is not curative.
Source Statement: Systemic therapy for recurrent ovarian cancer is not curative. As such, it is recognized that, to determine the optimal therapy, each patient needs to be assessed individually in terms of recurrence, sensitivity to platinum, toxicity, ease of administration, and patient preference.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: all patients with recurrent epithelial ovarian cancer
Guideline Action: Offer participation in clinical trials if appropriate.
Source Statement: All patients should be offered the opportunity to participate in clinical trials, if appropriate.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: TREATMENT_RESPONSE
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: platinum-sensitive recurrent ovarian cancer and a single platinum agent is not being considered because of toxicity or allergy
Guideline Action: Use monotherapy with paclitaxel, topotecan, or pegylated liposomal doxorubicin.
Source Statement: If a single platinum agent is not being considered (e.g., because of toxicity or allergy), then monotherapy with paclitaxel, topotecan, or pegylated liposomal doxorubicin is a reasonable treatment option.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: TREATMENT_RESPONSE
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: platinum-sensitive recurrent ovarian cancer with known BRCA1 or BRCA2 mutation, somatic or germline, after platinum-based chemotherapy for recurrent disease
Guideline Action: Use PARP inhibitor maintenance treatment post platinum-based chemotherapy.
Source Statement: PARP inhibitors are recommended for patients with known BRCA 1 or 2 mutation (somatic and germline) as maintenance treatment post platinum-based chemotherapy for recurrent disease.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: TREATMENT_RESPONSE
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: platinum-refractory or platinum-resistant recurrent ovarian cancer
Guideline Action: Set treatment goals to improving quality of life by extending symptom-free interval, reducing symptom intensity, increasing progression-free interval, or prolonging life if possible.
Source Statement: Lower levels of response to treatment are expected for this group; therefore, the goals of treatment should be to improve patient's quality of life by extending the symptom-free interval, reducing symptom intensity, increasing progression-free interval, or if possible, prolonging life.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: TREATMENT_RESPONSE
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: platinum-refractory or platinum-resistant recurrent ovarian cancer
Guideline Action: Use single-agent paclitaxel, topotecan, pegylated liposomal doxorubicin, or gemcitabine as reasonable treatment options.
Source Statement: Single-agent paclitaxel, topotecan, pegylated liposomal doxorubicin, and gemcitabine have demonstrated activity in this patient population and are reasonable treatment options.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: TREATMENT_RESPONSE
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: platinum-sensitive recurrent ovarian cancer
Guideline Action: Offer chemotherapy with biologics after discussing the safety profile.
Source Statement: Women with platinum-sensitive recurrent ovarian cancer should be offered chemotherapy with biologics after a discussion concerning the safety profile
proposed_actions_text:
- Start bevacizumab plus chemotherapy as if she were AURELIA-eligible.
- Treat the case as a straightforward candidate for the bevacizumab-based recurrent ovarian cancer pathway.
- Overlook the need to verify all AURELIA exclusion criteria before choosing the regimen.

patient_vignette: A 64-year-old woman with high-grade serous epithelial ovarian cancer presents with worsening abdominal discomfort and early satiety 5 months after completing her last platinum-based regimen. She has received three prior anticancer regimens, and CT shows progressive peritoneal disease. However, she also reports intermittent crampy abdominal pain and constipation, and the radiology report notes a mildly distended small-bowel loop with a transition point suggesting a low-grade partial bowel obstruction. She asks whether the next step should include bevacizumab with chemotherapy because she is still able to eat and has not required surgery.
proposed_actions: Use bevacizumab plus chemotherapy for recurrent disease.
Proceed as though she were an AURELIA-eligible patient.
Ignore the obstruction symptoms because she is not yet completely obstructed.

patient_vignette: A 67-year-old woman with a history of stage IV endometrioid endometrial adenocarcinoma returns with intermittent vaginal spotting, mild pelvic pressure, and a few tiny pulmonary nodules on surveillance CT that have remained unchanged for 12 months. She is ambulatory, has preserved renal function, and asks about treatment options, but she reports that her symptoms are still minimal and not interfering with daily activities. Her oncologist reviews systemic therapy options and notes that the disease is recurrent but not rapidly progressive. Because she is seeking a treatment plan, the team discusses whether to start chemotherapy now versus other options, including less intensive approaches. The visit focuses on balancing expected benefit against toxicity, especially since she has not yet had any life-threatening complications from her cancer.
proposed_actions: Start combination chemotherapy immediately because advanced/recurrent disease is present.
Choose a paclitaxel-containing multi-agent regimen as the default best-shrinkage option.
Frame the visit as a direct comparison between cisplatin/doxorubicin/paclitaxel and other cytotoxic regimens without considering lower-intensity strategies.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: adult patient with advanced stage or recurrent endometrial cancer excluding sarcomas and squamous cell carcinomas
Guideline Action: use combination chemotherapy rather than single-agent chemotherapy
Source Statement: Combination chemotherapy is favoured over single agent chemotherapy because of higher response rates.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: adult patient with advanced or recurrent endometrial cancer
Guideline Action: consider paclitaxel plus cisplatin/doxorubicin, recognizing improved response rate and median survival but increased toxicity
Source Statement: Paclitaxel in combination with cisplatin/doxorubicin chemotherapy improves both response rate and median survival; however, the use of this three-drug combination is associated with increased toxicity.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: adult patient with advanced or recurrent endometrial cancer and minimal symptoms or non-life threatening disease
Guideline Action: consider hormonal therapy
Source Statement: Hormonal therapy may be a therapeutic option for those patients with minimal symptoms or non-life threatening advanced or recurrent endometrial cancer.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: adult patient with advanced or recurrent endometrial cancer when choosing between cisplatin/doxorubicin/paclitaxel and doxorubicin/cisplatin
Guideline Action: weigh greater toxicity against about a three-month median survival increase before using the three-drug regimen
Source Statement: The decision to use the three-drug combination, consisting of cisplatin/doxorubicin/paclitaxel, should be made with consideration of both the greater toxicity and the three-month increase in median survival time in comparison with the two-drug doxorubicin/cisplatin regimen.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: adult patient with advanced or recurrent endometrial cancer
Guideline Action: note that recent data suggest no recurrence-free survival benefit for the three-drug combination and increased toxicity
Source Statement: However, recent data suggest no benefit to the three-drug combination in terms of recurrence-free survival and was associated with increased toxicity.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: adult patient with advanced or recurrent endometrial cancer
Guideline Action: consider doxorubicin/cisplatin as a comparator regimen with reported response rates of 28-45%
Source Statement: For double-agent chemotherapy, randomized trials of doxorubicin/cisplatin reported response rates ranging from 28-45%.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: adult patient with advanced or recurrent endometrial cancer
Guideline Action: consider doxorubicin/paclitaxel as a double-agent option with reported response rate of 43%
Source Statement: For double-agent chemotherapy, randomized trials of doxorubicin/cisplatin reported response rates ranging from 28-45%, other agents in combination with doxorubicin reported response rates of 30% (cyclophosphamide) and 43% (paclitaxel).

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: adult patient with advanced or recurrent endometrial cancer
Guideline Action: consider single-agent doxorubicin or platinum agents only with awareness of lower reported response rates
Source Statement: Single-agent chemotherapy has reported response rates as follows: doxorubicin 17-27% and platinum agents 21%.
proposed_actions_text:
- Start combination chemotherapy immediately because advanced/recurrent disease is present.
- Choose a paclitaxel-containing multi-agent regimen as the default best-shrinkage option.
- Frame the visit as a direct comparison between cisplatin/doxorubicin/paclitaxel and other cytotoxic regimens without considering lower-intensity strategies.

patient_vignette: A 67-year-old woman with a history of stage IV endometrioid endometrial adenocarcinoma presents with worsening vaginal bleeding and pelvic discomfort, and surveillance CT now shows a few pulmonary nodules with slight interval growth. She remains ambulatory, has good renal function, and wants treatment that offers the best chance of tumor shrinkage. Her oncology team reviews systemic therapy options and considers multi-agent chemotherapy because the cancer is recurrent and advanced. They also discuss paclitaxel-containing regimens and the tradeoff between response and toxicity. However, the notes mention that her recurrence is limited to the lung and pelvis and that the disease is not considered rapidly progressive or immediately threatening to organ function.
proposed_actions: Recommend hormonal therapy as the preferred option solely because the disease is recurrent.
De-emphasize chemotherapy on the assumption that any advanced recurrence with limited symptoms should default to endocrine treatment.
Apply the original chemotherapy selection framework without reassessing symptom severity and pace of progression.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: adult patient with advanced stage or recurrent endometrial cancer excluding sarcomas and squamous cell carcinomas
Guideline Action: use combination chemotherapy rather than single-agent chemotherapy
Source Statement: Combination chemotherapy is favoured over single agent chemotherapy because of higher response rates.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: adult patient with advanced or recurrent endometrial cancer
Guideline Action: consider paclitaxel plus cisplatin/doxorubicin, recognizing improved response rate and median survival but increased toxicity
Source Statement: Paclitaxel in combination with cisplatin/doxorubicin chemotherapy improves both response rate and median survival; however, the use of this three-drug combination is associated with increased toxicity.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: adult patient with advanced or recurrent endometrial cancer and minimal symptoms or non-life threatening disease
Guideline Action: consider hormonal therapy
Source Statement: Hormonal therapy may be a therapeutic option for those patients with minimal symptoms or non-life threatening advanced or recurrent endometrial cancer.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 60
Recommendation Class: CONDITIONAL_CONSIDER
Activation Condition: adult patient with advanced or recurrent endometrial cancer when choosing between cisplatin/doxorubicin/paclitaxel and doxorubicin/cisplatin
Guideline Action: weigh greater toxicity against about a three-month median survival increase before using the three-drug regimen
Source Statement: The decision to use the three-drug combination, consisting of cisplatin/doxorubicin/paclitaxel, should be made with consideration of both the greater toxicity and the three-month increase in median survival time in comparison with the two-drug doxorubicin/cisplatin regimen.

Source Statement: However, recent data suggest no benefit to the three-drug combination in terms of recurrence-free survival and was associated with increased toxicity.

patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man with progressive dysphagia, early satiety, and a 9-kg weight loss is diagnosed with metastatic gastroesophageal junction adenocarcinoma after CT shows liver and peritoneal metastases. Biopsy confirms adenocarcinoma, and testing reports HER2 as 1+ without overexpression. He has not received prior systemic therapy and is being evaluated for first-line treatment. His performance status is fair, and he is interested in starting treatment promptly. The oncology team discusses whether to add trastuzumab to chemotherapy because of the metastatic presentation, but the tumor is not HER2 overexpressing and this is his initial systemic regimen.
proposed_actions: Add trastuzumab to a fluoropyrimidine-based doublet because the disease is metastatic.
Select a HER2-targeted biologic alongside chemotherapy on the assumption that any HER2 staining qualifies.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Metastatic gastric or GEJ carcinoma that does not overexpress HER2 and first-line chemotherapy is being given
Guideline Action: Do not add a biological agent to the first-line chemotherapy regimen
Source Statement: In patients with metastatic gastric cancer or GEJ carcinoma not overexpressing human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER2), medical oncologists should not prescribe a biological agent in addition to a first-line chemotherapy regimen

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: RECURRENCE_OR_RESTAGING
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Gastric or GEJ carcinoma is recurrent or metastatic and HER2 is not overexpressed
Guideline Action: Do not prescribe a biological agent with first-line chemotherapy
Source Statement: In patients with metastatic gastric cancer or GEJ carcinoma not overexpressing human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER2), medical oncologists should not prescribe a biological agent in addition to a first-line chemotherapy regimen

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Advanced gastric or GEJ carcinoma and later-line therapy is being considered
Guideline Action: Do not prescribe an immune checkpoint inhibitor in addition to standard of care
Source Statement: In patients with gastric or GEJ carcinoma undergoing later lines of therapy, medical oncologists should not prescribe ICI in addition to standard of care.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Adult with advanced, non-resectable gastric or GEJ carcinoma starting first-line treatment
Guideline Action: Prescribe a fluoropyrimidine-oxaliplatin doublet or a fluoropyrimidine-irinotecan doublet regimen
Source Statement: Medical oncologists should prescribe either a fluoropyrimidine-oxaliplatin doublet or a fluoropyrimidine-irinotecan doublet regimen in the first-line treatment of patients with locally advanced, recurrent, or metastatic gastric and GEJ carcinoma.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Recurrent or metastatic gastric or GEJ carcinoma starting first-line treatment
Guideline Action: Add an immune checkpoint inhibitor to a fluoropyrimidine doublet chemotherapy regimen
Source Statement: In patients with recurrent or metastatic gastric and GEJ carcinoma, medical oncologists should prescribe an immune checkpoint inhibitor (ICI) in addition to a fluoropyrimidine doublet chemotherapy regimen in the first-line setting.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: HER2 overexpressing gastric or GEJ carcinoma starting first-line treatment
Guideline Action: Add trastuzumab to a fluoropyrimidine doublet chemotherapy regimen
Source Statement: In patients with HER2 overexpressing gastric or GEJ carcinoma, medical oncologists should prescribe the addition of trastuzumab to a fluoropyrimidine doublet chemotherapy regimen in the first-line setting.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: RECURRENCE_OR_RESTAGING
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Gastric or GEJ carcinoma is recurrent or metastatic and first-line treatment is being selected
Guideline Action: Use a fluoropyrimidine doublet chemotherapy regimen with an immune checkpoint inhibitor
Source Statement: In patients with recurrent or metastatic gastric and GEJ carcinoma, medical oncologists should prescribe an immune checkpoint inhibitor (ICI) in addition to a fluoropyrimidine doublet chemotherapy regimen in the first-line setting.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: RECURRENCE_OR_RESTAGING
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Gastric or GEJ carcinoma is recurrent or metastatic and first-line treatment is being selected
Guideline Action: Use a fluoropyrimidine-oxaliplatin doublet or fluoropyrimidine-irinotecan doublet regimen
Source Statement: Medical oncologists should prescribe either a fluoropyrimidine-oxaliplatin doublet or a fluoropyrimidine-irinotecan doublet regimen in the first-line treatment of patients with locally advanced, recurrent, or metastatic gastric and GEJ carcinoma.
proposed_actions_text:
- Add trastuzumab to a fluoropyrimidine-based doublet because the disease is metastatic.
- Select a HER2-targeted biologic alongside chemotherapy on the assumption that any HER2 staining qualifies.

patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man with progressive dysphagia, early satiety, and a 9-kg weight loss is diagnosed with metastatic gastroesophageal junction adenocarcinoma after CT shows liver and peritoneal metastases. Biopsy confirms adenocarcinoma, and testing shows HER2 is not overexpressed. He previously completed adjuvant fluoropyrimidine-oxaliplatin chemotherapy 7 months ago after resection of a localized gastric primary and now has biopsy-proven recurrence with distant metastases. He is being evaluated for first-line treatment for recurrent disease, and the oncology team is considering adding a biologic agent to chemotherapy because the disease is metastatic and treatment-naive in the metastatic setting.
proposed_actions: Add a biologic agent to first-line chemotherapy because the cancer is metastatic and HER2-negative.
Apply the initial-diagnosis metastatic recommendation without considering the prior adjuvant regimen.

patient_vignette: A 42-year-old quality-control technician in a cable-coating plant comes to occupational health after a routine air-monitoring review. The workplace uses acrylamide-based materials, and the hygienist is planning personal exposure sampling for the technician during a 3-hour shift near the mixing area. The technician has no symptoms, but the team wants a method that will accurately capture airborne acrylamide. They initially considered a 37-mm plastic cassette because it is commonly used for other dusts, but the revised plan uses a personal sampling train with a glass-fiber filter in a 13-mm Swinnex cassette followed by a silica gel tube. Sampling is set for 1.5 hours at 1 L/min to fit the technician’s short task assignment, and the filter will be placed into a vial with methanol only after the shift ends, several hours later, before GC analysis with a nitrogen/phosphorus detector.
proposed_actions: Proceed with the planned acrylamide personal sampling because the flowrate and device look correct.
Accept delayed transfer of the glass-fiber filter to methanol after the shift.
Use the collected sample as a valid comparison for workplace exposure despite the abbreviated run.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Using a 37-mm plastic cassette for acrylamide sampling
Guideline Action: Do not use plastic cassettes because recoveries are poor
Source Statement: Plastic cassettes (37-mm) yielded poor recoveries of acrylamide and are therefore unsuitable

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Monitoring airborne acrylamide exposure
Guideline Action: Use a personal sampling train with a glass-fiber filter in a 13-mm Swinnex cassette followed by a silica gel tube
Source Statement: A worker's exposure to airborne acrylamide should be determined by using a personal sampling train consisting of a glass-fiber filter in a Swinnex cassette (13-mm) followed by a silica gel tube

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Collecting airborne acrylamide samples
Guideline Action: Collect samples at a maximum flowrate of 1 liter/min for at least 2 hours and no more than 120 liters of air
Source Statement: Samples should be collected at a maximum flowrate of 1 liter/min for a minimum of 2 hr; the maximum air volume should be 120 liters

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: After collecting airborne acrylamide samples
Guideline Action: Extract acrylamide from the silica gel tube with methanol
Source Statement: The silica gel tube should then be treated with methanol to extract the acrylamide

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: After sampling airborne acrylamide on glass-fiber filters
Guideline Action: Transfer filters immediately to glass vials containing 1 ml methanol to avoid evaporation losses
Source Statement: An important step in this method is the transfer of the glass-fiber filters to glass vials containing 1 ml of methanol immediately after sampling to avoid losses of acrylamide from the filter by evaporation

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Analyzing airborne acrylamide samples
Guideline Action: Use gas chromatography with a nitrogen/phosphorus detector
Source Statement: Analysis should be conducted by gas chromatography using a nitrogen/phosphorus detector

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Using animal evidence to guide acrylamide exposure limits
Guideline Action: Base occupational exposure limits on animal studies showing exposure-response relationships
Source Statement: However, many studies demonstrate a relationship between exposure and adverse health effects in animals. These studies are the bases for occupational exposure limits recommended by many organizations and government agencies

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Evaluating acrylamide carcinogenicity in animals
Guideline Action: Treat acrylamide as an animal carcinogen based on rat and mouse studies
Source Statement: Studies in rats and mice indicate that acrylamide is an animal carcinogen
proposed_actions_text:
- Proceed with the planned acrylamide personal sampling because the flowrate and device look correct.
- Accept delayed transfer of the glass-fiber filter to methanol after the shift.
- Use the collected sample as a valid comparison for workplace exposure despite the abbreviated run.

patient_vignette: A 42-year-old quality-control technician in a cable-coating plant comes to occupational health after a routine air-monitoring review. The workplace uses acrylamide-based materials, and the hygienist is planning personal exposure sampling for the technician during a 3-hour shift near the mixing area. The technician has no symptoms, but the team wants a method that will accurately capture airborne acrylamide. The draft plan uses a personal sampling train with a glass-fiber filter in a 13-mm Swinnex cassette followed by a silica gel tube, with collection at 1 L/min. After sampling, the silica gel tube is intended to be extracted with isopropanol, and the laboratory will analyze the samples by gas chromatography using a flame ionization detector. The filters themselves will be transferred immediately into glass vials containing methanol.
proposed_actions: Approve the plan because the sampler and immediate filter handling appear appropriate.
Proceed with isopropanol extraction of the silica gel tube.
Send the samples for GC with a flame ionization detector.

patient_vignette: A 29-year-old man who has sex with men presents for HIV prevention counseling. He reports multiple male partners over the past 6 months, inconsistent condom use, and one recent bacterial STI. He has no fever, rash, sore throat, or other symptoms suggestive of acute HIV infection, and he had a negative HIV test 2 weeks ago. He asks about starting PrEP and wonders whether he can take it only on days he expects sex, or whether he should add another antiretroviral medication for extra protection. He is otherwise healthy, but today’s baseline labs show an eGFR of 38 mL/min/1.73 m2. He is eager to start immediately and plans to follow up in 3 months.
proposed_actions: Start daily oral TDF/FTC PrEP despite the low eGFR
Recommend coitally timed PrEP because he only has sex intermittently
Add another antiretroviral medication for extra protection
Proceed without rechecking HIV status or renal eligibility
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: considering any antiretroviral medication other than TDF/FTC or TDF for PrEP
Guideline Action: Do not use other antiretroviral medications for PrEP
Source Statement: The use of other antiretroviral medications for PrEP, either in place of or in addition to TDF/FTC (or TDF) is not recommended. (IIIA)

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: considering noncontinuous or coitally timed oral PrEP
Guideline Action: Do not prescribe oral PrEP for intermittent, episodic, or other noncontinuous daily use
Source Statement: The prescription of oral PrEP for coitally-timed or other noncontinuous daily use is not recommended. (IIIA)

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: adult MSM at substantial risk of HIV acquisition
Guideline Action: Offer daily oral PrEP with TDF/FTC as one prevention option
Source Statement: PrEP is recommended as one prevention option for sexually-active adult MSM (men who have sex with men) at substantial risk of HIV acquisition (IA)

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: heterosexually active adult men or women at substantial risk of HIV acquisition
Guideline Action: Offer daily oral PrEP with TDF/FTC as one prevention option
Source Statement: PrEP is recommended as one prevention option for adult heterosexually active men and women who are at substantial risk of HIV acquisition. (IA)

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: adult injection drug users at substantial risk of HIV acquisition
Guideline Action: Offer daily oral PrEP with TDF/FTC as one prevention option
Source Statement: PrEP is recommended as one prevention option for adult injection drug users (IDU) at substantial risk of HIV acquisition. (IA)

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: before prescribing PrEP
Guideline Action: Exclude acute and chronic HIV infection by symptom history and HIV testing immediately before prescribing
Source Statement: Acute and chronic HIV infection must be excluded by symptom history and HIV testing immediately before PrEP is prescribed. (IA)

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: prescribing PrEP
Guideline Action: Use daily TDF 300 mg co-formulated with FTC 200 mg (Truvada) as the approved regimen
Source Statement: The only medication regimen approved by the Food and Drug Administration and recommended for PrEP with all the populations specified in this guideline is daily TDF 300 mg co-formulated with FTC 200 mg (Truvada) (IA)

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient is taking PrEP
Guideline Action: Assess HIV infection at least every 3 months
Source Statement: HIV infection should be assessed at least every 3 months while patients are taking PrEP so that those with incident infection do not continue taking it. The 2-drug regimen of TDF/FTC is inadequate therapy for established HIV infection, and its use may engender resistance to either or both drugs. (IA)
proposed_actions_text:
- Start daily oral TDF/FTC PrEP despite the low eGFR
- Recommend coitally timed PrEP because he only has sex intermittently
- Add another antiretroviral medication for extra protection
- Proceed without rechecking HIV status or renal eligibility

patient_vignette: A 29-year-old man who has sex with men presents for HIV prevention counseling. He reports multiple male partners over the past 6 months, inconsistent condom use, and one recent bacterial STI. He has no fever, rash, sore throat, or other symptoms suggestive of acute HIV infection, and he had a negative HIV test 2 weeks ago. He asks about starting PrEP and wonders whether he can take it only on days he expects sex, or whether he should add another antiretroviral medication for extra protection. He is otherwise healthy and has normal renal function. On review of outside records, however, he was diagnosed with HIV 8 months ago at another clinic, took ART briefly, and then stopped after losing insurance; he has not had confirmatory testing since. He is still interested in prevention counseling and follow-up testing.
proposed_actions: Offer daily oral TDF/FTC PrEP as routine prevention
Reassure him that the recent negative HIV test clears him for PrEP
Suggest intermittent PrEP or adding another antiretroviral for extra protection
Schedule routine PrEP follow-up without addressing possible established HIV infection

Activation Condition: adult MSM at substantial risk of HIV acquisition
Guideline Action: Offer daily oral PrEP with TDF/FTC as one prevention option
Source Statement: PrEP is recommended as one prevention option for sexually-active adult MSM (men who have sex with men) at substantial risk of HIV acquisition (IA)

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: heterosexually active adult men or women at substantial risk of HIV acquisition
Guideline Action: Offer daily oral PrEP with TDF/FTC as one prevention option
Source Statement: PrEP is recommended as one prevention option for adult heterosexually active men and women who are at substantial risk of HIV acquisition. (IA)

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: adult injection drug users at substantial risk of HIV acquisition
Guideline Action: Offer daily oral PrEP with TDF/FTC as one prevention option
Source Statement: PrEP is recommended as one prevention option for adult injection drug users (IDU) at substantial risk of HIV acquisition. (IA)

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: before prescribing PrEP
Guideline Action: Exclude acute and chronic HIV infection by symptom history and HIV testing immediately before prescribing
Source Statement: Acute and chronic HIV infection must be excluded by symptom history and HIV testing immediately before PrEP is prescribed. (IA)

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: prescribing PrEP
Guideline Action: Use daily TDF 300 mg co-formulated with FTC 200 mg (Truvada) as the approved regimen
Source Statement: The only medication regimen approved by the Food and Drug Administration and recommended for PrEP with all the populations specified in this guideline is daily TDF 300 mg co-formulated with FTC 200 mg (Truvada) (IA)

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient is taking PrEP
Guideline Action: Assess HIV infection at least every 3 months
Source Statement: HIV infection should be assessed at least every 3 months while patients are taking PrEP so that those with incident infection do not continue taking it. The 2-drug regimen of TDF/FTC is inadequate therapy for established HIV infection, and its use may engender resistance to either or both drugs. (IA)
proposed_actions_text:
- Offer daily oral TDF/FTC PrEP as routine prevention
- Reassure him that the recent negative HIV test clears him for PrEP
- Suggest intermittent PrEP or adding another antiretroviral for extra protection
- Schedule routine PrEP follow-up without addressing possible established HIV infection

patient_vignette: A 54-year-old pet shop employee presents with 6 days of fever, headache, dry cough, and worsening fatigue. He reports cleaning several parakeet cages at work last week and says two birds had been “sneezing” and looked ill, but he still helped move them for sale. Today he is febrile to 39.1°C and mildly short of breath, but he is alert and hemodynamically stable. Chest exam shows scattered crackles. He asks whether he should keep working around the birds while he recovers. The shop owner mentions the birds were not being sold; they were already set aside for veterinary treatment after a recent diagnosis of avian chlamydiosis.
proposed_actions: Tell the shop not to buy or sell the birds because they appear sick
Focus on the avian chlamydiosis exposure and ignore that the birds are not part of a sale
Assume the bird-handling restriction is automatically triggered by any sick bird contact
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Bird has signs of avian chlamydiosis
Guideline Action: Do not buy or sell the bird.
Source Statement: Avoid purchasing or selling birds that have signs of AC.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Birds are being treated for avian chlamydiosis
Guideline Action: Do not expose treated birds to untreated birds or other potential sources of infection.
Source Statement: Because treated birds can be reinfected, they should not be exposed to untreated birds or other potential sources of infection.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Humans with psittacosis
Guideline Action: Use tetracyclines as the drugs of choice.
Source Statement: Tetracyclines are the drugs of choice (3 ).

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Most patients with psittacosis
Guideline Action: Treat with oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily or tetracycline hydrochloride 500 mg four times daily.
Source Statement: Most patients respond to oral therapy (100 mg of doxycycline administered twice a day or 500 mg of tetracycline hydrochloride administered four times a day).

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Severely ill patient with psittacosis needing initial treatment
Guideline Action: Give intravenous doxycycline hyclate 4.4 mg/kg/day divided into two infusions, up to 100 mg per dose.
Source Statement: For initial treatment of severely ill patients, doxycycline hyclate can be administered intravenously at a dosage of 4.4 mg/kg (2 mg/lb) body weight per day divided into two infusions per day (up to 100 mg per dose).

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Psittacosis treatment
Guideline Action: Continue treatment for at least 10-14 days after fever abates.
Source Statement: However, relapse can occur, and treatment must continue for at least 10-14 days after fever abates.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Patient with psittacosis
Guideline Action: Use standard infection-control precautions; do not use special isolation procedures such as private room, negative pressure airflow, or masks.
Source Statement: Standard infection-control precautions are sufficient for patients with psittacosis, and specific isolation procedures (e.g., private room, negative pressure air flow, and masks) are not indicated.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: Person in contact with infected birds
Guideline Action: Inform them about the disease and have them wear protective clothing, gloves, a disposable surgical cap, and an N95 or higher-efficiency respirator when cleaning cages or handling infected birds.
Source Statement: Inform all persons in contact with infected birds about the nature of the disease. Instruct them to wear protective clothing, gloves, a disposable surgical cap, and a respirator with an N95 rating or a higher-efficiency respirator when cleaning cages or handling infected birds.
proposed_actions_text:
- Tell the shop not to buy or sell the birds because they appear sick
- Focus on the avian chlamydiosis exposure and ignore that the birds are not part of a sale
- Assume the bird-handling restriction is automatically triggered by any sick bird contact

patient_vignette: A 54-year-old pet shop employee presents with 6 days of fever, headache, dry cough, and worsening fatigue. He reports cleaning several parakeet cages at work last week and says two birds had been “sneezing” and looked ill, but he still helped move them for sale. Today he is febrile to 39.1°C and mildly short of breath, but he is alert and hemodynamically stable. Chest exam shows scattered crackles. A chest radiograph obtained in clinic shows only mild peribronchial thickening without focal infiltrate or respiratory distress, and he is able to take oral medications.
proposed_actions: Start intravenous doxycycline because psittacosis is suspected
Treat as if he needs inpatient-level initial therapy
Use the severity of the bird exposure alone to justify IV doxycycline

- Treat as if he needs inpatient-level initial therapy

patient_vignette: A 39-year-old man works in a PVC flooring plant where softened plastics are cut and heat-sealed on the production line. He reports that the job involves handling plasticizers during manufacturing and processing, and he asks about a chemical used in the materials. He has no acute symptoms, but he is worried after learning the compound is common in flexible plastics. The occupational health nurse documents that his exposure occurs mainly in a nearby storage area where sealed drums are received and inventoried, rather than during addition to plastics or processing of softened products. The workplace ventilation is variable, but there is no evidence that he personally performs the production tasks associated with the material.
proposed_actions: Assume this is an occupational DEHP exposure during PVC production and document it as such.
Treat the workplace as a typical processing exposure with variable airborne concentrations based on the production line description.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 50
Recommendation Class: NO_RECOMMENDATION
Activation Condition: DEHP is used primarily as a softener in plastics, especially PVC; occupational exposure can occur during production, addition to plastics, or processing of softened plastics
Guideline Action: No clinical action specified
Source Statement: Occupational exposure occurs during the production of DEHP, during the addition of DEHP to plastics, and in the processing of softened plastics.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 50
Recommendation Class: NO_RECOMMENDATION
Activation Condition: DEHP exposure is being assessed in an occupational setting
Guideline Action: Consider that reported air concentrations in workplaces have ranged from very low to high levels depending on industry
Source Statement: Air concentrations between 0.01 and 2.0 mg/m3 were recorded... Concentrations between 1.7 and 66 mg/m3 were recorded... DEHP air concentrations between 0.09 and 0.16 mg/m3 were reported.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 50
Recommendation Class: NO_RECOMMENDATION
Activation Condition: DEHP is administered orally in rats
Guideline Action: Expect substantial gut hydrolysis to MEHP before absorption
Source Statement: DEHP is hydrolyzed in the gut.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 50
Recommendation Class: NO_RECOMMENDATION
Activation Condition: DEHP is given at high oral doses in rats
Guideline Action: Expect unmetabolized DEHP to appear in liver only after high doses
Source Statement: In the liver unhydrolyzed DEHP can only be found after administration of high doses.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 50
Recommendation Class: NO_RECOMMENDATION
Activation Condition: MEHP is administered orally
Guideline Action: Expect MEHP to be well absorbed
Source Statement: Experiments with p.o. administration of MEHP indicated that this metabolite was well absorbed.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 50
Recommendation Class: NO_RECOMMENDATION
Activation Condition: DEHP is administered intravenously in rats
Guideline Action: Expect rapid elimination from blood with short half-lives
Source Statement: Intravenously administered DEHP is rapidly eliminated from blood.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 50
Recommendation Class: NO_RECOMMENDATION
Activation Condition: DEHP exposure is being evaluated for distribution in the body
Guideline Action: Expect rapid distribution to liver, carcass, lung, and spleen after intravenous dosing
Source Statement: Radioactivity from DEHP was rapidly distributed to liver, carcass, lung and spleen when administered i.v.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 50
Recommendation Class: NO_RECOMMENDATION
Activation Condition: DEHP is administered orally
Guideline Action: Expect distribution mainly as MEHP
Source Statement: Perorally administered DEHP is mainly distributed as MEHP.
proposed_actions_text:
- Assume this is an occupational DEHP exposure during PVC production and document it as such.
- Treat the workplace as a typical processing exposure with variable airborne concentrations based on the production line description.

patient_vignette: A 46-year-old woman works in a PVC flooring plant where softened plastics are cut and heat-sealed on the production line. She asks the occupational health nurse about a chemical commonly used in flexible plastics because several coworkers have discussed it. She reports that the job involves handling plasticizers during manufacturing, but her own assignment is quality control in an isolated office adjacent to the plant, with no routine access to the production floor. Air monitoring in the facility has been variable, but she has not had direct task-based exposure to the softened-plastic processing area.
proposed_actions: Record her as having occupational DEHP exposure from PVC production and processing.
Apply the workplace air concentration note as if it directly reflects her personal exposure.

patient_vignette: A 3-year-old boy is brought to clinic in early October for a routine visit. He has a history of asthma with intermittent wheezing over the past year, including an urgent care visit for wheeze 4 months ago, but he is currently well and afebrile. His mother asks about the flu vaccine because the family wants him protected before flu season starts. He received one influenza vaccine dose last winter but missed the second dose because of a family move. The community has not yet reported influenza activity this season. The parent specifically asks whether he needs two doses again this year or just one.
proposed_actions: Automatically schedule 2 influenza vaccine doses 4 weeks apart as if this were his first season of vaccination.
Document that he is unvaccinated and needs the full two-dose primary series.
Apply the original first-season recommendation without checking prior vaccine history.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: child aged 2 through 4 years with asthma, recurrent wheezing, or wheezing/asthma in the past 12 months
Guideline Action: Do not give FluMist.
Source Statement: Children whose parents or caregivers answer 'yes' to this question and children who have asthma or who had a wheezing episode noted in the medical record within the past 12 months should not receive FluMist.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: person caring for severely immunosuppressed persons who require a protective environment
Guideline Action: Do not give FluMist / LAIV.
Source Statement: Persons who care for severely immunosuppressed persons who require a protective environment should not receive FluMist given the theoretical risk for transmission of the live-attenuated vaccine virus.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: child aged under 9 years
Guideline Action: Do not use Afluria.
Source Statement: ACIP does not recommend the U.S.-licensed CSL Biotherapies' TIV, Afluria, for children aged <9 years.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: person with a previous severe allergic reaction to influenza vaccine
Guideline Action: Do not give future influenza vaccine.
Source Statement: A previous severe allergic reaction to influenza vaccine, regardless of the component suspected to be responsible for the reaction, is a contraindication to future receipt of the vaccine.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: all persons aged 6 months or older
Guideline Action: Offer annual influenza vaccination every year.
Source Statement: Routine annual influenza vaccination is recommended for all persons aged ≥6 months.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: vaccination is being planned and influenza activity has not yet started in the community
Guideline Action: Vaccinate as soon as vaccine is available, before influenza activity begins if possible.
Source Statement: Vaccination optimally should occur before onset of influenza activity in the community. Therefore, vaccination providers should offer vaccination as soon as vaccine is available.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: influenza viruses are circulating in the community
Guideline Action: Continue offering vaccination throughout the influenza season.
Source Statement: Vaccination should be offered throughout the influenza season (i.e., as long as influenza viruses are circulating in the community).

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: INITIAL_DIAGNOSIS
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: child aged 6 months through 8 years receiving influenza vaccine for the first season
Guideline Action: Give 2 doses of influenza vaccine at least 4 weeks apart.
Source Statement: Children aged 6 months through 8 years require 2 doses of influenza vaccine (administered a minimum of 4 weeks apart) during their first season of vaccination to optimize immune response.
proposed_actions_text:
- Automatically schedule 2 influenza vaccine doses 4 weeks apart as if this were his first season of vaccination.
- Document that he is unvaccinated and needs the full two-dose primary series.
- Apply the original first-season recommendation without checking prior vaccine history.

patient_vignette: A 3-year-old boy is brought to clinic in early October for a routine visit. He has a history of asthma with intermittent wheezing over the past year, including an urgent care visit for wheeze 4 months ago, but he is currently well and afebrile. His mother asks about the flu vaccine because the family wants him protected before flu season starts. He has never received an influenza vaccine before. The community reported influenza activity began last week, and several families in his daycare have had influenza-like illness. The parent wonders whether they should wait until later in the month to vaccinate.
proposed_actions: State that vaccination should be deferred because the ideal pre-season window has passed.
Tell the family to wait until influenza activity declines before immunizing.
Treat the timing recommendation as inapplicable and skip offering the vaccine now.

patient_vignette: A 42-year-old man works in a battery recycling plant and presents for a routine occupational health visit. He reports no acute symptoms, but says his job involves handling lead-containing dust during dismantling and cleanup of lead-acid batteries. The company recently began air sampling in the work area, and the occupational nurse is reviewing exposure controls. His supervisor asks whether the current monitoring program is appropriate and whether workers like him should have medical surveillance. The patient has been on the job for 6 years, works full time, and spends most shifts near the smelting and sorting stations. However, he was transferred last month to a clerical role in the administrative office and no longer enters the production floor or participates in battery dismantling, cleanup, or smelting operations.
proposed_actions: Recommend the full lead-exposure control package as if he is still subject to inorganic lead exposure
Arrange biologic monitoring with whole blood lead testing immediately
Document ongoing lead surveillance solely based on his prior job title
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: workplace exposure is to inorganic lead
Guideline Action: Control employee exposure by adhering to the standard sections in this document.
Source Statement: recommends that employee exposure to inorganic lead in the workplace be controlled by adherence to the following sections.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: workplace exposure is to inorganic lead
Guideline Action: Apply the standard as a lifetime 8-hour day, 40-hour week protection target.
Source Statement: The standard is designed to protect the health and safety of workers for an 8-hour day, 40-hour week over a working lifetime; compliance with the standard should therefore prevent adverse effects of lead on the health and safety of workers.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: workplace exposure is to inorganic lead
Guideline Action: Use valid, reproducible, and available measurement techniques for compliance determinations.
Source Statement: The standard is measurable by techniques that are valid, reproducible, and available to industry and government agencies.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: workplace exposure is to inorganic lead
Guideline Action: Recognize that sufficient technology exists to comply with the recommended standard.
Source Statement: Sufficient technology exists to permit compliance with the recommended standard.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: workplace air exposure to inorganic lead
Guideline Action: Keep the 8-hour time-weighted average airborne lead concentration at or below 0.15 mg Pb/m^3.
Source Statement: workers shall not be exposed to inorganic lead at a concentration greater that 0.15 mg Pb/m^ determined as a time-weighted average (TWA) exposure for an 8-hour workday.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: environmental samples are collected for lead monitoring
Guideline Action: Collect environmental samples using Appendix I or an equivalent method, and analyze them using Appendix II or an equivalent method with comparable precision and accuracy.
Source Statement: Procedures for collection of environmental samples shall be as provided in Appendix I, or by an equivalent method. Analysis of samples shall be as provided in Appendix II, or by any method shown to be equivalent in precision and accuracy to the method specified in Appendix II.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: worker is subject to exposure to inorganic lead
Guideline Action: Make biologic monitoring available to the worker.
Source Statement: Biologic monitoring shall be made available to all workers subject to "exposure to inorganic lead."

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: worker is subject to exposure to inorganic lead
Guideline Action: Use whole blood lead testing, or urine lead testing as an alternative, to assess lead absorption.
Source Statement: It consists of sampling and analysis of whole blood, or alternatively, of urine for lead content.
proposed_actions_text:
- Recommend the full lead-exposure control package as if he is still subject to inorganic lead exposure
- Arrange biologic monitoring with whole blood lead testing immediately
- Document ongoing lead surveillance solely based on his prior job title

patient_vignette: A 42-year-old man works in a battery recycling plant and presents for a routine occupational health visit. He reports no acute symptoms, but says his job involves handling lead-containing dust during dismantling and cleanup of lead-acid batteries. The company recently began air sampling in the work area, and the occupational nurse is reviewing exposure controls. His supervisor asks whether the current monitoring program is appropriate and whether workers like him should have medical surveillance. The patient has been on the job for 6 years and spends most shifts near the smelting and sorting stations where inorganic lead exposure is possible. On review of the industrial hygiene log, the sampler used a field screen for lead particulates, but the actual compliance determination is being made from an unvalidated in-house colorimetric method that has not been shown to match a standard analytical procedure.
proposed_actions: Accept the in-house colorimetric result for compliance decisions
Proceed as though the environmental monitoring program satisfies lead standard requirements
Document that the air sampling methods are appropriate because samples were collected
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: workplace exposure is to inorganic lead
Guideline Action: Control employee exposure by adhering to the standard sections in this document.
Source Statement: recommends that employee exposure to inorganic lead in the workplace be controlled by adherence to the following sections.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: workplace exposure is to inorganic lead
Guideline Action: Apply the standard as a lifetime 8-hour day, 40-hour week protection target.
Source Statement: The standard is designed to protect the health and safety of workers for an 8-hour day, 40-hour week over a working lifetime; compliance with the standard should therefore prevent adverse effects of lead on the health and safety of workers.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: workplace exposure is to inorganic lead
Guideline Action: Use valid, reproducible, and available measurement techniques for compliance determinations.
Source Statement: The standard is measurable by techniques that are valid, reproducible, and available to industry and government agencies.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: workplace exposure is to inorganic lead
Guideline Action: Recognize that sufficient technology exists to comply with the recommended standard.
Source Statement: Sufficient technology exists to permit compliance with the recommended standard.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: workplace air exposure to inorganic lead
Guideline Action: Keep the 8-hour time-weighted average airborne lead concentration at or below 0.15 mg Pb/m^3.
Source Statement: workers shall not be exposed to inorganic lead at a concentration greater that 0.15 mg Pb/m^ determined as a time-weighted average (TWA) exposure for an 8-hour workday.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: environmental samples are collected for lead monitoring
Guideline Action: Collect environmental samples using Appendix I or an equivalent method, and analyze them using Appendix II or an equivalent method with comparable precision and accuracy.
Source Statement: Procedures for collection of environmental samples shall be as provided in Appendix I, or by an equivalent method. Analysis of samples shall be as provided in Appendix II, or by any method shown to be equivalent in precision and accuracy to the method specified in Appendix II.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: worker is subject to exposure to inorganic lead
Guideline Action: Make biologic monitoring available to the worker.
Source Statement: Biologic monitoring shall be made available to all workers subject to "exposure to inorganic lead."

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: worker is subject to exposure to inorganic lead
Guideline Action: Use whole blood lead testing, or urine lead testing as an alternative, to assess lead absorption.
Source Statement: It consists of sampling and analysis of whole blood, or alternatively, of urine for lead content.
proposed_actions_text:
- Accept the in-house colorimetric result for compliance decisions
- Proceed as though the environmental monitoring program satisfies lead standard requirements
- Document that the air sampling methods are appropriate because samples were collected

patient_vignette: A 34-year-old elementary school teacher comes to urgent care after hearing that several students in her district developed sudden vomiting and diarrhea over the past three days. She is visibly anxious and says parents are “panicking” on social media, asking whether the school cafeteria food was contaminated and whether this could be a deliberate poisoning. She has no fever or abdominal tenderness and is mainly worried about what to tell her family and coworkers. In the waiting room, she repeatedly asks for confirmation that the outbreak is “probably nothing serious” and wants to know the exact cause immediately. The clinic has received a preliminary update from public health that the illnesses appear to be from a self-limited norovirus cluster, and local officials are still finalizing the source and scope of the illness.
proposed_actions: Speculate that the cafeteria food was deliberately poisoned and discuss possible blame
Address liability or responsibility before the investigation is complete
Invoke bioterrorism language in the initial counseling
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: When the public may be worried or outraged
Guideline Action: Do not dismiss outrage or tell people they should not be afraid.
Source Statement: Don't Dismiss Outrage. ... When people are afraid and worried, the worst thing to do is pretend that they're not. The second worst thing is to tell them they shouldn't be.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: When giving reassurance during an early crisis
Guideline Action: Do not over-reassure or minimize the risk.
Source Statement: Don't over-reassure. ... Reassurance can backfire.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: When the public or media ask about cause, blame, or liability early in the event
Guideline Action: Do not discuss liability, blame, or cause before the situation is contained.
Source Statement: Avoid discussing liability: Questions of cause, blame, responsibility, and liability often follow a crisis. It is not appropriate to discuss them at the early stages.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: When the event involves a possible bioterrorism claim or rumor
Guideline Action: Do not speculate about bioterrorism; refer questions to law enforcement and coordinate statements.
Source Statement: CDC will not comment to the media about the possibility of an outbreak being a bioterrorist event; nor will it comment on the FBI's participation in an investigation unless first confirmed by the FBI to the media.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: When communicating during a crisis or emergency
Guideline Action: Use crisis and emergency risk communication principles to help people make the best possible decisions for their well-being.
Source Statement: CERC is the attempt by public health professionals to provide information that allows individuals, stakeholders, and entire communities to make the best possible decisions for their well-being during a crisis or emergency.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: When communicating during a crisis or emergency
Guideline Action: Be first, be right, be credible, express empathy, promote action, and show respect.
Source Statement: Throughout this book, six principles of effective crisis and risk communication are emphasized: 1. Be First... 2. Be Right... 3. Be Credible... 4. Express Empathy... 5. Promote Action... 6. Show Respect.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: When the public is affected by a crisis
Guideline Action: Provide simple, credible, accurate, consistent information on time.
Source Statement: When communicating in the initial phase of an emergency, it is important to present information that is simple, credible, accurate, consistent, and delivered on time.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: When people need to know what to do during a crisis
Guideline Action: Give people meaningful actions they can take and offer a range of responses.
Source Statement: Giving people meaningful things to do calms anxiety, helps restore order, and promotes a restored sense of control.
proposed_actions_text:
- Speculate that the cafeteria food was deliberately poisoned and discuss possible blame
- Address liability or responsibility before the investigation is complete
- Invoke bioterrorism language in the initial counseling

patient_vignette: A 34-year-old elementary school teacher comes to urgent care after hearing that several students in her district developed sudden vomiting and diarrhea within the same day. She is visibly anxious and says parents are “panicking” on social media, asking whether the school cafeteria food was contaminated and whether this could be a deliberate poisoning. She has no fever or abdominal tenderness and is mainly worried about what to tell her family and coworkers. In the waiting room, she repeatedly asks for confirmation that the outbreak is “probably nothing serious” and wants to know the exact cause immediately. The clinic has confirmed that the same students attended a school picnic and that local officials have already identified a common food source; the investigation is now closed and a press release has been issued with the final cause.
proposed_actions: Treat the visit like an unfolding emergency communication scenario
Avoid discussing the now-confirmed source and final cause
Focus on crisis messaging as if the event were still under active investigation

patient_vignette: A 42-year-old man is evaluated after a positive interferon-gamma release assay during work screening. He has no cough, fever, weight loss, or night sweats, and his chest radiograph is normal, consistent with latent tuberculosis infection. He reports drinking 4–6 beers nightly for years, although he says he has reduced to a few drinks only on weekends since his test result. He asks whether he can take the short-course rifampin plus pyrazinamide regimen because a coworker completed it. He has no known HIV infection and no prior liver disease, but he does take atorvastatin and recently started isoniazid prophylaxis at an outside clinic before being referred for TB counseling.
proposed_actions: Start rifampin plus pyrazinamide for latent TB because the chest radiograph is normal.
Reassure him that reduced weekend drinking makes the regimen acceptable.
Proceed despite the medication list because atorvastatin is the only relevant drug.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has latent tuberculosis infection
Guideline Action: do not generally offer rifampin plus pyrazinamide regimen
Source Statement: ATS and CDC now recommend that this regimen should generally not be offered to persons with LTBI.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has latent tuberculosis infection and is HIV-negative
Guideline Action: do not generally offer rifampin plus pyrazinamide regimen
Source Statement: ATS and CDC now recommend that this regimen should generally not be offered to persons with LTBI for either HIV-negative or HIV-infected persons.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient has latent tuberculosis infection and is HIV-infected
Guideline Action: do not generally offer rifampin plus pyrazinamide regimen
Source Statement: ATS and CDC now recommend that this regimen should generally not be offered to persons with LTBI for either HIV-negative or HIV-infected persons.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient with latent tuberculosis infection is taking other liver-injury medications
Guideline Action: do not offer rifampin plus pyrazinamide regimen
Source Statement: this regimen should never be offered to patients who 1) are concurrently taking other medications associated with liver injury

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient with latent tuberculosis infection drinks excessive alcohol
Guideline Action: do not offer rifampin plus pyrazinamide regimen
Source Statement: this regimen should never be offered to patients who 2) drink excessive amounts of alcohol, even if alcohol use is discontinued during treatment

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient with latent tuberculosis infection has underlying liver disease
Guideline Action: do not offer rifampin plus pyrazinamide regimen
Source Statement: this regimen should never be offered to patients who 3) have underlying liver disease

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient with latent tuberculosis infection has a history of isoniazid-associated liver injury
Guideline Action: do not offer rifampin plus pyrazinamide regimen
Source Statement: this regimen should never be offered to patients who 4) have a history of INH-associated liver injury.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: patient taking rifampin plus pyrazinamide has AST greater than five times upper limit of normal without symptoms
Guideline Action: discontinue rifampin plus pyrazinamide immediately and do not resume
Source Statement: Use of RZ should be discontinued immediately and not resumed for any of the following findings: 1) AT greater than five times the upper limit of normal range in an asymptomatic person
proposed_actions_text:
- Start rifampin plus pyrazinamide for latent TB because the chest radiograph is normal.
- Reassure him that reduced weekend drinking makes the regimen acceptable.
- Proceed despite the medication list because atorvastatin is the only relevant drug.

patient_vignette: A 42-year-old man is evaluated after a positive interferon-gamma release assay during work screening. He has no cough, fever, weight loss, or night sweats. A chest radiograph is normal except for a calcified right apical granuloma, and the clinician documents latent tuberculosis infection. He reports drinking 4–6 beers nightly for years, though he says he has been abstinent for the past two weeks. He asks whether he can take the short-course rifampin plus pyrazinamide regimen because a coworker completed it. He has no known HIV infection and no known liver disease, but he recalls that a prior course of isoniazid for exposure prophylaxis was stopped years ago because of mild nausea, not hepatitis. Baseline AST and ALT are within normal limits.
proposed_actions: Offer rifampin plus pyrazinamide because the prior isoniazid problem was only nausea.
Ignore the alcohol history since he has been abstinent for two weeks.
Use the short-course regimen because the chest radiograph is normal and he is HIV-negative.

Source Statement: ATS and CDC now recommend that this regimen should generally not be offered to persons with LTBI.
