patient_vignette: A 29-year-old warehouse worker presents to occupational health after a late-afternoon shift in a non-air-conditioned loading dock. He had been stacking boxes and moving pallets for about 90 minutes with only brief water breaks. On the site nurse’s check, his oral temperature is 37.6°C, and he reports marked fatigue, heavy sweating, and a throbbing headache. He says he feels better after resting indoors for 20 minutes, but the supervisor wants him to return to the dock to finish the shift. He is otherwise healthy and has no history of heat illness. The workplace had been unusually hot all day, and several coworkers were also complaining of the heat.
proposed_actions: Advise him not to return to work for the rest of the day because this is heat illness.
Document heat exposure and send him home immediately based on the headache and sweating alone.
Recommend stopping all further exposures as if the body temperature were above 38°C.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: heat exposure is prolonged or severe enough to cause body temperature above 38°C
Guideline Action: do not continue further exposures during the same day
Source Statement: There is abundant evidence that the physiological strain of an exposure which raises body temperature above 38°C is such as to contraindicate further exposures during the same day; it may take hours for complete recovery.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: employee exposure to heat in the workplace
Guideline Action: control heat exposure by requiring compliance with the work practice standard
Source Statement: NIOSH recommends that employee exposure to heat in the workplace be controlled by requiring compliance with the work practice standard set forth in the following sections.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: hot environmental conditions exceed WBGT 79°F
Guideline Action: apply the standard to all places of employment indoors and outdoors and to all employees except those required to wear impermeable protective clothing
Source Statement: The provisions of this standard are applicable to all places of employment, indoors and outdoors, and to all employees except those who are required to wear impermeable protective clothing.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: continuous exposure for one hour or intermittent exposure for two hours and WBGT exceeds 79°F for men or 76°F for women
Guideline Action: initiate one or a combination of the listed protective practices to keep body core temperature from exceeding 100.4°F
Source Statement: When exposure of an employee is continuous for one hour or intermittent for a period of two hours and the time-weighted average WBGT exceeds 79°F for men or 76°F for women, then any one or combination of the following practices shall be initiated to insure that the employee's body care temperature does not exceed 100.4°F:

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: employee is unacclimatized
Guideline Action: acclimatize over 6 days starting at 50 percent of anticipated total work load and time exposure on day 1, then increase by 10 percent daily to 100 percent on day 6
Source Statement: Unacclimatized employees shall be acclimatized over a period of 6 days. The acclimatization schedule shall begin with 50 percent of the anticipated total work load and time exposure on the first day, followed by daily 10 percent increments building up 100 percent total exposure on the sixth day.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: regular acclimatized employee returns after nine or more consecutive calendar days of leave
Guideline Action: use a 4-day reacclimatization schedule starting at 50 percent on day 1 and increasing by 20 percent daily to 100 percent on day 4
Source Statement: Regular acclimatized employees who return from nine or more consecutive calendar days of leave, shall undergo a four day acclimatization period. The acclimatization schedule shall begin with 50 percent of the anticipated total exposure on the first day, followed by daily 20 percent increments building up to 100 percent total exposure on the fourth day.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: regular acclimatized employee returns after four consecutive days of illness
Guideline Action: obtain medical permission to return to work and use a 4-day reacclimatization schedule as above
Source Statement: Regular acclimatized employees who return from four consecutive days of illness should have medical permission to return to the job, and should undergo a four day re-acclimatization period as defined in (2) above.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: workers are exposed to hot jobs
Guideline Action: implement a work and rest regimen to reduce peaks of physiological strain and improve recovery during rest periods
Source Statement: A work and rest regimen shall be implemented to reduce the peaks of physiological strain and to improve recovery during rest periods.
proposed_actions_text:
- Advise him not to return to work for the rest of the day because this is heat illness.
- Document heat exposure and send him home immediately based on the headache and sweating alone.
- Recommend stopping all further exposures as if the body temperature were above 38°C.

patient_vignette: A 29-year-old warehouse worker presents to occupational health after a late-afternoon shift in a climate-controlled loading dock. He had been stacking boxes and moving pallets for about 90 minutes with only brief water breaks. On the site nurse’s check, his oral temperature is 38.5°C, and he reports marked fatigue, heavy sweating, and a throbbing headache. He says he feels better after resting indoors for 20 minutes, but the supervisor wants him to return to the dock to finish the shift. He is otherwise healthy and has no history of heat illness. The workplace had been unusually hot outside, but the dock itself remained air-conditioned and below the company’s heat-alert threshold.
proposed_actions: Apply the workplace heat work-practice standard as if all employee heat exposure were uncontrolled hot-job exposure.
Institute a formal work-and-rest regimen specifically for hot jobs without confirming that the job site is actually hot.
Treat this as the same occupational heat-exposure scenario as an uncooled loading dock.

Source Statement: A work and rest regimen shall be implemented to reduce the peaks of physiological strain and to improve recovery during rest periods.
proposed_actions_text:
- Apply the workplace heat work-practice standard as if all employee heat exposure were uncontrolled hot-job exposure.
- Institute a formal work-and-rest regimen specifically for hot jobs without confirming that the job site is actually hot.
- Treat this as the same occupational heat-exposure scenario as an uncooled loading dock.

patient_vignette: A 29-year-old emergency department nurse sustains a needlestick injury while drawing blood from a patient with known untreated HIV infection and a high viral load. The needle was visibly blood-filled and penetrated deeply into her finger before she was able to wash the area. She presents within 45 minutes of the exposure. She is otherwise healthy, not pregnant, and has no contraindications to antiretroviral medications. The injury occurred while disposing of a phlebotomy needle after the blood draw was completed, and on exam the puncture is superficial with no visible blood on the device. This is an occupational exposure, but the source contact appears limited and low-volume rather than a clearly high-risk percutaneous inoculation.
proposed_actions: Start HIV postexposure chemoprophylaxis immediately as if this were a high-risk occupational needlestick.
Select a full PEP regimen including zidovudine and lamivudine without reassessing transmission risk.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: occupational exposure to HIV with negligible risk
Guideline Action: do not give postexposure prophylaxis
Source Statement: For exposures with negligible risk, PEP is not justified (Table 1).

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: exposure is not occupational exposure to HIV
Guideline Action: do not offer PEP
Source Statement: Not offer-PEP should not be offered because these are not occupational exposures to HIV (1 ).

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: skin exposure to HIV without increased risk
Guideline Action: do not give PEP because drug toxicity outweighs benefit
Source Statement: For skin exposures without increased risk, the risk for drug toxicity outweighs the benefit of PEP.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: suspected heroin overdose with coma or severe respiratory distress
Guideline Action: give naloxone
Source Statement: Naloxone remains the treatment of choice for coma and severe respiratory distress associated with possible drug overdose.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: new case of scopolamine-containing heroin overdose
Guideline Action: report promptly to local poison-control center and health department
Source Statement: new cases should be reported promptly to the local poison-control center and health department.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: occupational exposure to HIV with highest risk for transmission
Guideline Action: recommend chemoprophylaxis
Source Statement: Chemoprophylaxis should be recommended to exposed workers after occupational exposures associated with the highest risk for HIV transmission.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: considering any HIV postexposure prophylaxis regimen
Guideline Action: include zidovudine (ZDV)
Source Statement: At present, ZDV should be considered for all PEP regimens because ZDV is the only agent for which data support the efficacy of PEP in the clinical setting.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: HIV postexposure prophylaxis regimen is being selected
Guideline Action: usually add lamivudine (3TC) to ZDV
Source Statement: 3TC should usually be added to ZDV for increased antiretroviral activity and activity against many ZDV-resistant strains.
proposed_actions_text:
- Start HIV postexposure chemoprophylaxis immediately as if this were a high-risk occupational needlestick.
- Select a full PEP regimen including zidovudine and lamivudine without reassessing transmission risk.

patient_vignette: A 29-year-old emergency department nurse sustains a needlestick injury while drawing blood from a patient with known untreated HIV infection and a high viral load. The needle was visibly blood-filled and penetrated deeply into her finger before she was able to wash the area. She presents within 45 minutes of the exposure. She is otherwise healthy, not pregnant, and has no contraindications to antiretroviral medications. However, the source patient is her roommate and the blood draw occurred during a private home visit, not as part of an employment-related duty. The exposure itself is percutaneous and recent, but it did not arise from a workplace occupational activity.
proposed_actions: Recommend HIV chemoprophylaxis as for an occupational exposure.
Choose zidovudine-based PEP without first recognizing that this is not an occupational exposure.

patient_vignette: A 29-year-old man comes to a primary care visit 3 weeks before a planned 10-day trip to Lima, Peru. He has no chronic medical problems and no vaccine allergies. He received a full hepatitis A vaccine series in childhood but cannot find the records. He says he is healthy, takes no regular medications, and asks whether he can do anything now to reduce his risk before leaving. Because departure is soon, he would like the fastest protection available and is willing to be vaccinated today if needed.
proposed_actions: Administer a first hepatitis A vaccine dose immediately because travel is soon.
Recommend hepatitis A vaccination solely because he is traveling to Peru.
Advise that vaccine now is needed for immediate partial protection before departure.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: routine prevention of hepatitis A in the United States
Guideline Action: Implement routine hepatitis A vaccination for children nationwide.
Source Statement: All children should receive hepatitis A vaccine at age 1 year (i.e., 12-23 months).

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: state, county, or community already has a hepatitis A vaccination program for children aged 2-18 years
Guideline Action: Maintain the existing childhood hepatitis A vaccination program.
Source Statement: States, counties, and communities with existing hepatitis A vaccination programs for children aged 2-18 years are encouraged to maintain these programs.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: person is traveling to or working in a country with high or intermediate hepatitis A endemicity and is susceptible
Guideline Action: Vaccinate before departure or give immune globulin before departure; vaccine is preferred.
Source Statement: All susceptible persons traveling to or working in countries that have high or intermediate hepatitis A endemicity (Figure 4) should be vaccinated or receive IG before departure (Tables 1-4). Hepatitis A vaccination at the age-appropriate dose is preferred (Tables 2-4).

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: travel is planned and first vaccine dose can be given now
Guideline Action: Give the first hepatitis A vaccine dose as soon as travel is considered.
Source Statement: The first dose of hepatitis A vaccine should be administered as soon as travel is considered.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: traveler departs in less than 4 weeks and cannot or will not receive immune globulin
Guideline Action: Still give hepatitis A vaccine and inform the traveler protection may be incomplete for the next 2-4 weeks.
Source Statement: Travelers departing in <4 weeks who do not or cannot receive IG should nonetheless receive hepatitis A vaccine and be informed that they might not be optimally protected from acquiring hepatitis A in the immediate future (i.e., subsequent 2-4 weeks).

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: traveler is allergic to a vaccine component or declines vaccine
Guideline Action: Give a single dose of immune globulin 0.02 mL/kg; if travel lasts more than 2 months, use 0.06 mL/kg and repeat if travel lasts more than 5 months.
Source Statement: Travelers who are allergic to a vaccine component or who elect not to receive vaccine should receive a single dose of IG (0.02 mL/kg), which provides effective protection against hepatitis A for up to 3 months (Table 1). Travelers whose travel period is >2 months should be administered IG at 0.06 mL/kg; administration must be repeated if the travel period is >5 months (Table 1).

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: person is an adolescent or adult man who has sex with men
Guideline Action: Vaccinate against hepatitis A.
Source Statement: MSM (both adolescents and adults) should be vaccinated.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: person uses injection or noninjection illicit drugs
Guideline Action: Vaccinate against hepatitis A.
Source Statement: Vaccination is recommended for users of injection and noninjection illicit drugs.
proposed_actions_text:
- Administer a first hepatitis A vaccine dose immediately because travel is soon.
- Recommend hepatitis A vaccination solely because he is traveling to Peru.
- Advise that vaccine now is needed for immediate partial protection before departure.

patient_vignette: A 29-year-old man comes to a primary care visit 3 weeks before a planned 10-day trip to Cusco, Peru. He has never received hepatitis A vaccine and does not recall prior infection. He is generally healthy, takes no regular medications, and has no vaccine allergies. He asks whether he can still do anything to reduce his risk before leaving and says he prefers the quickest option. On further review, he explains that he is leaving for a private resort stay with bottled water, prearranged meals, and no planned exposure to local community settings.
proposed_actions: Give hepatitis A vaccine immediately because he is traveling to Peru soon.
Tell him protection may be incomplete for the next 2-4 weeks but vaccinate now anyway.
Treat him as needing pre-departure hepatitis A prophylaxis without further assessment.

patient_vignette: Mrs. L is a 78-year-old woman who lives independently in her own home. Over the past 6 months, she has had two near-falls while carrying laundry down the stairs and one minor fall in the kitchen, though she was not seriously injured. She has osteoarthritis and hypertension, takes a diuretic and a sedating sleep aid, and says she feels unsteady when getting up at night. Her daughter notes that the hallway is dimly lit and that loose rugs are present near the bedroom and bathroom. Mrs. L wants to stay active but has become less confident walking outside and has stopped attending her weekly neighborhood walks. At today’s visit, however, she says she has already moved to an assisted-living apartment with staff support and no longer manages her own home environment.
proposed_actions: Order a home visiting program to identify and remove loose rugs and lighting hazards
Treat this as a standard at-home fall-prevention referral
Document home environmental modification as the main intervention
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: older adults aged 65 years or older
Guideline Action: implement fall prevention efforts to reduce injury, disability, and death
Source Statement: Falls are the leading cause of injury deaths and disabilities among persons aged ≥65 years.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: older adults at risk of falling
Guideline Action: use multifaceted fall prevention programs that combine exercise, environmental modifications, education, medication review, and risk factor reduction
Source Statement: Research has established that effective fall intervention programs employ a multifaceted approach and incorporate both behavioral and environmental elements: exercises to improve strength and balance, environmental modifications, education about fall prevention, medication review and assessment to minimize side effects, and risk factor reduction.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: community-dwelling older adults with fall risk factors
Guideline Action: provide behavioral instructions, targeted training, exercise to increase strength, and medication adjustments
Source Statement: A total of 153 persons participated in an intervention that consisted of behavioral instructions and training to reduce specific risk factors ... exercise programs to increase strength, and medication adjustments.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: older adults living in the community
Guideline Action: increase physical activity that improves strength, balance, and coordination
Source Statement: Activities that improve strength, balance, and coordination can reduce the risk for falls and fall-related injuries among healthy and frail persons.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: older adults living at home
Guideline Action: address home hazards and use home-visiting programs to identify and correct fall risks
Source Statement: Because approximately 50%-60% of all falls among older adults occur at home, fall prevention programs should address home hazards that can contribute to falls.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: older adults with chronic diseases or hip fracture risk factors
Guideline Action: maximize control of concomitant chronic diseases and counsel against inappropriate weight loss
Source Statement: Medical approaches might include reducing fall risk factors (e.g., maximizing control of concomitant chronic diseases) and reducing hip fracture risk factors (e.g., counseling women aged ≥65 years against inappropriate weight loss).

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: nursing-home residents at high risk of falls
Guideline Action: implement intensive multifaceted interventions including environmental modifications, medication review, and attention to individual needs
Source Statement: A randomized trial ... evaluated an intensive, multifaceted intervention of extensive environmental modifications ... medication review, and increased attention to individual resident needs.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: women starting an exercise program
Guideline Action: start slowly at a level matched to current physical fitness and set realistic goals
Source Statement: Women starting exercise programs should be realistic about their goals and start slowly at frequency, duration, and intensity levels commensurate with their current physical fitness condition.
proposed_actions_text:
- Order a home visiting program to identify and remove loose rugs and lighting hazards
- Treat this as a standard at-home fall-prevention referral
- Document home environmental modification as the main intervention

patient_vignette: Mrs. L is a 78-year-old woman who lives independently in her own home. Over the past 6 months, she has had two near-falls while carrying laundry down the stairs and one minor fall in the kitchen, though she was not seriously injured. She has osteoarthritis and hypertension, takes a diuretic and a sedating sleep aid, and says she feels unsteady when getting up at night. Her daughter notes that the hallway is dimly lit and that loose rugs are present near the bedroom and bathroom. Mrs. L wants to stay active but has become less confident walking outside and has stopped attending her weekly neighborhood walks. She also reports a 14-pound unintentional weight loss over the past 8 months after starting a restrictive diet prescribed for a newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes program.
proposed_actions: Counsel her to stop the diet because of fracture risk
Apply the chronic-disease/weight-loss recommendation as written
Recommend weight gain solely to reduce falls

patient_vignette: A 15-year-old student is brought to a school health planning meeting after the district notices inconsistent food practices across campuses. The middle school is drafting a new nutrition policy because vending machines, classroom celebrations, and fundraisers currently vary by teacher and club. Parents have asked for healthier options, while students want foods that fit cultural preferences and after-school sports schedules. The school nurse reports that some teachers use candy as rewards, and the PTA is considering a fundraiser built around soda and chips. Administrators want a policy that can be rolled out this semester but also updated after feedback from families, staff, and students. The team is looking for a practical plan that supports healthy eating, but the draft proposal would be limited to the middle school only and would not be shared with the school health advisory council because the district wants a quick rollout before the semester ends.
proposed_actions: Approve the draft as a coordinated school nutrition policy without further stakeholder review.
Use the existing administrators and nurse alone to finalize the policy and promulgate it immediately.
Skip the nutrition advisory committee because the timeline is short.
rules_text:
Rule ID: R001
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: planning school food environment
Guideline Action: Discourage the sale of foods high in fat, sodium, and added sugars on school grounds and as part of fundraising activities.
Source Statement: In addition, schools should discourage the sale of foods high in fat, sodium, and added sugars (e.g., candy, fried chips, and soda) on school grounds and as part of fundraising activities.

Rule ID: R002
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 80
Recommendation Class: NOT_RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: teachers use food in classroom management
Guideline Action: Discourage teachers from using food to reward or discipline students.
Source Statement: Schools should discourage teachers from using food for disciplining or rewarding students.

Rule ID: R003
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: school-based nutrition education is being planned or implemented
Guideline Action: Use a comprehensive school health program to promote healthy eating.
Source Statement: School-based programs can play an important role in promoting lifelong healthy eating.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: developing school nutrition policy
Guideline Action: Adopt a coordinated school nutrition policy that promotes healthy eating through classroom lessons and a supportive school environment.
Source Statement: Policy: Adopt a coordinated school nutrition policy that promotes healthy eating through classroom lessons and a supportive school environment.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: developing school nutrition policy
Guideline Action: Include input from students, teachers, coaches, staff, administrators, food service personnel, nurses, counselors, public health professionals, and parents.
Source Statement: A school nutrition policy should be a brief document that incorporates input from all relevant constituents of the school community: students, teachers, coaches, staff, administrators, food service personnel, nurses, counselors, public health professionals, and parents.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: developing school nutrition policy
Guideline Action: Adapt the policy to local needs and the health concerns, food preferences, and dietary practices of different ethnic and socioeconomic groups.
Source Statement: The policy should meet local needs and be adapted to the health concerns, food preferences, and dietary practices of different ethnic and socioeconomic groups.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: developing school nutrition policy
Guideline Action: Include follow-up input and a process for revising the policy as needed.
Source Statement: The policy plan should include means of obtaining follow-up input from all parties and means of revising the plan as needed.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: developing school nutrition policy
Guideline Action: Use a nutrition advisory committee or nutrition subcommittee with student members to develop and promulgate the policy.
Source Statement: A nutrition advisory committee or a nutrition subcommittee of the school health advisory council having student members can develop and promulgate a coordinated school nutrition policy.
proposed_actions_text:
- Approve the draft as a coordinated school nutrition policy without further stakeholder review.
- Use the existing administrators and nurse alone to finalize the policy and promulgate it immediately.
- Skip the nutrition advisory committee because the timeline is short.

patient_vignette: A 15-year-old student is brought to a school health planning meeting after the district notices inconsistent food practices across campuses. The middle school is drafting a new nutrition policy because vending machines, classroom celebrations, and fundraisers currently vary by teacher and club. Parents have asked for healthier options, while students want foods that fit cultural preferences and after-school sports schedules. The school nurse reports that some teachers use candy as rewards, and the PTA is considering a fundraiser built around soda and chips. Administrators want a policy that can be rolled out this semester but also updated after feedback from families, staff, and students. The team is looking for a practical plan that supports healthy eating, but the current draft sets a one-size-fits-all menu and explicitly says it will not be revised after implementation because the district plans to keep the language identical across all campuses for at least three years.
proposed_actions: Treat the draft as an acceptable district nutrition policy and implement it unchanged.
Assume the same policy language can be copied to all campuses without modification.
Finalize the plan now and defer any feedback or revision until several years later.

Source Statement: School-based programs can play an important role in promoting lifelong healthy eating.

Rule ID: R004
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: developing school nutrition policy
Guideline Action: Adopt a coordinated school nutrition policy that promotes healthy eating through classroom lessons and a supportive school environment.
Source Statement: Policy: Adopt a coordinated school nutrition policy that promotes healthy eating through classroom lessons and a supportive school environment.

Rule ID: R005
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: developing school nutrition policy
Guideline Action: Include input from students, teachers, coaches, staff, administrators, food service personnel, nurses, counselors, public health professionals, and parents.
Source Statement: A school nutrition policy should be a brief document that incorporates input from all relevant constituents of the school community: students, teachers, coaches, staff, administrators, food service personnel, nurses, counselors, public health professionals, and parents.

Rule ID: R006
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: developing school nutrition policy
Guideline Action: Adapt the policy to local needs and the health concerns, food preferences, and dietary practices of different ethnic and socioeconomic groups.
Source Statement: The policy should meet local needs and be adapted to the health concerns, food preferences, and dietary practices of different ethnic and socioeconomic groups.

Rule ID: R007
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: developing school nutrition policy
Guideline Action: Include follow-up input and a process for revising the policy as needed.
Source Statement: The policy plan should include means of obtaining follow-up input from all parties and means of revising the plan as needed.

Rule ID: R008
Scenario: GENERAL
Priority: 70
Recommendation Class: RECOMMENDED
Activation Condition: developing school nutrition policy
Guideline Action: Use a nutrition advisory committee or nutrition subcommittee with student members to develop and promulgate the policy.
Source Statement: A nutrition advisory committee or a nutrition subcommittee of the school health advisory council having student members can develop and promulgate a coordinated school nutrition policy.
